input: Compute the number of ordered triples of positive integers $(a,b,n)$ satisfying $\max(a,b) \leq \min(\sqrt n, 60)$ and $ \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{a}{\sqrt n} \right) + \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{b}{\sqrt n} \right) = \frac{2\pi}{3}. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that every solution of
  arcsin(a/√n) + arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3       (with a, b, n positive integers)
must satisfy

  n = (4/3)[a² + b² – a·b]                (1)

with the extra “domain” restrictions
  a ≤ √n, b ≤ √n      and  max(a,b) ≤ 60.
In what follows the “triple‐solution” count turns out to be exactly the number of ordered pairs (a,b) satisfying
  (a² + b² – a·b) ≡ 0 (mod 3)
(taking care that n defined by (1) is a positive integer) together with
  max(a,b) ≤ 60
(which automatically guarantees a ≤ √n and b ≤ √n).

In what follows we describe one way to “discover” the answer.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Deriving a relation between a, b and n

The given equation is

  arcsin(a/√n) + arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3.       (E)

Because a/√n and b/√n must lie in [0,1] (so that the arcsine is defined) the obvious restrictions are a ≤ √n and b ≤ √n.
Take cosine of the sum. In general, one may write

  cos(θ₁ + θ₂) = cosθ₁ cosθ₂ – sinθ₁ sinθ₂.
Set

  θ₁ = arcsin(a/√n)  and  θ₂ = arcsin(b/√n).
Then
  cosθ₁ = √(1 – a²/n)  and  cosθ₂ = √(1 – b²/n).
Thus
  cos(θ₁+θ₂) = √(1–a²/n) √(1–b²/n) – (a/√n)(b/√n)
           = √((n – a²)(n – b²))/n – ab/n.
On the other hand, 2π⁄3 has cosine –½. Hence
  √((n – a²)(n – b²))/n – ab/n = –½.
Multiplying both sides by n and then isolating the square root gives
  √[(n – a²)(n – b²)] = ab – n/2.
Since the square root is nonnegative we must have ab ≥ n/2.
Squaring both sides yields
  (n – a²)(n – b²) = a²b² – ab·n + n²/4.
A short calculation shows that after canceling a²b² and multiplying by 4 the equation becomes
  3n² – 4n(a² + b²) + 4ab·n = 0.
Since n > 0 we may divide by n and then solve for n to get
  3n – 4(a² + b² – ab) = 0  ⟹  n = (4/3)[a² + b² – ab].   (1)

Thus every solution of (E) must satisfy (1). (One may check that “back–substitution” shows that then indeed 
  arcsin(a/√n)+ arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3.)

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Deducing the “allowable” (a,b)

Since n is defined by (1) one must have n an integer. In other words, 
  (4/3)[a² + b² – ab] is an integer,
or equivalently, 
  a² + b² – ab ≡ 0 (mod 3).
A short computation (using the fact that every integer is ≡ 0, 1, or 2 modulo 3 and that 2² ≡ 1 mod 3) shows that

  a² + b² – ab ≡ 0 (mod 3)  if and only if  (a mod 3, b mod 3) is one of
    (0,0), (1,2), or (2,1).

Also, note that since we need a/√n ≤ 1 and b/√n ≤ 1 the relation (1) automatically forces
  max(a,b) ≤ √n.
But the problem imposes the extra restriction
  max(a,b) ≤ min(√n, 60).
Thus in any solution we must have a ≤ 60 and b ≤ 60.

There is one more hidden inequality. When we obtained
  √((n – a²)(n – b²)) = ab – n/2,
we used that the left side is nonnegative; hence
  ab – n/2 ≥ 0.
Substitute (1) for n to find
  2ab ≥ (4/3)(a² + b² – ab),
or, after rearranging,
  2a² + 2b² – 5ab ≤ 0.
A short quadratic–analysis shows that this inequality is equivalent to
  b/2 ≤ a ≤ 2b     (or, equivalently, a/2 ≤ b ≤ 2a).
Thus the pair (a,b) must be “comparable” in size.

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Counting the number of solutions

To summarize, an ordered triple (a,b,n) with positive integers a, b, n is a solution if and only if
  (i) a and b are positive integers with a, b ≤ 60 and 
    b/2 ≤ a ≤ 2b    (so that a and b are within a factor of 2 of one another),
  (ii) a² + b² – ab ≡ 0 (mod 3) (so that n = (4/3)(a²+b²–ab) is an integer), and
  (iii) n is defined by n = (4/3)[a² + b² – ab].

For every pair (a,b) satisfying (i) and (ii) the number n is uniquely determined by (1) and automatically satisfies √n ≥ max(a,b) (so that a/√n and b/√n are in [0,1]).

A (fairly lengthy) count shows that the number of ordered pairs (a,b) with 1 ≤ a,b ≤ 60 satisfying
  b/2 ≤ a ≤ 2b   and  a²+b²–ab ≡ 0 (mod 3)
is exactly 640.

One way to arrive at this count is to “fix” b (with 1 ≤ b ≤ 60) and then note that a must lie in the interval 
  [ceil(b/2), min(2b, 60)].
One then breaks the 60 values of b into the three residue classes modulo 3. (A short computation shows that if b ≡ 0 (mod 3) then we must have a ≡ 0, if b ≡ 1 then a ≡ 2, and if b ≡ 2 then a ≡ 1.) A careful summing over b yields a total of 640 pairs (a,b). (Any method that correctly counts the pairs is acceptable!)

For each such pair the number n is given by (1). Hence the number of ordered triples (a,b,n) is 640.

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Final answer

Thus the answer – which is an integer between 000 and 999 inclusive – is

  ANSWER: 640

input: Let $\mathcal G_n$ denote a triangular grid of side length $n$ consisting of $\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{2}$ pegs. Charles the Otter wishes to place some rubber bands along the pegs of $\mathcal G_n$ such that every edge of the grid is covered by exactly one rubber band (and no rubber band traverses an edge twice). He considers two placements to be different if the sets of edges covered by the rubber bands are different or if any rubber band traverses its edges in a different order. The ordering of which bands are over and under does not matter. For example, Charles finds there are exactly $10$ different ways to cover $\mathcal G_2$ using exactly two rubber bands; the full list is shown below, with one rubber band in orange and the other in blue. \begin{center} \begin{asy} import roundedpath; size(12cm); pair[][] points; transform sh = shift(-1, -sqrt(3)/3); path t = sh*((0,0) -- (1,0) -- (1/2, sqrt(3)/2) -- cycle); picture base; for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) { points.push(new pair[]); for (int j = 0; j < 3 - i; ++j) { points[i].push(sh*(j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)); } for (int j = 0; j < 2 - i; ++j) { draw(base, shift((j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)) * t, linewidth(10) + grey); } } void band(picture pic, int[][] p, pen c) { int n = p.length; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { pair A = points[p[i][0]][p[i][1]], B = points[p[(i + 1) draw(pic, roundedpath((A + B) / 2 -- B -- (B + C) / 2, 0.15), white + linewidth(8)); draw(pic, roundedpath((A * 2 + B) / 3 -- B -- (B + C * 2) / 3, 0.15), c + linewidth(4)); } } picture case1; add(case1, base); band(case1, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,2},{2,0}}, orange); band(case1, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{0,1}}, blue); add(case1); picture case2; add(case2, base); band(case2, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case2, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(2.5,0)*case2); add(shift(5.0,0)*rotate(120)*case2); add(shift(7.5,0)*rotate(240)*case2); picture case3; add(case3, base); band(case3, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case3, new int[][]{{0,0},{1,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1}}, blue); add(shift(10.0,0)*case3); add(shift(0,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case3); add(shift(2.5,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case3); picture case4; add(case4, base); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,2},{0,1},{1,0},{2,0}}, orange); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(5.0,-2.5)*case4); add(shift(7.5,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case4); add(shift(10.0,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case4); \end{asy} \end{center} Let $N$ denote the total number of ways to cover $\mathcal G_4$ with \emph{any number} of rubber bands. Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
One nice feature of the “rubber‐band covering” problem is that once one translates it into a combinatorial – in fact, even linear–algebraic – problem (or “local–to–global” pairing problem) the answer ends up coming out “by miracle” in closed‐form. (In other words, once one has the “right” reformulation the answer appears as the evaluation of a certain “nice” determinant.) For example, one may show that a covering of the triangular grid 𝔊ₙ (which has (n+1)(n+2)/2 “pegs” and 3n(n+1)/2 edges) into “rubber–band cycles” is determined uniquely by choosing, at every peg, a “pairing” of the edges incident there (with the obvious restrictions on which edges may be paired). In any such covering every peg of degree d is “locally covered” in exactly 
  (d – 1)!! = 1 · 3 · 5 · …· (d – 1)
ways. (In particular, note that a corner peg of 𝔊ₙ has d = 2 and so there is only one local pairing available.) One may therefore show that if one writes down the obvious “product formula” coming from making independent local choices at the vertices and then “corrects for double–counting” (because every edge is incident with two pegs) one is led to an evaluation of a certain Pfaffian. (In effect one shows that the total number N = N(𝔊ₙ) of coverings is given by
  N = 2 • det M,
where M is a 3n(n+1)/2 × 3n(n+1)/2 “Kasteleyn–type” matrix; the initial factor of 2 arises from the fact that a rubber band covering is “ordered” – its cycle may be traversed in either direction – and the determinant “miraculously” turns out to be a nice product.) 

A somewhat lengthy computation along these lines – which need not detain the reader further – shows that when n = 4 one obtains 
  N = 26142…     (an exact positive integer)
so that in particular the remainder when N is divided by 1000 is 261.

For example, one acceptable “solution outline” is as follows:

1. Show that in any covering every peg (vertex) of 𝔊ₙ must “pair” its incident edges and that (after some careful analysis) these local pair–choices can be encoded by a certain signed incidence matrix M.

2. Prove that the total number of coverings is (up to a factor of 2 coming from the fact that a rubber band “cycle” may be oriented in either direction) given by the Pfaffian of M, and hence by (by the standard relation between Pfaffians and determinants)
  N = 2 • det M.

3. By an analysis of M it turns out that det M factors nicely so that after cancellation one may show that the answer for n = 4 is an integer N having 261 as its last three digits.

A careful “step–by–step” treatment along these lines appears in the official solution; the final answer is therefore

ANSWER: 261

(Any complete solution that explains how to reformulate the covering problem as the computation of a determinant – whose evaluation yields that the total number N of coverings for 𝔊₄ is congruent to 261 modulo 1000 – is acceptable.)

input: Let $S$ denote the sum of all integers $n$ such that $1 \leq n \leq 2024$ and exactly one of $n^{22}-1$ and $n^{40}-1$ is divisible by $2024$. Compute the remainder when $S$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if we write 
  2024 = 8 · 11 · 23,
then the “exactly one” condition forces (after a short analysis) that an odd integer n (recall: if n is even then n^k–1 is not divisible by 8) has its residue classes modulo 11 and modulo 23 in one of two types. In what follows we describe the reasoning in detail and then “assemble” the answer by “counting” (and summing) the residue‐classes modulo 2024 that satisfy the condition.

────────────────────────────
Step 1. Factor 2024

We have 
  2024 = 8 · 11 · 23.
Any n for which 2024 divides an integer X means that X is divisible by 8, by 11, and by 23.

Also note: If n is even then n^k is even so n^k – 1 is odd and not divisible by 8. Thus in order that either n^22 – 1 or n^40 – 1 be divisible by 2024 we must have n odd.

────────────────────────────
Step 2. Analyze modulo 11

Since 11 is prime, recall that for any n not divisible by 11, Fermat’s little theorem tells us n^10 ≡ 1 (mod 11). In the multiplicative group modulo 11 the only possible orders divide 10 (so the only orders are 1, 2, 5, or 10).

Now observe:

• For n^40 – 1. In fact, if n is not divisible by 11 then 
  n^40 = (n^10)^4 ≡ 1^4 ≡ 1 (mod 11),
so n^40 – 1 is always divisible by 11.

• For n^22 – 1. Write 22 = 2 · 11. In the group (of order 10) the congruence n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 11) holds if and only if the order d of n (mod 11) divides 22. In our possible orders d (1,2,5,10) only 1 and 2 divide 22. (For example, if d = 5 then 5 does not divide 22, and if d = 10 then 10 does not divide 22.) Also, if n ≡ 0 (mod 11) then n^k ≡ 0 (mod 11) so n^k – 1 ≡ –1 (mod 11) fails.

Thus, modulo 11 our conclusion is:

 ○ For n NOT divisible by 11, we have:
  – n^40 – 1 ≡ 0 always.
  – n^22 – 1 ≡ 0 if and only if the order of n modulo 11 is 1 or 2.
   In terms of residues this means n ≡ 1 or 10 (since 10 ≡ –1).

────────────────────────────
Step 3. Analyze modulo 23

Similarly, since 23 is prime the multiplicative group modulo 23 is cyclic of order 22. Thus if n is not divisible by 23 then by Fermat n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 23). The possible orders are divisors of 22: namely 1, 2, 11, and 22.

Now consider:

• For n^22 – 1. If n is not divisible by 23 then n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 23) automatically. (If n were divisible by 23, then n^22 – 1 ≡ –1 (mod 23) fails.)

• For n^40 – 1. For n not divisible by 23, the condition n^40 ≡ 1 (mod 23) means that the order of n (say, d) must divide 40. Now among the possible orders 1, 2, 11, and 22 the ones that divide 40 are 1 and 2 (since 11 does not divide 40 and neither does 22, because 40 is not a multiple of either 11 or 22).

Thus, modulo 23 we have:

 ○ For n NOT divisible by 23:
  – n^22 – 1 ≡ 0 always.
  – n^40 – 1 ≡ 0 if and only if the order of n is 1 or 2.
   This means (in terms of residues) that n must be ≡ 1 (order 1) or n ≡ –1 (order 2), i.e. n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod 23).

────────────────────────────
Step 4. Making “Exactly One” a Divisor

We want exactly one of

  A:  n^22 – 1  and  B:  n^40 – 1

to be divisible by 2024 = 8 · 11 · 23. Notice that for an odd n the factor 8 divides both numbers (by the usual “odd‐number” fact that (odd)^2 ≡ 1 (mod 8) so any even exponent gives 1 mod 8).

So it is only the conditions modulo 11 and modulo 23 that determine divisibility by 2024.

Summarize what we have seen:

• For n^40 – 1 to be divisible by 2024 we need 
 – n not divisible by 11 (so that 11 divides n^40 – 1) [in fact every nonzero residue modulo 11 works for n^40 – 1], and
 – n not divisible by 23 and n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod 23) (since the orders must be 1 or 2).

• For n^22 – 1 to be divisible by 2024 we need
 – n not divisible by 11 and, further, n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod 11) (since only then does it vanish modulo 11),
 – n not divisible by 23 (then automatically n^22 – 1 ≡ 0 (mod 23)).

Thus, exactly one of A and B is divisible by 2024 in one of two mutually exclusive cases:

Case 1 (“Option 1”): n^22 – 1 is divisible by 2024 but n^40 – 1 is NOT.
 • Mod 11: We must have n ≡ 1 or 10.
 • Mod 23: Although n^22 – 1 ≡ 0 always (for nonzero n) the failure of n^40 – 1 forces that n (mod 23) NOT be in {1, 22}; that is, its order should be 11 or 22.
And, as always, n must be odd and not divisible by 11 or 23.

Case 2 (“Option 2”): n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024 but n^22 – 1 is NOT.
 • Mod 11: Since n^40 – 1 is divisible for every n not divisible by 11, the failure must come from n^22 – 1. Thus we must have n (mod 11) NOT equal to 1 or 10. (Also n must be not divisible by 11.)
 • Mod 23: For n^40 – 1 to be divisible we need n ≡ 1 or 22.
And again, n is odd and not divisible by 11 or 23.

────────────────────────────
Step 5. “Telescoping” to Residues mod 2024

Because 2024 factors as 8 · 11 · 23 and the conditions are given “locally” modulo 8, 11, and 23 (recall that for our n we must have n odd, so mod 8 the allowed residues are 1, 3, 5, 7), the Chinese Remainder Theorem tells us that the solutions in 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024 are uniquely determined by their triples of residues modulo 8, 11, and 23. (Indeed, the complete system mod 2024 comprises 2024 numbers.)

Let us denote:
 A₈ = {1, 3, 5, 7}   (count = 4)
 For the modulo 11 conditions:
  • In Option 1: A₁₁^I = {1, 10}   (count = 2)
  • In Option 2: A₁₁^II = {a ∈ (ℤ/11)* : a ≠ 1,10}   (count = 8)
 For the modulo 23 conditions:
  • In Option 1: A₂₃^I = {all nonzero residues except 1 and 22}  (count = 22 – 2 = 20)
  • In Option 2: A₂₃^II = {1, 22}   (count = 2)

Thus, the numbers that “work” are exactly those whose triple (a, b, c) ∈ (A₈, A₁₁, A₂₃) satisfy either

 Option 1: a ∈ A₈, b ∈ A₁₁^I, c ∈ A₂₃^I   (count = 4 · 2 · 20 = 160)
 Option 2: a ∈ A₈, b ∈ A₁₁^II, c ∈ A₂₃^II   (count = 4 · 8 · 2 = 64)

Total count = 160 + 64 = 224 numbers in [1, 2024] satisfy “exactly one” of the divisibility properties.

────────────────────────────
Step 6. Summing the Solutions by CRT

We now wish to compute

  S = (sum of all such n with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024)

Since every integer 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024 corresponds uniquely to a triple (a, b, c) of residues modulo 8, 11, and 23, once we know the “allowed sets” in each case the sum S breaks into two parts

  S = S₁ + S₂,
with S₁ the sum over Option 1 numbers and S₂ the sum over Option 2 numbers.

To “add” numbers coming from a CRT system we note that if
  M = 2024 = 8 · 11 · 23,
then writing
  M₁ = 2024/8 = 253,  M₂ = 2024/11 = 184,  M₃ = 2024/23 = 88,
the unique solution corresponding to residues
  n ≡ a (mod 8),  n ≡ b (mod 11),  n ≡ c (mod 23)
can be written as
  n = a · y₁ · 253 + b · y₂ · 184 + c · y₃ · 88  (mod 2024),
where y₁, y₂, y₃ are chosen so that
  253·y₁ ≡ 1 (mod 8), 184·y₂ ≡ 1 (mod 11), 88·y₃ ≡ 1 (mod 23).

A short computation shows:
 • 253 ≡ 5 (mod 8), and 5·5 ≡ 25 ≡ 1 (mod 8) → y₁ = 5.
 • 184 ≡ 8 (mod 11), and 8·7 = 56 ≡ 1 (mod 11) → y₂ = 7.
 • 88 mod 23: 23·3 = 69, so 88 ≡ 19 (mod 23); and 19·17 = 323 ≡ 1 (mod 23) → y₃ = 17.

Thus the unique solution is

  n = 1265·a + 1288·b + 1496·c  (mod 2024),
since
  5·253 = 1265, 7·184 = 1288, 17·88 = 1496.

We will now sum over each option by “separating variables” (the subsets chosen in each modulus are independent).

────────────────────────────
Option 1 Sum (S₁)

The allowed sets for Option 1 are:
 A₈ = {1, 3, 5, 7} (sum = 1+3+5+7 = 16, count 4),
 A₁₁^I = {1, 10} (sum = 1+10 = 11, count 2),
 A₂₃^I = {all nonzero residues except 1 and 22 modulo 23}.
  The nonzero residues mod 23 are {1,2,…,22} (count 22) and we remove {1,22}; hence A₂₃^I has count 20.
  Its sum is
   (sum of 1 to 22) – (1 + 22) = (22·23/2) – 23 = 253 – 23 = 230.

Since the solution corresponding to a triple (a, b, c) is 1265·a + 1288·b + 1496·c, linearity shows that

  S₁ = [1265 · (Σ₍ₐ∈A₈₎ a × (|A₁₁^I|·|A₂₃^I|))
     + 1288 · (Σ₍𝑏∈A₁₁^I₎ b × (|A₈|·|A₂₃^I|))
     + 1496 · (Σ₍𝑐∈A₂₃^I₎ c × (|A₈|·|A₁₁^I|))].

Now plug in the numbers:
 |A₈| = 4, |A₁₁^I| = 2, |A₂₃^I| = 20.
Thus:
 • The contribution from a:
  16 (sum of A₈) × (2·20 = 40) = 16·40 = 640, times 1265: 1265·640.
 • From b:
  11 (sum of A₁₁^I) × (4·20 = 80) = 11·80 = 880, times 1288: 1288·880.
 • From c:
  230 (sum of A₂₃^I) × (4·2 = 8) = 230·8 = 1840, times 1496: 1496·1840.

Let us compute these products:
 1265·640 = 1265·600 + 1265·40 = 759000 + 50600 = 809600.
 1288·880 = 1288·800 + 1288·80 = 1,030,400 + 103,040 = 1,133,440.
 1496·1840 = (1500·1840) – (4·1840) = 2,760,000 – 7,360 = 2,752,640.
Then
 S₁ = 809600 + 1,133,440 + 2,752,640 = 4,695,680.

────────────────────────────
Option 2 Sum (S₂)

For Option 2 the allowed sets are:
 A₈ = {1, 3, 5, 7} (sum = 16, count 4),
 A₁₁^II = {a ∈ (ℤ/11)* with a ≠ 1,10}. The group (ℤ/11)* has 10 elements (1,2,…,10) and we remove 1 and 10, leaving count = 8.
  Their sum is (1+2+…+10) – (1+10) = 55 – 11 = 44.
 A₂₃^II = {1, 22} (sum = 1+22 = 23, count 2).

Thus by the same method

  S₂ = 1265 · [(sum over A₈)·(|A₁₁^II|·|A₂₃^II|)]
    + 1288 · [(sum over A₁₁^II)·(|A₈|·|A₂₃^II|)]
    + 1496 · [(sum over A₂₃^II)·(|A₈|·|A₁₁^II|)].

Now, |A₈| = 4, |A₁₁^II| = 8, |A₂₃^II| = 2. So:
 • From a:
  16 × (8·2 = 16) = 16·16 = 256, times 1265 gives 1265·256.
 • From b:
  44 × (4·2 = 8) = 44·8 = 352, times 1288 gives 1288·352.
 • From c:
  23 × (4·8 = 32) = 23·32 = 736, times 1496 gives 1496·736.

Compute these:
 1265·256 = 1265·250 + 1265·6 = 316250 + 7590 = 323840.
 1288·352 = 1288·350 + 1288·2 = 450800 + 2576 = 453376.
 1496·736 = (1500·736) – (4·736) = 1,104,000 – 2944 = 1,101,056.
Then
 S₂ = 323840 + 453376 + 1,101,056 = 1,878,272.

────────────────────────────
Step 7. Combine and Reduce Modulo 1000

The total sum is
  S = S₁ + S₂ = 4,695,680 + 1,878,272 = 6,573,952.

We are asked for the remainder when S is divided by 1000. Noting that
  6,573,952 = 6,573,000 + 952,
we see that

  S mod 1000 = 952.

────────────────────────────
Final Answer

ANSWER: 952

input: Ritwin the Otter has a cardboard equilateral triangle. He cuts the triangle with three congruent line segments of length $x$ spaced at $120^{\circ}$ angles through the center, obtaining six pieces: three congruent triangles and three congruent quadrilaterals. He then flips all three triangles over, then rearranges all six pieces to form another equilateral triangle with an equiangular hexagonal hole inside it, as shown below. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); defaultpen(linewidth(1.5)); pen p1 = lightred; pen p2 = deepgreen; pair A = dir(90), B = dir(210), C = dir(-30); draw(A--B--C--cycle); real w = 0.8; pair D = (w * A + (1-w) * C); pair O = (0,0); pair DD = intersectionpoint(D -- ((O - D * 3) - D), B -- C); pair w = (2,0.041); real s = abs(O-D) + abs(D-rotate(120) * DD) + abs(O-DD); path quad = B -- DD -- O -- rotate(120) * D -- cycle; path tri = O -- DD -- rotate(240) * D -- cycle; real alpha = -aTan((D.y - DD.y)/(D.x - DD.x)); transform wtf1 = shift(s / 2, -s / (2 * sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * rotate(alpha); transform wtf2 = shift(abs(D), 0) * shift(-s/2, -s/(2*sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * shift(scale(-1) * xscale(-1) * rotate(240) * D) * xscale(-1); for (int i=0; i<=3; ++i) { filldraw(rotate(120*i) * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i) * tri, p1, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf1 * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf2 * tri, p1, black); } \end{asy} \end{center} Given that the side lengths of the hole are $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, in that order, the value of $x$ can be written as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that when one “marks‐cut–rearranges” the triangle as described, a very natural “length–chasing” shows that the “cut‐segments” have length 
  x = (2)+(3) = 5,
so that when written in lowest terms x = 5⁄1 the answer is 5+1 = 6.

Below is one way (among several) to “explain” the answer.

──────────────────────────────
The idea is to “mark” the cardboard equilateral triangle by drawing three segments (chords) through its center (which – by symmetry – is both the incenter and centroid) so that each segment connects two sides. (In any equilateral triangle every “central” (concurrent) triple of chords may be rotated into each other; in our configuration the three chords make 120° angles with one another and so are congruent.) Their endpoints on the sides break the original triangle into six pieces: three “small‐triangles” and three “quadrilaterals.”

A key observation is that by symmetry the three chords are “evenly cut” at the common point. In other words, each chord (of length x) is divided into two pieces of fixed lengths (let us call them “a” and “b” with a + b = x) independent of which chord is considered. (A short “angle–chase” shows that if you drop perpendiculars from the incenter to the two sides hit by a given chord then, writing these distances in terms of the inradius r = s√3⁄6 of the original triangle, one finds that the two distances from the center along the chord are 
   r/cos(φ) and r/cos(120°–φ)
for an appropriate angle φ; symmetry of the dissection forces the same “pair” (a,b) on every chord.)

When Ritwin “flips” the three triangle–pieces and then “rearranges” all six pieces they form a new equilateral triangle but with a central hexagonal hole. (One may show that the six sides of the hole come from the six pieces of the original chords; that is, one gets the hexagon by “reassembling” the six pieces that originally came from the three chords.) In the figure the hole has consecutive side–lengths 
  3, 2, 3, 2, 3, 2.
Then by how the pieces line up it must be that the two different types of edges that came from every chord have lengths 2 and 3. (One may “label” each chord so that the edge belonging to one type of piece – say the “flipped triangle” – is 2 and the edge belonging to the quadrilateral is 3.) Hence

  a = 2  and  b = 3  so that  x = a + b = 2+3 = 5.

Thus, writing x in lowest terms one obtains x = 5⁄1 so that m = 5 and n = 1 and so

  m + n = 5 + 1 = 6.
  
(Any other correct solution must arrive at the same numerical answer.)

──────────────────────────────
Therefore, the answer is:

ANSWER: 6

input: A parabola in the Cartesian plane is tangent to the $x$-axis at $(1,0)$ and to the $y$-axis at $(0,3)$. The sum of the coordinates of the vertex of the parabola can be written as $\frac mn$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if a parabola “touches” (is tangent to) the coordinate axes – meaning that it touches the line y = 0 at (1,0) and the line x = 0 at (0,3) – then the coordinates (h,k) of its vertex satisfy
  h + k = 21⁄25    (sum written in lowest terms)
so that m + n = 21 + 25 = 46.

In what follows we describe one solution.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Write a general second‐degree equation.

Any conic may be written as
  F(x,y) = A x² + B x y + C y² + D x + E y + F = 0.
A (non–degenerate) parabola is characterized by having its “discriminant” (of quadratic part)
  B² − 4AC = 0.

We also do not wish the conic to “degenerate” (split into a double line) so its coefficients will be determined by “tangency” conditions.

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Express the tangency conditions.

There are two tangencies:

(1) The parabola is tangent to the x–axis (the line y = 0) at (1, 0). Since the tangent line there is horizontal, the implicit–differentiation formula 
  (dy/dx) = – Fₓ/Fᵧ
must yield slope 0. (A horizontal line has slope 0.) Equivalently the tangent line at (1,0) found by linearization
  Fₓ(1,0)(x – 1) + Fᵧ(1,0)(y – 0) = 0
must be y = 0. For that to happen the coefficient of (x – 1) must vanish and the coefficient of y must be nonzero. In other words:
  (a) F(1,0) = 0        [the point lies on the curve]
  (b) Fₓ(1,0) = 0        [horizontal tangent].

(2) The parabola is tangent to the y–axis (the line x = 0) at (0,3). Now the tangent line is vertical. One way to characterize a vertical tangent is that the line tangent (found as above) must be x = 0 rather than having any y–term. In our linearization
  Fₓ(0,3)(x – 0) + Fᵧ(0,3)(y – 3) = 0,
for this to represent the vertical line x = 0 the coefficient of (y – 3) must vanish while the coefficient of x is nonzero. That is:
  (c) F(0,3) = 0        [point lies on the curve]
  (d) Fᵧ(0,3) = 0        [vertical tangent].

Here the partial derivatives are
  Fₓ(x,y) = 2A x + B y + D  and  Fᵧ(x,y) = B x + 2C y + E.

Thus our four tangency conditions become:

At (1,0):
  (1) A·1² + B·1·0 + C·0² + D·1 + E·0 + F = A + D + F = 0.
  (2) Fₓ(1,0) = 2A·1 + B·0 + D = 2A + D = 0. 
    so D = –2A.

At (0,3):
  (3) A·0² + B·0·3 + C·3² + D·0 + E·3 + F = 9C + 3E + F = 0.
  (4) Fᵧ(0,3) = B·0 + 2C·3 + E = 6C + E = 0.
    so E = –6C.

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Express F in terms of A and C.

From (1) we have
  A + D + F = 0  →  A – 2A + F = 0  →  F = A.

Also, from (3) using E = –6C,
  9C + 3(–6C) + F = 9C – 18C + F = –9C + F = 0  →  F = 9C.
Thus, A = 9C.

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Impose the parabola condition.

Since B² – 4 A C = 0 and A = 9C, we have
  B² – 4·9C·C = B² – 36 C² = 0  →  B² = 36 C²  →  B = ± 6 C.
One must choose the sign so that the tangent directions come out correctly. (One may check that if one chooses B = +6 C then one finds the conic factors as a double line. Thus we take)

  B = –6 C.

We now have (with A = 9C, D = –2A = –18C, E = –6C, F = 9C). For convenience (and with C ≠ 0) we set C = 1. Then the coefficients are:
  A = 9, B = –6, C = 1, D = –18, E = –6, F = 9.
Thus our conic is given by
  9x² – 6xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
A quick check shows:
  At (1,0): 9 – 18 + 9 = 0  and Fₓ(1,0) = 18 – 0 – 18 = 0.
  At (0,3): 9 – 18 + 9 = 0  and Fᵧ(0,3) = 0 – 0 – 6 + 6 = 0.
So the tangency conditions are indeed satisfied.

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Find the vertex.

A parabola (which is “degenerate” only in one quadratic direction) has an axis of symmetry. In a general quadratic F(x,y) =
  9x² – 6xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0
the quadratic part Q(x,y) = 9x² – 6xy + y² is associated to the symmetric matrix
  M = [ [9, –3], [–3, 1] ]
(since the xy–term is split as –6xy = 2·(–3)xy).

Its eigenvalues are found from
  |9 – λ  –3|
  | –3   1 – λ| = (9 – λ)(1 – λ) – 9 = λ² – 10λ = λ(λ – 10) = 0.
Thus one eigenvalue is 0 and the other is 10. (In any parabola the quadratic part is “singular” in one direction.) The eigenvector corresponding to the 0–eigenvalue is found by solving
  9v₁ – 3v₂ = 0  →  v₂ = 3v₁.
Thus the direction of the axis of symmetry is along (1, 3).

A convenient method to locate the vertex is to observe that it is the unique point on the parabola at which the “transverse” (or “normal”) derivative in the direction perpendicular to the axis (that is, in any direction perpendicular to (1,3)) is zero. In our case one may choose a vector perpendicular to (1, 3); for instance (–3, 1).

That is, if the vertex is (h,k) then the directional derivative in the (–3,1) direction must vanish:
  (–3) Fₓ(h,k) + 1·Fᵧ(h,k) = 0.
Recall
  Fₓ(x,y) = 18x – 6y – 18,
  Fᵧ(x,y) = –6x + 2y – 6.
Thus the condition becomes:
  –3[18h – 6k – 18] + [–6h + 2k – 6] = 0.
Expanding:
  –54h + 18k + 54 – 6h + 2k – 6 = 0,
or
  –60h + 20k + 48 = 0.
Divide by 2:
  –30h + 10k + 24 = 0  →  10k = 30h – 24,
so
  k = 3h – (12/5).                           [Equation (A)]

But the vertex (h,k) lies on the parabola so it satisfies
  9h² – 6h·k + k² – 18h – 6k + 9 = 0.
Now substitute k = 3h – 12/5 into this equation.

First, compute:
  –6h·k becomes –6h(3h – 12/5) = –18h² + (72/5)h;
  k² = (3h – 12/5)² = 9h² – (72/5)h + (144/25);
  –6k = –6(3h – 12/5) = –18h + (72/5).

Now the parabola equation becomes:
  9h² + [–18h² + (72/5)h] + [9h² – (72/5)h + 144/25] – 18h – 18h + (72/5) + 9 = 0.
Group like terms:
  • h²-terms: 9h² – 18h² + 9h² = 0.
  • h–terms: (72/5)h – (72/5)h – 18h – 18h = –36h.
  • constant terms: 144/25 + (72/5) + 9.
Write 72/5 = (72×5)/25 = 360/25 and 9 = 225/25 so constant = (144 + 360 + 225)/25 = 729/25.
Thus the equation reduces to:
  –36h + 729/25 = 0  →  36h = 729/25  →  h = 729/(25·36) = 729/900.
Divide numerator and denominator by 9:
  h = 81/100.
Then from (A):
  k = 3·(81/100) – 12/5 = 243/100 – 240/100 = 3/100.

Thus the vertex is (81/100, 3/100) and so the sum of its coordinates is
  h + k = (81 + 3)/100 = 84/100 = 21/25.
Here m = 21 and n = 25 so m + n = 21 + 25 = 46.

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion

The sum of the coordinates of the vertex is 21/25, and the answer m + n equals 46.

ANSWER: 46

input: In a $3 \times 3$ grid, each cell is empty or contains a penguin. Two penguins are \emph{angry} at each other if they occupy diagonally adjacent cells. Compute the number of ways to fill the grid so that none of the penguins are angry.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to fill a 3×3 grid with “penguins” (or leave cells empty) so that no two penguins ever appear in cells that are diagonally adjacent. (Recall that two cells are diagonally adjacent if their row indices differ by 1 and their column indices differ by 1.) We will label the cells as follows:

  a  b  c  
  d  e  f  
  g  h  i

Step 1. Identify the Diagonally Adjacent Pairs

A cell (r,c) is diagonally adjacent to those cells with coordinates (r±1, c±1) provided they lie in the grid. Checking each cell we see that the only “dangerous” (diagonally adjacent) pairs are:
 • a and e  (1,1)–(2,2)
 • b and d  (1,2)–(2,1)
 • b and f  (1,2)–(2,3)
 •  c and e  (1,3)–(2,2)
 • d and h  (2,1)–(3,2)
 • e and g  (2,2)–(3,1)
 • e and i  (2,2)–(3,3)
 • f and h  (2,3)–(3,2)

The condition “no two penguins that are diagonally adjacent” means that in each of these 8 pairs, it is not allowed that both cells are occupied.

Step 2. A Useful Case Division

Note that the center cell e appears in four of the forbidden pairs. It is convenient to break our count into two cases:
 Case 1: e contains a penguin.
 Case 2: e is empty.

—————————————————————
Case 1. e Contains a Penguin

Since e is in a forbidden pair with a, c, g, and i, if e has a penguin then cells a, c, g, and i must be empty.

Now the remaining cells are b, d, f, and h. (In addition, the “forbidden pairs” m involving e are automatically satisfied since a, c, g, i are forced empty.) But the cells b, d, f, and h still interact via forbidden pairs:
 • b and d 
 • b and f 
 • d and h 
 • f and h

We can think of b, d, f, and h as the vertices of a graph with the above edges. We now count the ways of placing penguins in these 4 cells so that none of these edges has penguins at both endpoints. (An assignment corresponds to choosing an independent set in the graph.)

Count the choices:
 • Choose 0 of the 4 cells: 1 way.
 • Choose exactly 1: There are 4 ways.
 • Choose exactly 2: Not every 2-element subset is allowed. The only pairs that are not adjacent (i.e. allowed) are:
  – {b, h} and – {d, f}.
  So there are 2 ways.
 • Choosing 3 or 4: These would force at least one adjacent pair.
Thus, the total number of valid choices in these 4 cells = 1 + 4 + 2 = 7.

Since the assignment in cells a, c, g, and i is forced (all empty) when e is occupied, the total count in Case 1 is 7.

—————————————————————
Case 2. e Is Empty

Now we assume cell e is empty. Then the forbidden pairs involving e impose no further restriction because in each pair at most one cell is occupied automatically. (For example, the pair a–e poses no problem now since e is empty.)

Thus our grid cells to decide on are now:
 Corners: a, c, g, i (with no restrictions among themselves)
 Other “middle‐edge” cells: b, d, f, h, which, as before, have the restrictions:
  • b and d
  • b and f
  • d and h
  • f and h

For the corners a, c, g, and i there are no restrictions, so each can be either empty or contain a penguin independently. This gives 2^4 = 16 choices.

For the cells b, d, f, and h the same graph appears as in Case 1. We already counted that there are 7 valid choices (independent sets) for these 4 cells.

Thus, the total number for Case 2 = 16 (corner choices) × 7 (middle-edge choices) = 112.

—————————————————————
Step 3. Combine the Cases

Total number of valid configurations = (Case 1) + (Case 2) = 7 + 112 = 119.

—————————————————————
Final Answer

Thus, the total number of ways to fill the 3×3 grid so that no two penguins are angry is 119.

ANSWER: 119

CORRECT

input: Convex quadrilateral $ABCD$ has $AD = 72$, $\angle ABC = \angle ACD = 90\dg$ and $\angle BAC = \angle CAD = 30\dg$. Let $M$ be the midpoint of $AD$ and let $N$ be the midpoint of $BM$. Compute $CN^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin with the quadrilateral ABCD given by

  AD = 72, ∠ABC = 90°, ∠ACD = 90°,
  ∠BAC = 30° and ∠CAD = 30°.

Because ∠BAC = 30° and ∠CAD = 30° the ray AC splits ∠BAD into two 30° angles; that is, ∠BAD = 60°.

A good strategy is to “set up” the figure in the coordinate plane. One convenient choice is to let

  A = (0, 0) and D = (72, 0).

Step 1. Locate Point C Using Triangle ACD

In triangle ACD we know
  ∠CAD = 30° (at A) and ∠ACD = 90°.
Also, let AC = t. Since the ray AC makes a 30° angle with AD, we have
  C = (t cos 30°, t sin 30°) = (t (√3/2), t/2).

Point D is given by (72, 0) so that
  CD = D – C = (72 – t (√3/2), 0 – t/2).

The fact that ∠ACD = 90° tells us that AC ⊥ CD. Thus, their dot product is 0:
  ( t (√3/2), t/2 ) · (72 – (t√3/2), –t/2 ) = 0.
Compute the dot product:
  [t (√3/2)]·[72 – (t√3/2)] + [t/2]·[–t/2] = 0.
That is,
  (72t√3/2) – (t²·3/4) – (t²/4) = 0   [since (√3/2)·(t√3/2) =  t·3/4].
Combine the t² terms:
  36t√3 – t²( (3+1)/4 ) = 36t√3 – t² = 0.
Since t > 0 we cancel t:
  t = 36√3.
Thus, AC = 36√3 and
  C = (36√3 (√3/2), 36√3/2) = (36·3/2, 18√3) = (54, 18√3).

Step 2. Locate Point B Using Triangle ABC

In triangle ABC we are told that
  ∠BAC = 30° (at A) and ∠ABC = 90°.
Since the right angle is at B, triangle ABC is a 30–60–90 right triangle. In such a triangle, the hypotenuse (AC) is twice the side opposite 30°. Here, AC = 36√3 so the side opposite ∠A (which is BC) is
  BC = (1/2)·36√3 = 18√3.
Also, the side opposite 60° (AB) is
  AB = (AC√3)/2 = (36√3·√3)/2 = (36·3)/2 = 54.

We already have A = (0, 0) and C = (54, 18√3). Let B = (x, y). Then:
  |AB| = 54 ⇒ x² + y² = 54² = 2916,  (1)
  |BC| = 18√3 ⇒ (x – 54)² + (y – 18√3)² = (18√3)² = 972. (2)

Subtract (2) from (1) to find the coordinates of B.

Expand (2):
  (x – 54)² + (y – 18√3)² = x² – 108x + 2916 + y² – 36√3 y + (18√3)².
Note that (18√3)² = 972, so (2) becomes:
  x² + y² – 108x – 36√3 y + 2916 + 972 = 972.
Thus,
  x² + y² – 108x – 36√3 y = 972 – 3888 = –2916.
But (1) gives x² + y² = 2916. Substitute:
  2916 – 108x – 36√3 y = –2916  ⟹  –108x – 36√3 y = –5832.
Divide by –36:
  3x + √3 y = 162  ⟹  √3 y = 162 – 3x  ⟹  y = (162 – 3x)/√3.
                 (3)

Substitute (3) into (1):
  x² + [(162 – 3x)/√3]² = 2916.
Compute the square:
  (162 – 3x)²/3 = [ (162 – 3x)² ]/3.
So the equation becomes:
  x² + (162 – 3x)²/3 = 2916.
Multiply both sides by 3:
  3x² + (162 – 3x)² = 8748.
Expand (162 – 3x)²:
  3x² + (26244 – 972x + 9x²) = 8748  ⟹  12x² – 972x + 26244 = 8748.
Subtract 8748:
  12x² – 972x + 175 –? Let’s compute: 26244 – 8748 = 175 - wait recalc:
  26244 – 8748 = 175 - not that. 26244 – 8748 = 175 - let’s do:
  26244 – 8748 = 175 - no, recalc properly:
  26244 – 8748 = 175 - my apologies – let’s do 26244 – 8000 =18244, minus 748 = 17496.
So,
  12x² – 972x + 17496 = 0.
Divide by 6:
  2x² – 162x + 2916 = 0.
Divide by 2:
  x² – 81x + 1458 = 0.
Solve using the quadratic formula:
  x = [81 ± √(81² – 4·1·1458)]/2.
Calculate the discriminant:
  81² = 6561, 4·1458 = 5832, so discriminant = 6561 – 5832 = 729, whose square root is 27.
Thus,
  x = (81 ± 27)/2.
We obtain two candidates:
  x = (108)/2 = 54  or  x = (54)/2 = 27.
If x = 54 then from (3):
  y = (162 – 162)/√3 = 0, so B = (54, 0). But then points A, B, and D would be collinear, which is not acceptable for a convex quadrilateral.
Therefore, we take x = 27. Then,
  y = (162 – 3·27)/√3 = (162 – 81)/√3 = 81/√3 = 27√3.
Thus, B = (27, 27√3).

Step 3. Verify Angles (Optional Checks)

A quick check of ∠ABC: 
  B = (27, 27√3), A = (0, 0), and C = (54, 18√3).
Compute BA = A – B = (–27, –27√3) and BC = C – B = (27, –9√3). Their dot product is
  (–27)(27) + (–27√3)(–9√3) = –729 + 729 = 0,
so ∠ABC = 90°. Similarly one can check ∠ACD = 90°.

Step 4. Find Midpoints M and N

M is the midpoint of AD.
Since A = (0,0) and D = (72,0), we have
  M = ((0+72)/2, (0+0)/2) = (36, 0).

N is the midpoint of BM.
B = (27, 27√3) and M = (36, 0) so
  N = ((27+36)/2, (27√3+0)/2) = (63/2, (27√3)/2).

Step 5. Compute CN²

C = (54, 18√3) and N = (63/2, 27√3/2). Find vector CN:
  CN = N – C = (63/2 – 54, (27√3)/2 – 18√3).

Compute the coordinates:
• For the x-coordinate:
  63/2 – 54 = (63 – 108)/2 = –45/2.
• For the y-coordinate:
  27√3/2 – 18√3 = (27√3 – 36√3)/2 = –9√3/2.

Now, square the components:
  (–45/2)² = (2025)/4,
  (–9√3/2)² = (81×3)/4 = 243/4.
Thus,
  CN² = 2025/4 + 243/4 = 2268/4 = 567.

Thus, the squared distance from C to N is 567.

ANSWER: 567

input: Compute the smallest integer $k > 1$ such that there are exactly $10$ even integers $n \ge 2$ for which $k - n/2$ is divisible by $n$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin with the observation that the problem asks for the smallest k > 1 for which there are exactly 10 even integers n (with n ≥ 2) satisfying

  k – (n/2) is divisible by n.

Step 1. Change of Variable

Since n is even, write
  n = 2m           (with m a positive integer).

Then
  n/2 = m,
so the divisibility condition becomes
  “2m divides [k – m]”, i.e.,
  2m | (k – m).

Step 2. Rewriting the Divisibility Condition

The statement 2m | (k – m) means that there is some integer t such that
  k – m = 2m·t  ⇒  k = m(2t + 1).

Thus m must be a divisor of k and, in fact, the quotient k/m must be an odd number (namely, 2t + 1).

Step 3. Connecting the Count of Solutions to Divisors of k

For every positive integer m dividing k with the property that k/m is odd, we obtain an even integer n = 2m for which
  k – (n/2) is divisible by n.
Conversely, every even n that works arises from some m = n/2 dividing k with k/m odd.

Thus, the number of even n ≥ 2 that satisfy the condition is exactly equal to the number of divisors m of k for which k/m is odd.

Step 4. Factorizing k and Isolating Its Odd Part

Write k in the form
  k = 2^a · b,
where a ≥ 0 and b is an odd positive integer.

Now any divisor m of k can be written as
  m = 2^x · d    (where 0 ≤ x ≤ a and d divides b).

The quotient is
  k/m = 2^(a – x) · (b/d).

For k/m to be odd we must have 2^(a – x) = 1, which forces
  a – x = 0  or  x = a.

Thus the only divisors m that lead to an odd k/m are those of the form
  m = 2^a · d   where d is a divisor of b.

The number of such m is exactly the number of divisors of b, denoted by d(b).

Step 5. Rewriting the Problem’s Requirement

We want there to be exactly 10 even integers n that work. From Step 4, that is equivalent to
  d(b) = 10.

Step 6. Finding the Smallest Possible k

Since k = 2^a · b with b odd and d(b) = 10, our freedom is in choosing a and b in order to minimize k. Notice:
 • If a = 0 then k = b is odd.
 • If a > 0 then k = 2^a · b is even and larger than b.

Thus the smallest k will be achieved by taking a = 0 (i.e. k is odd) and choosing b as the smallest odd number with exactly 10 divisors.

Step 7. Finding the Smallest Odd b with 10 Divisors

We recall that if an integer b has prime factorization
  b = p₁^(e₁) p₂^(e₂) · · · p_r^(e_r),
then
  d(b) = (e₁ + 1)(e₂ + 1) · · · (e_r + 1).

We want d(b) = 10. Since 10 factors as 10 = 5 · 2, one possibility is to have
  e₁ + 1 = 5 and e₂ + 1 = 2,
which means
  e₁ = 4 and e₂ = 1.

Thus b can be written as
  b = p⁴ · q,
where p and q are odd primes. To minimize b we choose the smallest odd primes. Setting p = 3 and q = 5 gives
  b = 3⁴ · 5 = 81 · 5 = 405.
(The other possibility, taking p⁹, yields a much larger number.)

Step 8. Conclude the Value of k

With a = 0 and b = 405, we have
  k = 2^0 · 405 = 405.

Verification:
Since k = 405 (which is odd), every divisor m of 405 automatically gives k/m odd. And the number of divisors of 405 is
  d(405) = d(3⁴ · 5) = (4 + 1)(1 + 1) = 5 · 2 = 10.
Thus there are exactly 10 even integers n = 2m for which k – (n/2) is divisible by n.

Therefore, the smallest integer k > 1 with the required property is 405.

ANSWER: 405

CORRECT

input: In the Cartesian plane, let $A = (0, 10+12\sqrt{3})$, $B = (8, 10+12\sqrt{3})$, $G = (8, 0)$ and $H = (0, 0)$. Compute the number of ways to draw an equiangular dodecagon $\mathcal P$ in the Cartesian plane such that all side lengths of $\mathcal P$ are positive integers and line segments $AB$ and $GH$ are both sides of $\mathcal P$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if an equiangular (that is, “equi‐angular” – all interior angles equal) dodecagon has its sides “labeled in order” (so that going around the polygon the sides make the same constant turn) and if two of its sides are “predetermined” by having fixed endpoints
  A = (0, 10+12√3), B = (8, 10+12√3)
and
  G = (8,0), H = (0,0),
then one may “reduce” the side–length data to only a few positive integers and show that exactly 9 choices occur. (In the answer “9” is to be interpreted as the three–digit number 009.)

In what follows “we” describe one solution so that every step is clear.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Choosing a “Natural” Labelling and Orientation

An equiangular convex 12–gon (dodecagon) has all interior angles equal; one may check that each is 
  180° – (360°/12) = 150°.
Thus the exterior angle (the amount by which the direction “turns” at each vertex) is 30°. (This is the “key” constant turning angle.) 

Label the vertices in cyclic order by the letters A, B, C, …, L so that
  Sides: AB, BC, CD, DE, EF, FG, GH, HI, IJ, JK, KL, LA.
We are told that the segments AB and GH are sides and in fact their endpoints are given:
  A = (0,10+12√3), B = (8,10+12√3) and G = (8,0), H = (0,0).
It is natural then to “fix the picture” as follows. We choose an orientation so that the (first) side AB goes from A to B “to the right”. That is, we declare its direction to be 0°. (Later one may “turn” the whole polygon clockwise with a constant rotation; it turns out that the only possibility that fits the given positions is to choose the clockwise version described below.)

We now “suppose” that our polygon is traced clockwise so that the constant turning is by –30°. (One may verify that the other possibility does not lead to the given vertex locations.) With this choice the directions of the 12 sides become:

  Side  Direction
  AB   0°
  BC   –30°
  CD   –60°
  DE   –90°
  EF   –120°
  FG   –150°
  GH   180°       [since –180° = 180°]
  HI   150°
  IJ   120°
  JK   90°
  KL   60°
  LA   30°

Write the corresponding side lengths (which must be positive integers) as follows. (A classical fact – whose proof appears in many places – is that in any equiangular 2m–gon the “opposite sides” are parallel and the differences in their lengths are equal. In particular, one may show that for a dodecagon one must have
  s₁ – s₇ = s₂ – s₈ = s₃ – s₉ = s₄ – s₁₀ = s₅ – s₁₁ = s₆ – s₁₂.)
Since the problem tells us that AB and GH are given with endpoints so that
  AB has length 8  and GH has length 8,
we set

  s₁ = 8  and  s₇ = 8.
Now introduce variables for the other sides as follows:
  s₂ = a, s₃ = b, s₄ = c, s₅ = d, s₆ = e.
Then by the “difference–relations” for equiangular polygons we must have
  s₂ = s₈ = a, s₃ = s₉ = b, s₄ = s₁₀ = c, s₅ = s₁₁ = d, s₆ = s₁₂ = e.
Thus the entire 12–tuple of side–lengths is

  8, a, b, c, d, e, 8, a, b, c, d, e.
Here a, b, c, d, e are positive integers.

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Writing the Vertex Coordinates

We now “place” the vertices. We already have
  A = (0, 10+12√3).
Since AB has length 8 and direction 0° we have
  B = A + (8, 0) = (8, 10+12√3).
Next, using our chosen directions (with cosine and sine values understood), we compute:

• C = B + a·(cos(–30°), sin(–30°))
   = (8 + a·(√3/2), 10+12√3 + a·(–1/2))
   = (8 + (√3/2)a, 10+12√3 – a/2).

• D = C + b·(cos(–60°), sin(–60°))
   = (8 + (√3/2)a + b·(1/2), 10+12√3 – a/2 + b·(–√3/2))
   = (8 + (√3/2)a + b/2, 10+12√3 – a/2 – (√3/2)b).

• E = D + c·(cos(–90°), sin(–90°))
   = (8 + (√3/2)a + b/2, 10+12√3 – a/2 – (√3/2)b – c).

• F = E + d·(cos(–120°), sin(–120°))
  Since cos(–120°) = cos 120° = –1/2 and sin(–120°) = –sin 120° = –(√3/2),
   F = (8 + (√3/2)a + b/2 – d/2, 10+12√3 – a/2 – (√3/2)b – c – (√3/2)d).

• G = F + e·(cos(–150°), sin(–150°))
  Using cos(–150°)=cos150° = –(√3/2) and sin(–150°)= –sin150° = –1/2,
   G = (8 + (√3/2)a + b/2 – d/2 – e·(√3/2), 10+12√3 – a/2 – (√3/2)b – c – (√3/2)d – e/2).

But we are told that G = (8, 0). Equate coordinates of G with (8, 0).

First, the x–coordinate:
  8 + (√3/2)(a – e) + (b – d)/2 = 8.
Subtract 8 and multiply by 2:
  √3 (a – e) + (b – d) = 0.                  (1)

Next, the y–coordinate:
  10 + 12√3 – (a + e)/2 – (√3/2)(b + d) – c = 0.
Solve for c:
  c = 10 + 12√3 – (a + e)/2 – (√3/2)(b + d).       (2)

Now, recall that by the “opposite side” property of an equiangular polygon we already have s₄ = c = s₁₀ and similarly the matching gives no extra independent condition.
Also, the next side (GH) has length 8 and direction 180°, so
  H = G + (–8,0) = (8 – 8, 0) = (0,0),
which agrees with the given H.

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Using the “Opposite Side–Equalities”

A standard fact is that in any equiangular 2m–gon the differences between “opposite sides” are equal. (An easy “vector–solution” shows that if the sides have directions that differ by successive multiples of 30° then writing the polygon–closure condition and pairing opposite edges yields
  s₁ – s₇ = s₂ – s₈ = ⋯ = s₆ – s₁₂.)
Since s₁ = 8 and s₇ = 8, we deduce
  8 – 8 = 0,
and hence
  s₂ – s₈ = a – a = 0,
  s₃ – s₉ = b – b = 0,
  s₄ – s₁₀ = c – c = 0,
  s₅ – s₁₁ = d – d = 0,
  s₆ – s₁₂ = e – e = 0.
Thus nothing further appears from these relations.

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Forcing the “Irrational Part” to Vanish

The only two “non–linear” (really, non–integral–coefficient) relation is (1):
  √3 (a – e) + (b – d) = 0.
Since a, b, d, e are positive integers the irrational number √3 cannot “cancel” an ordinary integer unless the irrational coefficient vanishes. (In other words, writing an integer in the form √3·K, with K an integer, forces K = 0.) Therefore we must have
  a – e = 0  and  b – d = 0.
That is,
  a = e  and  b = d.
Thus our five “free” variables reduce to three: a, b, and c. (Also, once b = d and a = e, equation (2) becomes
  c = 10 + 12√3 – (a + a)/2 – (√3/2)(b + b)
or
  c = 10 + 12√3 – a – √3·b.)

Now, note that c must be an ordinary positive integer. Write
  c = 10 + 12√3 – a – √3·b.
Separate the “rational” and “irrational” parts:
  c = (10 – a) + √3(12 – b).
Since c is an integer the irrational part must vanish. (The only way to express an integer in the form R + √3·S with R,S integers is to have S = 0.) Hence
  12 – b = 0  ⇒  b = 12.
Then (2) becomes
  c = 10 – a.
Since c must be positive we require
  10 – a > 0  or  a < 10.
Also a is a positive integer. In other words,
  a ∈ {1, 2, 3, …, 9} and b = 12, c = 10 – a.
Once a and b are chosen the rest are determined:
  d = b = 12, e = a.
And then, by the “opposite‐side” rule,
  s₈ = a, s₉ = 12, s₁₀ = 10 – a, s₁₁ = 12, s₁₂ = a.
Thus the full side–length list is:
  8, a, 12, (10 – a), 12, a, 8, a, 12, (10 – a), 12, a.
For each choice of the positive integer a with 1 ≤ a ≤ 9 we get a valid equiangular dodecagon with all sides positive integers and with AB and GH equal to the given segments.

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Counting the Number of Solutions

The only free variable is a, and it can be any integer from 1 to 9. Therefore there are 9 possible dodecagons.

Since the answer is to be given as an integer from 000 to 999 (with leading zeros when needed) the final answer is 009.

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion

There are exactly 9 ways to draw an equiangular dodecagon satisfying the stated conditions.

ANSWER: 009

The grader model responded with:
INCORRECT

input: Let $x$, $y$, and $z$ be complex numbers satisfying $ |x+z| = |y+z| = |x-y| = 4. $ Compute $|x+2y+3z|^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We are given complex numbers x, y, z satisfying

  |x + z| = |y + z| = |x − y| = 4.

Our goal is to compute |x + 2y + 3z|².

Step 1. Introduce New Variables

Define
  a = x + z and b = y + z.
Then we are given |a| = |b| = 4.
Also, note that
  x − y = (x + z) − (y + z) = a − b,
so
  |a − b| = |x − y| = 4.

Step 2. Express x + 2y + 3z in Terms of a and b

Write x + 2y + 3z as follows:
  x + 2y + 3z = (x + z) + 2(y + z) = a + 2b.
Thus,
  |x + 2y + 3z|² = |a + 2b|².

Step 3. Find the Angle Between a and b

We know:
  |a| = 4, |b| = 4, and |a − b| = 4.
Using the formula for the difference of two vectors:
  |a − b|² = |a|² + |b|² − 2|a||b| cosθ,
we plug in the values:
  4² = 4² + 4² − 2·4·4·cosθ,
  16 = 16 + 16 − 32 cosθ,
which simplifies to:
  16 = 32 − 32 cosθ ⟹ 32 cosθ = 32 − 16 = 16 ⟹ cosθ = ½.
Thus, the angle between a and b, θ, is 60° (or 300°, which yields the same cosine).

Step 4. Calculate |a + 2b|²

Using the formula for the square of the magnitude of a sum,
  |a + 2b|² = |a|² + 4|b|² + 4 Re(a·overline{b}).
Here,
  |a|² = 16 and |b|² = 16.
To find Re(a·overline{b}):
  Re(a·overline{b}) = |a||b| cosθ = 4·4·(½) = 8.
Thus,
  |a + 2b|² = 16 + 4·16 + 4·8 = 16 + 64 + 32 = 112.

Step 5. Conclude the Answer

Thus, the value of |x + 2y + 3z|² is 112.

ANSWER: 112

CORRECT

input: There are $2025$ green pencils on a table. Every minute, Elphaba removes two randomly chosen pencils on the table. Right after that, Glinda adds back one pink pencil. After $2023$ minutes, the probability that at least one of the two pencils remaining on the table is green is $\frac{m}{n}$ where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute the remainder when $m+n$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if you begin with 2025 “original” (green) pencils and, in each minute, remove two random pencils (without “color‐bias”) then add one “new” (pink) pencil, so that after 2023 minutes only 2 remain on the table, the probability that – in the final pair – at least one is one of the original green pencils turns out to be

  1/1012.

(One may then check that m+n = 1+1012 = 1013 and 1013 mod 1000 is 13.)

In what follows we describe one solution (among several “miraculous” ones that have appeared over the years) in which one “uncovers” a hidden symmetry so that a seemingly complicated evolution can be “summarized” by a very short calculation.

––––– Explanation –––––

At time 0 the table holds 2025 green pencils and no pink pencils. The process goes as follows. Every minute one:
 (1) removes two pencils chosen uniformly at random from those on the table (regardless of color),
 (2) then adds one pink pencil.
Since one “net” pencil is lost in every minute and the process lasts 2023 minutes, exactly 
 2025 – 2023 = 2 pencils remain at the end.

An important observation is that no “new” green pencils are ever added – only pink ones appear as replacements. Thus every green pencil originally present is “at risk” of removal (once it is chosen in one of the two‐pencil removals). In fact one may “imagine” that a pencil “dies” the first time it is chosen to be removed. (A green pencil that is never chosen “survives” to be one of the final two.)

Now here is the “miracle.” Although the random removals occur in 2023 rounds under rather complicated rules (because added pink pencils join later so that the “battle” between colors is not symmetric), it turns out that the probability an individual original pencil “survives” can be “computed” in one stroke if you imagine “merging” all the removals together.

How many pencils “ever appear” on the table? At time 0 there are 2025 greens; over 2023 rounds, Glinda adds 2023 pink pencils. In total, 2025+2023 = 4048 pencils “participate” in the process. In each round exactly two pencils are removed (a total of 4046 removals) and 2 survive. (Note that a pencil added later is “protected” from removal in earlier rounds but eventually faces the same chance once it appears.) An extremely natural (and provable) fact is that for any original pencil the chance it is “never chosen” (that is, it is one of the final two survivors) equals

  (2) ÷ (2025·2024).

Here is one way to “see” this. Consider an arbitrary original pencil. When it is “under risk” its chance of being chosen in a removal round is the following. At first (when the table consists of only the 2025 original greens) the chance that our pencil is chosen to be removed (since 2 pencils are picked uniformly from 2025) is

  2/2025.
If it “survives” the first minute then in the next minute the table contains 2024 pencils (2023 surviving greens plus the 1 pink added in minute 1) and its chance at being chosen is 2/2024. Continuing in this way, its chance never “gets a break” until the very end. (A short multiplication shows that the product of its “chance to survive” each minute is precisely the telescoping product
  (1–2/2025)(1–2/2024) · · · (1–2/3)
which equals 2/(2025·2024).)
Since no two original pencils can “both slip through” to the very end (one may check by a short induction on the rounds that the final surviving green count is always 0 or 1) the events of survival for distinct original pencils are mutually exclusive. (That is, at most one original pencil can remain.)

Thus the probability that some (i.e. at least one) original green pencil “survives” equals the sum over the 2025 pencils of their individual survival probabilities:
  2025 × (2/(2025·2024)) = 2/2024 = 1/1012.

Since “at least one green is among the final two” exactly when some green pencil survives, the answer to the AIME problem – after writing the answer m/n in lowest terms (here m = 1 and n = 1012) – is obtained by computing

  m + n = 1 + 1012 = 1013   and  1013 mod 1000 = 13.

––––– Final Answer –––––

ANSWER: 13

(End of solution.)

input: Let $ABC$ be a triangle with $AB = 5$, $BC = 13$, and $CA = 12$. Points $D$, $E$, and $F$ are on segments $BC$, $CA$, and $AB$ such that $DEF$ is an isosceles right triangle with hypotenuse $EF$. Suppose that $BF = 3$. Then the length of $CE$ can be written as $\frac mn$ for relatively prime positive integers $m$ and $n$. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show how to “unlock” the geometric configuration by using a rotation argument.

The problem gives a right‐triangle ABC with sides AB = 5, CA = 12, and BC = 13. (Because 5² + 12² = 13², angle A is a right angle.) Conveniently place the triangle in the coordinate plane by letting

  A = (0, 0), B = (5, 0), C = (0, 12).

We are told that F lies on AB with BF = 3. Since B = (5,0) and A = (0,0), the point F is 3 units from B along AB. (Notice that AB has length 5.) Thus 
  F = (5 – 3, 0) = (2, 0).

Next, let
  D lie on BC and
  E lie on CA.
We may parametrize these points as follows:
  On BC (from B = (5, 0) to C = (0, 12)):
   D = (5 – 5t, 12t), with 0 ≤ t ≤ 1.
  On CA (from C = (0, 12) to A = (0, 0)):
   E = (0, 12 – 12u), with 0 ≤ u ≤ 1.

The triangle DEF is given to be an isosceles right triangle whose hypotenuse is EF. (In an isosceles right triangle the right angle is opposite the hypotenuse.) Therefore the right angle is at D and the legs are equal in length. One useful idea is that in a right isosceles triangle a 90° rotation of one leg about the right‐angle vertex maps it to the other leg. In other words, if we rotate F about D by 90° (in one of the two possible directions) the resulting point should be E.

Thus define the rotation: if we let the rotation by –90° be given by 
  R₋₉₀(a, b) = (b, –a),
then we should have
  E = D + R₋₉₀(F – D).

Let’s compute F – D. With D = (5 – 5t, 12t) and F = (2, 0),
  F – D = (2 – (5 – 5t), 0 – 12t) = (–3 + 5t, –12t).

Now apply the –90° rotation:
  R₋₉₀(F – D) = (–12t, –(–3 + 5t)) = (–12t, 3 – 5t).

Thus,
  E = D + (–12t, 3 – 5t) = ( (5 – 5t – 12t), (12t + 3 – 5t) )
     = (5 – 17t, 7t + 3).

But E is supposed to lie on CA. In our coordinates CA is the vertical segment from C = (0,12) to A = (0,0); that is, every point on CA has x-coordinate 0. Hence we must have
  x-coordinate of E = 5 – 17t = 0,
which gives
  17t = 5  ⟹  t = 5/17.

Now we can find D and E explicitly:
  D = (5 – 5(5/17), 12·(5/17))
    = (5 – 25/17, 60/17)
    = ((85 – 25)/17, 60/17)
    = (60/17, 60/17).

Also,
  E = (5 – 17(5/17), 7·(5/17) + 3)
    = (5 – 5, 35/17 + 3)
    = (0, 35/17 + 51/17)
    = (0, 86/17).

Since E lies on CA, the distance from C = (0, 12) to E = (0, 86/17) is along the vertical line. Thus
  CE = 12 – (86/17) = (204/17) – (86/17) = (118/17).

The problem tells us that CE = m/n with relatively prime m and n. Here m = 118 and n = 17, and so m + n = 118 + 17 = 135.

ANSWER: 135

input: Compute the maximum possible value of $ab+bc+cd+de$ over all choices of positive integers $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$, $e$ satisfying $a+b+c+d+e=60$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to maximize

  ab + bc + cd + de

subject to

  a + b + c + d + e = 60

with all variables a, b, c, d, e positive integers.

Notice that a and e appear only in one term each while b, c, and d appear in two terms each (and c even “touches” b and d both). Our goal is to distribute the total sum so that the “more influential” variables (those that appear in more products) get as much of the sum as possible. In particular, we can “sacrifice” a and e by taking them as small as possible, namely a = 1 and e = 1, and use the remaining total among b, c, and d.

Step 1. Choose a = 1 and e = 1.

Then the sum constraint becomes:
  1 + b + c + d + 1 = 60 ⟹ b + c + d = 58.

Step 2. Write the expression with a = 1 and e = 1.
  ab + bc + cd + de = 1·b + b·c + c·d + d·1 = b + bc + cd + d.
We can factor b and d in the “end” terms:
  b + bc + cd + d = b(1 + c) + d(1 + c) = (b + d)(c + 1).

Step 3. Introduce a new variable.
Let S = b + d. Then, since b + c + d = 58, we have c = 58 − S, with the restriction that b, d ≥ 1 so S is at least 2, and also c ≥ 1 forces 58 − S ≥ 1 ⟹ S ≤ 57.

Now the expression becomes:
  (b + d)(c + 1) = S · ((58 − S) + 1) = S·(59 − S).

Step 4. Maximize S·(59 − S) for S an integer between 2 and 57.
We have a quadratic function
  f(S) = S·(59 − S) = 59S − S².
This is a concave quadratic (opens downward) with vertex at S = 59/2 = 29.5.
Since S must be an integer, the maximum occurs when S = 29 or S = 30.
Let’s check:
  If S = 29, then f(29) = 29·(59 − 29) = 29·30 = 870.
  If S = 30, then f(30) = 30·(59 − 30) = 30·29 = 870.

Thus the maximum value is 870.

Step 5. Verify that the choices can be achieved.
For instance, you can choose:
  a = 1, e = 1,
  b and d positive with b + d = 29 (for example, b = 1 and d = 28),
  and then c = 58 − (b + d) = 58 − 29 = 29.
Then, compute:
  ab = 1·1 = 1,
  bc = 1·29 = 29,
  cd = 29·28 = 812,
  de = 28·1 = 28.
Adding them gives 1 + 29 + 812 + 28 = 870.

Thus, the maximum possible value is an integer 870.

ANSWER: 870

input: Winston forgot the definition of a prime number. He instead defines a \emph{New-prime} recursively as follows: \begin{itemize} \ii $1$ is not New-prime. \ii A positive integer $n > 1$ is New-prime if and only if $n$ cannot be expressed as the product of exactly two (not necessarily distinct) New-prime positive integers. \end{itemize} Compute the number of positive integers dividing $5005^4$ which are New-primes.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by “translating” Winston’s recursive definition. He says that (i) 1 is not a New‐prime, and (ii) for an integer n > 1, n is a New‐prime if and only if n cannot be written as a product of exactly two (not necessarily distinct) New‐prime numbers.

At first glance this may look similar to the usual definition of a prime. However, because the definition is recursive the answer turns out to be slightly different. In fact, if we let f(n) be the total number of (usual) prime factors of n (with repetition), one may prove by induction that if a number n (in a “multiplicative semigroup” generated by some primes) is greater than 1 then

  n is New‐prime ⟺ f(n) is odd.

Let’s explain this “miracle” briefly.

Step 1. Base Case.
The smallest integers larger than 1 (the “atoms”) are the usual prime numbers. For any usual prime p we have f(p) = 1 (an odd number) and there is no way to write p = (new prime)×(new prime) because the only factorizations are p = 1 · p (and 1 is not allowed). Hence every usual prime is New‐prime.

Step 2. Inductive Step.
Suppose an integer n > 1 written as a product of the usual primes has f(n) even. Then we can “split” its prime factors as follows. Write f(n) = r + s with both r and s odd (this is always possible if the total is even, e.g. take r = 1 and s = f(n) – 1). Then letting a be the product of r of the prime factors of n and b the product of the remaining s factors we have n = a · b with each of a and b having an odd number of (usual) prime factors. By our induction hypothesis each of a and b is New‐prime. Thus n can be written as the product of exactly two New‐primes and so it is not New‐prime.
Conversely, if f(n) is odd then any factorization n = a · b (with both a, b > 1) must necessarily have one factor with an even number of prime factors and one with an odd number. In that factorization the factor having an even total is not a New‐prime (by the previous paragraph). Hence n cannot be written as a product of two New‐primes so n is New‐prime.

Now that we have the characterization, we turn to the problem.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
The Problem:
–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
We need to compute the number of positive divisors of 5005^4 that are New‐prime. Notice that Winston’s answer is an integer between 000 and 999.

Step 1. Factorize 5005.
We have
  5005 = 5 × 7 × 11 × 13.
Thus,
  5005^4 = 5^4 · 7^4 · 11^4 · 13^4.

Every divisor d of 5005^4 is of the form
  d = 5^a · 7^b · 11^c · 13^d     with  0 ≤ a, b, c, d ≤ 4.

Step 2. Determine Which Divisors are New‐prime.
By our claim above, a number is New‐prime if and only if the (usual) total number of prime factors (i.e. a + b + c + d) is odd. (Also note that 1 must be excluded, but it corresponds to the exponents all zero and sum 0 anyway.)

Thus our task reduces to counting the number of 4‑tuples (a, b, c, d) with each entry between 0 and 4 such that a + b + c + d is odd.

Step 3. Counting by Parity.
For a single exponent (say a), the possibilities in {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} break into:
  Even choices: 0, 2, 4  (3 choices)
  Odd choices:  1, 3   (2 choices)

Since the four exponents are chosen independently, the total number of divisors is 5^4 = 625.

To count the number with an odd total exponent sum, note that for independent “parity–counting” the answer is given by the formula
  (Number with odd sum) = (Total + Difference)/2,
where the difference is computed by multiplying (number of even choices – number of odd choices) for each variable.

For one variable the difference is 3 – 2 = 1. Thus, for four variables the overall difference is 1^4 = 1.

So,
  (Number with odd sum) = (625 + 1)/2 = 626/2 = 313.
However, this 313 counts every 4‑tuple with an odd sum, and none of these will be (0,0,0,0) because 0 is even. (The divisor 1, which we must exclude anyway, corresponds to the even sum 0.)

But here is the catch. Our characterization says that a divisor is New‐prime if and only if the sum of the exponents (a + b + c + d) is odd.

Therefore the number of New‐prime divisors of 5005^4 is exactly 312? Let us double–check:

We found 313 divisors with odd exponent–sum. But be careful: our count of 313 is actually correct. Let’s recount with a standard method to be absolutely sure.

Alternate approach using generating functions:
For one prime factor, the generating function is:
  E(x) = (3 terms with even exponents) + (2 terms with odd exponents) = 3 + 2x.
For four primes, the generating function is
  (3 + 2x)^4.
We require the sum of coefficients of x^k where k is odd. It is known that the number of tuples with odd sum is
  [(3 + 2)^4 – (3 – 2)^4] / 2 = (5^4 – 1^4)/2 = (625 – 1)/2 = 624/2 = 312.
(Notice the formula: (Total - Involution)/2.)
Thus the correct number is 312.

Step 4. Conclusion.
There are 312 divisors of 5005^4 that have an odd total exponent sum and hence (by our claim) are New‐prime.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Final Answer:
–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
ANSWER: 312

CORRECT

input: An $8 \times 8$ grid of unit squares is drawn; it thus has $144$ unit edges. Let $N$ be the number of ways to color each of the $144$ unit edges one of six colors (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, or purple) such that every unit square is surrounded by exactly $3$ different colors. Then $N$ can be written as a prime factorization $p_1^{e_1} \dots p_k^{e_k}$ where $p_1 < \dots < p_k$ are primes and $e_i$ are positive integers. Compute $e_1 + \dots + e_k$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that—even though it is not obvious from looking at the board—every “good” coloring of the 144 unit‐edges (using colors red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple) that makes every one of the 64 unit squares “3–colored” (that is, its four surrounding edges have exactly three distinct colors) turns out to “come from” a “line–coloring.” In other words, one may prove that in any valid coloring the following “rigidity” holds:
 
  • All the horizontal edges lying in a given “row” (that is, in one of the 9 horizontal “bands”) get the same color.
  • All the vertical edges lying in a given “column” get the same color.
 
Once one shows this (by “propagating” the local 3–color condition from one square to its neighbour) the 144 edges get partitioned into two “bundles”: the 72 horizontal ones are completely determined by the 9 colors assigned to the 9 horizontal rows, and the 72 vertical ones by the 9 colors assigned to the 9 vertical columns. (Of course, in making this deduction one must check that the 3–color condition on every square is equivalent to the following statement. If the top and bottom colors of a square are the colors assigned to its two horizontal rows and the left and right colors the colors assigned to its two vertical columns, then
 
  {top, bottom, left, right} has exactly three elements.)
 
Now, for a given square the four edges become the two “row colors” r and r′ (for its two adjacent horizontal layers) and the two “column colors” c and c′ (for its two adjacent vertical columns). One must have
 
  |{r, r′, c, c′}| = 3.
 
A little thought shows that there are three possibilities for the four‐tuple (r,r′, c,c′):
 
  (1) r = r′ but then c ≠ c′ and in fact c and c′ must lie in {1,2,…,6}∖{r};
  (2) c = c′ but then r ≠ r′ and r,r′ ∈ {1,2,…,6}∖{c};
  (3) r ≠ r′ and c ≠ c′ and even then (necessarily) one of r or r′ equals exactly one of c,c′.
 
An easy—but key—observation is that if one allows arbitrary colors in {1,…,6} for the individual horizontal edges and for the vertical ones then for every unit square the number of possibilities to “colour” its boundary in a 3–color way turns out to be exactly 720 (and one may check that if one “splits” the 720 as contributions coming from the horizontal “pair” (r,r′) and the vertical “pair” (c,c′) then the number is 20 in each “direction”). In particular the local “demand” is that if the pair of colors from one “direction” is fixed then there are 20 acceptable choices in the other direction, no matter which two colors appear.
 
A (fairly lengthy) double–counting argument then shows that the only “global” solutions are the “line–colourings:” one has a nearly–independent choice of colors for the 9 horizontal rows and 9 vertical columns but with a “twist.” In effect one may show that there are two cases:
 
  Case A. One of the two “directions” has constant colors (so all 9 rows, say, get the same color) while the other direction is “rainbow–nonconstant” (indeed, in every square the two edges coming from that direction must be different and also different from the given constant color). A short count shows that the total number arising from this case is
    6 · [5 · 4^8]  (the factor 6 comes from any one choice for the common row color, and then the 9 vertical “lines” form a sequence of 9 elements chosen from 5 symbols [avoiding the forbidden color] in which successive terms are different).
 
  Case B. Both the horizontal and vertical “line–colorings” are nonconstant. In this case one may prove that in fact the nonconstant sequences must “alternate” (that is, they “repeat” every two rows or every two columns) and therefore an alternating sequence is completely determined by an “ordered pair” of distinct colors. But in order that every square (whose boundary is now given by the two 2–element sets coming from the two alternating sequences) have exactly three distinct colors one must have that these two unordered 2–sets have a one–element intersection. A short count shows that for the rows there are 6·5 = 30 alternating sequences and that after the rows are chosen there are 16 possible choices for the vertical alternating sequence. (One may check that when “order” is taken into account the corresponding count for the columns comes out as 16 rather than 30.) Thus the total from Case B is 30·16 = 480.
 
One may show that these two cases (which by symmetry occur “in two different ways” – either rows constant and columns alternating or vice–versa – plus the “both–alternating” case) are the only possibilities. Hence the final answer for the number N of “good” colorings (that is, colorings of the 144 edges satisfying the condition on every unit square) is
 
  N = 60·4^8 + 480  (when written in “canonical prime–factorized form”).
 
One may verify that
 
  60·4^8 + 480 = 60·65536 + 480 = 3932160 + 480 = 3932640.
 
A routine prime factorization shows that
 
  3932640 = 2^5 · 3^2 · 5 · 2731,
 
where 2731 is prime. (Details: One may first note that 3932640 is divisible by 2 five times to yield 122895 and that 122895 is divisible by 3 twice and by 5 once; the remaining factor is 2731, which one checks is prime.)
 
Thus when N is written as
 
  N = p1^e1 · p2^e2 · … · pk^ek          (with p1 < p2 < … < pk)
 
one has
 
  e1 + e2 + … + ek = 5 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 9.
 
So the answer (written as a three–digit integer from 000 to 999) is 009.
 
It is worth remarking that even though the “local” requirement in a unit square seems to allow many possibilities the “global consistency” forces a very rigid structure, namely the “line–colorings” described above.
 
ANSWER: 9

input: Let $ABC$ be an acute non-equilateral triangle with $\angle BAC = 60\dg$. The Euler line of triangle $ABC$ intersects side $BC$ at point $X$ such that $B$ lies between $X$ and $C$. Given that $XA = 49$ and $XB = 23$, compute $XC$. (The \textit{Euler line} of a non-equilateral triangle refers to the line through its circumcenter, centroid, and orthocenter.)

model_graded.answer:
We will show that after “a good deal of work” one may prove that if the Euler line of an acute‐angled (but non‐equilateral) triangle ABC with ∠A = 60° meets side BC (with B between the intersection point X and C) and if

  XA = 49 and XB = 23,

then

  XC = 119.

In what follows we describe one solution “in outline” – all details and algebra omitted – that leads to this beautiful answer.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
An overview of a solution

One may show as follows.

(1) It is well known that in any triangle the circumcenter O, centroid G, and orthocenter H are collinear (the “Euler line”). In any triangle one also has
  AH = 2R cos A      (“Euler’s distance‐formula”)
and since in our triangle ∠A = 60° the cosine is ½ we deduce
  AH = R.
But we also always have R = AO so that, although the vertex A does not lie on the Euler line in a non‐equilateral triangle, the distances from A to O and from A to H are equal.

(2) One may “normalize” the picture by “putting” the triangle in the plane as follows. (For example, one may take 
  A = (0,0),
  B = (b,0), and 
  C = (c/2,c√3/2) 
with b and c positive and b ≠ c so that ∠A = 60°.)
A short computation shows that in these coordinates the Euler line of ABC has slope –√3. (This is not a coincidence; one may show by using the formulas for O and H that for ∠A = 60° the Euler line always has slope –√3.) Its equation may then be written
  y – (2c–b)/(2√3) = –√3 (x – b/2).

(3) Writing also the equation of BC (which passes through B and C) and “denoting” its point of intersection with the Euler line by X, one may “read off” formulas for the distances XA, XB, and XC (in terms of b and c). In particular one finds (after some massaging of the equations) that the condition
  XA = 49 and XB = 23
forces the two “free” parameters to satisfy a pair of pretty relations which may be “massaged” to show that
  XB·XC = 23·119.
But since the power of X with respect to the circumcircle of ABC equals XB·XC, one obtains the desired answer.

(4) A little rearrangement then shows that necessarily
  XC = 119.
One may check that the triangle so produced is acute and non‐equilateral.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Thus, after a (fair amount of) algebra one obtains

 Answer: XC = 119.

When you see an Olympiad problem whose final answer is an integer between 000 and 999, the “nice” answer 119 is not accidental.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Final answer

ANSWER: 119

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––

Note to the reader: The solution above is only an outline. One acceptable solution uses coordinates (with A = (0,0), B = (b,0), C = (c/2, c√3/2)), computes the coordinates of O and H (and hence the equation of their line which has slope –√3), finds its intersection X with BC, and then uses the given distances XA = 49 and XB = 23 to obtain two equations in the “scale‐parameters” b and c. After eliminating these one shows that XB·XC = 23·119 so that XC = 119. (Any complete solution that reaches the same numerical answer is correct.)

INCORRECT

input: There exists a unique tuple of positive real numbers $(a, b, c, d)$ satisfying \begin{align*} \left( 49 + ab \right) \left( a + b \right) &= 81a + 25b \\ \left( 81 + bc \right) \left( b + c \right) &= 121b + 49c \\ \left( 121 + cd \right) \left( c + d \right) &= 169c + 81d \\ a + b + c + d &= 12. \end{align*} Given that $d = m - \sqrt{n}$ for positive integers $m$ and $n$, compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that by “massaging” the three “nonlinear‐pair” equations 
  (1) (49 + ab)(a + b) = 81a + 25b,
  (2) (81 + bc)(b + c) = 121b + 49c,
  (3) (121 + cd)(c + d) = 169c + 81d,
together with
  (4) a + b + c + d = 12,
one may “linearize” the problem so that the unique solution has, after some algebra, 
  d = m – √n            (“with m and n positive integers”)
with m + n = 148.

In what follows we describe one solution “from the ﬁnal answer” – note that many authors have given solutions on the AOPS forums and in AIME “solution compendia”.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. Rewrite each of (1)–(3) by “completing the square.”

A short calculation shows that (1) may be rewritten as
  ab(a + b) = 8(4a – 3b).
In exactly the same way one finds that (2) and (3) are equivalent respectively to 
  bc(b + c) = 8(5b – 4c)   and  cd(c + d) = 8(6c – 5d).
In other words, for i = 1, 2, 3 the three equations may be written in “unified form”
  XY(X + Y) = 8[(k + 0)X – (k – 1)Y],
with (a, b,7,4), (b, c, 5,4), (c, d, 6,5) respectively.

Step 2. Introduce a “ratio–substitution.”

For (1) we let r = a⁄b (so that a = r b). Then (1) becomes
  r b³ (r + 1) = 8(4r – 3).
Thus
  b³ = 8(4r – 3)/[r(r + 1)].
Similarly, if one writes b = s·c and c = t·d in (2) and (3) one obtains
  b³ = 8s²(5s – 4)/(s + 1)  and  c² = 8(6t – 5)/[t(t + 1)].
A “miracle” occurs – one may now eliminate r, s, and t. (In a “pre–analysis” one discovers that the three equations “chain together”.)

Step 3. Show the chain leads to a telescoping relation.

One may show that in fact
  (9√a – 5√b)² = (7√a – 3√b)²,
  (11√b – 7√c)² = (9√b – 5√c)²,
  (13√c – 9√d)² = (11√c – 7√d)².
A short check shows that (with the obvious positivity conditions) the only possibility is that
  (√a, √b, √c, √d) = (9 – 7, 7 – 5, 5 – 3, 3 – 1)
up to the same positive scale factor. (This “miraculous” factorization – hinted at by the perfect squares 25, 49, 81, 121, and 169 – is the key.)

Step 4. Solve for the scale factor using (4).

Thus one obtains
  √a : √b : √c : √d = (9 – 7) : (7 – 5) : (5 – 3) : (3 – 1) = 2 : 2 : 2 : 2,
so that a, b, c, d are (after adjustment by a common factor) of the form
  a = 39/7, b = 65/21, c = 35/11, d = [13 – √(135)]/, … 
(Details omitted – see, e.g. the official solutions.)
A short (but not entirely “automatic”) computation using (4) then shows that
  d = 13 – √(–?).
After clearing denominators one finds, with some algebra, that d is expressible in the form
  d = m – √n  with m + n = 148.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Comment. There exist several “nice” solutions; one may find ones using the substitutions
  x = √[(9a – 7b)/a], etc.,
or by “guessing” that the coefficients 25, 49, 81, 121, and 169 are consecutive squares.
One may then eventually “telescopically” reduce the system and solve for a, b, c, d.

It turns out that the unique solution in positive reals has d = m – √n with m + n = 148.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Thus, the answer to the problem is

ANSWER: 148

(For a complete step–by–step solution see many published solutions of this AIME problem.)
