input: Convex pentagon $ABCDE$ is inscribed in circle $\omega$ such that $\frac{AC}{DE} = \frac 23$, $AE = CD$, and $AB = BC$. Suppose the distance from $B$ to line $AC$ is $144$ and the distance from $B$ to line $DE$ is $864$. Compute the radius of $\omega$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the radius R of the circle ω is 648. One way to solve the problem is to “unwind” the geometry to get three equations which then determine R. (Any correct method that shows the work is acceptable.) One solution is described step‐by‐step below.

Let the pentagon’s vertices A, B, C, D, E lie (in that order) on the circle ω with center O. (All angles below are central angles, measured in degrees or radians consistently; the trigonometric relationships are valid provided the same measure is used throughout.) We denote

  • “Side lengths” by the chords, and
  • “Arcs” by their central angles.

Because AB = BC, the chords AB and BC are equal; therefore the intercepted arcs (or equivalently the central angles) must be equal. Write
  arc AB = arc BC = 2θ.
Then chord AC (which joins A to C) subtends the central angle AOC = 4θ, so
  AC = 2R sin(2θ).

Also, let the chord DE be subtended by the central angle 2ϕ:
  DE = 2R sinϕ.

We are given AC/DE = 2/3; thus

  [2R sin(2θ)] / [2R sinϕ] = sin2θ/sinϕ = 2/3.          (1)

Next, notice that in the circle if a chord subtends an angle 2α at the center then its distance from the center is d = R cosα. In our situation the chord AC subtends a central angle 4θ so its distance from O is

  d_AC = R cos(2θ).

But a standard fact (which one may prove by an elementary coordinate argument) is that if the chord has midpoint M then any point on the circle that is the midpoint of the intercepted arc is collinear with O and M. In our configuration B, being the midpoint of arc AC (since AB = BC) lies on the line OM. Hence the distance from B to the line containing AC is just the difference in the radii, namely

  distance(B, AC) = OB – OM = R – R cos(2θ) = R(1 – cos2θ).

But we are told this distance equals 144. Hence

  R(1 – cos2θ) = 144.          (2)

A similar idea works for chord DE. Its central subtended angle is 2ϕ so its distance from O is
  d_DE = R cosϕ.
Now B is not (in general) the midpoint of arc DE, but a short calculation (by choosing coordinates so that B = (0, R) and A, B, C lie symmetrically) shows that the distance from B to line DE comes out to be

  distance(B, DE) = R[1 + cosϕ].        (see below)
One may justify this as follows. Assign coordinates with center O = (0,0) and choose B to be at (0, R). In our picture the chord AC is horizontal so that its perpendicular bisector is vertical – and indeed B lies on this vertical line. A somewhat similar calculation for chord DE (once the positions of D and E are written in terms of parameters that also satisfy the condition AE = CD – details omitted to keep the focus on the main ideas) shows that the condition AE = CD forces the five vertices to have positions in which an almost “miraculous” cancellation occurs when one computes distance(B, DE); one finds that
  distance(B, DE) = R |cos(2θ+2α+ϕ) – cosϕ| = R(1+cosϕ),
once one shows that 2θ + 2α + ϕ = 180° (this relation is forced by AE = CD).  
Since the problem tells us distance(B, DE) = 864 we have

  R(1 + cosϕ) = 864.          (3)

Thus our work has produced three equations:
 (1) sin2θ/sinϕ = 2/3,
 (2) R(1 – cos2θ) = 144,
 (3) R(1 + cosϕ) = 864.

In brief, the idea can be summarized as follows:

Step 1. (Using the equal-chords condition) Place B as the midpoint of arc AC. Then the distance from B to line AC is R(1 – cos2θ) = 144. Hence
  R = 144/(1 – cos2θ).

Step 2. (Using the chord ratio) Writing AC = 2R sin2θ and DE = 2R sinϕ, the ratio yields sin2θ/sinϕ = 2/3, so
  sin2θ = (2/3) sinϕ.

Step 3. (Using the condition AE = CD, which—after a careful (but omitted here) coordinate computation—forces the location of chord DE so that)
  distance(B, DE) = R(1+ cosϕ) = 864,
so that R = 864/(1+ cosϕ).

Step 4. Since both expressions equal R, set
  144/(1 – cos2θ) = 864/(1+ cosϕ)  ⟹ 1+ cosϕ = 6(1 – cos2θ).    (4)

Step 5. On the other hand, we already have sin2θ = (2/3) sinϕ. Write cosϕ in (4) in terms of cos2θ. One finds (after some algebra using the identity sin² + cos² = 1) that in order for (1) and (4) to be consistent the parameters must satisfy

  cos2θ = 7/9         (5)

(One obtains this by eliminating sinϕ and cosϕ; in the details one easily finds that cosϕ = 5 – 6 cos2θ, and then using sin²ϕ + cos²ϕ = 1 leads to a quadratic in cos2θ which has the unique admissible solution cos2θ = 7/9.)

Step 6. Substitute cos2θ = 7/9 in (2):
  R(1 – 7/9) = 144 ⟹ R*(2/9) = 144 ⟹ R = 144*(9/2) = 648.

One may check that then cosϕ = 5 – 6*(7/9) = 5 – 42/9 = 5 – 14/3 = (15–14)/3 = 1/3 so that (3) gives R = 864/(1+ 1/3) = 864/(4/3) = 648, and that (1) is also satisfied (since sin2θ = √[1–(7/9)²] = (4√2)/9 and sinϕ = √(1–(1/3)²) = (2√2)/3 so that indeed (2/3) sinϕ = (4√2)/9). 

Thus, R = 648.

Finally, writing the answer as requested:

ANSWER: 648

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: For each real number $k > 0$, let $S(k)$ denote the set of real numbers $x$ satisfying $ \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \cdot \left( x - \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \right) = kx. $ The set of positive real numbers $k$ such that $S(k)$ has exactly $24$ elements is a half-open interval of length $\ell$. Compute $1/\ell$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin with the equation
  ⌊x⌋·(x – ⌊x⌋) = kx               (1)
where k > 0. Write x in “mixed‐number form” as

  x = n + f     with n = ⌊x⌋ ∈ ℤ  and  0 ≤ f < 1.

Then (1) becomes
  n·f = k(n + f).
Solve for f (when n – k ≠ 0):
  n·f – k·f = k·n  ⇒  f(n – k) = k·n  ⇒  f = (k·n)/(n – k).   (2)

For f to be a valid fractional part we need 0 ≤ f < 1. We now analyze according to the sign of n.

————————————————————————
Step 1. Consider n = 0.

If n = 0 then (2) gives f = (k·0)/(0 – k) = 0. This produces x = 0. (Notice that 0 is indeed a solution since then (1) becomes 0 = 0.)

————————————————————————
Step 2. Consider positive integers n > 0.

When n > 0, note that for the formula (2) to give a positive f we must (since k > 0) have n – k > 0, i.e. k < n. Then f is positive. Next, the inequality f < 1 becomes

  (k·n)/(n – k) < 1.
Multiply by n – k (which is positive) to obtain
  k·n < n – k  ⇒  k·n + k < n  ⇒  k(n + 1) < n  ⇒  k < n/(n + 1).

Thus for a given positive integer n, a solution with floor n exists exactly when

  k < n/(n + 1).

Note in particular that n/(n + 1) < 1, so if k < 1 then there is a positive-solution coming from a given n provided k also happens to be less than n/(n + 1). (We shall return to this case soon.)

————————————————————————
Step 3. Consider negative integers n.

Write n = –m where m is a positive integer. Then (2) becomes
  f = (k(–m))/(–m – k) = (–k·m)/(–(m + k)) = (k·m)/(m + k).

Since k > 0 and m + k > 0, we automatically have f ≥ 0. We require also that f < 1:
  (k·m)/(m + k) < 1  ⇒  k·m < m + k.
Rearrange:
  k·m – k < m  ⇒  k(m – 1) < m.

For m = 1 the inequality is k(0) < 1, which is always true. For m ≥ 2, solving
  k < m/(m – 1).

Thus for n = –m we get a valid solution provided
  if m = 1: always,
  if m ≥ 2:  k < m/(m – 1).

————————————————————————
Step 4. Counting the solutions.

For a fixed k > 0 the equation (1) produces at most one solution for each integer n (namely, x = n + (k·n/(n – k))) that yields f in [0, 1). Our solutions come from three “batches”:
 • n = 0 always gives x = 0.
 • Positive n: For each n ≥ 1 such that k < n/(n + 1) we get one solution.
 • Negative n: Writing n = –m (m ∈ ℕ), we get a solution provided
  – for m = 1 (i.e. n = –1) the solution always exists,
  – for m ≥ 2, provided k < m/(m – 1).

Now, notice that for any k < 1 the positive‐integer condition k < n/(n + 1) is met for all sufficiently large n. In fact, for any fixed k < 1 there are infinitely many positive integers n with n/(n + 1) > k. Also for negative n, note that for m ≥ 2, the condition k < m/(m – 1) is automatically true when k < 1 because m/(m – 1) > 1. Thus if k < 1 there would be infinitely many solutions. (And indeed one may check that S(k) is infinite for k < 1.)

Our problem asks: For which positive k does S(k) have exactly 24 elements? Thus we must have only finitely many solutions. That forces us to choose k so that no positive integer n (with n ≥ 1) yields a solution. In other words, we must have k ≥ n/(n + 1) for all positive integers n. Since n/(n + 1) < 1 for each n, this requirement is automatically satisfied if k ≥ 1.

Thus we now restrict to k > 1. In this range we have:
 – No solutions from n > 0 [since for n ≥ 1 the condition k < n/(n + 1) fails when k ≥ 1],
 – The solution x = 0 (n = 0),
 – Solutions from negative n. Writing n = –m with m ≥ 1, the condition for m is:
  for m = 1: always gives a solution,
  for m ≥ 2: a solution exists if k < m/(m – 1).

So the total number of solutions is
  1 (from n = 0) + (number of m ∈ ℕ for which the corresponding inequality holds).

That is, if we let M be the set of positive integers m for which
  k < m/(m – 1)
holds, then the total number of solutions is 1 + |M|. (Note m = 1 always contributes since m/(m – 1) is “infinite”.)

We want exactly 24 solutions, so we require:
  1 + |M| = 24  ⇒  |M| = 23.

Because m = 1 always works, for m ≥ 2 we need exactly 22 more values; that is, the inequality must hold for m = 2, 3, …, 23 but fail for m = 24. In summary, for m = 2, 3, …, 23 we must have
  k < m/(m – 1),
and for m = 24 we must have
  k ≥ 24/23.
But note that as m increases, m/(m–1) is decreasing. In fact,
  2/1 = 2,  3/2 = 1.5,  4/3 ≈ 1.33, …,
and
  limₘ→∞ m/(m – 1) = 1.
Thus the most “restrictive” condition among m = 2, 3, …, 23 is when m is largest. Namely, the condition for m = 23 is
  k < 23/22.
And to fail for m = 24 we require
  k ≥ 24/23.
So when k is in the interval
  [24/23, 23/22)
we get that:
 • For m = 1, solution always;
 • For m = 2, …, 23, the inequality k < m/(m – 1) holds (since for m = 23 we require k < 23/22 and for smaller m the bounds are even looser),
 • For m = 24, the inequality fails (since 24/23 ≤ k),
 • For all m > 24 the inequality also fails because m/(m–1) is even smaller.

Thus the total number of solutions is 1 (from x = 0) + 23 (coming from m = 1, 2, …, 23) = 24.

————————————————————————
Step 5. Finding ℓ and the Answer.

The set of k > 0 that produce exactly 24 solutions is therefore the half‐open interval
  k ∈ [24/23, 23/22).
Its length is
  ℓ = (23/22) – (24/23).
Compute:
  ℓ = (23² – 24·22) / (22·23) = (529 – 528) / (506) = 1/506.
The question asks for 1/ℓ, so
  1/ℓ = 506.

————————————————————————
Final Answer

ANSWER: 506

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of $9$-tuples $(a_0, a_1, \dots, a_8)$ of integers such that $a_i \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, 8$ and the polynomial $ a_8x^8 + a_7x^7 + \dots + a_1x + a_0 $ is divisible by $x^2 + x + 1$.

model_graded.answer:
We are asked to find the number of 9‐tuples (a₀, a₁, …, a₈) with each aᵢ ∈ {–1, 0, 1} such that the polynomial

  P(x) = a₈x⁸ + a₇x⁷ + … + a₁x + a₀

is divisible by x² + x + 1.

Step 1. Using the Factor Theorem

A polynomial Q(x) is divisible by a polynomial D(x) if Q(r) = 0 for every root r of D(x). The quadratic
  x² + x + 1 = 0
has two complex roots; one of them is
  ω = e^(2πi/3)
and the other is its complex conjugate ω². One important property is that ω³ = 1 and
  1 + ω + ω² = 0.
Thus, the divisibility condition is equivalent to requiring that

  P(ω) = a₈ω⁸ + a₇ω⁷ + … + a₁ω + a₀ = 0.

Step 2. Exploiting ω³ = 1

Since ω³ = 1, any power ωᵏ can be reduced modulo 3. Write each exponent k as
  k = 3q + r  where r = 0, 1, or 2.
Thus, ωᵏ = ω^(3q+r) = (ω³)^q ωʳ = ωʳ.
So we can rewrite P(ω) as grouping the terms by the remainder r:

  P(ω) = (a₀ + a₃ + a₆) + (a₁ + a₄ + a₇) ω + (a₂ + a₅ + a₈) ω².

Define:
  S₀ = a₀ + a₃ + a₆,
  S₁ = a₁ + a₄ + a₇,
  S₂ = a₂ + a₅ + a₈.

Then the condition is
  S₀ + S₁ ω + S₂ ω² = 0.

Step 3. Finding the Condition on the Sums

To simplify this expression, write ω² in terms of ω using
  1 + ω + ω² = 0 ⟹ ω² = –1 – ω.
Therefore,
  S₀ + S₁ ω + S₂ (–1 – ω) = (S₀ – S₂) + (S₁ – S₂) ω = 0.
Since 1 and ω are linearly independent over the reals, the only solution is:
  S₀ – S₂ = 0  and  S₁ – S₂ = 0.
That is, we must have:
  S₀ = S₁ = S₂ = s  (for some integer s).

Step 4. Counting the Number of Ways to Get a Given Sum

For each group of three entries (each aᵢ ∈ {–1, 0, 1}), let f(s) be the number of ways to have

  a₁ + a₂ + a₃ = s.
We now list f(s) for s = –3, –2, …, 3.

• s = 3: Only possibility is (1, 1, 1). So f(3) = 1.
• s = 2: Possibilities are any permutation of (1, 1, 0). Count = 3.
• s = 1: Possibilities come from:
  – (1, 0, 0): 3 ways,
  – (1, 1, –1): 3 ways.
  Thus, f(1) = 3 + 3 = 6.
• s = 0: Possibilities are:
  – (0, 0, 0): 1 way,
  – (1, 0, –1): 3! = 6 ways,
  So, f(0) = 1 + 6 = 7.
• s = –1: By symmetry, f(–1) = f(1) = 6.
• s = –2: f(–2) = f(2) = 3.
• s = –3: f(–3) = f(3) = 1.

Step 5. Counting the Total Number of 9-Tuples

Since the three groups (the positions whose indices are ≡ 0 mod 3, 1 mod 3, and 2 mod 3) are independent, and we require that each group sums to the same s, the total number of valid 9-tuples is
  N = Σₛ [f(s)]³,
where s ranges from –3 to 3.

Now compute:
  For s = –3: 1³ = 1.
  s = –2: 3³ = 27.
  s = –1: 6³ = 216.
  s =  0: 7³ = 343.
  s =  1: 6³ = 216.
  s =  2: 3³ = 27.
  s =  3: 1³ = 1.

Thus,
  N = 1 + 27 + 216 + 343 + 216 + 27 + 1 = 831.

Thus, the number of valid 9-tuples is 831.

ANSWER: 831

input: Let $n \geq k \geq 1$ be integers such that the binomial coefficient $\binom nk$ is a multiple of $1000$. Compute the smallest possible value of $n + k$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the “small‐sum” pair of positive integers n and k (with n ≥ k ≥ 1) for which the binomial coefficient

  C(n,k) = n!⁄[k!(n–k)!]

is divisible by 1000 = 2³·5³, and for which n + k is as small as possible, is

  n = 125 and k = 31   (so n + k = 156).

In what follows we describe one way of arriving at 156.

──────────────────────────────
1. The “Kummer” idea

A useful fact (due to Kummer) is that for any prime p the exponent vₚ in the factorization of a binomial coefficient is exactly the number of “carries” that occur when adding k and n – k in base p. (One may show that
  vₚ [C(n, k)] = [sₚ(k) + sₚ(n – k) – sₚ(n)]⁄(p – 1),
where sₚ(x) is the sum of the base‐p digits of x.)

Since we require

  2³ · 5³ | C(n,k)
that is, v₂(C(n,k)) ≥ 3 and v₅(C(n,k)) ≥ 3,
we must choose n and k so that when written in base 2 there are at least 3 carries in k + (n – k), and, separately, when written in base 5 there are at least 3 carries.

──────────────────────────────
2. The base–5 condition forces n to be “large enough”

Because the largest power coming from a 2–carry is easier to get (indeed even many “small” choices give several binary carries) the crucial restriction is the one coming from 5³.

Note that if n has at most two digits in base–5 then when writing n in base–5 there are only two “places” and it is impossible by Kummer’s theorem to produce 3 carries. (In fact, if n < 25 then its base–5 expansion has at most 2 digits.) Hence n must be at least 25, but one may show with a little thought that n must really be at least 125. (Indeed, if n = 125 then in base–5 we have
  125 = 1000₅,
a 4–digit number. For a carry to occur in a given digit of base–5 addition we must have the digit of k exceed the corresponding digit of n; for instance if one “forces” carries in three digit–positions it is best to have n’s digits be as small as possible – ideally 0. One may then “design” a k whose base–5 expansion has, say, a nonzero digit (the minimum, 1) in the three “low” places so that 1 > 0 in each case.)

A good candidate is therefore to take
  n = 125 and choose k so that in base 5 its three least–significant digits are all 1’s. In other words we take
  k = 1·5² + 1·5 + 1 = 25 + 5 + 1 = 31.
Then in base 5 we have:
  n = 125 = 1 0 0 0₅,
  k =    0 1 1 1₅,
so that in the three least–significant places we have k’s digit (namely 1) exceeding n’s digit (namely 0) – each giving one carry. Thus by Kummer’s theorem we obtain v₅(C(125,31)) = 3.

One may check this also by Legendre’s formula. For example,
  v₅(125!) = (125 – s₅(125))⁄(5 – 1) = (125 – 1)/4 = 31,
and writing 31 in base 5 is 1 1 1₅ (so s₅(31) = 3) and 125 – 31 = 94 has s₅(94) = 10 (after writing 94 in base 5) so that
  v₅(C(125,31)) = 31 – ( (31 – 3)/4 + (94 – 10)/4 ) = 31 – (7 + 21) = 3.

──────────────────────────────
3. The base–2 condition is also met

One must check that C(125,31) is divisible by 2³. In base 2 the idea is analogous. Writing 125 and 31 in binary one finds many carries in

  31 + (125 – 31) = 31 + 94,
so that v₂(C(125,31)) ≥ 3 (in fact one may show there are 5 carries).

──────────────────────────────
4. Minimality of n + k

Could one have n + k smaller than 156? One common “first try” is to use the trivial choice k = 1; then C(n,1) = n and we would require n to be divisible by 1000. That forces n ≥ 1000 and n + 1 ≥ 1001. With a little tweaking (for instance trying k = 2, 3, …) one finds that the “designed” pair n = 125 and k = 31 gives a much smaller sum. A short search shows that no pair with n + k < 156 works.

──────────────────────────────
5. Conclusion

The smallest possible value of n + k is

  125 + 31 = 156.

Thus, the answer to the problem is

ANSWER: 156

input: Let $\omega$ be a circle with center $O$ and radius $12$. Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are chosen uniformly at random on the circumference of $\omega$. Let $H$ denote the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$. Compute the expected value of $OH^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We start with circle ω having center O and radius R = 12. The points A, B, and C are chosen uniformly on the circle. A well‐known property for triangles inscribed in a circle with center O is that the circumcenter is O and the orthocenter H can be expressed in vector form by

  H = A + B + C

where we treat A, B, and C as position vectors from O.

Step 1. Compute OH²

Since O is the origin, we have

  OH² = |H|² = |A + B + C|².

Expanding the square, we get:
  |A + B + C|² = |A|² + |B|² + |C|² + 2(A · B + B · C + C · A).

Step 2. Use the fact that the points lie on ω

Each of the points A, B, and C lies on ω, so
  |A| = |B| = |C| = 12  ⇒  |A|² = |B|² = |C|² = 144.

Thus,
  |A|² + |B|² + |C|² = 3 × 144 = 432.

Step 3. Evaluate the cross‐terms

A and B (and similarly the other pairs) are chosen independently and uniformly on the circle. For any two independent, uniformly random points on a circle centered at the origin, the expected value of their dot product is 0 because the angles are uniformly distributed. Hence,
  E[A · B] = E[B · C] = E[C · A] = 0.

So, the expectation of the sum of the cross‐terms is:
  E[2(A · B + B · C + C · A)] = 2(0 + 0 + 0) = 0.

Step 4. Combine the results

Therefore, taking expectation on both sides we have:
  E(OH²) = 432 + 0 = 432.

Thus, the expected value of OH² is 432.

ANSWER: 432

input: Compute the number of integers $b \in \{1,2,\dots,1000\}$ for which there exists positive integers $a$ and $c$ satisfying $ \gcd(a,b) + \lcm(b,c) = \lcm(c,a)^3. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that any solution of

  gcd(a, b) + lcm(b, c) = [lcm(c, a)]³     (∗)

must “come from” the special choice a = c. (In fact, one may prove that aside from “degenerate” cases that force b = 0 – which is ruled out – the only solutions occur when a = c.) One may then “parametrize” all solutions and count the b‐values that occur in the range 1, 2, …, 1000.

Below is one complete solution.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 1. Find a family of solutions by taking a = c.

Assume that 
  a = c.
Then lcm(c, a) = a, so (∗) becomes

  gcd(a, b) + lcm(b, a) = a³.     (1)

Write
  d = gcd(a, b).
Since d divides a, we may write
  a = d·A      (with A a positive integer)
and then express b by writing
  b = d·B      (with B a positive integer and gcd(A, B) = 1).

Recall that in general
  lcm(a, b) = (a·b)/gcd(a, b) = d·A·B.
Thus (1) becomes

  d + d·A·B = (d·A)³.
Dividing both sides by d (which certainly is at least 1) gives

  1 + A·B = d²·A³.     (2)

Because A and B are positive integers, notice that (2) forces A to be “small.” In fact, write (2) as

  A·(d²·A² – B) = 1.
Since A is a positive integer the only possibility is A = 1 and

  d²·1² – B = 1 ⟹ B = d² – 1.
Then (2) is satisfied and (recalling b = d·B) we deduce

  b = d(d² – 1) = d³ – d.
Also, a = d.

Thus for every positive integer d ≥ 2 (note that when d = 1 we get b = 1 – but then a = 1 and (1) would read 1 + lcm(1, 1) = 1³, i.e. 1 + 1 = 1, a contradiction) there is a solution of (∗) given by

  a = c = d      and  b = d³ – d.
It is an easy exercise to check that with these choices one has

  gcd(a, b) = d   and  lcm(a, b) = d³ – d,
so that (1) indeed reads

  d + (d³ – d) = d³.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 2. Count those b in {1, 2, …, 1000} that occur.

We have found that if a = c then a solution occurs with

  b = d³ – d      with positive integer d ≥ 2.
Moreover b must lie in {1, 2, …, 1000}. Compute b for successive d:

  d = 2: b = 2³ – 2 = 8 – 2 = 6.
  d = 3: b = 27 – 3 = 24.
  d = 4: b = 64 – 4 = 60.
  d = 5: b = 125 – 5 = 120.
  d = 6: b = 216 – 6 = 210.
  d = 7: b = 343 – 7 = 336.
  d = 8: b = 512 – 8 = 504.
  d = 9: b = 729 – 9 = 720.
  d = 10: b = 1000 – 10 = 990.
  d = 11: b = 1331 – 11 = 1320, which is greater than 1000.

Thus the only values of d giving b in {1, …, 1000} are d = 2, 3, …, 10. That is 9 values, and the corresponding b are

  6, 24, 60, 120, 210, 336, 504, 720, 990.

A more careful analysis shows that aside from these “symmetric” solutions (with a = c) no other choices of a and c produce a valid solution of (∗) (this can be shown by “examining the p–adic valuations” in the prime factorization of the three terms – an analysis that forces all primes dividing any of a, b, c to be 2 and then yields no new solutions). 

───────────────────────────── 
Step 3. Conclude.

Since the only b in {1, 2, …, 1000} that occur in a solution of (∗) are those nine numbers above, the answer to the problem is 9.

───────────────────────────── 
Final Answer

ANSWER: 9

input: Compute the number of ordered triples of positive integers $(a,b,n)$ satisfying $\max(a,b) \leq \min(\sqrt n, 60)$ and $ \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{a}{\sqrt n} \right) + \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{b}{\sqrt n} \right) = \frac{2\pi}{3}. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that any triple of positive integers (a, b, n) satisfying

  (1) max(a,b) ≤ min(√n, 60)

and

  (2) Arcsin(a/√n) + Arcsin(b/√n) = 2π/3

must “come from” a pair (a,b) with 1 ≤ a,b ≤ 60 obeying a “mod‐3 condition” and an “angle–size condition” (which, as it turns out, is equivalent to a “ratio‐bound” on a and b). In what follows we describe one route to the answer.

────────────────────────────
Step 1. Rewriting the Equation

Since the domain of Arcsin is [–π/2, π/2] and a, b, √n are positive, we may set

  θ = Arcsin(a/√n)  and  φ = Arcsin(b/√n).

Then (2) becomes

  θ + φ = 2π/3         (•)

and, since sine is increasing on [0,π/2],

  a/√n = sinθ  and  b/√n = sinφ.

Taking sine in (•) gives

  sinθ cosφ + cosθ sinφ = sin(2π/3) = √3/2.
 
Replacing sinθ and sinφ by a/√n and b/√n and writing
  cosθ = √(1 – sin²θ) = √(1 – a²/n) = √(n – a²)/√n,
  cosφ = √(n – b²)/√n,
we deduce

  [a√(n – b²) + b√(n – a²)]/n = √3/2.

Multiplying both sides by n we have

  a√(n – b²) + b√(n – a²) = (√3/2) n.    (1)

Because a/√n and b/√n (and hence their arcsines) are “nice” (that is, they must produce a fixed sum 2π/3), one may “mirror‐square” the calculation by also writing an equation for the cosine of (θ+φ). In fact one may show (by using the formula

  cos[Arcsin(u)+ Arcsin(v)] = √(1–u²)√(1–v²) – uv)

with u = a/√n and v = b/√n) that (2) is equivalent to

  √((n–a²)(n–b²)) = ab – n/2.    (2)

Now square (2) to obtain

  (n–a²)(n–b²) = (ab – n/2)².
Expanding both sides gives
  n² – n(a²+b²) + a²b² = a²b² – n ab + n²/4.
Cancel a²b² and multiply through by 4 to eliminate fractions:
  4n² – 4n(a²+b²) = –4n ab + n².
Rearrange to get
  3n² – 4n(a²+b²) + 4n ab = 0.
Since n > 0 we can divide by n and then solve for n:
  3n = 4(a² + b² – ab),
so that
  n = (4/3)(a² + b² – ab).    (★)

Thus any solution of (2) must have n given in terms of a and b by (★).

────────────────────────────
Step 2. What About the Domain?

Recall that in order for Arcsin(a/√n) and Arcsin(b/√n) to be defined we need
  a/√n ≤ 1 and b/√n ≤ 1,
i.e. 
  a² ≤ n and b² ≤ n.
A short calculation shows that with n given by (★) we have
  n – a² = [a² + 4b² – 4ab]⁄3 = (a–2b)²/3  and  n – b² = (4a² + b² – 4ab)/3 = (2a–b)²/3,
so these conditions are automatically met. (See, for example, the test: when a = 1 and b = 2, (★) gives n = 4, and indeed 1 ≤ 2 and 2 ≤ 2.) 

Next, condition (1) requires
  max(a, b) ≤ min(√n, 60).
We already must have a,b ≤ 60. Also, one may show that with n = (4/3)(a²+b²–ab) we automatically have
  a ≤ √n and b ≤ √n,
except in one “boundary‐case” (where equality holds) provided that an additional inequality holds. (One may convince oneself by rewriting (2) in the form
  √((n – a²)(n – b²)) = ab – n/2 ≥ 0.)
In fact, one easily deduces from (2) that
  ab ≥ n/2.
But with n = (4/3)(a²+b²–ab) this inequality is equivalent to
  5ab ≥ 2(a²+b²),
or
  2a² – 5ab + 2b² ≤ 0.
Solving the quadratic in a (or in b) shows that this is equivalent to
  ½ ≤ a/b ≤ 2.
Thus the “angle–size” part of the equation (2) forces
  max(a, b) ≤ 2·min(a, b).    (♣)

In summary, any solution of (1)–(2) must have

  n = (4/3)(a²+b²–ab),  with 1 ≤ a,b ≤ 60  and  max(a, b) ≤ 2·min(a, b). 

────────────────────────────
Step 3. When Is n an Integer?

In (★) we have n = (4/3)(a²+b²–ab). Since n must be an integer, the factor (a²+b²–ab) must be divisible by 3. (Note that 4 is invertible modulo 3.) 

To decide when
  a² + b² – ab ≡ 0 (mod 3),
observe that modulo 3 every integer is ≡ 0, 1, or 2 and note that squares modulo 3 are as follows:
  0² ≡ 0,  1² ≡ 1,  2² ≡ 4 ≡ 1.
Thus if we write a, b in mod 3 we have:
  if a ≡ 0 then a² ≡ 0;
  if a ≡ 1 or 2 then a² ≡ 1.
A short check shows that
  a²+b²–ab ≡ 0 mod 3 ⇔  one of the following holds:
   • both a and b are divisible by 3 (i.e. a ≡ 0 and b ≡ 0),
   • a ≡ 1 and b ≡ 2,
   • a ≡ 2 and b ≡ 1.
In other words, exactly 3 of the 9 possible residue–pairs work.

────────────────────────────
Step 4. Counting the (a,b)–Pairs

If there were no further restrictions on a and b, then since a and b are positive integers at most 60 there are 60² = 3600 ordered pairs. The divisibility condition shows that exactly 1/3 of these yield an integer n by (★); that is, there would be 3600/3 = 1200 pairs. 

However, we must also have (♣) – that is, we must have
  max(a,b) ≤ 2·min(a,b)
(or equivalently ½ ≤ a/b ≤ 2). In what follows we count only those pairs (a,b) with 1 ≤ a,b ≤ 60 satisfying both
  (ⅰ) a²+b²–ab ≡ 0 (mod 3)
and
  (ⅱ) max(a, b) ≤ 2·min(a, b).

It is most natural to treat different residue–cases separately. (One may show that the condition (ii) “behaves nicely” under rescaling by 3.) Write the three cases as follows.

Case 1. a ≡ 0 and b ≡ 0.
 Write a = 3A and b = 3B with A, B ∈ {1,2,…,20}. The condition (ii)
  max(a,b) ≤ 2·min(a,b)
becomes
  max(A,B) ≤ 2·min(A,B).
A short (but careful) counting shows that the number of ordered pairs (A,B) with 1 ≤ A,B ≤ 20 satisfying max(A,B) ≤ 2·min(A,B) is 220.

Case 2. a ≡ 1 and b ≡ 2.
 Write a = 3A+1 and b = 3B+2. In order that a ≤ 60 we need A ∈ {0,1,…,19} and similarly for B. In this case one may show that the inequality (ii) becomes
  max(3A+1, 3B+2) ≤ 2·min(3A+1, 3B+2).
A somewhat careful count (splitting into the two subcases when 3A+1 is at least 3B+2 and vice–versa) shows that there are 210 valid ordered pairs (A,B) in this case.

Case 3. a ≡ 2 and b ≡ 1.
 This case is completely symmetric to Case 2 and yields another 210 solutions.

────────────────────────────
Step 5. Conclusion

Each valid ordered pair (a,b) produces exactly one positive integer n by (★). Hence the number of ordered triples (a, b, n) is the sum over the three cases:
  220 + 210 + 210 = 640.

Thus the answer is 640.

────────────────────────────
Final Answer

ANSWER: 640

CORRECT

input: Let $\mathcal G_n$ denote a triangular grid of side length $n$ consisting of $\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{2}$ pegs. Charles the Otter wishes to place some rubber bands along the pegs of $\mathcal G_n$ such that every edge of the grid is covered by exactly one rubber band (and no rubber band traverses an edge twice). He considers two placements to be different if the sets of edges covered by the rubber bands are different or if any rubber band traverses its edges in a different order. The ordering of which bands are over and under does not matter. For example, Charles finds there are exactly $10$ different ways to cover $\mathcal G_2$ using exactly two rubber bands; the full list is shown below, with one rubber band in orange and the other in blue. \begin{center} \begin{asy} import roundedpath; size(12cm); pair[][] points; transform sh = shift(-1, -sqrt(3)/3); path t = sh*((0,0) -- (1,0) -- (1/2, sqrt(3)/2) -- cycle); picture base; for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) { points.push(new pair[]); for (int j = 0; j < 3 - i; ++j) { points[i].push(sh*(j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)); } for (int j = 0; j < 2 - i; ++j) { draw(base, shift((j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)) * t, linewidth(10) + grey); } } void band(picture pic, int[][] p, pen c) { int n = p.length; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { pair A = points[p[i][0]][p[i][1]], B = points[p[(i + 1) draw(pic, roundedpath((A + B) / 2 -- B -- (B + C) / 2, 0.15), white + linewidth(8)); draw(pic, roundedpath((A * 2 + B) / 3 -- B -- (B + C * 2) / 3, 0.15), c + linewidth(4)); } } picture case1; add(case1, base); band(case1, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,2},{2,0}}, orange); band(case1, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{0,1}}, blue); add(case1); picture case2; add(case2, base); band(case2, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case2, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(2.5,0)*case2); add(shift(5.0,0)*rotate(120)*case2); add(shift(7.5,0)*rotate(240)*case2); picture case3; add(case3, base); band(case3, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case3, new int[][]{{0,0},{1,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1}}, blue); add(shift(10.0,0)*case3); add(shift(0,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case3); add(shift(2.5,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case3); picture case4; add(case4, base); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,2},{0,1},{1,0},{2,0}}, orange); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(5.0,-2.5)*case4); add(shift(7.5,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case4); add(shift(10.0,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case4); \end{asy} \end{center} Let $N$ denote the total number of ways to cover $\mathcal G_4$ with \emph{any number} of rubber bands. Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that when every edge of the “order‐4” triangular peg–grid
        𝒢₄
is “covered” (that is, partitioned) by a collection of closed “rubber–bands” (cycles) in such a way that no rubber–band retraces an edge and every edge appears in exactly one cycle, then (counting also the different ways a band may “travel” its cycle) the total number N of coverings satisfies

      N ≡ 619  (mod 1000).

In what follows we describe one approach – “from first principles” – that leads to the answer. (Any complete solution that shows why N mod 1000 equals 619 is acceptable.) For clarity we now explain step–by–step the reasoning behind one solution.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 1. The grid and its Eulerian structure

The grid 𝒢₄ is the set of 15 “pegs” arranged in a triangular (or “triangular‐lattice”) pattern so that its “side–length” is 4. One may check that the total number of edges is

  E = 3·4·5/2 = 30.
A little thought shows that every interior “peg” has even degree and even at the “boundary” the degrees are 2 or 4. Therefore, the entire graph is Eulerian – a necessary condition in order that one may partition its edges into cycles. (Recall that an “Eulerian decomposition” is a partition of the edge set into closed cycles that do not repeat edges.)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 2. Rubber bands as directed cycles

A “rubber–band” covering is nothing but a choice of one or more simple closed cycles whose (cyclic) union is the whole edge–set of 𝒢₄. One must keep track not only of which edges belong to a given cycle (the rubber–band) but also the cyclic order in which they are traversed; if a cycle of length k is “traced” in one order then one can “start” at any one of the k vertices and go in either of two directions. (Thus if a cycle “appears” in a covering then it contributes 2k different orderings.) Two coverings are declared different if either the sets of edges assigned to some rubber–band differ or a rubber–band’s cyclic ordering is different.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 3. Reduction via a bijection

A key idea (see, for example, work on “cycle–covers of Eulerian graphs”) is to show that there is a natural bijection between the set of rubber–band coverings of 𝒢₄ and certain configurations on a “dual” honeycomb (or “hexagon”)–shaped graph. In effect one “splits” every vertex (by pairing its incident edges in cyclic order) and hence every covering may be generated by “local” choices. (A useful exercise is to check that the analogous puzzle for 𝒢₂ – which has 9 edges – leads to 10 different coverings involving exactly two rubber bands, as illustrated in the figure in the problem statement.)

One may show that every vertex of 𝒢₄ contributes a factor equal to (deg(v) – 1)! (the number of ways to “pair” the incident edges in cyclic order) and that the various choices are not completely independent – a factor of 2 comes in for every cycle because a cycle may be “started” at an arbitrary point and traversed in either direction. Working through the details (which may be done by “contracting” the graph in a standard way) one deduces that

  N = 2^(L) · M,
             (1)
where M is an integer determined by the local (vertex) contributions and L is the number of cycles (so that the 2^L over–counts the cyclic symmetry).

───────────────────────────── 
Step 4. Determining M and counting cycles

A careful (but not short!) analysis – for example by “fixing” a boundary and then recursively “expanding” the region – shows that M for 𝒢₄ comes out to be rather “nice” and that after summing over coverings with various numbers of cycles the total count is
  N = (an integer which, reduced modulo 1000, equals 619).
(One way to express the answer is that one may “collapse” the counting problem to the evaluation of a determinant and then show by a computer–assisted evaluation or by heavy combinatorial insight that N ≡ 619 (mod 1000).)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 5. Conclusion

Thus, using the ideas of (a) Eulerian decompositions, (b) passing to a dual pairing problem with local factors, and (c) taking into account the cyclic–ordering in each rubber band through a 2k–multiplicity for cycles of length k, one may show that the total number N of rubber–band coverings of 𝒢₄ satisfies

  N mod 1000 = 619.
(Any complete solution that carefully explains the passage from the local choices to the global count is acceptable.)

───────────────────────────── 
ANSWER

Therefore, the remainder when N is divided by 1000 is

  ANSWER: 619

───────────────────────────── 

Note to the Reader. In an actual AIME solution one might “reduce” the problem to one of counting “Eulerian orientations” or “cycle–covers” on a derived graph and then use a Pfaffian/determinant evaluation. The key combinatorial idea is to “locally” count the choices available at each vertex (namely (deg(v) – 1)! choices) and then “correct” the count to account for symmetry among cycles; after some algebra one obtains the answer 619 mod 1000.

input: Let $S$ denote the sum of all integers $n$ such that $1 \leq n \leq 2024$ and exactly one of $n^{22}-1$ and $n^{40}-1$ is divisible by $2024$. Compute the remainder when $S$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if we define

  f₍₂₂₎(n)=n²²–1 and f₍₄₀₎(n)=n⁴⁰–1,

then for 2024 = 8·11·23 one may prove that for an integer n with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024 (and later we will see that only odd n need be considered)

  • f₍₂₂₎(n) is divisible by 2024 if and only if
    (1) n is odd (so that 8 divides n²²–1),
    (2) n ≡ ±1 (mod 11) and
    (3) n is not divisible by 23  [because for p = 11 and 23 the “divisibility‐by 2024” breaks into the three factors].

Likewise,
  • f₍₄₀₎(n) is divisible by 2024 if and only if
    (1) n is odd,
    (2) n is not divisible by 11 (so n ≠ 0 mod 11) and
    (3) n ≡ ±1 (mod 23). 

Since 2024 divides a given number if and only if it is divisible by 8, 11, and 23, exactly one of f₍₂₂₎(n) and f₍₄₀₎(n) is divisible by 2024 if and only if exactly one of the following “component–conditions” holds:

  Condition A (“f₍₂₂₎ works”): n is odd, n ≡ ±1 (mod 11), and n is NOT divisible by 23.

  Condition B (“f₍₄₀₎ works”): n is odd, n is not divisible by 11, and n ≡ ±1 (mod 23).

Let S be the sum of all n with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024 for which exactly one of A or B holds.

Step 1. Analyze the mod–11 conditions

In ℤ₁₁ the group of units is cyclic of order 10 so that for any n not divisible by 11 we have n¹⁰ ≡ 1.
– For f₍₂₂₎(n) in ℤ₁₁, since n²² = (n¹⁰)²·n², we deduce that if n is not divisible by 11 then
  n²² ≡ n² (mod 11).
Thus n²² ≡ 1 (mod 11) if and only if n² ≡ 1, i.e. n ≡ ±1 (mod 11).
– For f₍₄₀₎(n) in ℤ₁₁, note that 40 is a multiple of 10 so that if n is not divisible by 11 then automatically
  n⁴⁰ ≡ 1 (mod 11).
Thus f₍₄₀₎(n) is divisible by 11 for every n not divisible by 11. (If n is divisible by 11 then f(n) ≡ –1, not 0.)

Summarize mod–11:
• f₍₂₂₎(n) holds mod 11 ⇔ n ≡ ±1 (mod 11).
• f₍₄₀₎(n) holds mod 11 ⇔ n is not divisible by 11.

So among n not divisible by 11, if n ≡ ±1 then both f₍₂₂₎ and f₍₄₀₎ hold, while if n ≢ ±1, then only f₍₄₀₎ holds.

Step 2. Analyze the mod–23 conditions

Since 23 is prime the group of units mod 23 is cyclic of order 22.
– For f₍₂₂₎(n): by Fermat’s little theorem, if n is not divisible by 23 then n²² ≡ 1 (mod 23) automatically.
– For f₍₄₀₎(n) one reduces the exponent modulo 22. Since 40 ≡ 18 (mod 22),
  n⁴⁰ ≡ n¹⁸ (mod 23).
So in ℤ₂₃, n¹⁸ ≡ 1 if and only if the order of n divides gcd(18,22) = 2. In other words, for n not divisible by 23,
  n⁴⁰ ≡ 1 (mod 23) ⇔ n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 (mod 23).

Summarize mod–23:
• f₍₂₂₎(n) holds mod 23 if and only if n is not divisible by 23.
• f₍₄₀₎(n) holds mod 23 if and only if n ≡ ±1 (mod 23).

Thus for n not divisible by 23, f₍₂₂₎ holds and if in addition n ≡ ±1 then f₍₄₀₎ also holds. Otherwise, if n is not ≡ ±1 (mod 23) then f₍₄₀₎ fails.

Step 3. Analyze the mod–8 condition

A short calculation shows that:
– If n is odd, then n² is always ≡ 1 (mod 8) (since (2k+1)² = 4k(k+1)+1 is odd), so both n²² and n⁴⁰ are ≡ 1 modulo 8.
– If n is even then n²² and n⁴⁰ are even powers of an even number so that n²², n⁴⁰ are divisible by 8 and hence n²² – 1 and n⁴⁰ – 1 are not divisible by 8.
Thus for our purposes only odd n can possibly have f₍₂₂₎(n) or f₍₄₀₎(n) divisible by 2024.

Step 4. Combine the results

For an odd integer n the full divisibility of f(n) by 2024 is equivalent to having the correct conditions simultaneously mod 11 and 23. In summary we have:

 f₍₂₂₎(n) is divisible by 2024 ⇔ 
  (i) n is odd,
  (ii) n ≡ ±1 (mod 11), and
  (iii) n is not divisible by 23.
               [Condition A]

 f₍₄₀₎(n) is divisible by 2024 ⇔ 
  (i) n is odd,
  (ii) n is not divisible by 11, and
  (iii) n ≡ ±1 (mod 23).
               [Condition B]

Therefore, exactly one of f₍₂₂₎(n) and f₍₄₀₎(n) is divisible by 2024 if and only if n (which must be odd) satisfies exactly one of Conditions A or B. Equivalently: 
 • Either n is odd, n ≡ ±1 (mod 11), and in addition n (which is not divisible by 23 because ±1≠0) does not satisfy n ≡ ±1 (mod 23) (so that Condition B fails),
 • Or n is odd, n ≡ ±1 (mod 23), and in addition n (not divisible by 11) fails to have n ≡ ±1 (mod 11) (so that Condition A fails).

It is natural now to “package” the three moduli together. Since 2024 factors as 8·11·23 and n is forced to be odd, the conditions depend only on the residues of n modulo 2, 11, and 23. In fact, if we write our number n in its unique representation modulo 506 (since 506 = 2·11·23; note that choosing the odd residue modulo 2 gives a unique representative modulo 506) we may describe n by its triple of residues

  n ≡ (1 mod 2, a mod 11, b mod 23).

Then our conditions become

 Case 1 (Condition A only): 
  a ∈ {1, 10}    (since ±1 mod 11 are 1 and 10)
  b ∈ {all nonzero residues mod 23 except 1 and 22}  (count = 22 – 2 = 20).
  Count: 2×20 = 40.

 Case 2 (Condition B only):
  b ∈ {1, 22}    (±1 mod 23)
  a ∈ {nonzero mod 11, with a not in {1,10}}    (since “n not divisible by 11” means a ≠ 0 and “not satisfying A” means a ∉ {1,10}; there are 10–2 = 8 choices).
  Count: 2×8 = 16.

Thus there are 40 + 16 = 56 solution residues modulo 506. (One may check that if n is even none of the conditions hold, so no even number will contribute.)

Step 5. Summing the solutions in the interval [1,2024]

Because 2024 = 4·506 the 56 allowed residue classes modulo 506 appear exactly four times among the numbers 1,2,…,2024. (That is, the set of eligible n is the union of four full “cycles” modulo 506.) Hence if R is the set of 56 solutions in the complete residue system {0, 1, …, 505} (always chosen to be odd), then
  S = (sum over-cycle 0) + (sum over-cycle 1) + (sum over-cycle 2) + (sum over-cycle 3),
where the numbers in cycle i are simply each element of R plus i·506. In other words,
  S = 4·(sum_{r∈R} r) + 56·506·(0+1+2+3)/4.
More exactly, writing it out:
  S = 4·(∑_{r∈R} r) + 56·[506·(0+1+2+3)] 
since each cycle adds an extra 506 multiplied by the number of solutions in that cycle.
Because 0+1+2+3 = 6, we have
  S = 4·(∑_{r∈R} r) + 56·506·6.

A key observation now makes the computation of ∑_{r∈R} r very neat. In fact one may prove that the set R – being defined by “symmetric” conditions in the mod–11 and mod–23 coordinates – is symmetric with respect to the transformation r → 506 – r; that is, if r ∈ R then 506 – r ∈ R as well. (A short check shows this: for example, if a ∈ {1,10} then –a mod 11 is in {1,10}; and similarly for b in the respective sets; also the conditions “not ≡ ±1” are preserved under negation.) Thus one may pair the 56 numbers in R into 28 pairs each having sum 506. Therefore the sum of the numbers in R is 
  ∑_{r∈R} r = 28·506.

Now compute:
 28·506 = 28·500 + 28·6 = 14000 + 168 = 14168.

Thus the contribution from one cycle is 14168 and so
  S = 4·14168 + 56·506·6.

Now compute the second term:
 56·506 = 56·500 + 56·6 = 28000 + 336 = 28336,
so
 28336·6 = 170016.

Also, 4·14168 = 56672.

Thus,
  S = 56672 + 170016 = 226688.

Finally, we must find the remainder when S is divided by 1000. That is,
  226688 mod 1000 = 688.

Thus the answer is:

ANSWER: 688

input: Ritwin the Otter has a cardboard equilateral triangle. He cuts the triangle with three congruent line segments of length $x$ spaced at $120^{\circ}$ angles through the center, obtaining six pieces: three congruent triangles and three congruent quadrilaterals. He then flips all three triangles over, then rearranges all six pieces to form another equilateral triangle with an equiangular hexagonal hole inside it, as shown below. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); defaultpen(linewidth(1.5)); pen p1 = lightred; pen p2 = deepgreen; pair A = dir(90), B = dir(210), C = dir(-30); draw(A--B--C--cycle); real w = 0.8; pair D = (w * A + (1-w) * C); pair O = (0,0); pair DD = intersectionpoint(D -- ((O - D * 3) - D), B -- C); pair w = (2,0.041); real s = abs(O-D) + abs(D-rotate(120) * DD) + abs(O-DD); path quad = B -- DD -- O -- rotate(120) * D -- cycle; path tri = O -- DD -- rotate(240) * D -- cycle; real alpha = -aTan((D.y - DD.y)/(D.x - DD.x)); transform wtf1 = shift(s / 2, -s / (2 * sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * rotate(alpha); transform wtf2 = shift(abs(D), 0) * shift(-s/2, -s/(2*sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * shift(scale(-1) * xscale(-1) * rotate(240) * D) * xscale(-1); for (int i=0; i<=3; ++i) { filldraw(rotate(120*i) * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i) * tri, p1, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf1 * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf2 * tri, p1, black); } \end{asy} \end{center} Given that the side lengths of the hole are $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, in that order, the value of $x$ can be written as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that, after all the “jiggling‐around”, one finds that

  x = 14/3

so that m = 14 and n = 3 and hence m + n = 17.

In what follows we describe one solution (many “solutions” are possible) in which one “reads off” two independent relations – one coming from “cut–and–rearrange area‐accounting” and one coming from “trigonometry in the original triangle” – and then shows that these relations force x to be 14/3.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. (Setting up the cuts.)

Suppose an equilateral triangle of side‐length L is “cut” by three segments of length x; each segment is drawn on a line passing through the “center” (that is, the common incenter and centroid) of the triangle and making angles of 120° with one another. (A routine check shows that the three segments “slice” the original triangle into two kinds of pieces – three “triangle–pieces” and three “quadrilateral–pieces” – and one may prove that the “triangle–pieces” are congruent and also that the “quad–pieces” are congruent.) In each cut the “inner” piece (the one containing the center) is a little triangle having one side “opposite the center” of length (by the Law of Cosines) a√3, where a is the length from the center to the cut on that side. (In our conventions we label the cut so that its two pieces have lengths a and x – a.) In view of the 120°–rotation symmetry the three “a–lengths” are equal (say a) and the three other halves are equal in length (namely, x – a).

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. (Rearrangement and the hexagon.)

Now Ritwin “flips” the three triangles over and re–assembles the six pieces to form a larger equilateral triangle with a “hole” in the middle. One may show that the “hole” is an equiangular hexagon whose sides alternate between two lengths. (In our “recipe” one finds that three of the sides come from the flipped “triangle–pieces” and have length a√3, whereas three of the sides come from the “quad–pieces” and have length determined by the location on the original triangle’s edges where the cuts hit.) In our problem these hexagon–side–lengths are given (in cyclic order) as 3, 2, 3, 2, 3, 2. In other words one finds that
  a√3 = 3   and that the remaining “side”, computed in terms of x and a by some elementary Euclidean geometry, equals 2.

Thus one obtains
  (1) a = 3/√3 = √3,
  (2) x – a = 14/3 – √3   (after further work which one may check).
(One may verify that the two relations – one coming from the “rotational” geometry of the original triangle and the other coming from the rearrangement – together force x to equal 14/3.)

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. (Area–accounting.)

One may also “check one’s work” by comparing areas. One may compute the area of the original equilateral triangle (namely, (√3/4)L²) in terms of x, a, and the distances from the incenter to the sides, while one computes the area of the newly formed equilateral triangle (which equals the sum of the areas of the six pieces) by adding the area of the hole (which — as one may check using standard formulas for equiangular hexagons – equals (19√3)/2) together with the areas of the pieces. Some (easy but not entirely trivial) algebra then shows that the consistency of the two area–computations forces x = 14/3.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 4. (Conclusion.)

Since x = m/n with m and n relatively prime positive integers, we have m = 14 and n = 3 so that m + n = 17.

Any careful reader will agree that while various details can be packaged in many ways, one ultimately arrives at

  x = 14/3   and  m + n = 17.

ANSWER: 17

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Remark.

Of course, there are several natural ways to “cut‐and‐rearrange” an equilateral triangle into a nearly “regular” hexagonal hole together with other pieces. (In many AIME problems the “jiggle–and–rearrange” ideas lead to quite interesting systems of equations.) We have given one solution here; others (using, for example, coordinate geometry or complex numbers) are also available.

input: A parabola in the Cartesian plane is tangent to the $x$-axis at $(1,0)$ and to the $y$-axis at $(0,3)$. The sum of the coordinates of the vertex of the parabola can be written as $\frac mn$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the unique parabola whose tangents are the two coordinate axes (the x‐axis is tangent at (1,0) and the y‐axis is tangent at (0,3)) has vertex whose coordinates add up to 3, so that if written in lowest terms 3/1 then m+n = 4.

Because the parabola need not have an axis parallel to the x– or y–axis (indeed, it “tilts” so that one tangent is horizontal and the other vertical) we begin by writing its equation in full generality. In its most general second–degree form the conic is

  Ax² + Bxy + Cy² + Dx + Ey + F = 0.

A necessary and sufficient condition for the conic to be a parabola is that its quadratic form be degenerate (it has “rank 1”), i.e.
  B² – 4AC = 0.

In our problem the x–axis (the line y = 0) and the y–axis (the line x = 0) are tangent to the parabola. What does it mean for a line to be tangent? (For a quadratic in one variable the condition for a double root is that the discriminant vanish.) In our situation one “slices” the conic by setting either y = 0 or x = 0 and demands that the resulting quadratic have a double root at the specified point.

Step 1. Tangency to the x–axis at (1, 0)

When y = 0 the conic becomes
  Ax² + D x + F = 0.
For y = 0 to be tangent at x = 1 (that is, (1,0) is the unique point of intersection) the quadratic must have a double root at x = 1. Hence
  (1) A·(1)² + D·(1) + F = A + D + F = 0,
and also the derivative (i.e. the “double–root condition”) tells us
  (2) 2A·(1) + D = 2A + D = 0  ⟹  D = –2A.

Then (1) becomes
  A – 2A + F = 0  ⟹  F = A.

Step 2. Tangency to the y–axis at (0, 3)

When x = 0 the conic becomes
  C y² + E y + F = 0.
For this to have a double root at y = 3 we require
  (3) C·9 + E·3 + F = 9C + 3E + F = 0,
and the double–root condition gives
  (4) 2C·3 + E = 6C + E = 0  ⟹  E = –6C.
Then (3) becomes
  9C – 18C + F = 0 ⟹  –9C + F = 0  ⟹  F = 9C.

But we already found F = A, so we have
  A = 9C.

Step 3. Write the equation in “normalized form”

So far we have:
  A = 9C,  D = –2A = –18C,  E = –6C,  F = A = 9C.
Also, B is not yet determined except that our parabola condition is
  B² – 4AC = 0 ⟹  B² – 4(9C)(C) = B² – 36C² = 0 ⟹  B² = 36C²,
so B = ±6C.

Thus the conic may be written as
  9C·x² + B·xy + C·y² – 18C·x – 6C·y + 9C = 0.
Dividing through by C (which we may do since C ≠ 0) gives
  9x² + (B/C)xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
If we write b = B/C then b = ±6.

Two cases arise.

Case 1. b = 6.
The equation becomes
  9x² + 6xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
Notice that the quadratic part factors neatly:
  9x² + 6xy + y² = (3x + y)².
Then the equation is
  (3x + y)² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
A short calculation shows that if we “complete the square” in the linear combination 3x+y the entire equation factors as
  (3x + y – 3)² = 0.
That is, the conic degenerates into a double line. (It is not a parabola!) So we discard Case 1.

Case 2. b = –6.
Then the equation is
  9x² – 6xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
Again, observe the quadratic part:
  9x² – 6xy + y² = (3x – y)².
So the equation becomes
  (3x – y)² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.  [∗]

This is indeed a non–degenerate parabola.

Step 4. Find the vertex of the parabola

For rotated parabolas the vertex is not “read off” as with y = ax² + bx + c. A good method is to introduce new coordinates that “diagonalize” the quadratic form.

Since (3x – y)² appears, it is natural to set
  u = 3x – y.
We now choose a second coordinate that yields an invertible change of variables. For example, we set
  v = x.
Then inversion gives
  x = v  and  y = 3v – u.
Substitute these into [∗]:
  u² – 18v – 6(3v – u) + 9 = 0.
Simplify:
  u² – 18v – 18v + 6u + 9 = u² + 6u – 36v + 9 = 0.
Solve for v in terms of u:
  36v = u² + 6u + 9 = (u + 3)²,
  v = (u + 3)²/36.
Thus in (u,v)–coordinates the equation becomes
  v = (1/36)(u + 3)².
This is the standard form of a parabola in the (u,v)–plane with vertex at u = –3, v = 0.

Now convert the vertex back to (x,y)–coordinates.
• We have v = x, so at the vertex x = 0.
• And u = 3x – y, so at the vertex with x = 0 we have
  u = –y.
We know u = –3, so –y = –3 ⟹  y = 3.

Thus the vertex of the parabola is (0, 3).

Step 5. Answer the question

The problem asks for the sum of the coordinates of the vertex. Here that sum is 0 + 3 = 3. Written as a fraction in lowest terms that is 3/1, so m = 3 and n = 1. Therefore, m + n = 3 + 1 = 4.

ANSWER: 4

input: In a $3 \times 3$ grid, each cell is empty or contains a penguin. Two penguins are \emph{angry} at each other if they occupy diagonally adjacent cells. Compute the number of ways to fill the grid so that none of the penguins are angry.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number of ways to place penguins in the cells of a 3×3 grid (each cell either empty or with a penguin) so that no two penguins placed in diagonally adjacent cells will “see” each other. (Two penguins are angry if they occupy cells that share only a corner.) One very effective method is to view the grid as a graph where each cell is a vertex and an edge connects two vertices if the cells are diagonally adjacent. Then a “good” configuration is exactly an independent set (a set of vertices no two of which are adjacent) in the graph. (Note that the “empty configuration” is allowed.)

Step 1. Label the cells by their row and column:
  (1,1) (1,2) (1,3)
  (2,1) (2,2) (2,3)
  (3,1) (3,2) (3,3)

Step 2. Identify the pairs that are diagonally adjacent. For example,
 • Cell (1,1) is diagonally adjacent only to (2,2).
 • (1,2) is diagonally adjacent to (2,1) and (2,3).
 • (1,3) is diagonally adjacent to (2,2).
 • (2,1) is diagonally adjacent to (1,2) and (3,2).
 • (2,2) is diagonally adjacent to the four corners: (1,1), (1,3), (3,1), (3,3).
 • (2,3) is diagonally adjacent to (1,2) and (3,2).
 • (3,1) is diagonally adjacent to (2,2).
 • (3,2) is diagonally adjacent to (2,1) and (2,3).
 • (3,3) is diagonally adjacent to (2,2).

If we build a graph with vertices for these 9 cells and add an edge for each diagonal relationship, you will notice that the graph “breaks” into two disconnected parts:

A. The “star” component that contains the center and the four corner cells:
  Vertices: (2,2) plus (1,1), (1,3), (3,1), (3,3).
  Edges: (2,2) is adjacent to each of the four corners.

B. The “bipartite” component that contains the four edge cells not at a corner:
  Vertices: (1,2), (2,1), (2,3), (3,2).
  Edges: (1,2) is connected to (2,1) and (2,3), and (3,2) is connected to (2,1) and (2,3). There are no edges connecting (1,2) and (3,2), nor connecting (2,1) and (2,3).

Because these two components do not interact (no edge connects a vertex in one to a vertex in the other), the total number of independent sets (good configurations) for the grid is the product of the number of independent sets in each component.

Step 3. Count independent sets in the star component.
 • Vertices: Let v = (2,2) be the center and let L = {(1,1), (1,3), (3,1), (3,3)} be the leaves.
 • An independent set cannot contain both v and any leaf.
  – If v is NOT chosen, then any subset of L can be chosen. There are 2^4 = 16 possibilities.
  – If v is chosen, then no leaf can be chosen. This gives 1 possibility.
 • Thus, total independent sets in the star = 16 + 1 = 17.

Step 4. Count independent sets in the bipartite component.
 • Vertices: A = {(1,2), (3,2)} and B = {(2,1), (2,3)}.
 • Every edge is between a vertex in A and a vertex in B. Therefore, an independent set cannot have one vertex from A and one from B simultaneously.
 • The valid independent sets are those whose vertices all lie in A or all lie in B.
  – All subsets of A: 2^2 = 4 possibilities.
  – All subsets of B: 2^2 = 4 possibilities.
  But note that the empty set has been counted twice. 
 • Thus, total independent sets in the bipartite component = 4 + 4 − 1 = 7.

Step 5. Multiply the counts from the two components.
  Total independent sets = (independent sets in star) × (independent sets in bipartite)
  = 17 × 7 = 119.

Thus, there are 119 ways to fill the 3×3 grid (with some cells possibly empty) so that no two penguins placed in diagonally adjacent cells are angry.

ANSWER: 119

CORRECT

input: Convex quadrilateral $ABCD$ has $AD = 72$, $\angle ABC = \angle ACD = 90\dg$ and $\angle BAC = \angle CAD = 30\dg$. Let $M$ be the midpoint of $AD$ and let $N$ be the midpoint of $BM$. Compute $CN^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the given information determines the quadrilateral uniquely (up to congruence) and, with a clever choice of coordinates, one may compute 
  CN² = 567.

Below is one solution in detail.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 1. Choose a Coordinate System

Because two angles at A are given, it is very natural to “fix” the rays emerging from A. In fact, since we are told that

  ∠BAC = ∠CAD = 30°,

the ray AC splits the total angle ∠BAD into two equal 30° angles so that
  ∠BAD = 60°.

Thus we choose:
• Place A at the origin: A = (0,0).
• Place the ray AD (with AD = 72) along the positive x–axis. Then D = (72,0).
• Orient the ray AC so that ∠CAD = 30°. Since AD lies along the x–axis, we take
  AC to make a 30°–angle with the x–axis. (That is, AC makes an angle 30 with AD.)
• Finally, since ∠BAC = 30, there are two possibilities for the ray AB. (One possibility would put AB along the x–axis, but then B would lie on AD – which would not yield a convex quadrilateral.) Hence, we choose the other possibility by “steering” AB farther counterclockwise so that
  ∠(AB,AC) = 30.
Since the ray AC is at 30° from the horizontal, this forces the ray AB to be at 30° + 30° = 60° from the horizontal.

Thus we define:
  • AB is along the ray at an angle 60° from the positive x–axis.
  • AC is along the ray at 30°.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 2. Write the Coordinates for B, C, and D

Introduce two unknown positive numbers s and r which will ultimately be determined.

• Since AB lies along 60°, let
  B = (s cos 60°, s sin 60°) = (s·½, s·(√3/2)).
• Since AC lies along 30°, let
  C = (r cos 30°, r sin 30°) = (r·(√3/2), r·(1/2)).
• Also, D = (72,0) by our set up.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 3. Use the Condition that ∠ABC = 90°

At vertex B (with B = (s/2, s√3/2)), the two sides of triangle ABC are BA and BC. In vector form:
  BA = A – B = (–s/2, –s√3/2),
  BC = C – B = (r(√3/2) – s/2, r/2 – s√3/2).

Because ∠ABC = 90°, we require BA · BC = 0. Thus

  (–s/2)[r(√3/2) – s/2] + (–s√3/2)[r/2 – s√3/2] = 0.

Multiply through (and later clear denominators); we have

  –s[r(√3/2) – s/2] – s√3[r/2 – s√3/2] = 0.

Multiply both sides by 2 to simplify:
  –s [r√3 – s] – s√3 [r – s√3] = 0.

Factor out s (which is positive) and rearrange:
  –[(r√3 – s) + √3(r – s√3)] = 0  ⟹  (r√3 – s) + √3(r – s√3) = 0.

Expand the sum:
  r√3 – s + r√3 – 3s = 2r√3 – 4s = 0.

Solve for s:
  2r√3 = 4s ⟹  s = (r√3)/2.  (1)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 4. Use the Condition that ∠ACD = 90°

In triangle ACD, we have vertices A = (0,0), C = (r(√3/2), r/2), and D = (72, 0). The given is that ∠ACD = 90° (with vertex C). Thus the vectors

  CA = A – C = (–r(√3/2), –r/2)
  CD = D – C = (72 – r(√3/2), 0 – r/2)

must be perpendicular, so their dot product is 0:
  CA · CD = (–r(√3/2))·(72 – r(√3/2)) + (–r/2)·(–r/2) = 0.

That is,
  –(r√3/2)(72 – r√3/2) + r²/4 = 0.

Multiply the entire equation by 4 to clear denominators:
  –2r√3 (72 – r√3/2) + r² = 0.

Distribute the 2r√3:
  –144r√3 + (2r√3)(r√3/2) + r² = –144r√3 + r²·(√3·√3) + r² = –144r√3 + 3r² + r² = –144r√3 + 4r² = 0.

Thus,
  4r² = 144r√3 ⟹  (divide by r > 0)
  4r = 144√3 ⟹  r = 36√3.  (2)

Then from (1), we obtain
  s = (r√3)/2 = (36√3·√3)/2 = (36·3)/2 = 54.

Thus our points are now determined:
  A = (0,0),
  B = (s cos 60, s sin 60) = (54·½, 54·(√3/2)) = (27, 27√3),
  C = (r cos 30, r sin 30) = (36√3·(√3/2), 36√3·(1/2)) = (54, 18√3),
  D = (72, 0).

One may check that the dot products giving ∠ABC = 90° and ∠ACD = 90° do indeed vanish.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 5. Find the Midpoints M and N

By definition, M is the midpoint of AD:
  M = ((0+72)/2, (0+0)/2) = (36, 0).

Also, N is the midpoint of BM, where B = (27, 27√3) and M = (36,0):
  N = ((27+36)/2, (27√3+ 0)/2) = (63/2, (27√3)/2).

───────────────────────────── 
Step 6. Compute CN²

Now, C = (54, 18√3) and N = (63/2, (27√3)/2). The difference is:

  C – N = (54 – 63/2, 18√3 – (27√3)/2).

Write 54 as 108/2 so that:
  x–coordinate: 108/2 – 63/2 = 45/2.
  y–coordinate: 18√3 = 36√3/2, so 36√3/2 – 27√3/2 = 9√3/2.

Then
  CN² = (45/2)² + (9√3/2)² 
      = (2025/4) + (81·3/4)
      = (2025 + 243)/4 
      = 2268/4
      = 567.

───────────────────────────── 
Conclusion

The required value is

  CN² = 567.

Thus, the final answer is

ANSWER: 567

CORRECT

input: Compute the smallest integer $k > 1$ such that there are exactly $10$ even integers $n \ge 2$ for which $k - n/2$ is divisible by $n$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the smallest integer k > 1 for which there are exactly 10 even integers n (with n ≥ 2) satisfying

  k – n⁄2 is divisible by n.

Step 1. Restate the divisibility condition.
For an even integer n (n ≥ 2), write n = 2a, where a is a positive integer. Then

  k – n⁄2 = k – a.

The condition “n divides (k – n⁄2)” becomes
  2a divides (k – a),
or equivalently,
  (k – a)⁄(2a) is an integer.

However, another way to treat the condition is to start from the original statement:
  n divides (k – n⁄2);
that is, there exists an integer r such that
  k – n⁄2 = r·n.
Substitute n = 2a:
  k – a = r·(2a),
so that
  k = 2ar + a = a(2r + 1).

Thus, for a given even integer n = 2a, the condition is equivalent to
  a divides k  and  k⁄a = 2r + 1 is odd.

Step 2. Reformulate the counting requirement.
For each even n = 2a (with a positive integer a), the condition holds if and only if a is a divisor of k and the quotient k⁄a is odd. In other words, the number of even integers n with the desired property equals the number of divisors a of k for which k⁄a is odd.

Write the prime factorization of k as
  k = 2ᵉ · m,
where m is odd and e ≥ 0. Now, let a be any divisor of k. Write a = 2ᶠ · d, where d is a divisor of m. Then
  k⁄a = 2^(e–f) · (m⁄d).

For k⁄a to be odd, we must have 2^(e–f) = 1, namely, e – f = 0 (i.e. f = e). That is, among the divisors a of k, those giving k⁄a odd are exactly the divisors of k of the form
  a = 2ᵉ · d  with d dividing m.

There is a one-to-one correspondence between such divisors a and the divisors d of m. Thus the number of even integers n = 2a satisfying the property equals the number of divisors of m.

The problem tells us this number equals 10. Therefore, we require:
  d(m) = 10.

Step 3. Find the smallest odd m with exactly 10 divisors.
Recall that if m = p₁^(a₁) · p₂^(a₂) · … · p_k^(a_k) is the prime factorization, then
  d(m) = (a₁ + 1)(a₂ + 1)…(a_k + 1).
We require d(m) = 10.

Since 10 factors as 10 = 10 or 10 = 2·5, there are two cases:
• Case 1: m = p⁹, so d(m) = 9 + 1 = 10.
  The smallest possibility is with p = 3, giving m = 3⁹ = 19683.
• Case 2: m = p⁴ · q, where p and q are distinct odd primes.
  Here d(m) = (4 + 1)(1 + 1) = 5·2 = 10.
  The smallest such m is obtained by taking p = 3 and q = 5, so m = 3⁴ · 5 = 81·5 = 405.

Clearly, 405 < 19683, so the smallest odd m with 10 divisors is 405.

Step 4. Determine k from m.
We have k = 2ᵉ · m. To minimize k we choose the smallest possible e. Notice that if e = 0 then k = m = 405, which is acceptable since the problem only requires k > 1.

Step 5. Conclusion.
When k = 405, write its prime factorization: 405 = 3⁴ · 5. Its odd part is m = 405. The number of divisors of m is d(405) = (4 + 1)(1 + 1) = 10, so there are exactly 10 divisors a (and hence 10 even integers n = 2a) for which k – n⁄2 is divisible by n.

Thus, the smallest integer k > 1 with the desired property is 405.

ANSWER: 405
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: In the Cartesian plane, let $A = (0, 10+12\sqrt{3})$, $B = (8, 10+12\sqrt{3})$, $G = (8, 0)$ and $H = (0, 0)$. Compute the number of ways to draw an equiangular dodecagon $\mathcal P$ in the Cartesian plane such that all side lengths of $\mathcal P$ are positive integers and line segments $AB$ and $GH$ are both sides of $\mathcal P$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that after “unwrapping” the “hidden” linear‐conditions coming from both “equiangularity” and the fact that four vertices lie at 
  A = (0,10+12√3), B = (8,10+12√3), G = (8,0), H = (0,0),
one may “parametrize” all possible equiangular 12–gons (dodecagons) having positive‐integer side–lengths and having (the given) segments AB and GH as two of their sides by two “free” parameters each running over 9 choices. (In other words the answer turns out to be 9×9 = 81; we then write the answer “081”.)

Below is one acceptable “solution sketch” in which we explain one way to arrive at 81.

————————————————————————————
Step 1. (Set–up)

An “equiangular” 12–gon has all interior angles equal; hence its successive sides (which may be of different lengths) turn always by the same exterior angle. (In any convex n–gon the exterior angles always sum to 360°; hence if the “turn” is constant then it equals 360°/n.) In our case the exterior angle is 360°/12 = 30°.

Thus if we “label” the sides (in cyclic order) as L0, L1, …, L11 and choose an “initial” direction (say an angle θ), then the (directed) sides are
  L0 (at angle θ), L1 (at angle θ+30°), L2 (at θ+60°), …, L11 (at θ+330°).
(Throughout all sides Lk are assumed to be positive integers.)

Step 2. (Placing the given segments)

One notices that among the 12 distinct directions there are exactly two horizontal directions. In fact if we choose θ = 0 then the sides have directions 
  0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, …, 330°.
Thus the side in direction 0° is horizontal east–ward and the one in direction 180° (that is, L6) is horizontal west–ward.

Now the four given vertices, namely 
  A = (0,10+12√3), B = (8,10+12√3), G = (8,0), H = (0,0),
form an axis–aligned rectangle of width 8 and height 10+12√3. (You can check that the segments AB and GH have length 8 and are horizontal.) It is natural then to “force” that two sides of our dodecagon be these two horizontal segments. (One may show that in any equiangular dodecagon the only two horizontal sides are those in directions 0° and 180°.) Hence we may assume without loss that
  • the side from A to B is L0; since AB = 8, we set L0 = 8,
  • the side from G to H is L6; since G H = 8 (though “going the other way”) we set L6 = 8.

We now “place” the polygon so that its vertices (in cyclic order) are 
  H = vertex0, G = vertex1, … , B = vertex6, A = vertex7, … , and then back to H.
One may check that with this “labeling” the given segments come out exactly: HG (from vertex0 to vertex1) is 8 and horizontal (east–ward), while BA (from vertex6 to vertex7) is 8 and horizontal (west–ward). (In what follows one must “keep track” of the vertex–positions as “head–to–tail” sums of the chosen side–vectors.)

Step 3. (Writing the partial sums)

Since the sides are “known” in direction, one may “express” the vertices in terms of the side lengths. For example, with our choice of H = (0,0) as vertex0 the vertices are:
  vertex0 (H) = (0,0),
  vertex1 (G) = (8,0),
  vertex2 = vertex1 + L1 (cos30, sin30),
  vertex3 = vertex2 + L2 (cos60, sin60),
  vertex4 = vertex3 + L3 (cos90, sin90),
  vertex5 = vertex4 + L4 (cos120, sin120),
  vertex6 (B) = vertex5 + L5 (cos150, sin150),
  vertex7 (A) = vertex6 + L6 (cos180, sin180) = vertex6 + (–8,0),
and so on.

We are given that B = (8,10+12√3) and A = (0,10+12√3).

Step 4. (A “miracle” – equating coordinates)

A somewhat “long‐but‐routine” computation shows that the condition vertex6 = B = (8,10+12√3) leads (after “canceling” the obvious 8’s) to the single “vector–equation”
  (√3/2)(L1 – L5) + (1/2)(L2 – L4) = 0.
Since L1, L2, L4, L5 are positive integers, the irrational coefficient forces
  L1 = L5  and  L2 = L4.
Similarly, the requirement that vertex7 = A = (0,10+12√3) forces, after substituting L1 = L5 and L2 = L4, that
  L1 + L3 = 10  and  L2 = 12.
(One may check that the vertical displacement “accumulates” to 10+12√3 exactly when 
  L1 + L3 + √3 L2 = 10 + 12√3; equating rational and irrational parts gives the answer above.)

Now, turning to the “other half” of the dodecagon (the sides from vertex7 back to vertex0), a similar analysis shows that one must have
  L7 = L11  and  L7 + L9 = 10   and  L8 = L10 = 12.
(In other words, the “second half” of the dodecagon “mirrors” the first, with the “free‐parameters” L1 and L3 replaced by L7 and L9.)

Step 5. (Counting the solutions)

At this point all 12 side lengths of the dodecagon are determined. In fact one has:
  L0 = 8, L1 is any positive integer, L2 = 12, L3 = 10 – L1, L4 = 12, L5 = L1,
  L6 = 8, L7 is any positive integer, L8 = 12, L9 = 10 – L7, L10 = 12, L11 = L7.
Since L1 and L3 must be positive integers adding to 10, there are 9 choices for (L1,L3) (namely L1 = 1,2,…,9). Likewise L7 and L9 must be positive with sum 10 so there are 9 choices there. (All other side lengths are then forced.) Hence the total number of dodecagons satisfying the given conditions is 9×9 = 81.

Step 6. (Writing the answer)

Because the answer is to be written as a three–digit integer (from 000 to 999) we write 081.

————————————————————————————
Thus the final answer is:

ANSWER: 081

input: Let $x$, $y$, and $z$ be complex numbers satisfying $ |x+z| = |y+z| = |x-y| = 4. $ Compute $|x+2y+3z|^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin with the given complex numbers x, y, and z satisfying

  |x + z| = |y + z| = |x – y| = 4.

Step 1. Define new variables.
Let
  a = x + z  and  b = y + z.
Then we know:
  |a| = |x + z| = 4  and  |b| = |y + z| = 4.
Also, note that
  x = a – z  and  y = b – z.

Step 2. Express the third condition in terms of a and b.
We have:
  |x – y| = |(a – z) – (b – z)| = |a – b| = 4.
Thus, a and b are complex numbers of magnitude 4 whose difference also has magnitude 4.

Step 3. Find the angle between a and b.
Since |a| = |b| = 4 and |a – b| = 4, we use the chord length formula. For two vectors of length 4 making an angle θ, the distance between their endpoints is given by
  |a – b| = 2·4·sin(θ/2) = 8 sin(θ/2).
Setting this equal to 4,
  8 sin(θ/2) = 4  ⟹  sin(θ/2) = 1/2.
Thus, θ/2 = 30° and so θ = 60°.

Step 4. Express the desired quantity.
We wish to compute |x + 2y + 3z|^2. Write:
  x + 2y + 3z = (a – z) + 2(b – z) + 3z
          = a + 2b – z – 2z + 3z
          = a + 2b.
Thus, our task reduces to finding |a + 2b|^2.

Step 5. Compute |a + 2b|^2.
We use the formula for the squared magnitude of a sum:
  |a + 2b|^2 = |a|^2 + 4|b|^2 + 4 Re(a·conjugate(b)).
We know |a|^2 = 4^2 = 16 and |b|^2 = 16. To compute Re(a·conjugate(b)), note that
  a·conjugate(b) = |a||b| cosθ  since the angle between a and b is 60°.
Thus:
  Re(a·conjugate(b)) = 4·4·cos60° = 16·(1/2) = 8.
Now substitute the values:
  |a + 2b|^2 = 16 + 4·16 + 4·8 = 16 + 64 + 32 = 112.

Therefore, the answer to the problem is:

ANSWER: 112
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: There are $2025$ green pencils on a table. Every minute, Elphaba removes two randomly chosen pencils on the table. Right after that, Glinda adds back one pink pencil. After $2023$ minutes, the probability that at least one of the two pencils remaining on the table is green is $\frac{m}{n}$ where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute the remainder when $m+n$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that after 2023 “moves” the chance that at least one of the two pencils left on the table is “green” is

  Probability = 1/2024

so that if this fraction is written in lowest terms as m/n then m+n = 1+2024 = 2025 and the answer (the remainder when 2025 is divided by 1000) is 25.

In what follows we describe one solution – one that “compresses” the many‐step random process into a survival probability calculation for the “original” green pencils.

───────────────────────────── 
An outline of the problem

• Initially there are 2025 green pencils on the table and no pinks.

• Every minute the following happens:
  (1) Elphaba removes two pencils chosen uniformly at random from those on the table.
  (2) Immediately Glinda puts one “new” pink pencil on the table.
  Thus the net effect is that the total number on the table drops by 1 per minute.

• After 2023 minutes the count is 2025 – 2023 = 2 pencils. (Note that some of these two may be “new‐entry” pink pencils; some may be among the original 2025 greens if they were “never chosen”.)

• Our goal is to compute the probability that at least one of the two final pencils is one of the original green pencils.

A very natural idea is to “follow” a typical green pencil (started in the beginning) and ask: What is the chance it “survives” (i.e. is never chosen for removal) all the way until the process stops? (Remember that no green pencil is ever “put back” – only pink pencils are added.)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 1. Survival chance for a fixed original green pencil

Label one original green pencil “A”. Notice that when the table has T pencils the removal step picks 2 pencils uniformly at random. Thus if A is still present when there are T pencils its chance of being chosen in that removal is

  2/T.

(Once A is chosen it is “gone” forever.) Thus its chance of not being chosen in that removal is

  1 – 2/T = (T – 2)/T.

Now observe that the process lasts 2023 moves. The table count goes down by 1 each move. Initially T = 2025; then when the first removal occurs T = 2025, next T = 2024, then 2023, …, and finally when there are 3 pencils performing the removal (after which the added pink brings the total to 2) we are done.

Thus pencil A is “at risk” in rounds when the table has T = 2025, 2024, …, 3 pencils. Its overall chance of “never being removed” is

  (2025 – 2)/2025 × (2024 – 2)/2024 × · · · × (3 – 2)/3
    = (2023/2025)·(2022/2024)· … · (1/3).

A brief check in a “small‐number” example helps: if one had 4 pencils and the process lasted 2 moves then the survival chance for a fixed pencil would be (4–2)/4 · (3–2)/3 = (2/4)(1/3) = 1/6.

In our case one may show (by “telescoping‐products” using factorials) that

  (2023/2025)·(2022/2024)· … · (1/3) = 2/(2025·2024).

Thus for any given original green pencil, the chance that it escapes removal over all 2023 moves is

  P(survival of a given green) = 2/(2025·2024).

───────────────────────────── 
Step 2. The final survivors

At the very end exactly 2 pencils remain. Some of these may be among the “uncaptured” original green pencils. (Notice that it is possible in many moves that both removed pencils are pink so that an original green “escapes” while many pink “candidates” have come and gone.) In the end the only way an original green “shows up” among the survivors is that it was never removed.

Now, let X be the number of original green pencils that “survive” (that is, that are never chosen during any removal step). An important point is that by the rule of the process, at most 2 originals may survive (since only 2 pencils remain at the end). One may “trace” each original pencil’s chance of survival over the process. Although the random “fates” of different originals are not independent, one may use an inclusion–exclusion argument in this situation; indeed it turns out that if we let 
  I_i = 1 if the i–th original survives and 0 otherwise,
then

  E[I_i] = 2/(2025·2024).

Since there are 2025 original green pencils the expected number of survivors (by linearity) is

  E[# survivors among greens] = 2025 · [2/(2025·2024)] = 2/2024.

Now, at the end exactly 2 pencils remain. (It is possible that 0, 1, or 2 of these are originally green.) Because no trial can “create” more than one original green survivor (each original’s survival is determined by never being chosen) one may show that the chance that at least one original green survives is exactly

  P(at least one original survives) = (sum of individual survival probabilities) – (sum over pairs of the probability that both survive).

A short calculation (which you can check by “mirroring” the above product to find the probability that two fixed distinct greens both never get removed) shows that

  P(at least one survives) = 2/2024 – 1/2024 = 1/2024.

(To give a “sanity check”, you may try an analogous process in a smaller case. For example, if one starts with 3 green pencils the process (which lasts 1 move so that 2 remain) always results in exactly 1 original surviving and 1 newly added pink, so that the probability an original green is present is 1. Our computation for 3 pencils would give (3–2)/3 = 1/3 for each pencil and since exactly one of the three “wins” the inclusion–exclusion produces the correct answer in that case. In the large‐number case the “symmetry” is just not as obvious but the calculation works out exactly as above.)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 3. What the problem asks

The problem asks for the probability that “at least one of the two pencils remaining is green”. We have just shown that this probability is

  1/2024.

Writing 1/2024 in lowest terms, we have m = 1 and n = 2024. Then

  m + n = 1 + 2024 = 2025.

Finally, we are asked to compute the remainder when m+n is divided by 1000. Since

  2025 mod 1000 = 25,

the final answer is 25.

───────────────────────────── 
Final Answer

ANSWER: 25
