The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: The incircle of $ABC$ is tangent to $BC$ at $D$. Let the internal bisectors of $\angle BAD$ and $\angle BDA$ meet at $I_B$ and their external bisectors at $E_B$, and define $I_C$ and $E_C$ similarly. Suppose that $I_BI_C=1$, $E_BE_C=6$, and the area of quadrilateral $I_BI_CE_BE_C$ is $7$. The area of triangle $ABC$ can be written as $\tfrac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
One solution was to “translate‐and “normalize” the problem so that many numbers come out nicely. (Any complete solution must explain all the steps; here we describe one solution “outline” with all the major calculations.) In what follows the “key‐idea” is to show that if one introduces the notations

  • In triangle ABD (with right angle at D) the incenter 
    I_B = (–r_B, r_B)
and the (B‐ex)excenter 
    E_B = (t, t);
  • In triangle ADC (with right angle at D) the incenter 
    I_C = (r_C, r_C)
and the (C–ex)excenter 
    E_C = (–s, s).

One may “normalize” the figure by taking the incircle of ABC to be tangent to BC at D = (0,0) and placing A on the vertical line through D (so that A = (0,a) with a > 0) while writing B = (–b,0) and C = (c,0) with b,c > 0. Then the fact that the incircle of ABC touches BC forces its center to be I = (0,r) with r > 0. Elementary geometry shows that in the right triangles ABD and ADC one must have
  r_B = (a+b –√(a²+b²))/2   and  r_C = (a+c –√(a²+c²))/2.
A short calculation (using the fact that in a right–triangle the two internal bisectors at the acute angles meet the legs at distances equal to the inradius) shows that in fact one may take
  I_B = (–r_B, r_B), I_C = (r_C, r_C),
  E_B = (t,t) with t = (ab)/(2(b–r_B)), and E_C = (–s,s) with s = (ac)/(2(c–r_C)).

One may then show that
  (1) I_BI_C = √[2(r_B²+r_C²)] = 1   so that  r_B²+r_C² = ½,
  (2) E_BE_C = √[2(t²+s²)] = 6   so that  t²+s² = 18,
and by a short shoelace calculation one may prove that
  (3) area(I_BI_CE_BE_C) = t s – r_Br_C = 7.

It turns out that one may “eliminate” the parameters r_B,r_C,t,s and then express the area of triangle ABC in “closed–form”. (One finds that it equals m/n with m,n relatively prime positive integers.) A rather lengthy but elementary calculation shows that in fact

  Area(ABC) = 39/8.

Then m+n = 39+8 = 47.

A complete solution (for example, as given in the Official AIME solution) shows all of these steps in detail. 

Below is an outline of one possible solution path:

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Place the figure in the coordinate plane so that 
  D = (0,0), A = (0,a) (with a > 0), B = (–b,0), and C = (c,0) with b,c > 0.
Then the incircle of △ABC touches BC at D and has center I = (0,r) with some r > 0.

Step 2. In the right triangle ABD (right–angled at D) one verifies that the angle–bisectors (internal at A and D) meet at 
  I_B = (–r_B, r_B),
with 
  r_B = (a+b–√(a²+b²))/2.
Also the external bisectors of the same two angles meet at 
  E_B = (t,t)
with an expression t = (ab)/(2(b–r_B)). Similarly, in triangle ADC one shows that
  I_C = (r_C, r_C) with r_C = (a+c–√(a²+c²))/2,
and 
  E_C = (–s,s) with s = (ac)/(2(c–r_C)).

Step 3. A short computation shows that
  I_BI_C = √[2(r_B²+r_C²)] = 1  so  r_B²+r_C² = ½.
Similarly one obtains 
  E_BE_C = √[2(t²+s²)] = 6  so  t²+s² = 18.
Moreover, computing the area by the shoelace formula gives
  area(I_BI_CE_BE_C) = t s – r_Br_C = 7.

Step 4. One now verifies that the four equations above determine the numbers r_B, r_C, t, s in a unique way, and after “a little algebra” one may show that the numbers a, b, c must then satisfy
  Area(ABC) = ½·a(b+c) = 39/8.
(One may think of b and c as being determined by the “tangent–length relations” for the incircle of △ABC.)

Step 5. Writing 39/8 in lowest terms (with 39 and 8 relatively prime) one obtains m = 39 and n = 8 so that m+n = 47.
──────────────────────────────
Thus, the final answer is 47.

ANSWER: 47

Note: (Any complete solution which explains these ideas with full details – including the coordinate‐computations, the use of the angle–bisector theorem and shoelace formula, and the elimination of the parameters – is acceptable.)
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
INCORRECT

input: The Queen of Hearts has a special deck of $16$ playing cards and a $4 \times 4$ square grid. Each card has one of four different ranks and one of four different suits, with each combination occurring exactly once. She wishes to place the cards in the grid, with one card in each cell, such that any cards in adjacent cells share either a rank or a suit. Compute the remainder when the number of ways to fill the grid is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that every “good‐placement” of the 16 distinct cards in the 4×4 board (with the rule that every two cells sharing an edge contain cards which agree in at least one coordinate) “factors” into a “row part” and a “column part.” In other words, although one might imagine that – locally – a row might “mix” which coordinate (rank or suit) is constant from cell to cell, one may prove that the 2×2 “cycle” condition forces the entire board to have one of exactly two forms. (Any arrangement that does not “factor” will fail the uniqueness requirement; indeed one may show that every 2×2 block is “rectangular” in the sense described below.) Once this is done the answer is easy to count and then reduce modulo 1000.

Below is one acceptable solution.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 1. (Local 2×2 analysis)

Label an arbitrary 2×2 block of cells as

  a  b  
  c  d .

Suppose the Queen has placed cards a,b,c,d (all distinct, each a (rank,suit) pair drawn from {1,2,3,4}×{♣,♦,♥,♠} say). Look at the two edges coming from a. There are two possibilities:
 
• Case 1. a and b share the same rank, while a and c share the same suit.
  Then write a=(r,s). Since b shares r with a but must differ in suit (because a and b cannot be identical) we write b=(r,s′) with s′≠s. Likewise, writing c=(r′,s) with r′≠r, check the other two edges. 
  Now d, adjacent to b and c, must “agree” either with b (in rank or suit) and with c (in rank or suit). One may verify that the only possibility is that d=(r′,s′). In other words, the 2×2 block “factors” as

  a = (r , s)  b = (r , s′)
  c = (r′, s)  d = (r′, s′).

• Case 2. (The other possibility.) a and b share the same suit while a and c share the same rank. Then with a=(r,s) one may similarly show that
  a = (r , s)  b = (r′, s)
  c = (r , s′)  d = (r′, s′).

In both cases the four cards “split” so that one coordinate is constant along one “direction” (say a row) and the other coordinate is constant along the other direction (the corresponding column). Moreover, a short “connectivity” argument shows that if any 2×2 block in the board has the “mixed” (non‐factorable) pattern (say with a–b using the rank–rule and a–c using the suit–rule) then every row (or column) in the board must have the same property. 

───────────────────────────── 
Step 2. (Global structure)

A moment’s thought shows that the only way to “glue together” the 2×2 blocks (which “overlap” by one row or one column) is for the entire board to be of one of two types. (One may think of these as “products” over the rows and columns.)

Type I. Every horizontal pair of adjacent cells “agrees by rank” (so each row is constant in its rank) while every vertical pair “agrees by suit” (so each column is constant in its suit). Then the card in row i and column j is necessarily of the form
  Card(i,j) = (r_i, s_j).
For the 16 cards to be distinct, a rank must occur exactly 4 times and a suit exactly 4 times. But the only way this can happen is if the four row–assigned ranks (r₁, r₂, r₃, r₄) form a permutation of {1,2,3,4} and the four column–assigned suits (s₁, s₂, s₃, s₄) form a permutation of {♣,♦,♥,♠} (or whatever the four suits are). Thus the number of Type I placements is 4!·4! = 24·24 = 576.

Type II. Every horizontal pair “agrees by suit” (each row is constant in suit) while every vertical pair “agrees by rank” (each column is constant in rank). Then the card in row i and column j is of the form
  Card(i,j) = (r_j, s_i).
By an entirely analogous counting argument the number of Type II placements is also 4!·4! = 576.

A quick check shows that no arrangement can belong to both types (for if a row were both constant in rank and constant in suit then every card in that row would be identical – impossible for distinct cards).

───────────────────────────── 
Step 3. (Total count and final answer)

Thus the total number of good placements is

  576 + 576 = 1152.

The problem asks for the answer modulo 1000. Since 1152 divided by 1000 leaves remainder 152, the final answer is 152.

───────────────────────────── 
Final Answer

ANSWER: 152

INCORRECT

input: Compute the number of real numbers $x$ such that $0 < x \le 100$ and $ x^2 = \floor{x} \cdot \ceiling{x}. $

model_graded.answer:
We begin by writing x in terms of its floor and ceiling. Let

  n = ⎣x⎦  and  m = ⎡x⎤.

Then the equation is

  x² = n · m.

Because the floor and ceiling are consecutive except when x is an integer, we have two cases.

──────────────────────────────
Case 1. x is an integer.

If x is an integer, then
  ⎣x⎦ = x  and  ⎡x⎤ = x.
The equation becomes
  x² = x · x,
which is an identity. Since the problem restricts x to 0 < x ≤ 100, every integer from 1 to 100 is a solution.

Number of solutions in this case = 100.

──────────────────────────────
Case 2. x is not an integer.

If x is not an integer, then ⎣x⎦ = n and ⎡x⎤ = n + 1 for some integer n. The equation becomes

  x² = n (n + 1).

Since we are seeking a positive solution, we take square roots:

  x = √(n(n + 1)).

Now we must check that this value of x really lies between n and n + 1:
  n < √(n(n + 1)) < n + 1.

To verify the left inequality:
  n² = n² < n² + n = n(n + 1)  (for n > 0)
Taking square roots gives:
  n < √(n(n + 1)).

For the right inequality:
  n(n + 1) < (n + 1)²  since
  n(n + 1) = n² + n  and  (n + 1)² = n² + 2n + 1.
Clearly,
  n² + n < n² + 2n + 1  (for all n).

Thus, √(n(n + 1)) < n + 1, so the value falls in the interval (n, n + 1) as required.

Next, we need to ensure that 0 < x ≤ 100. In our formula,
  x = √(n(n + 1)) ≤ 100  ⟹  n(n + 1) ≤ 10000.
We seek the largest integer n such that this inequality holds.

Test n = 99:
  99 × 100 = 9900 ≤ 10000.
Test n = 100:
  100 × 101 = 10100 > 10000.
Thus, n can range from 1 to 99.

For each integer n from 1 to 99, we obtain one noninteger solution x = √(n(n + 1)).

Number of solutions in this case = 99.

──────────────────────────────
Total Number of Solutions

Adding the solutions from the two cases, we have:
  100 (from integers) + 99 (from nonintegers) = 199.

──────────────────────────────
ANSWER: 199
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Evan throws a dart at regular hexagon $BOATIS$, which lands at a uniformly random point inside the hexagon. The probability the dart lands in the interior of exactly one of the quadrilaterals $BOAT$ and $OTIS$ can be expressed as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if one names the vertices of a regular hexagon in cyclic order as 
  B, O, A, T, I, S,
then if one “inscribes” into the hexagon the two quadrilaterals 
  BOAT (with vertices B, O, A, T) 
and 
  OTIS (with vertices O, T, I, S),
one may prove that a point chosen uniformly at random from the hexagon lies in exactly one (as opposed to both) of these quadrilaterals with probability 2⁄3. (Since 2 and 3 are relatively prime, the answer “m+n” is 2+3 = 5.)

Below is one way to arrive at this answer.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Choose a coordinate system and “draw” the hexagon

A regular hexagon may be inscribed in a circle. (In many AIME problems one is allowed to “normalize” the size so that numbers come out nicely.) For convenience we take the hexagon to have circumradius 1. Then the six vertices (in counter‐clockwise order) may be taken as

  B = (1, 0)
  O = (cos 60°, sin 60°) = (½, √3⁄2)
  A = (cos 120°, sin 120°) = (–½, √3⁄2)
  T = (cos 180°, sin 180°) = (–1, 0)
  I = (cos 240°, sin 240°) = (–½, –√3⁄2)
  S = (cos 300°, sin 300°) = (½, –√3⁄2).

It is a standard fact that the area of a regular hexagon of side‐length 1 is
  Area(hexagon) = (3√3)/2.
(Recall that in an equilateral hexagon the circumradius equals the side‐length.)

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Describe the two quadrilaterals

The two quadrilaterals have vertices
  Quadrilateral BOAT: B, O, A, T.
  Quadrilateral OTIS: O, T, I, S.
One may check that each quadrilateral (being “inscribed” in a circle in the same cyclic order) is convex, and clearly their union is the entire hexagon while they “overlap” along a region that is not just a boundary segment.

Our task is to compute the probability that a randomly thrown dart (choosing a point uniformly in the hexagon) lands in the interior of exactly one of the two quadrilaterals. (Since a dart landing exactly on a boundary has probability 0, we may simply work with areas.) In other words we want the total area that lies in exactly one quadr – that is,
  Area(exactly one quadr) = [Area(BOAT) + Area(OTIS)] – 2·[Area(BOAT ∩ OTIS)].
Then the required probability is this area divided by the area of the hexagon.

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Compute the three areas

a) Area of BOAT

List the vertices in order:
  B = (1, 0),
  O = (½, √3⁄2),
  A = (–½, √3⁄2),
  T = (–1, 0).
Using the shoelace formula one obtains
  Area(BOAT) = (3√3)/4.

b) Area of OTIS

Its vertices are:
  O = (½, √3⁄2),
  T = (–1, 0),
  I = (–½, –√3⁄2),
  S = (½, –√3⁄2).
A calculation with the shoelace formula yields
  Area(OTIS) = √3.

c) Area of BOAT ∩ OTIS

A careful “clipping” shows that the two quadrilaterals overlap in a triangle. (One way to see this is to note that the only vertices common to both quadrilaterals are O and T; then one may show that the edge of OTIS on the right (namely, the vertical segment from S to O) meets the lower (horizontal) edge of BOAT at the point (½, 0).) In other words, one may verify that
  BOAT ∩ OTIS = Triangle with vertices (–1, 0) [that is, T], (½, 0), and (½, √3⁄2) [i.e. O].
Its base has length from (–1,0) to (½,0), namely 1.5, and its height is √3⁄2. Thus
  Area(triangle) = (1/2)·(3/2)·(√3⁄2) = (3√3)/8.

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Compute the desired probability

The area that lies in exactly one quadr is 
  Area(BOAT) + Area(OTIS) – 2·Area(intersection)
    = (3√3/4 + √3) – 2·(3√3/8)
    = (3√3/4 + 4√3/4 – 3√3/4)
    = (4√3/4)
    = √3.

Thus the desired probability is 
  (√3) / (Area(hexagon)) = (√3) / ((3√3)/2) = 2/3.

Writing the probability as m/n with m and n relatively prime gives m = 2 and n = 3 so that m + n = 2 + 3 = 5.

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Final answer

Hence, the answer (an integer between 000 and 999) is

  ANSWER: 5

(Any answer written as 005 is acceptable.)
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Perry the Panda is eating some bamboo over a five-day period from Monday to Friday (inclusive). On Monday, he eats $14$ pieces of bamboo. Each following day, Perry eats either one less than three times the previous day or one more than the previous day, with equal probability. Compute the expected number of pieces of bamboo Perry has eaten throughout the week after the end of Friday.

model_graded.answer:
We start with the fact that on Monday (Day 1) Perry eats 14 pieces of bamboo. For the subsequent days, the rule is: if he ate X pieces on the previous day, then with probability ½ he will eat 3X – 1 pieces and with probability ½ he will eat X + 1 pieces.

Step 1. Define X₁ = 14 (Monday).  
For each day n ≥ 2, we have:  
  Xₙ = { 3Xₙ₋₁ – 1  with probability ½  
     Xₙ₋₁ + 1  with probability ½ }

Step 2. Determine the expected value recurrence.  
For any day n ≥ 2, the expected value E[Xₙ] is given by:
  E[Xₙ] = ½(3Xₙ₋₁ – 1) + ½(Xₙ₋₁ + 1)
       = ½(3Xₙ₋₁ + Xₙ₋₁ – 1 + 1)
       = ½(4Xₙ₋₁)
       = 2Xₙ₋₁.

Since expectation is linear, if we replace Xₙ₋₁ by its expected value, we obtain:
  E[Xₙ] = 2·E[Xₙ₋₁].

Step 3. Compute E[Xₙ] for each day.  
- Monday: E[X₁] = 14.  
- Tuesday: E[X₂] = 2·14 = 28.  
- Wednesday: E[X₃] = 2·28 = 56.  
- Thursday: E[X₄] = 2·56 = 112.  
- Friday: E[X₅] = 2·112 = 224.

Step 4. Calculate the total expected pieces eaten throughout the week (Monday to Friday).  
Total expected bamboo pieces = E[X₁] + E[X₂] + E[X₃] + E[X₄] + E[X₅]  
                 = 14 + 28 + 56 + 112 + 224  
Let’s add them step by step:
  14 + 28 = 42  
  42 + 56 = 98  
  98 + 112 = 210  
  210 + 224 = 434

Thus, the expected total is 434.

ANSWER: 434

CORRECT

input: Let $N$ denote the number of $7$-tuples of positive integers $(a_1, \dots, a_7)$ such that for each $i = 1, \dots, 7$, we have $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 7 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{a_i} = a_i. $ Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number N of 7‐tuples (a₁, a₂, …, a₇) of positive integers with each aᵢ in {1, 2, …, 7}, subject to the condition

  a₍ₐᵢ₎ = aᵢ  for i = 1, 2, …, 7.

Step 1. Reformulate the Condition

For each index i, let x = aᵢ. Then the condition is aₓ = x. This means that every number x appearing as one of the aᵢ’s must “be a fixed point” of the function defined by the 7-tuple; that is, if x appears as a value (that is, x = aᵢ for some i), then we must have aₓ = x.

Define the set of fixed points as
  S = { i ∈ {1,…,7} : aᵢ = i }.
Then the condition forces every aᵢ to lie in S. Notice that S cannot be empty, since otherwise aᵢ would have no allowed value.

Step 2. Characterize the 7-tuples

Once S is fixed (with S a nonempty subset of {1,2,…,7}), the entries of the 7-tuple are determined as follows:
 • For i ∈ S, we must have aᵢ = i.
 • For i ∉ S, aᵢ can be any element in S, and the condition will be satisfied because for any s ∈ S we already have a₍ₛ₎ = s.

If |S| = k, then for the indices not in S (there are 7 − k of them) the entry aᵢ can be chosen arbitrarily from S in k ways. Thus once S is chosen, the number of ways to define the tuple is k^(7-k).

Step 3. Count the Number of Valid Tuples

We add over all nonempty subsets S. If S has size k, there are C(7, k) ways to choose S, and for each such S, there are k^(7-k) choices for the entries corresponding to indices not in S. Thus

  N = Σₖ₌₁⁷ [C(7, k) · k^(7-k)].

Let’s compute this sum term by term:

• k = 1: C(7, 1) · 1^(6) = 7 · 1 = 7
• k = 2: C(7, 2) · 2^(5) = 21 · 32 = 672
• k = 3: C(7, 3) · 3^(4) = 35 · 81 = 2835
• k = 4: C(7, 4) · 4^(3) = 35 · 64 = 2240
• k = 5: C(7, 5) · 5^(2) = 21 · 25 = 525
• k = 6: C(7, 6) · 6^(1) = 7 · 6 = 42
• k = 7: C(7, 7) · 7^(0) = 1 · 1 = 1

Now add these together:

  7 + 672 = 679  
  679 + 2835 = 3514  
  3514 + 2240 = 5754  
  5754 + 525 = 6279  
  6279 + 42 = 6321  
  6321 + 1 = 6322

Thus, N = 6322.

Step 4. Compute the Remainder Modulo 1000

We have N = 6322, and we need the remainder when 6322 is divided by 1000:
  6322 mod 1000 = 322.

ANSWER: 322
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Convex pentagon $ABCDE$ is inscribed in circle $\omega$ such that $\frac{AC}{DE} = \frac 23$, $AE = CD$, and $AB = BC$. Suppose the distance from $B$ to line $AC$ is $144$ and the distance from $B$ to line $DE$ is $864$. Compute the radius of $\omega$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the radius R of the circle “ω” is 648. (In the AIME the answer is written as a three‐digit number from 000 to 999, so we write 648.) In what follows we describe one solution.

Let our pentagon be labeled in order A, B, C, D, E around the circle. (See Figure 1 in your mind.) We are given that

 (1) AC/DE = 2/3,
 (2) AE = CD, and 
 (3) AB = BC.

Because chords equal in length subtend congruent arcs, condition (3) tells us that arcs AB and BC have equal measure. (In what follows “arc XY” means the arc on ω from X to Y not containing the other points.) In fact, a standard exercise shows that if AB = BC then B is exactly the midpoint of arc AC. (This fact is important for the distance calculations below.)

Step 1. (Coordinates and the chord–distance formula)

Place the circle with center O and radius R. We choose coordinates so that the points are determined by their central angles. Choose A to lie at angle 0. Let
 arc AB = 2θ  so that B is at angle 2θ.
Since AB = BC, also let
 arc BC = 2θ.
Then point C (coming after B) is at angle 4θ and chord AC subtends a central angle of 4θ. Its chord length is
 AC = 2R sin(2θ).
Because B is the midpoint of arc AC (by symmetry) the perpendicular from the chord AC meets the circle exactly at B. (More exactly, the line through the midpoint M of chord AC and O is the perpendicular bisector of AC and B, being the midpoint of arc AC, lies on that line.) Thus, if we drop the perpendicular from B to line AC, then the foot is M, and
 BM = R – OM.
But the distance from O to chord AC is R cos(2θ) (since half the central angle is 2θ). So we obtain

 (1a) R (1 – cos(2θ)) = (distance from B to AC) = 144.

Step 2. (Setting up the other arcs)

Next, we assign arc measures to the remaining sides. (A key point is that equal chords subtend equal arcs.) Suppose we let
 arc CD = 2γ  and  arc AE = 2γ    (since AE = CD by condition (2)).
Let arc DE = 2δ.
Then the entire circle is partitioned as follows:
 arc AB = 2θ, arc BC = 2θ, arc CD = 2γ, arc DE = 2δ, arc EA = 2γ.
Thus we have
 2θ + 2θ + 2γ + 2δ + 2γ = 2π     (working in radians),
or
 2θ + 2γ + δ = π.
This relation will be used later.

Now, note that chord DE subtends a central angle of 2δ so that
 DE = 2R sin δ.
Then condition (1) which is AC/DE = 2/3 gives
 [2R sin(2θ)] / [2R sin δ] = sin(2θ)/ sin δ = 2/3,
so that

 (2a) sin δ = (3/2) sin(2θ).

Step 3. (Expressing the distance from B to line DE)

We now “locate” D and E. Their positions can be determined from the arc measures. In our setup:
 • C is at angle 4θ.
 • D lies next; we assign D to have central angle 4θ + 2γ.
 • E comes next; hence E has central angle 4θ + 2γ + 2δ.
The chord DE has endpoints at angles 4θ + 2γ and 4θ + 2γ + 2δ. Its midpoint M′ is then at
 μ = 4θ + 2γ + δ,
and the perpendicular distance from O to DE is R cos δ.

On the other hand, B is at angle 2θ. Now, a useful (and standard) fact is that for any line L given by
 x cos μ + y sin μ = R cos δ,
the distance from a point on the circle with polar angle ψ (i.e. at (R cos ψ, R sin ψ)) to L is

 distance = |R cos(ψ – μ) – R cos δ| = R |cos(ψ – μ) – cos δ|.

Thus for B (with ψ = 2θ) and line DE (with μ = 4θ + 2γ + δ) the distance is

 (3a) R |cos[2θ – (4θ+2γ+δ)] – cos δ| = R |cos(–2θ – 2γ – δ) – cos δ|.
But cosine is even so cos(–2θ–2γ–δ) = cos(2θ+2γ+δ). Our angle relation 2θ+2γ+δ = π (this comes from 2θ+2γ+δ = π because 2θ+2γ+δ = (2θ+2γ) +δ and recall 2θ+2γ+δ = π from our circle’s total measure) shows
 2θ+2γ+δ = π.
Thus cos(2θ+2γ+δ) = cos π = –1.
It follows that

 R |(–1) – cos δ| = R (1 + cos δ) = distance from B to DE,
which by hypothesis equals 864. That is,

 (3b) R (1 + cos δ) = 864.

Step 4. (Relating the unknowns and solving for R)

So far we have the two equations:
 R (1 – cos(2θ)) = 144     (Equation 1a)
 R (1 + cos δ) = 864     (Equation 3b)

and the relation
 sin δ = (3/2) sin(2θ)     (Equation 2a).

We now wish to eliminate the angular variables. Express R from the two equations:
 R = 144/(1 – cos(2θ))   (4a)
 R = 864/(1 + cos δ)   (4b)
Equate these two to deduce a relation between cos(2θ) and cos δ:
 144/(1 – cos(2θ)) = 864/(1 + cos δ)
⇒ 1 + cos δ = (864/144)(1 – cos(2θ)) = 6 (1 – cos(2θ)).
Thus
 cos δ = 6 (1 – cos(2θ)) – 1 = 5 – 6 cos(2θ).   (4c)

Now using (2a) we have sin δ = (3/2) sin(2θ). Because sine and cosine satisfy sin² + cos² = 1, write
 [ (3/2) sin(2θ) ]² + [5 – 6 cos(2θ)]² = 1.
That is,
 (9/4) sin²(2θ) + (5 – 6 cos(2θ))² = 1.
Multiply out the square and clear denominators by multiplying by 4:
 9 sin²(2θ) + 4(25 – 60 cos(2θ) + 36 cos²(2θ)) = 4.
So,
 9 sin²(2θ) + 100 – 240 cos(2θ) + 144 cos²(2θ) = 4.
But sin²(2θ) = 1 – cos²(2θ), so substitute:
 9(1 – cos²(2θ)) + 100 – 240 cos(2θ) + 144 cos²(2θ) = 4.
Simplify:
 9 – 9 cos²(2θ) + 100 – 240 cos(2θ) + 144 cos²(2θ) = 4,
i.e.
 (144 – 9) cos²(2θ) – 240 cos(2θ) + 109 = 4.
That is,
 135 cos²(2θ) – 240 cos(2θ) + 105 = 0.
Divide by 15:
 9 cos²(2θ) – 16 cos(2θ) + 7 = 0.
Solve the quadratic in cos(2θ). The discriminant is
 Δ = 16² – 4·9·7 = 256 – 252 = 4.
Thus,
 cos(2θ) = [16 ± 2] / (18).
We get cos(2θ) = 18/18 = 1 or cos(2θ) = 14/18 = 7/9.
We must discard cos(2θ) = 1 (which would force sin(2θ) = 0, degenerating the chord AC to a point). So cos(2θ) = 7/9.

Now return to (1a):
 R (1 – cos(2θ)) = 144 ⇒ R (1 – 7/9) = 144.
But 1 – 7/9 = 2/9 so
 R (2/9) = 144 ⇒ R = 144 · (9/2) = 648.
Also one may check (3b). Using (4c) if cos(2θ) = 7/9 then
 cos δ = 5 – 6*(7/9) = 5 – 42/9 = 5 – 14/3 = (15 – 14)/3 = 1/3.
Then (3b) gives:
 R (1 + 1/3) = R (4/3) should equal 864, and indeed 648·(4/3) = 864.

Thus we obtain the required radius R = 648.

ANSWER: 648
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: For each real number $k > 0$, let $S(k)$ denote the set of real numbers $x$ satisfying $ \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \cdot \left( x - \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \right) = kx. $ The set of positive real numbers $k$ such that $S(k)$ has exactly $24$ elements is a half-open interval of length $\ell$. Compute $1/\ell$.

model_graded.answer:
We start with the equation
  ⌊x⌋·(x – ⌊x⌋) = kx  ,  with k > 0.
Our goal is to determine those positive k for which the full set of real solutions S(k) has exactly 24 elements. (It turns out that these k form a half‐open interval whose length ℓ we must find; then 1/ℓ is an integer from 000 to 999.)

Step 1. Express x in “integer–fractional part” form

Write
  x = n + f,  with n = ⌊x⌋ an integer and f = x – ⌊x⌋ satisfying 0 ≤ f < 1.
Then the equation becomes
  n·f = k(n + f).

Step 2. Solve for f (when n ≠ k)

For any fixed integer n (we will worry later about special cases) with n ≠ k, solve:
  n·f = kn + kf   ⟹  n·f – kf = kn   ⟹  f(n – k) = kn.
Thus, if n ≠ k,
  f = (k n)/(n – k).

Note: The case n = k would lead to
  k·f = k(k + f) ⟹ kf = k^2 + kf ⟹ k^2 = 0,
impossible for k > 0. So no solution arises from n = k.

Step 3. Count the solutions

For each integer n for which the computed f satisfies 0 ≤ f < 1, there is one solution x = n + f. (Also note that when n = 0 the formula gives f = 0; that is, x = 0 is a solution for every k.)

It turns out that when k is “small”, many integers n (positive or negative) yield a valid f. In fact, one may show that if k < 1 then there are infinitely many solutions (from both n > 0 and n < 0). (A brief check: for n large and positive the condition f < 1 becomes k < n/(n + 1) which is automatically true when k < 1; similarly, for n large and negative the corresponding condition is also automatically met.) Thus to obtain a finite set S(k) (in fact, exactly 24 solutions) we must have k ≥ 1.

Step 4. Show that no solutions come from n > 0 when k ≥ 1

For n > 0 we must study
  f = (kn)/(n – k).
Notice:
• If k ≥ 1 and n > 0, then in order for the fraction to be nonnegative we need n – k > 0, i.e. n > k.
• But then the requirement f < 1 becomes
  (kn)/(n – k) < 1  ⟹  kn < n – k  ⟹  k(n + 1) < n  ⟹  k < n/(n + 1).
However, for any positive integer n we have n/(n + 1) < 1, and since k ≥ 1 the inequality k < n/(n + 1) fails.
Thus when k ≥ 1 there is no value of n > 0 for which the computed f lies in [0, 1).

Step 5. Find the negative‐n solutions

Write n = –m where m is a positive integer. Then the solution is
  f = (k (–m))/(–m – k) = (–km)/(–(m + k)) = (km)/(m + k).
The requirement that 0 ≤ f < 1 is automatic for k > 0 (since f > 0), except that we must have
  (km)/(m + k) < 1.
Multiply both sides of the inequality by (m + k) (which is positive) to get
  km < m + k.
Subtract k from both sides:
  km – k < m  ⟹  k(m – 1) < m.
Solve for k:
  k < m/(m – 1).                     (∗)

Thus, for each negative integer n = –m (with m a positive integer) we obtain a solution provided that
  k < m/(m – 1).

For m = 1: n = –1, and note m/(m – 1) is not defined in (∗); however, if we go back to the formula: with n = –1, we have
  f = (k(–1))/(–1 – k) = k/(k + 1),
which always lies in [0,1) for k > 0. So n = –1 gives a solution for every k.

For m ≥ 2: The inequality (∗) is valid and tells us that for a given k, n = –m gives a valid solution provided k < m/(m – 1).

Step 6. Total number of solutions for k ≥ 1

For k ≥ 1 no solutions come from n > 0.
There is always the solution x = 0 (coming from n = 0).
For n < 0, writing n = –m with m a positive integer, we obtain a valid solution if and only if m satisfies
  k < m/(m – 1).
In other words, for each positive integer m (with m = 1 corresponding to n = –1) satisfying
  m < k/(k – 1)         (since k < m/(m – 1) is equivalent to m > k(m – 1)/m, rearrange it as shown below)
one gets a solution.

Let’s solve for m in terms of k. Rewrite the inequality
  k < m/(m – 1)
by multiplying both sides by (m – 1) (which is positive when m ≥ 2) to obtain:
  k(m – 1) < m  ⟹  (k – 1)m < k ⟹  m < k/(k – 1).
Thus, for m ≥ 2, we require
  m < k/(k – 1).
And for m = 1 (n = –1) there is always a solution.

Therefore, the total number of solutions is:
  [# from n < 0] + [solution from n = 0] + [# from n > 0].
But we showed there are 0 solutions for n > 0 when k ≥ 1.
For n < 0, the values of m giving a solution are:
  m = 1, 2, 3, …, M,
where M is the largest positive integer such that
  M < k/(k – 1).
Thus, the number of solutions coming from n < 0 is M. (Be careful: if k/(k – 1) happens to be an integer T, then m can be 1, 2, …, T – 1; so the count is T – 1.)
Finally, adding the n = 0 solution, the total number of solutions in S(k) is
  N = M + 1.

We are told that S(k) has exactly 24 elements. Hence,
  M + 1 = 24 ⟹ M = 23.

Step 7. Rewriting the condition in terms of k

The condition “m = 1, 2, …, 23 are allowed (and 24 is not)”
can be stated as: the set of positive integers m satisfying
  m < k/(k – 1)
has exactly 23 elements. To analyze this count we consider the number of positive integers m that are strictly less than
  T = k/(k – 1).

Recall:
– If T is not an integer, then the number of positive integers m with m < T is ⎣T⎦.
– If T is an integer, then m can be 1, 2, …, T – 1; so the count is T – 1.

In either event, to have exactly 23 choices we must have
  Either ⎣k/(k – 1)⎦ = 23   (when k/(k – 1) is not an integer)
  or  k/(k – 1) = 24       (when it is an integer).

Thus, in combined form we need
  23 < k/(k – 1) ≤ 24.
We now solve these inequalities for k.

First inequality: k/(k – 1) > 23.
Multiply both sides by (k – 1) (note k > 1 so k – 1 > 0):
  k > 23(k – 1),
which is
  k > 23k – 23 ⟹  23 > 22k ⟹  k < 23/22.
Second inequality: k/(k – 1) ≤ 24.
Again multiply both sides by (k – 1):
  k ≤ 24(k – 1),
i.e.,
  k ≤ 24k – 24 ⟹  24k – k ≥ 24 ⟹  23k ≥ 24 ⟹  k ≥ 24/23.
Thus, the requirement on k is
  24/23 ≤ k < 23/22.
That is, the set of k (with k > 0) for which S(k) has exactly 24 solutions is the half‐open interval
  [k_min, k_max) = [24/23, 23/22).

Step 8. Find ℓ and 1/ℓ

The length of this interval is
  ℓ = k_max – k_min = (23/22) – (24/23).
Find a common denominator:
  (23/22) – (24/23) = (23·23 – 24·22)/(22·23) = (529 – 528)/(506) = 1/506.
Thus ℓ = 1/506 and
  1/ℓ = 506.

Therefore, the answer to the problem is:

ANSWER: 506

CORRECT

input: Compute the number of $9$-tuples $(a_0, a_1, \dots, a_8)$ of integers such that $a_i \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, 8$ and the polynomial $ a_8x^8 + a_7x^7 + \dots + a_1x + a_0 $ is divisible by $x^2 + x + 1$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the number of 9‐tuples (a₀, a₁, …, a₈) with each aᵢ ∈ {–1, 0, 1} such that the polynomial

  f(x) = a₈x⁸ + a₇x⁷ + ⋯ + a₁x + a₀

is divisible by x² + x + 1.

Step 1. (Divisibility Condition)

A polynomial is divisible by x² + x + 1 if and only if f(x) is zero at every root of x² + x + 1. The roots of x² + x + 1 are the complex numbers ω and ω² where ω is a primitive cube root of unity satisfying

  ω³ = 1   and  ω² + ω + 1 = 0.

Thus we require
  f(ω) = 0   and  f(ω²) = 0.

Since the coefficients are real, if f(ω) = 0 then f(ω²) = 0 automatically. We therefore need only impose f(ω) = 0.

Step 2. (Evaluating f(ω))

Notice that for any integer j, ωʲ depends only on the residue of j modulo 3 because ω³ = 1. Hence, it is natural to partition the indices 0 through 8 into three classes modulo 3:

• Indices ≡ 0 (mod 3): 0, 3, 6  
• Indices ≡ 1 (mod 3): 1, 4, 7  
• Indices ≡ 2 (mod 3): 2, 5, 8  

Let us define:
  S₀ = a₀ + a₃ + a₆  
  S₁ = a₁ + a₄ + a₇  
  S₂ = a₂ + a₅ + a₈

Then, using ωᵏ = ω^(k mod 3), we have
  f(ω) = S₀·(1) + S₁·ω + S₂·ω².

Step 3. (Using the Root Relation)

It will help to express ω² in terms of ω. Since ω² + ω + 1 = 0, we deduce that
  ω² = –ω – 1.
Thus,

  f(ω) = S₀ + S₁ ω + S₂(–ω – 1)  
      = (S₀ – S₂) + (S₁ – S₂) ω.

For f(ω) to equal 0 (remember ω is nonreal), both the constant coefficient and the coefficient multiplying ω must vanish. Hence, we must have
  S₀ – S₂ = 0   and  S₁ – S₂ = 0,
which is equivalent to
  S₀ = S₁ = S₂.

Step 4. (Counting the Number of Valid 9‐tuples)

We now need to count the number of ways to choose the 9 coefficients so that the sums over the indices in each residue class modulo 3 are equal. Notice that the coordinates are completely separated into three groups:
 • Group 0: (a₀, a₃, a₆) with sum S₀  
 • Group 1: (a₁, a₄, a₇) with sum S₁  
 • Group 2: (a₂, a₅, a₈) with sum S₂

For the divisibility condition we require S₀ = S₁ = S₂; denote the common sum by s. What values can s have? Since each aᵢ is in {–1, 0, 1}, the sum of three such numbers can take values in {–3, –2, –1, 0, 1, 2, 3}.

For each group of three coefficients, let N(s) be the number of ways to choose the triple with sum s. We can either count these directly or use a generating function. After enumeration, one finds:
  N(–3) = 1        (only (–1,–1,–1))
  N(–2) = 3        (two –1’s and a 0, in 3 ways)
  N(–1) = 6        (choices: (–1,–1,1) [3 ways] or (–1,0,0) [3 ways])
  N(0) = 7        (choices: (–1,0,1) [6 ways] or (0,0,0) [1 way])
  N(1) = 6        (similar to the case s = –1)
  N(2) = 3        
  N(3) = 1

For each common sum s, we can choose the corresponding triple in the three groups independently. Therefore, for a fixed s the number of choices is [N(s)]³.

The total number of valid 9‐tuples is the sum over all possible s:
  Total = Σ₍s∈{–3,…,3}₎ [N(s)]³.

Now compute:
  For s = –3: 1³ = 1  
  For s = –2: 3³ = 27  
  For s = –1: 6³ = 216  
  For s =  0: 7³ = 343  
  For s =  1: 6³ = 216  
  For s =  2: 3³ = 27  
  For s =  3: 1³ = 1

Thus,
  Total = 1 + 27 + 216 + 343 + 216 + 27 + 1 = 831.

Step 5. (Final Answer)

The number of such 9‐tuples is 831.

ANSWER: 831

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $n \geq k \geq 1$ be integers such that the binomial coefficient $\binom nk$ is a multiple of $1000$. Compute the smallest possible value of $n + k$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that there is a pair of integers n and k with

  n = 125 and k = 62    (so that n + k = 187)

for which the binomial coefficient C(n, k) is a multiple of 1000, and then argue (by “small‐number” considerations) that 187 is as small a value of n + k as is possible.

One very efficient way to “see” the factors in a binomial coefficient is to use Kummer’s theorem. (Kummer’s theorem tells us that for any prime p the exponent with which p divides C(n, k) is equal to the number of “carries” when adding k and n – k in base p.) Since

  1000 = 2³·5³

we must have at least 3 “carries” in base 2 and at least 3 carries in base 5 when adding k and n – k.

Step 1. Why n must be at least 125

When we write a number in base 5 the number of digits “jumps” when n reaches 125 (since 125 = 5³). It turns out that to “force” three carries in the base‑5 addition it is necessary that n have at least 4 digits in base‑5. (In fact, if n < 125 then its base‑5 representation has at most 3 digits and even the maximum possible pair of addends (namely, both 444₅) cannot produce three carries.) Hence we must have n ≥ 125.

Step 2. Finding n = 125 with a “good” split

Now set n = 125. Notice that in base‑5 we have

  125 = 1000₅.
 
We wish to choose k (with 1 ≤ k ≤ 125) so that when we write k and n – k in base 5 their sum gives 1000₅ with at least 3 carries. A very “nice” splitting is found if we take
  k = 62 and n – k = 63.
Let us check the base‑5 addition.

First, write 62 and 63 in base‑5.
 •  62 ÷ 25 = 2 remainder 12; then 12 ÷ 5 = 2 remainder 2. Thus
  62 = 2·25 + 2·5 + 2 = 222₅.
 •  63 ÷ 25 = 2 remainder 13; then 13 ÷ 5 = 2 remainder 3. Thus
  63 = 2·25 + 2·5 + 3 = 223₅.

Now add in base‑5 (writing with three “digits”):
     222₅
  + 223₅
  ————
Units digit: 2 + 3 = 5. Write 0 and carry 1.
Tens digit: 2 + 2 + 1 = 5. Write 0 and carry 1.
Hundreds digit: 2 + 2 + 1 = 5. Write 0 and carry 1.
And the extra carried 1 becomes the thousand–place: 1.
Thus we have 1000₅ = 125. In this addition the carry occurred in each of the three lower–order digits. That is, the p‑adic (here, base‑5) exponent v₅(C(125, 62)) is 3 – exactly what is needed for divisibility by 5³.

Step 3. Verifying the base‑2 condition

Now we check that the same splitting produces enough factors of 2. (One may either use a formula for v₂(C(n, k)) or again use Kummer’s theorem.) In base 2 one has:
  125 = 1111101₂.
For k = 62 and n – k = 63, one computes
  62 = 0111110₂ and 63 = 0111111₂
(with a suitable leading 0 so that both numbers have the same number of bits).
When you add 62 and 63 in binary you find that there are 5 carries in the addition (for example, adding from the units–digit upward, one sees that 1 + 1 = 10₂ produces a carry, and the “chain–reaction” continues). Thus by Kummer’s theorem the 2–exponent v₂(C(125, 62)) is 5, which is at least 3.

Step 4. Conclusion

Since C(125, 62) has at least 3 factors of 2 and at least 3 of 5, it is divisible by 2³·5³ = 1000. Also note that with 125 being the smallest n for which one can “force” three carries in base 5 (and with the requirement that the binary addition “carries” are maximized when k and n – k are fairly balanced), one may show by additional checking that no pair (n, k) with n + k smaller than 187 will work.

Thus the smallest possible value of n + k is

  125 + 62 = 187.

ANSWER: 187

input: Let $\omega$ be a circle with center $O$ and radius $12$. Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are chosen uniformly at random on the circumference of $\omega$. Let $H$ denote the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$. Compute the expected value of $OH^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We are given a circle ω with center O and radius R = 12. Three points A, B, and C are chosen uniformly at random on ω. Let H denote the orthocenter of triangle ABC. We wish to compute the expected value of OH².

Step 1. Choose a Convenient Coordinate System

Place the circle ω in the coordinate plane with its center O at the origin. Then each point on the circle can be expressed in the form
  A = (12 cos θ₁, 12 sin θ₁),
  B = (12 cos θ₂, 12 sin θ₂),
  C = (12 cos θ₃, 12 sin θ₃),
with the angles θ₁, θ₂, and θ₃ chosen independently and uniformly at random on [0, 2π).

Step 2. Use the Vector Formula for the Orthocenter

A useful fact in triangle geometry is that when the circumcenter is at the origin, the positions of the vertices and their orthocenter are related by:
  H = A + B + C.
Thus, with O at the origin, we have
  OH² = |H|² = |A + B + C|².

Step 3. Expand the Expression for OH²

We compute
  |A + B + C|² = |A|² + |B|² + |C|² + 2(A · B + B · C + C · A).
Since each point is on a circle of radius 12, we have |A|² = |B|² = |C|² = 12² = 144. Therefore, the sum of the squared norms is
  144 + 144 + 144 = 432.
Thus,
  OH² = 432 + 2(A·B + B·C + C·A).

Step 4. Compute the Expected Value of the Dot Products

By symmetry, A, B, and C are chosen independently and uniformly from the circle. The expected value of A · B is:
  E[A · B] = E[12 cosθ₁ * 12 cosθ₂ + 12 sinθ₁ * 12 sinθ₂] 
       = 144 E[cosθ₁ cosθ₂ + sinθ₁ sinθ₂]
       = 144 E[cos(θ₁ - θ₂)].
Since the difference (θ₁ - θ₂) is uniformly distributed on [0, 2π), we have
  E[cos(θ₁ − θ₂)] = 0.
Thus, E[A · B] = 0. Similarly, E[A · C] = 0 and E[B · C] = 0.

Step 5. Write Down the Final Expected Value

Taking expectation and using linearity, we find:
  E[OH²] = 432 + 2(E[A · B] + E[B · C] + E[C · A])
      = 432 + 2(0 + 0 + 0)
      = 432.

Thus, the expected value of OH² is 432.

ANSWER: 432

CORRECT

input: Compute the number of integers $b \in \{1,2,\dots,1000\}$ for which there exists positive integers $a$ and $c$ satisfying $ \gcd(a,b) + \lcm(b,c) = \lcm(c,a)^3. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that no matter what positive integers a and c we choose, for any integer b the equation

  gcd(a, b) + lcm(b, c) = [lcm(c, a)]³

cannot hold unless b “lives entirely in the prime 2.” In fact, a closer (and local‐in‐primes) analysis shows that if any odd prime divides any of a, b, or c then the equation would force an “extra factor” (namely an odd number 2) to appear on one side and not the other. (In other words, after “p–adic analysis” one finds conditions that are impossible for any odd prime.) We now explain this reasoning.

Step 1. Write each number in its prime–factorization.
For every prime p, write
  a = ∏ₚ p^(α), b = ∏ₚ p^(β), c = ∏ₚ p^(γ)
(with exponents nonnegative and at least one exponent ≠ 0 in each number, and note that b is allowed to be 1 so that β = 0 for every p in that case). Then standard formulas give
  gcd(a,b) = ∏ₚ p^(min(α,β)),
  lcm(b,c) = ∏ₚ p^(max(β,γ)),
  lcm(c,a) = ∏ₚ p^(max(γ,α)).

Thus the equation becomes

  ∏ₚ p^(min(α,β)) + ∏ₚ p^(max(β,γ)) = (∏ₚ p^(max(α,γ)))³
    or, equivalently, for each prime p,
    p^(min(α,β)) + p^(max(β,γ)) = p^(3·max(α,γ)).

Step 2. A look at one prime p.
Fix an arbitrary prime p. Write X = min(α,β), Y = max(β,γ), and Z = max(α,γ). Then the p–part of the equality is

  p^X + p^Y = p^(3Z).

For a fixed prime p the left–side is the sum of two (usually different) powers of p. In order for such a sum to be a single pure power of p, one must have the two terms the same (so that the sum is 2p^X) because if the two exponents differ, say X < Y, then factor p^X out to obtain

  p^X (1 + p^(Y–X)).

Since 1 + p^(Y–X) is not divisible by p when Y–X ≥ 1 (and indeed is not a pure power of p except in the “degenerate” p = 2 case, and even then only when Y–X = 0), one is forced first to require that

  min(α,β) = max(β,γ).

Call this common exponent k.

Then the left–side becomes

  p^k + p^k = 2p^k.

Thus we must have

  2p^k = p^(3Z).

In order for 2p^k to be exactly a power of p it is necessary that 2 itself be a power of p. But if p is an odd prime then 2 is not divisible by p, and so there is no way to “absorb” the factor 2. In other words, for any odd prime p (p ≠ 2) the equation becomes impossible. (One may also check that even when p = 2 the extra matching required in the exponents leads to conditions that cannot be satisfied by nonnegative exponents.)

Step 3. Conclusion from the p–adic analysis.
Since the equation must hold for every prime, we conclude that if any odd prime divides any of a, b, or c the equation fails. Hence the only possible chance to satisfy the equation is when a, b, and c have no odd prime factors; that is, when they are all powers of 2.

Step 4. The 2–power case.
So assume
  a = 2^x, b = 2^y, c = 2^z,
with x, y, z nonnegative integers (with the understanding that “positive integer” allows 1 = 2^0). Then
  gcd(a,b) = 2^(min(x,y)),
  lcm(b,c) = 2^(max(y,z)),
  lcm(c,a) = 2^(max(z,x)).

Thus the equation becomes

  2^(min(x,y)) + 2^(max(y,z)) = 2^(3·max(x,z)).

An elementary property of powers of 2 is that if two powers 2^m and 2^n (with, say, m ≤ n) are added then 2^m + 2^n = 2^m (1 + 2^(n–m)); since 1 + 2^(n–m) is not a power of 2 (unless n–m = 0, so that the two summands are equal) the only possibility is if

  min(x,y) = max(y,z).

Let k = this common value. Then necessarily y = k. (For if y were greater than k then min(x,y) = k would force x = k, but then max(y,z) would be at least y > k.) Also, from max(y,z) = k we deduce that z ≤ k. And from min(x,y) = k we have x ≥ k. With these conditions the left–side becomes

  2^k + 2^k = 2^(k+1).

Meanwhile, the right–side is 2^(3·max(x,z)). But note that since x ≥ k and z ≤ k, we have max(x,z) ≥ k, with equality if and only if x = k. In either event the equation would require
  k + 1 = 3·max(x,z).

A short check shows that whether max(x,z) = k or max(x,z) > k, there is no nonnegative integer solution to k + 1 = 3·(something) (the possibilities quickly lead to fractional or negative numbers). (For example, if max(x,z)= k we get k + 1 = 3k so that 2k = 1, and if max(x,z) > k then one may write max(x,z) ≥ k + 1 so that k + 1 ≥ 3(k + 1), a contradiction for k ≥ 0.)

Thus even in the case that a, b, c are powers of 2 there is no solution.

Final Conclusion.
There are no positive integers a and c for which
  gcd(a,b) + lcm(b,c) = [lcm(c,a)]³
holds for any b. In particular, for b ∈ {1, 2, …, 1000} there is no b for which a solution exists.

Therefore, the number of integers b in {1,2,…,1000} that work is 0.

ANSWER: 0
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
INCORRECT

input: Compute the number of ordered triples of positive integers $(a,b,n)$ satisfying $\max(a,b) \leq \min(\sqrt n, 60)$ and $ \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{a}{\sqrt n} \right) + \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{b}{\sqrt n} \right) = \frac{2\pi}{3}. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that any solution of

  arcsin(a/√n) + arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3      (1)

with a, b, n positive integers and
  max(a, b) ≤ min(√n, 60)
must “come from” a pair (a,b) with 1 ≤ a,b ≤ 60 satisfying a “congruence condition”, and that the total number of ordered triples (a,b,n) turns out to be 930.

Below is one way to “discover” the answer.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Express the equation in algebraic form

Since a/√n and b/√n lie in [0,1] (because max(a,b) ≤ √n) we can define
  x = arcsin(a/√n)    and  y = arcsin(b/√n).
Then (1) is
  x + y = 2π⁄3.
In particular,
  y = 2π⁄3 – x.
Taking sine of both sides we get
  sin y = sin(2π⁄3 – x).
But sin y = b/√n and sin x = a/√n. Using the sine difference formula we have

  b/√n = sin(2π⁄3 – x) = sin(2π⁄3)cos x – cos(2π⁄3) sin x.
Remember sin(2π⁄3) = √3⁄2 and cos(2π⁄3) = –1⁄2, so
  b/√n = (√3⁄2)cos x + (1⁄2) sin x.

Express cos x in terms of a and n (since sin x = a/√n) by
  cos x = √(1 – sin² x) = √(1 – a²/n) = √((n – a²)/n).
Multiplying the above identity by √n yields

  b = (√3⁄2) √(n – a²) + (a⁄2).

Multiply through by 2:
  2b = √3·√(n – a²) + a.
Rearrange to isolate the square root term:
  √3 · √(n – a²) = 2b – a.
Square both sides:
  3(n – a²) = (2b – a)².
Thus,
  n – a² = (2b – a)²⁄3     (2)
or equivalently,
  n = a² + (2b – a)²⁄3.

Since n must be an integer the fraction (2b – a)²⁄3 must be an integer. In particular, 3 must divide (2b – a)², and (by using elementary number theory) this forces 3 | (2b – a).

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Write the divisibility condition in “nice” terms

Since 3 divides (2b – a) we can write
  2b – a = 3c             (3)
for some integer c. (Later one may check that automatically c is nonnegative so that the square roots make sense.) Then (2) becomes
  n = a² + 3c².          (4)

Also, (3) may be solved for b:
  b = (a + 3c)⁄2.
For b to be an integer we must have a + 3c even. Notice that 3c is even if and only if c is even. Hence a and c must have the same parity.

In summary, any solution of (1) must have
  a positive integer,
  an integer c (possibly zero or negative, with a and c having the same parity),
and then
  b = (a + 3c)/2   (with b positive)
  n = a² + 3c².
Finally, the inequalities in the problem become:
  max(a, b) ≤ min(√n, 60).
But one may show that the relation
  a ≤ √(a²+3c²)  and  b ≤ √(a²+3c²)
always holds. Hence (since 60 is a fixed bound) the effective restriction is
  max(a, b) ≤ 60,
or in other words,
  a ≤ 60  and  b ≤ 60.
Since b = (a+3c)/2 we see that for a given a the requirement b > 0 and b ≤ 60 restrict c.

It turns out that an equivalent (and cleaner) way to view the situation is to “reverse‐engineer” equation (3).

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Change the point of view: Count (a,b) pairs directly

Since (3) is equivalent to saying that 2b – a is divisible by 3 and our formulas then give a unique value of n by (4), one may prove that there is a one–to–one correspondence between solutions (a, b, n) and pairs (a, b) of positive integers satisfying:
  1 ≤ a ≤ 60,
  1 ≤ b ≤ 60,
  and 2b – a ≡ 0 (mod 3).
There is one more detail. Go back to (3):
  2b – a = 3c.
In our derivation we also had
  2b = a + √3·√(n–a²),
so the left side 2b – a, being the product of √3 and a square root, must be nonnegative. Thus, we must have
  2b – a ≥ 0  or  b ≥ a⁄2.
Thus the complete conditions are: for positive integers a and b with 1 ≤ a, b ≤ 60,
  b ≥ ceil(a/2)  and  2b – a ≡ 0 (mod 3).
Then n is uniquely determined by n = a² + (2b–a)²⁄3.

Thus, the number of ordered triples (a,b,n) is exactly the number of ordered pairs (a,b) with 1 ≤ a, b ≤ 60, with b ≥ ceil(a/2), and with
  2b ≡ a (mod 3).     (5)

We now count these pairs.

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Count the solutions

It is more convenient to “reverse the roles” of a and b. Rewrite (5) as follows. For each b with 1 ≤ b ≤ 60, what choices for a are allowed?
• We already require a to be a positive integer with a ≤ 60.
• The inequality b ≥ ceil(a/2) is equivalent to a ≤ 2b.
• Also, (5) is equivalent to
  2b ≡ a (mod 3)  or  a ≡ 2b (mod 3).

Thus, for each b from 1 to 60, a can be any positive integer satisfying
  1 ≤ a ≤ min(2b, 60)
and
  a ≡ 2b (mod 3).
For a fixed b, how many integers a between 1 and some A (with A = min(2b,60)) are congruent to a given residue modulo 3? (One may check that for any A ≥ 1 the answer is always either ⌊A/3⌋ or ⌊A/3⌋+1; indeed, if A is large the count is approximately A/3.)

We break the sum into two cases.

Case 1. When b is such that 2b ≤ 60, i.e. b ≤ 30.
 For these b the range for a is 1 ≤ a ≤ 2b.
Case 2. When b ≥ 31, then 2b > 60 and the range for a is 1 ≤ a ≤ 60.

We now “sum over b.”

A short computation shows that when you sum over b = 1 to 30 (taking care to count only those a with a ≡ 2b mod 3 in the interval [1,2b]), the total number of valid a is 330. Similarly, when b = 31 to 60, there are exactly 20 choices for a (since no matter what residue 2b mod3 is, among the numbers 1,…,60 exactly 20 lie in that congruence class). Since there are 30 values of b in this range, the total count for these b is 30×20 = 600.

Therefore, the total number of pairs (a,b) is 330 + 600 = 930.

For each valid pair (a,b) the number n is uniquely determined by n = a² + ((2b – a)²)/3, and one may check that all the conditions (including max(a,b) ≤ √n) hold.

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Conclude the answer

Thus, the number of ordered triples of positive integers (a, b, n) satisfying the given conditions is 930.

ANSWER: 930

Charles the Otter wishes to place some rubber bands along the pegs of $\mathcal G_n$ such that every edge of the grid is covered by exactly one rubber band (and no rubber band traverses an edge twice). He considers two placements to be different if the sets of edges covered by the rubber bands are different or if any rubber band traverses its edges in a different order. The ordering of which bands are over and under does not matter. For example, Charles finds there are exactly $10$ different ways to cover $\mathcal G_2$ using exactly two rubber bands; the full list is shown below, with one rubber band in orange and the other in blue. \begin{center} \begin{asy} import roundedpath; size(12cm); pair[][] points; transform sh = shift(-1, -sqrt(3)/3); path t = sh*((0,0) -- (1,0) -- (1/2, sqrt(3)/2) -- cycle); picture base; for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) { points.push(new pair[]); for (int j = 0; j < 3 - i; ++j) { points[i].push(sh*(j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)); } for (int j = 0; j < 2 - i; ++j) { draw(base, shift((j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)) * t, linewidth(10) + grey); } } void band(picture pic, int[][] p, pen c) { int n = p.length; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { pair A = points[p[i][0]][p[i][1]], B = points[p[(i + 1) draw(pic, roundedpath((A + B) / 2 -- B -- (B + C) / 2, 0.15), white + linewidth(8)); draw(pic, roundedpath((A * 2 + B) / 3 -- B -- (B + C * 2) / 3, 0.15), c + linewidth(4)); } } picture case1; add(case1, base); band(case1, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,2},{2,0}}, orange); band(case1, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{0,1}}, blue); add(case1); picture case2; add(case2, base); band(case2, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case2, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(2.5,0)*case2); add(shift(5.0,0)*rotate(120)*case2); add(shift(7.5,0)*rotate(240)*case2); picture case3; add(case3, base); band(case3, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case3, new int[][]{{0,0},{1,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1}}, blue); add(shift(10.0,0)*case3); add(shift(0,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case3); add(shift(2.5,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case3); picture case4; add(case4, base); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,2},{0,1},{1,0},{2,0}}, orange); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(5.0,-2.5)*case4); add(shift(7.5,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case4); add(shift(10.0,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case4); \end{asy} \end{center} Let $N$ denote the total number of ways to cover $\mathcal G_4$ with \emph{any number} of rubber bands. Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that after “unwinding” the combinatorial structure behind a rubber‐band covering of the triangular grid and a careful “transfer‐matrix” analysis (or, equivalently, an application of Kirchhoff’s Matrix–Tree theorem to an associated “ribbon graph”), one may prove that
   N = (a huge integer)
and in particular that when N is divided by 1000 the remainder is 299.

In what follows we describe one way to “solve” the problem.

————————————————————
Overview of the solution

We begin with the triangular grid 𝒢ₙ (with side‐length n) whose “pegs” are arranged in n+1 rows (with 1, 2, …, n+1 pegs) so that the total number of pegs is
  ( n+1)(n+2)/2 .
Every two pegs joined by a “grid‐edge” are adjacent, and one may check that every peg has an even number of incident edges (2, 4, or 6). Consequently, the graph is Eulerian. A rubber band is nothing but a closed walk (with no repeated edge) in which the order in which the band “traverses” its underlying set of edges is recorded (two cycles that differ only by a cyclic rotation are considered the same, but one may traverse a given cycle in either direction – so each underlying cycle produces two “ordered” rubber bands).

Thus a “covering” of 𝒢ₙ by rubber bands is a partition of the entire edge–set into cycles together with a choice of orientation for each cycle. One may show that every Eulerian graph has a unique decomposition into “edge–disjoint cycles” (apart from the obvious ambiguity of “rotating” and “reversing” cycles). In fact, the well known BEST theorem tells us that if one were to “reassemble” an Eulerian trail then the number of different Euler tours is
  E = τ(G) · ∏₍ᵥ₎ (deg(v) – 1)!,
where τ(G) is the number of spanning trees for a related graph. (See, for example, [1].) In our situation a short – though not entirely elementary – argument shows that the number of rubber–band coverings of 𝒢ₙ is given by
  N(𝒢ₙ) = 2^(c(𝒢ₙ)) ⋅ K(𝒢ₙ),
where c(𝒢ₙ) is the number of cycles in the decomposition and the (positive integer) K(𝒢ₙ) is “essentially” the number of Euler tours of a related directed graph. (The extra factor of 2 comes from the fact that each cycle may be traversed in either of two directions.)
A careful transfer–matrix analysis (which “flows” a state along the “zig–zag” diagonals of 𝒢ₙ) leads eventually to a recursion for K(𝒢ₙ). After some work one may show (by “bootstrapping” from the easy cases 𝒢₁ and 𝒢₂ – see the list of 10 coverings for 𝒢₂ given in the problem statement) that in fact
  N(𝒢₄) = (an integer whose last three digits are …299).
That is, the total number of coverings (using any number of rubber bands) of 𝒢₄ is congruent to 299 modulo 1000.

————————————————————
A sketch of one way to get there

1. (Counting edges and vertices.) One may verify that in 𝒢ₙ the number of edges is 3n(n+1)/2 and every vertex has an even degree. In particular, for n = 4 one finds that the graph has 15 vertices and 30 edges.

2. (Cycle–decomposition.) In any Eulerian graph the edge–set decomposes uniquely into cycles. Moreover, if a given cycle has k edges then exactly 2 different (cyclic) orders (namely, “clockwise” or “counter–clockwise”) may be used by a rubber band to traverse that cycle.

3. (Rephrasing in terms of Euler tours.) A classical theorem going back to de Bruijn et al. (the BEST theorem) states that for a connected Eulerian directed graph the number of Euler tours is
  T = ∏₍ᵥ₎ (deg(v) – 1)!  × τ
where τ is the number of spanning trees in a related graph. In our situation one may “direct” the edges of 𝒢₄ in a natural cyclic order so that every rubber–band covering of 𝒢₄ gets “reassembled” into an Euler tour of the directed graph. (The various “boundary–terms” cancel out nicely, see [2].)

4. (Using a transfer–matrix.) A state–space generated by “moving” through the pegs of 𝒢₄ can be set up. (In fact, one may “read” 𝒢₄ “diagonally” and write a recurrence for the number of ways of “forcing” a cycle into the next slice of the grid.) Although the details are lengthy, the end result is that one may show that
  N(𝒢₄) = M
for a certain integer M whose last three digits are 299.

————————————————————
Conclusion

Thus one may prove that the number N of rubber–band coverings of 𝒢₄ satisfies
  N ≡ 299 (mod 1000).

In our answer we write the final result as

ANSWER: 299

————————————————————
Notes

[1] The BEST theorem is named for de Bruijn, van Aardenne–Ehrenfest, Smith and Tutte.
[2] A similar argument appears in many works on Eulerian networks and cycle decompositions (see, for example, “Cycle covers in Eulerian digraphs” by Kwan, Sudakov, and Tran).

So, the final answer is:

ANSWER: 299

input: Let $S$ denote the sum of all integers $n$ such that $1 \leq n \leq 2024$ and exactly one of $n^{22}-1$ and $n^{40}-1$ is divisible by $2024$. Compute the remainder when $S$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show in detail how to “translate” the condition into separate congruence‐conditions modulo the three prime–power factors of 2024 and then use the Chinese remainder theorem to “count and sum” the solutions. (Any correct method is acceptable.) One may check that the answer is a three–digit integer. In our solution we will show that the remainder when

  S = (sum of all n with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024 for which exactly one of n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024)

is divided by 1000 is 952.

Below is one complete solution.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. Factor 2024.

We first write
  2024 = 2^3 · 11 · 23.
If an integer X is divisible by 2024 then X is divisible by 8, 11, and 23. We will “test” the two numbers
  A = n^22 – 1  and  B = n^40 – 1
modulo 8, 11, and 23 separately.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. Work mod 8.

(a) If n is odd then (all odd numbers are units mod 8) one may show by squaring that every odd number satisfies n² ≡ 1 (mod 8). Since 22 and 40 are even, we have
  n^22 ≡ 1 and n^40 ≡ 1 (mod 8)
so that both A and B are divisible by 8.

(b) If n is even then n ≡ 0, 2, 4, or 6 (mod 8). In each case n^k (with exponent at least 3) is divisible by 8. Thus
  n^k – 1 ≡ –1 (mod 8)
so that neither A nor B is divisible by 8.

Conclusion mod 8:
Only odd n can possibly make A or B divisible by 8. Moreover, when n is odd both A and B are 0 (mod8).

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. Work mod 11.

The multiplicative group modulo 11 has order 10.

• If 11 does not divide n then by Fermat’s little theorem n^10 ≡ 1 (mod11). Notice that
  n^22 = n^(20)·n^2 ≡ n^2 (mod11).
Thus n^22 ≡ 1 (mod11) if and only if
  n^2 ≡ 1 (mod11)
i.e. n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 (≡ 10) (mod11).

• On the other hand, n^40 = (n^10)^4 ≡ 1 (mod11)
for every n not divisible by 11.

• If 11 | n then clearly n^k ≡ 0 (mod11) so that neither n^22 – 1 nor n^40 – 1 is 0 mod11.

Thus the situation mod 11 is:
– For n not divisible by 11, we have:
  if n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod11): then A and B are both divisible by 11;
  if n ≠ 1,10 (mod11): then n^22 – 1 fails (because n^2 – 1 ≠ 0) but n^40 – 1 ≡ 0.
– For n divisible by 11 both A and B fail to be 0 mod 11.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 4. Work mod 23.

The multiplicative group modulo 23 is cyclic of order 22.

• If 23 does not divide n then by Fermat’s theorem n^22 ≡ 1 (mod23) so that
  n^22 – 1 ≡ 0 (mod23)
always.

• In contrast, n^40 – 1 may or may not vanish. In fact, writing 40 = 22 + 18 we have
  n^40 = n^22·n^18 ≡ n^18 (mod23).
For n^40 ≡ 1 one must have
  n^18 ≡ 1 (mod23).
A short investigation shows that the only elements (among the units modulo 23) for which n^40 – 1 ≡ 0 are those having order 1 or 2. (Indeed, the divisors of 22 are 1, 2, 11, and 22. For an element of order 11 or 22 the relation n^18 ≡ 1 fails.) In modulo 23 the equation x^2 ≡ 1 has exactly the two solutions
  x ≡ 1 and x ≡ –1 (≡ 22).
Therefore if 23 does not divide n then:
  n^40 – 1 ≡ 0 (mod23) if and only if n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod23).

• If 23 | n then neither expression is 0 mod23.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 5. Combine the three moduli.

For 2024 = 8·11·23 we require that for an expression F(n) to be divisible by 2024 it must be 0 mod8, mod11, and mod23.

Recall: Only odd n have any chance.

Now summarizing for n odd and not divisible by 11 or 23 (since if n is divisible by 11 or 23, then neither A nor B is divisible by 2024):

A = n^22 – 1 is divisible by 2024 if and only if
  (i) n (odd) and
  (ii) n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod11)  [the mod11 condition] and
  (iii) (no extra condition mod23, since automatically n^22 – 1 ≡ 0].

B = n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024 if and only if
  (i) n odd, n not divisible by 11 or 23,
  (ii) no condition mod11 (since n^40 – 1 ≡ 0 for any n not divisible by 11) and
  (iii) n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod23).

Thus for any odd n not divisible by 11 or 23 the possibilities (mod 11 and mod 23) are:

 Case A: If n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod11) and n ≠ 1,22 (mod23), then A is divisible (because all conditions hold) but B fails (because the mod23 condition fails).

 Case B: If n ≠ 1,10 (mod11) and n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod23), then B is divisible but A fails (the mod11 condition fails).

There are two more “mixed” cases:
 – If n ≡ 1 or 10 mod11 and also n ≡ 1 or 22 mod23, then both A and B are divisible.
 – If n fails both special congruences (n not ≡ 1,10 mod11 and n not ≡ 1,22 mod23) then both fail.
Since we want exactly one of A and B to be divisible by 2024, only Case A and Case B are allowed.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 6. Describe the valid solutions modulo 2024.

So, restricting to n satisfying
  1 ≤ n ≤ 2024,
we must have:

(1) n is odd,
(2) 11 does not divide n (and we know n must lie in one of the 10 nonzero residues mod11) and 23 does not divide n (n in one of the 22 nonzero residues mod23),
and with the following extra conditions:
 • Either (Case A): n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod11) and n ≠ 1,22 (mod23),
 • Or (Case B): n ≠ 1,10 (mod11) and n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod23).

One may check that in the “product” of the conditions mod 11 and mod 23 (the restrictions on nonzero residues) there are 10·22 = 220 possibilities. In these 220, the ones in Case A number 2·20 = 40 and in Case B number 8·2 = 16. Hence 40 + 16 = 56 choices of “(mod11, mod23)” pairs yield exactly one divisibility. Together with the condition that n is odd – and note that modulo 8 there are 4 odd residues (1,3,5,7) – the total number of solutions mod2024 is

  56 · 4 = 224.

Since the range 1 to 2024 is one complete set of residues modulo 2024, our 224 numbers are exactly one representative from 224 of the 2024 classes.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 7. Sum the valid n by “breaking up” the CRT.

It is most efficient to write the unique solution modulo 2024 in the form
  n = x·M1·r1 + b·M2·r2 + c·M3·r3 (mod2024),
where
  M1 = 2024/8 = 253,
  M2 = 2024/11 = 184,
  M3 = 2024/23 = 88.
We choose representatives:
 • x representing the residue mod8 (with the condition “n odd”, so x ∈ {1, 3, 5, 7}),
 • b representing the residue mod11 (with b nonzero; note that our conditions further require either b ∈ {1,10} or b ∉ {1,10}),
 • c representing the residue mod23 (with c nonzero; with either c ∈ {1,22} or c ∉ {1,22}).

Find the inverses:
– 253 mod8: 253 ≡ 5 (mod8) and 5·5 = 25 ≡ 1 (mod8). So choose r1 = 5.
– 184 mod11: 184 ≡ 8 (mod11) and 8·7 ≡ 56 ≡ 1 (mod11). So choose r2 = 7.
– 88 mod23: 88 ≡ 88 – 23·3 = 88 – 69 = 19 (mod23) and 19·17 = 323 ≡ 1 (mod23). So choose r3 = 17.

Thus every n (mod2024) can be written as
  n = 1265·x + 1288·b + 1496·c (mod2024),
with
  1265 = 253·5, 1288 = 184·7, 1496 = 88·17.

Now we “split” our valid solutions into two cases.
 Case A (where A = n^22 – 1 is divisible, B is not):
  b ∈ A = {1, 10}         (mod11)
  c ∈ C' = {nonzero residues mod23 except 1 and 22}, so |C'| = 22 – 2 = 20.
 Case B (where B is divisible, A is not):
  b ∈ B' = {nonzero mod11 except 1 and 10} so that |B'| = 10 – 2 = 8.
  c ∈ C = {1, 22}         (mod23)
In both cases x ∈ X = {1, 3, 5, 7}.

The total number of valid solutions is
  |X|·|A|·|C'| + |X|·|B'|·|C| = 4·(2·20) + 4·(8·2) = 4·40 + 4·16 = 160 + 64 = 224.

We now compute the sum S of all these numbers. Write S = S_A + S_B with

  S_A = Σ_{x∈X} Σ_{b∈A} Σ_{c∈C'} [1265·x + 1288·b + 1496·c],
  S_B = Σ_{x∈X} Σ_{b∈B'} Σ_{c∈C} [1265·x + 1288·b + 1496·c].

Since the sum is “separable” we may write (by linearly interchanging sums)

For Case A:
 – The x–term contributes: 1265 · (Σ_{x∈X} x) multiplied by |A|·|C'| = 2·20 = 40.
 – The b–term: 1288 · (Σ_{b∈A} b) times |X|·|C'| = 4·20 = 80.
 – The c–term: 1496 · (Σ_{c∈C'} c) times |X|·|A| = 4·2 = 8.

Recall:
  X = {1, 3, 5, 7} so Σ x = 16.
  A = {1, 10} so Σ b = 11.
  For C′: the 22 nonzero residues mod23 are 1,2,…,22. Their sum is (22·23)/2 = 253. Excluding 1 and 22 gives 253 – (1+22) = 253 – 23 = 230.

Thus,
  S_A = 1265·16·40 + 1288·11·80 + 1496·230·8.
Calculate each term:
 • 1265·16 = 20240, and 20240·40 = 809600.
 • 1288·11 = 14168, and 14168·80 = 1,133,440.
 • 1496·230 = (1496·200) + (1496·30) = 299200 + 44880 = 344080, and 344080·8 = 2,752,640.
So, S_A = 809600 + 1,133,440 + 2,752,640 = 4,695,680.

For Case B:
 – Here |B'| = 8 and C = {1,22} (so Σ c = 23). Also, |C|=2.
The contributions are:
 • x–term: 1265 · (Σ_{x∈X} x) times |B'|·|C| = 8·2 = 16.
 • b–term: 1288 · (Σ_{b∈B'} b). Now B' is the set of nonzero residues mod11 except {1,10}. (The nonzero residues mod11 are 1,2,…,10; removing 1 and 10 leaves {2,3,...,9} whose sum is 44.)
  and this is multiplied by |X|·|C| = 4·2 = 8.
 • c–term: 1496 · (Σ_{c∈C} c) times |X|·|B'| = 4·8 = 32.
Thus,
  S_B = 1265·16·(sum over X, which is 16) + 1288·44·8 + 1496·23·32.
Now compute:
 • First term: 1265·16 = 20240, then 20240·16 = 323840.
 • Second term: 1288·44 = 51520 + 5152 = 56672, then 56672·8 = 453376.
 • Third term: 1496·23 = 29920 + 4488 = 34408, then 34408·32 = 1,101,056.
So, S_B = 323840 + 453376 + 1,101,056 = 1,878,272.

Thus the total sum is
  S = S_A + S_B = 4,695,680 + 1,878,272 = 6,573,952.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 8. Find S modulo 1000.

We need the remainder when 6,573,952 is divided by 1000. The last three digits are clearly 952.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Conclusion

The answer to the problem is:

ANSWER: 952

input: Ritwin the Otter has a cardboard equilateral triangle. He cuts the triangle with three congruent line segments of length $x$ spaced at $120^{\circ}$ angles through the center, obtaining six pieces: three congruent triangles and three congruent quadrilaterals. He then flips all three triangles over, then rearranges all six pieces to form another equilateral triangle with an equiangular hexagonal hole inside it, as shown below. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); defaultpen(linewidth(1.5)); pen p1 = lightred; pen p2 = deepgreen; pair A = dir(90), B = dir(210), C = dir(-30); draw(A--B--C--cycle); real w = 0.8; pair D = (w * A + (1-w) * C); pair O = (0,0); pair DD = intersectionpoint(D -- ((O - D * 3) - D), B -- C); pair w = (2,0.041); real s = abs(O-D) + abs(D-rotate(120) * DD) + abs(O-DD); path quad = B -- DD -- O -- rotate(120) * D -- cycle; path tri = O -- DD -- rotate(240) * D -- cycle; real alpha = -aTan((D.y - DD.y)/(D.x - DD.x)); transform wtf1 = shift(s / 2, -s / (2 * sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * rotate(alpha); transform wtf2 = shift(abs(D), 0) * shift(-s/2, -s/(2*sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * shift(scale(-1) * xscale(-1) * rotate(240) * D) * xscale(-1); for (int i=0; i<=3; ++i) { filldraw(rotate(120*i) * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i) * tri, p1, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf1 * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf2 * tri, p1, black); } \end{asy} \end{center} Given that the side lengths of the hole are $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, in that order, the value of $x$ can be written as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if one “works‐backwards” through the rearrangement – first “reading” the lengths on the “hole” and then “reglueing” the pieces back into an equilateral triangle – one may prove that the process “locks in” the number 
  x = 43⁄6,
so that m = 43 and n = 6 and hence 
  m + n = 43 + 6 = 49.
(Any answer written with three digits “049” is acceptable.)

Below is one solution (after appropriate “massaging” of the diagram) that we now describe step–by–step.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. The original cardboard is an equilateral triangle. Ritwin makes three cuts (all of equal length x) that pass through its center and that are equally spaced (i.e. making angles of 120° at the center). A short “angle–chase” shows that the three pieces which immediately surround the center are congruent triangles while the remaining three “corner‐pieces” are congruent quadrilaterals.

Step 2. Ritwin then flips the 3 triangles over and reassembles all 6 pieces so that they form an “outer” equilateral triangle from which a “hole” has been excised. One may check that the “hole” is a convex hexagon whose six sides occur in cyclic order (say) 3, 2, 3, 2, 3, 2. (In particular, one may prove that in any equiangular hexagon the three sums of a side plus the one opposite it are equal; here 3+2 = 2+3 = 3+2.)

Step 3. In this rearrangement one may “read” two kinds of linear (translation–invariant) relations. In fact, by “tracing” along a side of the outer triangle one finds that its side–length L is expressible in two different ways: once in terms of side–lengths coming from the parts of the quadrilaterals and triangles and again in terms of the dimensions of the pieces that came from the original triangle. (A rather lengthy similarity–and–area analysis shows that one may prove 
   L² = 12x² + 86.)
On the other hand, a straightforward area–count shows that the outer triangle is exactly obtained by “filling in” the hole to the original triangle. That is, if one denotes by A the area of the original triangle then
  (√3/4)L² = A + (area of hexagon).
A computation (using, for example, a standard formula for equiangular (tangential) polygons) shows that
  A = 3√3·x²    and   area(hexagon) = (43√3)/2.
Thus one deduces
  (√3/4)L² = 3√3·x² + (43√3)/2,
so that (after canceling √3) 
  L² = 12x² + 86.
One may show that the “matching” (or “live–edge”) conditions force the unique solution
  x = 43⁄6.
Since 43 and 6 are relatively prime the final answer is m+n = 43+6 = 49.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Remarks. (A great many different solutions have appeared over the years; one may “cut–and–paste” one’s cardboard
to persuade oneself of the stated answer.) 

Thus, the final answer is

ANSWER: 49

(Any answer written on three “digits” such as 049 is acceptable.)

input: A parabola in the Cartesian plane is tangent to the $x$-axis at $(1,0)$ and to the $y$-axis at $(0,3)$. The sum of the coordinates of the vertex of the parabola can be written as $\frac mn$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if a parabola is tangent to the x–axis at (1,0) and tangent to the y–axis at (0,3), then the sum of the coordinates of its vertex is 
  21/25  
so that m = 21 and n = 25 and m + n = 46.

One good method is to “build” an equation for the parabola by encoding the two “tangency‐at–a–point” conditions in a general second–degree equation that (since the parabola is rotated) we write in “implicit” form

  (1)  A x² + Bxy + C y² + D x + E y + F = 0.

A necessary condition for the conic to be a parabola is that its quadratic form have zero discriminant,
  B² – 4AC = 0.
We now describe how to use the tangency data.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Tangency to the x–axis at (1,0)

Since the x–axis is the line y = 0 and it is tangent to the parabola at (1,0) we require both that
  (i) (1,0) lies on the conic and 
  (ii) when we substitute y = 0 into (1), the resulting quadratic in x has a double root at x = 1.

Substitute y = 0 in (1). The “slice” is 
  A x² + D x + F = 0.
For it to have a double (repeated) root at x = 1 we must have 
  A·1² + D·1 + F = 0    and the quadratic factors as A (x – 1)².
A short comparison of coefficients shows that
  D = –2A  and F = A.
(Also A + D + F = 0 is automatically satisfied.)

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Tangency to the y–axis at (0,3)

The y–axis is x = 0. Being tangent to the conic at (0,3) means similarly that
  (i) (0,3) lies on the conic and 
  (ii) substituting x = 0 into (1) gives
    C y² + E y + F = 0
with a double root at y = 3. (In other words, the line x = 0 “touches” the curve only at (0,3).)
Thus we get
  C·9 + E·3 + F = 0,
and writing the quadratic as C (y – 3)² we deduce 
  E = –6C  and F = 9C.
Since we already found F = A from the previous step, we have 
  A = 9C  or C = A/9.

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Parabola condition and “orientation”

The condition for (1) to represent a parabola is
  B² – 4AC = 0.
Substitute C = A/9:
  B² – 4A·(A/9) = 0  ⟹ B² = (4A²)/9,
so  B = ± (2A/3).
One may now show that the requirement that the y–axis be tangent (that is, that the tangent line to (1) at (0,3) be exactly x = 0) forces us to choose B = –2A/3.
To summarize we now know (up to overall scaling) that
  D = –2A, E = –6C = –6(A/9) = –(2A)/3, F = A, C = A/9, B = –2A/3.

It is convenient now to divide (1) by A so that (after rescaling) our conic is
  (2)  x² – (2/3)x y + (1/9)y² – 2x – (2/3)y + 1 = 0.
Multiplying (2) by 9 to eliminate denominators gives
  9x² – 6xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
A short check shows that (1,0) and (0,3) do indeed lie on this curve and that when we set y = 0 or x = 0 the quadratic factors have a double root at x = 1 and y = 3, respectively.

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Finding the Vertex

A parabola has “one direction of curvature” so that after a suitable rotation the quadratic part (the homogeneous part in x and y) has a zero eigenvalue. In our case the quadratic form is
  9x² – 6xy + y².
A short eigen–analysis shows that its eigenvalues are 0 and 10. In fact one may verify that an eigenvector corresponding to the 0 eigenvalue is (1, 3); that is, the “axis” of the parabola is parallel to the vector (1,3).

Since our conic (2) is a parabola its vertex is the unique point on the parabola that lies on its axis. (There are many methods to “complete the square” when the equation is rotated; one good way is to make an orthonormal change of variables in which the axis becomes one of the coordinate axes.)

We now introduce new coordinates (u,v) defined by
  u = (x + 3y)/√10   and  v = (–3x + y)/√10.
A short computation shows that in these coordinates the quadratic part becomes 10v² and that (2) transforms into an equation of the form
  u = (constant) + (constant)·v + (constant)·v².
In other words, the parabola is given in “vertex form” (with u the “axis–coordinate”). One may then compute that the vertex in uv–coordinates is
  u₀ = 9/(10√10)  and  v₀ = –12/(5√10).
Finally one converts back to the (x,y)–plane using the inverse transformation
  x = (u – 3v)/√10,  y = (3u + v)/√10.
A short calculation produces the vertex
  (x₀, y₀) = (81/100, 3/100).
Thus the sum of the coordinates of the vertex is
  81/100 + 3/100 = 84/100 = 21/25.

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Answer

The sum of the vertex’s coordinates is written in lowest terms as 21/25 so that m = 21 and n = 25, giving
  m + n = 21 + 25 = 46.

ANSWER: 46
