input: The Queen of Hearts has a special deck of $16$ playing cards and a $4 \times 4$ square grid. Each card has one of four different ranks and one of four different suits, with each combination occurring exactly once. She wishes to place the cards in the grid, with one card in each cell, such that any cards in adjacent cells share either a rank or a suit. Compute the remainder when the number of ways to fill the grid is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
One nice “end‐game” answer turns out to be 
    2·(4!)·(4!) = 1152   so the answer modulo 1000 is 152.
In what follows we describe one way to “see” a solution.

Below is one solution– “type‐classification”– approach.

Recall that our 16–card deck consists of one card of each “(rank,suit)–pair” where there are 4 ranks and 4 suits. We wish to put the cards one–per–cell into a 4×4 board so that any two cells sharing an edge get cards that agree in at least one coordinate (that is, either the same rank or the same suit).

For any two adjacent cells the condition is “share rank OR share suit”. One may show that any placement with the stated property “forces” a very strong structure. In fact one may prove that every valid arrangement has the following property: for each row the four cards all have a common “constant” coordinate (that is, either they all have the same rank or they all have the same suit) and likewise for each column. (A short proof is by “walking” along a row. Either one of the three horizontal edges “chooses” the rank–equality option – in which case by a short‐chain argument the entire row must have the same rank – or every horizontal adjacent pair uses the suit–equality option – forcing the entire row to have the same suit. And a similar argument works for each column.) Moreover, one may check that the vertical adjacencies then force that the rows and columns “switch roles:” either every row is constant in rank and every column is constant in suit or every row is constant in suit and every column is constant in rank. (Any “mixing” would force a vertical edge to have two different “witnesses” of a match, thereby “colliding” two different cards of the same rank or suit; but the deck has – by assumption – four distinct cards in each rank and suit.)

Thus any valid arrangement is “of product‐form”. For example, in the first case one fixes a permutation (r₁, r₂, r₃, r₄) of the 4 ranks and a permutation (s₁, s₂, s₃, s₄) of the 4 suits, and places in cell (i,j) the card (rᵢ,sⱼ). (Then cells in the same row share the common rank rᵢ, and those in the same column share the common suit sⱼ.) In the second case one “swaps” the roles of rank and suit.

Now, since a permutation of 4 objects can be chosen in 4! ways, the number of arrangements of the first type is 
  4!×4! = 24×24 = 576.
There is an entirely analogous family (with “rows constant in suit and columns constant in rank”) which also gives 576 arrangements.
Since every valid arrangement must be of one of these two types the total number of placements is 
  576 + 576 = 1152.
Finally, taking 1152 modulo 1000 one obtains 152.

It turns out that with a bit of extra work one may prove that no “exotic” arrangement exists.

Thus the final answer is

ANSWER: 152
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
INCORRECT

input: Compute the number of real numbers $x$ such that $0 < x \le 100$ and $ x^2 = \floor{x} \cdot \ceiling{x}. $

model_graded.answer:
Let x be a real number with 0 < x ≤ 100. Write n = ⎣x⎦ and m = ⎡x⎤. We need to solve

  x² = n · m.

There are two cases.

Case 1. x is an integer.
  Then n = m = x, and the equation becomes
    x² = x · x,
which is always true. Since 0 < x ≤ 100 and x is an integer, x can be any of 1, 2, …, 100. This gives 100 solutions.

Case 2. x is not an integer.
  Then n = ⎣x⎦ and m = n + 1, so the equation becomes
    x² = n(n + 1).
Taking the positive square root (since x > 0) gives
    x = √(n(n + 1)).
For this x to have ⎣x⎦ = n and ⎡x⎤ = n + 1, we must have
    n < √(n(n + 1)) < n + 1.
The left inequality is clear since √(n(n + 1)) > √(n²) = n. For the right inequality, note that
    (n + 1)² = n² + 2n + 1 > n² + n,
so √(n² + n) < n + 1. Thus, the inequalities hold.

Now, to ensure x = √(n(n + 1)) is at most 100, require
    n(n + 1) ≤ 10000.
Solving n² + n − 10000 ≤ 0, we find the positive root of the equality is
    n = (–1 + √40001)/2 ≈ 99.5.
Since n is an integer and x is not an integer here, we must have n from 1 up to 99. This gives 99 solutions.

Thus, the total number of solutions is
    100 (from integer x) + 99 (from noninteger x) = 199.

ANSWER: 199
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Evan throws a dart at regular hexagon $BOATIS$, which lands at a uniformly random point inside the hexagon. The probability the dart lands in the interior of exactly one of the quadrilaterals $BOAT$ and $OTIS$ can be expressed as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if a dart lands uniformly at random in the regular hexagon “BOATIS” (with vertices named in order B, O, A, T, I, S) then the probability that the dart hits the interior of exactly one of the quadrilaterals BOAT and OTIS is 
  5/6,
so that if expressed in lowest terms as m/n we have m = 5, n = 6 and m + n = 11.

One very “nice‐coordinate” solution is as follows.

Step 1. Set Up the Hexagon

Place a regular hexagon of side‐length 1 in the plane. A standard choice is to let its vertices be:
  B = (1, 0)
  O = (½, √3⁄2)
  A = (–½, √3⁄2)
  T = (–1, 0)
  I = (–½, –√3⁄2)
  S = (½, –√3⁄2).

Then the hexagon “BOATIS” has vertices in that order. (It is easy to check that the distance between consecutive vertices is 1.)

Recall that the area of a regular hexagon of side length 1 is
  A_hexagon = (3√3)/2.

Step 2. The Two Quadrilaterals

The two quadrilaterals are formed by taking four consecutive vertices:
 Quadrilateral BOAT has vertices B, O, A, T.
 Quadrilateral OTIS has vertices O, T, I, S.

Our goal is to find the total area of the region that is in exactly one of these quadrilaterals. (That is, a point is “in exactly one” if it lies inside BOAT or inside OTIS but not in their overlap.)

Step 3. Compute the Areas of the Quadrilaterals

(a) Use the shoelace formula to compute the area of BOAT. Its vertices are
  B = (1, 0), O = (½, √3⁄2), A = (–½, √3⁄2), T = (–1, 0).

The shoelace formula gives
  A_BOAT = ½ |[1·(√3⁄2) + (½)·(√3⁄2) + (–½)·0 + (–1)·0] – [0·(½) + (√3⁄2)·(–½) + (√3⁄2)·(–1) + 0·1]|.
Compute the “forward sum”:
  1·(√3⁄2) = √3⁄2,
  (½)(√3⁄2) = √3⁄4,
and the other terms are 0. So forward sum = (√3⁄2 + √3⁄4) = 3√3⁄4.

Now compute the “backward sum”:
  0 + (√3⁄2)(–½) = –√3⁄4,
  (√3⁄2)(–1) = –√3⁄2,
  + 0. So backward sum = –(√3⁄4 + √3⁄2) = –3√3⁄4.

Thus,
  A_BOAT = ½ · (3√3⁄4 – (–3√3⁄4)) = ½ · (6√3⁄4) = 3√3⁄4.

(b) Now, compute the area of OTIS. Its vertices are
  O = (½, √3⁄2), T = (–1, 0), I = (–½, –√3⁄2), S = (½, –√3⁄2).
Applying the shoelace formula:
  A_OTIS = ½ |[ (½)(0) + (–1)(–√3⁄2) + (–½)(–√3⁄2) + (½)(√3⁄2) ]
       – [ (√3⁄2)(–1) + 0·(–½) + (–√3⁄2)(½) + (–√3⁄2)(½) ]|.

Let’s compute the forward sum:
  (½)(0) = 0,
  (–1)(–√3⁄2) = √3⁄2,
  (–½)(–√3⁄2) = √3⁄4,
  (½)(√3⁄2) = √3⁄4.
So the forward sum = √3⁄2 + √3⁄4 + √3⁄4 = √3⁄2 + (√3⁄2) = √3.

Now the backward sum:
  (√3⁄2)(–1) = –√3⁄2,
  plus 0,
  (–√3⁄2)(½) = –√3⁄4,
  (–√3⁄2)(½) = –√3⁄4.
Thus, backward sum = –√3⁄2 – √3⁄4 – √3⁄4 = –√3.
So,
  A_OTIS = ½ |√3 – (–√3)| = ½ · (2√3) = √3.

Step 4. Find the Area of Intersection

One may check (or argue by symmetry) that the two quadrilaterals overlap (their “common” region is not empty since they share the vertices O and T). In fact, note that the union of the two quadrilaterals covers the entire hexagon. (Indeed, the two quadrilaterals have vertices
  BOAT: B, O, A, T and OTIS: O, T, I, S,
so the union has all six vertices B, O, A, T, I, S.)

Thus writing
  area(BOAT ∪ OTIS) = A_BOAT + A_OTIS – A_overlap,
and noting that BOAT ∪ OTIS = hexagon (with area 3√3⁄2), we have

  3√3⁄2 = (3√3⁄4 + √3) – A_overlap.
Write √3 as 4√3⁄4 so that
  3√3⁄2 = (3√3⁄4 + 4√3⁄4) – A_overlap = 7√3⁄4 – A_overlap.
Solve for A_overlap:
  A_overlap = 7√3⁄4 – 3√3⁄2.
Since 3√3⁄2 = 6√3⁄4, we deduce
  A_overlap = (7√3 – 6√3)⁄4 = √3⁄4.

Step 5. Find the Area “In Exactly One” of the Quadrilaterals

A point lies in exactly one of BOAT or OTIS if it lies in the region of BOAT that is not in OTIS, or in the region of OTIS that is not in BOAT. Their total area is

  Area_exactly_one = (A_BOAT – A_overlap) + (A_OTIS – A_overlap)
              = (3√3⁄4 – √3⁄4) + (√3 – √3⁄4)
              = (2√3⁄4) + ( (4√3⁄4) – √3⁄4)
              = (√3⁄2) + (3√3⁄4)
              = (2√3⁄4 + 3√3⁄4) = 5√3⁄4.

Step 6. Compute the Desired Probability

Since the dart lands uniformly in the hexagon, the probability that it falls in exactly one quadrilateral is the ratio of the “exactly one” area to the area of the hexagon. Thus

  Probability = (5√3⁄4) / (3√3⁄2)
         = (5√3⁄4) × (2⁄3√3) = (5×2)/(4×3) = 10/12 = 5/6.

Since 5 and 6 are relatively prime, m = 5 and n = 6, so that m + n = 5 + 6 = 11.

Therefore, the final answer is

ANSWER: 11
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
INCORRECT

input: Perry the Panda is eating some bamboo over a five-day period from Monday to Friday (inclusive). On Monday, he eats $14$ pieces of bamboo. Each following day, Perry eats either one less than three times the previous day or one more than the previous day, with equal probability. Compute the expected number of pieces of bamboo Perry has eaten throughout the week after the end of Friday.

model_graded.answer:
We are told that on Monday Perry eats 14 pieces of bamboo. For each subsequent day (Tuesday through Friday), his consumption depends on the previous day’s consumption, x, in one of two equally likely ways:

  • Option 1: 3x − 1  
  • Option 2: x + 1

Let xₙ denote the number of pieces Perry eats on day n (with n = 1 for Monday, n = 2 for Tuesday, and so on).

Step 1. Determine the Expected Value Recurrence

For n ≥ 1, the expected number for the next day is:
  E[xₙ₊₁] = ½(3xₙ – 1) + ½(xₙ + 1).

Taking expectation (and using linearity of expectation), we have:
  E[xₙ₊₁] = ½(3E[xₙ] – 1) + ½(E[xₙ] + 1).

Now simplify:
  E[xₙ₊₁] = ½(3E[xₙ] + E[xₙ] – 1 + 1) = ½(4E[xₙ]) = 2E[xₙ].

Thus, the expected number of pieces on the next day is exactly twice the previous day’s expected value.

Step 2. Compute the Expected Pieces for Each Day

Since Monday (day 1) entails eating x₁ = 14 pieces, we have:
  E[x₁] = 14.
Then:
  E[x₂] = 2E[x₁] = 2·14 = 28.
  E[x₃] = 2E[x₂] = 2·28 = 56.
  E[x₄] = 2E[x₃] = 2·56 = 112.
  E[x₅] = 2E[x₄] = 2·112 = 224.

Step 3. Determine the Expected Total Pieces by End of Friday

We add the expected amounts from Monday through Friday:
  Total = E[x₁] + E[x₂] + E[x₃] + E[x₄] + E[x₅]
  Total = 14 + 28 + 56 + 112 + 224.

Calculate the sum:
  14 + 28 = 42  
  42 + 56 = 98  
  98 + 112 = 210  
  210 + 224 = 434

Thus, the expected number of pieces of bamboo eaten by the end of Friday is 434.

ANSWER: 434
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $N$ denote the number of $7$-tuples of positive integers $(a_1, \dots, a_7)$ such that for each $i = 1, \dots, 7$, we have $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 7 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{a_i} = a_i. $ Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number N of 7‐tuples (a₁, a₂, …, a₇) with each aᵢ ∈ {1, 2, …, 7} that satisfy
  aₐᵢ = aᵢ  for every i = 1, 2, …, 7.

Step 1. Reformulate as a Function Problem
Define a function f: {1, 2, …, 7} → {1, 2, …, 7} by f(i) = aᵢ. Then the given condition becomes:
  f(f(i)) = f(i)  for all i.
This says that f is idempotent.

Step 2. Understand Idempotent Functions
For any idempotent function f, if we define F to be the set of fixed points of f (that is, F = { i : f(i) = i }), then:
• For every i in F we must have f(i) = i, and
• For every i not in F, the image f(i) must be in F (since applying f twice must yield f(i) unchanged).
Thus, every idempotent function on {1, 2, …, 7} is completely determined by choosing a nonempty subset F ⊆ {1, 2, …, 7} (the fixed points) and then assigning, for every i ∉ F, an element from F as f(i).

Step 3. Counting the Functions
Let |F| = j, where j can be any integer from 1 to 7. Then:
• The number of ways to choose F is C(7, j).
• For each i not in F (there are 7 – j such elements), we can choose f(i) freely from F in j ways.
Thus, for a fixed F of size j the number of functions is j^(7 – j).

Summing over all possible sizes j, we have:
  N = Σ (from j = 1 to 7) [C(7, j) · j^(7 – j)].

Step 4. Compute the Sum Term‐by‐Term
For j = 1: C(7, 1) · 1^(6) = 7 · 1 = 7.
For j = 2: C(7, 2) · 2^(5) = 21 · 32 = 672.
For j = 3: C(7, 3) · 3^(4) = 35 · 81 = 2835.
For j = 4: C(7, 4) · 4^(3) = 35 · 64 = 2240.
For j = 5: C(7, 5) · 5^(2) = 21 · 25 = 525.
For j = 6: C(7, 6) · 6^(1) = 7 · 6 = 42.
For j = 7: C(7, 7) · 7^(0) = 1 · 1 = 1.

Now, summing these:
  7 + 672 = 679,
  679 + 2835 = 3514,
  3514 + 2240 = 5754,
  5754 + 525 = 6279,
  6279 + 42 = 6321,
  6321 + 1 = 6322.
Thus, N = 6322.

Step 5. Compute the Remainder When N Is Divided by 1000
Divide 6322 by 1000:
  6322 mod 1000 = 322.

ANSWER: 322
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Convex pentagon $ABCDE$ is inscribed in circle $\omega$ such that $\frac{AC}{DE} = \frac 23$, $AE = CD$, and $AB = BC$. Suppose the distance from $B$ to line $AC$ is $144$ and the distance from $B$ to line $DE$ is $864$. Compute the radius of $\omega$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if a convex pentagon ABCDE is inscribed in a circle ω in that order and satisfies

  (1) AC/DE = 2/3,
  (2) AE = CD, and
  (3) AB = BC,

and if the perpendicular distances from B to lines AC and DE are 144 and 864 respectively, then the radius R of ω is 648.

In what follows the “step‐by‐step” solution is given.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. (Introducing notation by “reading off” equal chords)

Because the pentagon is inscribed in ω and the vertices appear consecutively in the order A, B, C, D, E we may “mark off” the arcs. In particular, since AB = BC the chords subtend equal central angles. (That is, if O is the center, then ∠AOB = ∠BOC.) Let
  arc(AB) = arc(BC) = α   (in degrees or radians – the answer is independent of units).
Then the chord AC (joining A and C) subtends the central angle AOC = 2α and so by the standard chord–radius formula
  AC = 2R sin( (2α)/2 ) = 2R sinα.

Next, denote by β and γ the measures of the arcs corresponding to chords CD and DE respectively. (We will soon see that the other condition AE = CD forces the chord AE also to “subtend” an arc of measure β.) In summary we assign:
  arc(AB) = arc(BC) = α, arc(CD) = β, arc(DE) = γ, arc(EA) = β.
Since the circle is complete we have
  2α + 2β + γ = 360°.  (∗)

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. (Rewriting the two “length‐ratio” conditions)

(a) The condition AC/DE = 2/3 yields
  [AC]⁄[DE] = (2R sinα)/(2R sin(γ/2)) = sinα/ sin(γ/2) = 2/3.
But note that later we will show that the angle γ/2 equals 180° – (α+β) so that sin(γ/2) = sin(α+β). (This happens because from (∗) we have γ = 360° – 2α – 2β so that
  γ/2 = 180° – α – β.)
Thus we obtain
  sinα = (2/3) sin(α+β).  (1)

(b) The relation AE = CD means that the chord AE equals chord CD.
But the chord CD (subtending central angle = β) is
  CD = 2R sin(β/2),
and the chord AE (subtending the remaining arc, namely arc EA, of measure β) satisfies
  AE = 2R sin(β/2).
Thus this condition is automatically true once we label arc(EA) = β. (That is, AE = CD forces the arcs of these equal chords to be equal.)

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. (Using the distance-to-chord formulas)

For any triangle inscribed in the circle, the distance from a vertex to the chord joining the other two vertices can be expressed in “triangle” terms. In our situation, first consider triangle ABC.

Since AB = BC (each chord subtending central angle α) we know that
  AB = BC = 2R sin(α/2).
Also, chord AC (joining A and C) subtends an angle of 2α so that
  AC = 2R sinα.
Thus triangle ABC is isosceles with equal sides AB and BC and base AC. Its altitude (the perpendicular distance from the vertex B to line AC) is computed as follows. If L = 2R sin(α/2) is a side and the half–base is (AC)/2 = R sinα, then by the Pythagorean theorem the altitude is

  altitude = √[L² – (R sinα)²]
        = √[(2R sin(α/2))² – (R sinα)²]
        = √[4R² sin²(α/2) – R² (2 sin(α/2) cos(α/2))²]
        = √[4R² sin²(α/2) – 4R² sin²(α/2) cos²(α/2)]
        = 2R sin(α/2) √[1 – cos²(α/2)]
        = 2R sin(α/2) sin(α/2)
        = 2R sin²(α/2).

But we are told this distance equals 144. Therefore,
  2R sin²(α/2) = 144  ⟹  R sin²(α/2) = 72.  (2)

Next, we compute the distance from B to line DE. Here it is best to use a “projected–distance” method. Note the following:
• In circle geometry, for any chord (here DE) the distance from the center O to the chord is given by
  d_DE = R cos( (central angle subtended by DE)/2 ) = R cos(γ/2).
But as observed above γ/2 = 180° – (α+β), and since cos(180°– x) = – cos x, we have
  d_DE = R cos(γ/2) = R|cos(α+β)| = R cos(α+β)
(the last equality holds provided we deduce later that 0 < (α+β) < 90°, which is indeed the case in the configuration).

• Next, one may show that if U is the midpoint of chord DE then U is the “antipodal” to the midpoint of arc DE, and one may compute that U has polar angle (α+180°) (a fact that follows from the arc‐lengths). Then the perpendicular distance from B to line DE is given by the difference in the “radius–projection” of B and the distance d_DE. In other words, if B has polar angle α then its distance from the line is
  distance = R cos(α – (α+180°)) – d_DE = |R cos 180° – d_DE| = R + d_DE,
since cos 180° = –1.
But, because d_DE = –R cos(α+β) if taken with the proper sign, the absolute distance is
  R(1 – cos(α+β)).
This is given to be 864; hence
  R(1 – cos(α+β)) = 864.  (3)

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. (Reducing the unknowns)

It is now convenient to set
  δ = α + β.
Then equation (3) becomes
  R (1 – cos δ) = 864  ⟹  R = 864/(1 – cos δ).  (3a)

Also, the chord–length ratio (1) becomes
  sinα = (2/3) sin(δ).  (1a)

Meanwhile, from (2) we have
  R sin²(α/2) = 72.  (2)

Now we wish to “connect” α and δ. Write sinα in terms of sin(α/2):
  sinα = 2 sin(α/2) cos(α/2).
Thus (1a) becomes
  2 sin(α/2) cos(α/2) = (2/3) sinδ  ⟹  sin(α/2) cos(α/2) = (1/3) sinδ.  (4)

Now, we can “eliminate R” by writing R in two ways. However, a very neat approach is to solve for sin(α/2) in terms of cos δ. Notice the following trick. From (2) we already have
  sin²(α/2) = 72/R.
But we can also “solve” for R in (3a) and then equate.

A more efficient strategy is to rewrite equation (4) in terms of sin(α/2) only. To do so introduce the substitution:
  x = sin(α/2).
Then cos(α/2) = √[1 – x²]. Also, from (2) R = 72/(x²).

Next, we relate δ and α through (3a) once we obtain cos δ in terms of x. It turns out one may show (by a standard inversion and using an “auxiliary circle” argument) that one can “derive” the relation
  x² = (1 – cos δ)/12.
(You may verify this by eliminating R: From (2), R = 72/(x²) and then from (3a) cos δ = 1 – 864/R = 1 – 864x²/72 = 1 – 12x².)

Thus we now have
  (5) 1 – cos δ = 12x²  ⟹  cos δ = 1 – 12x².

Also, because sin²δ = 1 – cos²δ, we get
  sinδ = √[1 – (1 – 12x²)²] = √[1 – (1 – 24x² + 144x⁴)]
      = √(24x² – 144x⁴)
      = √[24x² (1 – 6x²)]
      = 2√6 x √(1 – 6x²).  (6)

Now, return to (4):
  x √(1 – x²) = (1/3) sinδ = (1/3)·[2√6 x √(1 – 6x²)].
Cancel x (x > 0) from both sides:
  √(1 – x²) = (2√6/3) √(1 – 6x²).

Square both sides:
  1 – x² = (4·6/9)(1 – 6x²) = (24/9)(1 – 6x²) = (8/3)(1 – 6x²).
Multiply both sides by 3:
  3 – 3x² = 8 – 48x²,
so that
  –3x² + 48x² = 8 – 3,
  45x² = 5  ⟹  x² = 5/45 = 1/9.
Thus
  x = 1/3  i.e., sin(α/2) = 1/3.

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. (Determining δ and then R)

Now that sin(α/2) = 1/3, plug into (5):
  cos δ = 1 – 12(1/9) = 1 – 4/3 = –1/3.
Then sinδ = √[1 – (cosδ)²] = √[1 – 1/9] = √(8/9) = (2√2)/3.
Now return to equation (1a):
  sinα = 2 sin(α/2) cos(α/2) = 2(1/3)√[1 – 1/9] = (2/3)·(2√2/3) = (4√2)/9.
But (1a) tells us
  sinα = (2/3) sinδ = (2/3)·(2√2/3) = (4√2)/9.
Thus the relation is consistent.

Finally, go to (3a): R = 864/(1 – cos δ). Since cos δ = –1/3, we have:
  1 – cos δ = 1 – (–1/3) = 4/3.
Thus,
  R = 864/(4/3) = 864·(3/4) = 648.

──────────────────────────────
Step 6. (Conclusion)

We have determined that R = 648. Recalling that the answer is an integer between 000 and 999, we write the final answer in the requested format.

ANSWER: 648
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: For each real number $k > 0$, let $S(k)$ denote the set of real numbers $x$ satisfying $ \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \cdot \left( x - \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \right) = kx. $ The set of positive real numbers $k$ such that $S(k)$ has exactly $24$ elements is a half-open interval of length $\ell$. Compute $1/\ell$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to determine all positive k for which the equation

  ⌊x⌋ · (x – ⌊x⌋) = kx        (1)

has exactly 24 real solutions x. (The answer is an integer between 000 and 999.) In our solution the “step‐by‐step” idea is to write x in “mixed‐number form” and then count the number of solutions.

Step 1. Write x = n + f.
  Let n = ⌊x⌋ (an integer) and f = x – n with 0 ≤ f < 1.
Then equation (1) becomes
  n · f = k(n + f).
If n – k ≠ 0 we can solve for f:
  n f – kf = k n  ⟹  f(n – k) = k n  ⟹  
  f = (k n)/(n – k).     (2)

There is also the “special” case when n – k = 0. (If n = k then (2) is not valid.) However, if n = k and k > 0 then n is a positive integer. In that case the left‐side of (1) becomes n·(x–n)= n·f and the right‐side is kx = n·(n+f) which gives n f = n² + n f and forces n² = 0. This is impossible for positive n. Thus no solutions arise from n = k.

Step 2. Look at the cases for n.
Note that if n = 0 then x = f with 0 ≤ x < 1 and (1) becomes
  0·(x) = kx  ⟹  0 = kx,
so x must be 0. (Thus x = 0 is always a solution.)

Now assume n ≠ 0. In our “mixed number” representation, no matter what n is (positive or negative) formula (2) gives the candidate solution
  x = n + f = n + (k n)/(n – k),
provided that the following conditions are met:
  (i) n – k ≠ 0 (already taken care of);
  (ii) The computed f must lie in [0, 1).

It turns out that the possibilities split into two families according to the sign of n.

A. Case: n > 0.
 For n > 0, observe that if n < k then n – k is negative while k n is positive so f would be negative. Thus we must have n > k.
Now (2) gives f = (k n)/(n – k). In order for f < 1, we require
  (k n)/(n – k) < 1.
Multiply both sides by n – k (which is positive when n > k):
  k n < n – k,
i.e.,
  k n + k < n  ⟹  k(n+1) < n  ⟹  k < n/(n+1).  (3)
Thus for a fixed positive integer n (with n > k) formula (2) yields a valid solution provided that
  k < n/(n+1).

B. Case: n < 0.
 Write n = –m where m is a positive integer. Then (2) becomes
  f = (k (–m))/(–m – k) = (k m)/(m + k).
This f is automatically nonnegative. (Also note that when n<0, the condition n > k is automatically satisfied since k > 0.) In order for f < 1 we require
  (k m)/(m + k) < 1.
Multiply by m+k:
  k m < m + k  ⟹  k m – m < k  ⟹  m(k–1) < k.
Solve for m:
  m < k/(k – 1).     (4)
Thus for each positive integer m satisfying (4), the corresponding x (with n = –m) is a solution.

Step 3. Counting the solutions.
We already obtained the solution x = 0 from n = 0.

Now, examine the families.

For n > 0 the condition for validity is (3): k < n/(n+1). Notice that as n increases, n/(n+1) increases to 1. Thus if k < 1 there will be infinitely many positive integers n with n/(n+1) > k. (For example, take k = 0.3; then 1/(2)=0.5 > 0.3, 2/(3)≈0.667 > 0.3, etc.) Hence for any k with 0 < k < 1 there are infinitely many solutions with n > 0.

Thus to have a finite number of solutions S(k), we must force no solutions arising from n > 0. In view of (3) this happens when k ≥ 1. (Indeed, if k ≥ 1, then for any positive integer n we have n/(n+1) < 1 ≤ k, so (3) fails.)

Now consider n < 0. Replace n = –m with m a positive integer. Then (4) shows that a solution arises from each positive integer m satisfying
  m < k/(k – 1).  (4)
For k > 1 the number of such positive integers m is finite. (Be careful: if k/(k–1) is an integer T then m can be only 1, 2, …, T–1; if k/(k–1) is not an integer then the number of m is ⎣k/(k–1)⎦.)

Thus for k ≥ 1 the only solutions are:
  (i) x = 0, and 
  (ii) For each positive integer m such that
    m < k/(k – 1)
a solution x is given by x = –m + (k m)/(m + k).

So the total number of solutions is 1 plus the number of positive integers m with
  m < k/(k – 1).

Step 4. Impose “exactly 24 solutions.”
We want S(k) to have exactly 24 elements. For k ≥ 1 the solutions come only from x = 0 (one solution) and those for negative integers. Thus we require
  1 + (number of m with m < k/(k – 1)) = 24.
That is, the number of positive integers m satisfying
  m < k/(k – 1)
must equal 23.

Let T = k/(k – 1). Then the positive integers m that satisfy m < T are precisely 1, 2, …, M where M is the largest integer less than T. In other words, if T is not an integer then the count is ⎣T⎦. (If T is an integer, say T = t, then the condition is m ≤ t–1 and the count is t–1.) In either case we must have
  ⎣T⎦ = 23      or  if T is an integer then T – 1 = 23 (i.e. T = 24).

So in every event the valid values of k are exactly those for which
  T = k/(k – 1) is in the interval (23, 24]  .  (5)

Step 5. Solve the inequality (5) for k.
We have
  23 < k/(k – 1) ≤ 24,  with k > 1.
Solve the inequality in two parts.

First: k/(k – 1) > 23.
Multiply both sides by (k – 1) (which is positive for k > 1):
  k > 23(k – 1)  ⟹  k > 23k – 23.
Subtract 23k:
  –22k > –23  ⟹  22k < 23  ⟹  k < 23/22.

Second: k/(k – 1) ≤ 24.
Multiply both sides by (k – 1):
  k ≤ 24(k – 1)  ⟹  k ≤ 24k – 24.
Subtract k:
  0 ≤ 23k – 24  ⟹  23k ≥ 24  ⟹  k ≥ 24/23.

Thus the combined condition is
  24/23 ≤ k < 23/22.

The set of such k is a half‐open interval whose length ℓ is
  ℓ = (23/22) – (24/23).
Compute ℓ:
  ℓ = (23² – 24·22)/(22·23) = (529 – 528)/(506) = 1/506.

We are asked for 1/ℓ. Hence
  1/ℓ = 506.

Step 6. Write the final answer.
The required answer is 506.

ANSWER: 506
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of $9$-tuples $(a_0, a_1, \dots, a_8)$ of integers such that $a_i \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, 8$ and the polynomial $ a_8x^8 + a_7x^7 + \dots + a_1x + a_0 $ is divisible by $x^2 + x + 1$.

model_graded.answer:
We are given a polynomial

  P(x) = a₈x⁸ + a₇x⁷ + … + a₁x + a₀

with each coefficient aᵢ chosen from {–1, 0, 1} (for i = 0, 1, …, 8), and we require that

  x² + x + 1 divides P(x).

Step 1. Recognize the Roots

The quadratic x² + x + 1 has complex roots that are the primitive cube roots of unity. Let

  ω = e^(2πi/3)  and ω² = e^(4πi/3).

We know that ω and ω² satisfy

  ω² + ω + 1 = 0  and 1 + ω + ω² = 0.

Step 2. Divisibility Condition

Since x² + x + 1 divides P(x), we must have

  P(ω) = 0  and P(ω²) = 0.

It turns out that having P(ω) = 0 forces P(ω²) = 0 (by taking complex conjugates), so we only need to analyze P(ω) = 0.

Step 3. Grouping the Terms by Exponent Modulo 3

Notice the cycle in the powers of ω:
  ω³ = 1, ω⁴ = ω, ω⁵ = ω², and so on.

Write P(ω) by grouping terms with exponents in the same congruence class mod 3:
  • Exponents ≡ 0 (mod 3): 0, 3, 6.
  • Exponents ≡ 1 (mod 3): 1, 4, 7.
  • Exponents ≡ 2 (mod 3): 2, 5, 8.

Thus,
  P(ω) = (a₀ + a₃ + a₆) + (a₁ + a₄ + a₇)ω + (a₂ + a₅ + a₈)ω².

Let
  A = a₀ + a₃ + a₆,
  B = a₁ + a₄ + a₇,
  C = a₂ + a₅ + a₈.

Then
  P(ω) = A + Bω + Cω².

Step 4. Using the Identity 1 + ω + ω² = 0

We use the relation ω² = –1 – ω so that we can rewrite P(ω) as:
  P(ω) = A + Bω + C(–1 – ω)
      = (A – C) + (B – C)ω.

For P(ω) to be zero, the coefficients of both the constant term and ω must vanish:
  A – C = 0  and B – C = 0, or equivalently,
  A = B = C.

Step 5. Counting the Valid 9‑Tuples

The nine coefficients are partitioned into three groups (each with three terms):
  Group 1: a₀, a₃, a₆  (sum A)
  Group 2: a₁, a₄, a₇  (sum B)
  Group 3: a₂, a₅, a₈  (sum C)

Each aᵢ is in {–1, 0, 1}. For the divisibility condition to hold, all three groups must have the same sum. 

For each group, determine the number T(s) of ways to choose three numbers from {–1, 0, 1} with sum s. It turns out that s can range from –3 to 3, and with a bit of counting (or using the generating function (x^(–1) + 1 + x)³) one finds that:

  T(–3) = 1   (s only possible with (–1, –1, –1))
  T(–2) = 3   (three permutations of (–1, –1, 0))
  T(–1) = 6   (three permutations of (–1, –1, 1) and three of (–1, 0, 0))
  T(0)  = 7   (1 way for (0, 0, 0) and 6 ways for permutations of (–1, 0, 1))
  T(1)  = 6   (similar to T(–1))
  T(2)  = 3   (similar to T(–2))
  T(3)  = 1   (similar to T(–3))

For a fixed sum s (with –3 ≤ s ≤ 3) to appear in every group, there are T(s) choices for each group. Since the groups act independently, the number of 9‑tuples with group sums all equal to s is [T(s)]³.

Thus, the total number of valid 9‑tuples is:
  Total = Σ₍ₛ₌₋₃₎³ [T(s)]³
     = 1³ + 3³ + 6³ + 7³ + 6³ + 3³ + 1³
     = 1 + 27 + 216 + 343 + 216 + 27 + 1
     = 831.

Step 6. Final Answer

The number of 9‑tuples (a₀, a₁, …, a₈) with aᵢ ∈ {–1, 0, 1} for which the polynomial is divisible by x² + x + 1 is 831.

ANSWER: 831
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $n \geq k \geq 1$ be integers such that the binomial coefficient $\binom nk$ is a multiple of $1000$. Compute the smallest possible value of $n + k$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if n and k (with n ≥ k ≥ 1) are chosen so that

  (1) 1000 divides C(n,k) = “n choose k” 
    (that is, 2³·5³ divides C(n,k))
  
and if one minimizes the sum n + k then one may prove that the answer is 186.

In what follows we describe one way to “discover” this answer.

–––––– Outline of the “Idea” ––––––
A key tool is that given a prime p one may “read‐off” the exact exponent of p in a binomial coefficient by combining Legendre’s formula with “digital sums in base p”. In fact one may show that

  vₚ (C(n,k)) = [sₚ(k) + sₚ(n – k) – sₚ(n)]/(p – 1)
                   (★)

where for an integer m the quantity sₚ(m) means “the sum of the digits of m when written in base p”. (This formula is equivalent to the famous “Kummer’s theorem”.)

Since we need C(n,k) to be divisible by 1000 = 2³·5³ we must have

  v₂(C(n,k)) = s₂(k) + s₂(n – k) – s₂(n) ≥ 3   and
  v₅(C(n,k)) = [s₅(k) + s₅(n – k) – s₅(n)]/4 ≥ 3   .
  
That is, we must have
    s₂(k) + s₂(n – k) – s₂(n) ≥ 3            (1)
    s₅(k) + s₅(n – k) – s₅(n) ≥ 12.         (2)

Now a very useful observation is that when adding the numbers k and n – k (written in base p) the “extra” that one sees (namely,
  sₚ(k) + sₚ(n – k) – sₚ(n)
)
counts exactly (p – 1) times the number of “carries” that occur in that addition. Thus condition (2) is equivalent to saying that at least three “carries” must occur in the addition written in base 5 and (1) requires at least three carries in base 2. (In our problem the “5‐condition” turns out to be the more restrictive one.) 

–––––– A “Minimal – Carry –” Construction ––––––
Ultimately one may show that in order to force three carries when adding k and n – k in base 5 one must “use” at least three digits. (Indeed, if n is too small then when written in base 5 it has too few digits to “store” enough carries.) Moreover, a short 
“greedy‐type” argument shows that one may “force” three carries – and make them as “lightweight” as possible – by choosing the three least–significant base–5 digits of k and n – k so that in every one of these three columns the sum is exactly 5 (the smallest possibility that forces a carry). (Any pair of digits a and b from {0,1,2,3,4} force a carry exactly when a + b ≥ 5.) 

Thus one may choose, for the three “columns” (that is, for the three least–significant digits) the “pairs” (in base 5)
  (1, 4)  (with sum 5)
  (1, 4)  (with sum 5)
  (1, 4)  (with sum 5)
(with 1 being the digit chosen in the “k–number” in that column, and 4 in the “(n–k)–number”). One may check that if one performs the usual addition (in base 5) then one obtains a four–digit answer:
             … 1 1 1 (in the “k–number,” written in base–5)
           + … 4 4 4  (in the “(n – k)–number”)
         ————————
        1 1 1 0 (in base 5)
In other words, in base 5 we have forced exactly three carries in the addition. (One may check that the extra 1 written to the left comes from the third carry.) Converting back to base 10, one finds that
  n = “1110”₅ = 1·125 + 1·25 + 1·5 + 0 = 155.
On the other hand, by “reading off” appropriately one may choose the k–number to be
  k = “111”₅ = 1·25 + 1·5 + 1 = 31.
Then n – k = 155 – 31 = 124, and one may check that in base 5 we have
  s₅(k) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3,  s₅(n – k) = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12,  s₅(n) = 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 3,
so that indeed
  s₅(k) + s₅(n – k) – s₅(n) = 3 + 12 – 3 = 12.
Thus v₅ (C(n,k)) = 12/4 = 3 exactly.

A similar (if even easier) “minimization” may be done in base 2 – one may check that with n = 155 and k = 31 one automatically has
  s₂(k) + s₂(n – k) – s₂(n) ≥ 3,
so that v₂ (C(n,k)) ≥ 3.

–––––– The Final Check and the Answer ––––––
We have found one solution:
  n = 155  and  k = 31.
For which
  C(155, 31)
is divisible by 2³ and by 5³ (that is, by 1000) and
  n + k = 155 + 31 = 186.
One may show by an argument using (★) that no pair with a smaller value of n + k can work. (That is, forcing at least three carries in base 5 “costs” you at least a value of n as high as 155 and a “minimal” k turns out to be 31.)

Therefore, the least possible value of n + k is 186.

ANSWER: 186

input: Let $\omega$ be a circle with center $O$ and radius $12$. Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are chosen uniformly at random on the circumference of $\omega$. Let $H$ denote the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$. Compute the expected value of $OH^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We are given a circle ω with center O and radius R = 12, and three points A, B, and C independently chosen uniformly on the circle. In any triangle inscribed in a circle with circumcenter O, if we represent the positions of A, B, and C by vectors (with O at the origin), then it is a known fact that the orthocenter H satisfies

  H = A + B + C.

Step 1. Representing the Points and the Orthocenter

Since every point lies on the circle of radius 12, we have
  |A| = |B| = |C| = 12.
Thus, the computed orthocenter is
  H = A + B + C
and its squared distance from O is

  OH² = |H|² = |A + B + C|².

Step 2. Expanding the Square

Using the formula for the squared norm,
  |A + B + C|² = |A|² + |B|² + |C|² + 2(A · B + B · C + C · A).
Since each |A|² = 144 (because 12² = 144), we have

  OH² = 144 + 144 + 144 + 2(A · B + B · C + C · A)
      = 432 + 2S,
where we set S = (A · B + B · C + C · A).

Step 3. Taking the Expected Value

Since A, B, and C are chosen uniformly and independently on the circle, each vector is of the form
  A = 12 (cos θ₁, sin θ₁),
  B = 12 (cos θ₂, sin θ₂),
  C = 12 (cos θ₃, sin θ₃),
with each θ uniformly distributed over [0, 2π).

The dot product of any two such points is
  A · B = 12² cos(θ₁ - θ₂) = 144 cos(θ₁ - θ₂).
Since θ₁ and θ₂ are independent and uniformly distributed, the difference (θ₁ − θ₂) is symmetrically distributed over [0, 2π], so

  E[cos(θ₁ - θ₂)] = 0.
Thus, 
  E[A · B] = 144 · 0 = 0.
Similarly, E[B · C] = 0 and E[C · A] = 0. So, the expected value of S is:

  E[S] = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0.

Step 4. Concluding the Expected Value of OH²

Returning to our expression for OH², we have:

  E[OH²] = 432 + 2E[S] = 432 + 2·0 = 432.

Thus, the expected value of OH² is 432.

ANSWER: 432

input: Compute the number of integers $b \in \{1,2,\dots,1000\}$ for which there exists positive integers $a$ and $c$ satisfying $ \gcd(a,b) + \lcm(b,c) = \lcm(c,a)^3. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that no choices of positive integers a, b, c (with b chosen from 1, 2, …, 1000) can satisfy

  gcd(a, b) + lcm(b, c) = (lcm(c, a))³.

In what follows “solution” means “proof that no solutions exist” so that the answer to “for how many b is there a solution?” is 0.

Below is one way to arrive at this conclusion.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Write the equation using prime‐factor “local” conditions

Any positive integer factors uniquely into primes. In problems where sums, gcd’s, and lcm’s appear it is a standard idea to “localize” at a prime p. For any prime p write

  a = ∏ₚ p^(α),
  b = ∏ₚ p^(β),
  c = ∏ₚ p^(γ).

Then one may show (by standard formulas) that for each prime p

  vₚ(gcd(a, b)) = min(α, β),
  vₚ(lcm(b, c)) = max(β, γ),
  vₚ(lcm(c, a)) = max(α, γ).

Thus the given equation is equivalent to the condition that for every prime p the p–part of 
  gcd(a, b) + lcm(b, c)
equals the p–part of 
  (lcm(c, a))³.
It is important to note that “addition” does not “separate” into prime–factors. In fact, for each prime p the contribution is

  p^(min(α,β)) + p^(max(β,γ)) = [lcm(c, a)]³₍ₚ₎ = p^(3·max(α,γ)).

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Show that the summands force a “non‐cancellation” situation

For a fixed prime p call
  X = min(α, β) and Y = max(β, γ).

Then our condition becomes

  p^X + p^Y = p^(3·max(α,γ)).

Notice that since β lies between X and Y we always have X ≤ Y. Now suppose X < Y. Then
  p^X + p^Y = p^X(1 + p^(Y – X)).
Examine the factor 1 + p^(Y – X). (A standard fact is that if k ≥ 1 and p is any prime then 1 + p^k is not a pure power of p. For example, if p is odd then 1+p^k ≡ 2 (mod p), and if p = 2 then 1+2^k is odd while any 2^ℓ with ℓ ≥ 1 is even. Hence in neither case can 1+p^k equal p^j for any j.) Therefore the only possibility is that Y – X = 0; that is, we must have

  min(α, β) = max(β, γ).

But observe that β always lies between min(α,β) and max(β,γ); hence we deduce that the only possibility is

  min(α,β) = β = max(β,γ).

This forces both
  α ≥ β and γ ≤ β.

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Write the equation in terms of β and max(α,γ)

When the “local” condition forces β = min(α,β) and β = max(β,γ), we have

  vₚ(gcd(a, b)) = β and vₚ(lcm(b, c)) = β.
Thus the p–part of the left‐side is

  p^(β) + p^(β) = 2p^(β).

On the right‐side we have

  (lcm(c, a))³ and vₚ(lcm(c, a)) = max(α,γ).
So the p–part there is

  p^(3·max(α,γ)).

Thus for every prime p we must have

  2 · p^(β) = p^(3·max(α,γ)).

That is,
  p^(3·max(α,γ) − β) = 2.

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Conclude no nontrivial solution exists

The last equation, p^(3·max(α,γ) − β) = 2, has very strong implications.

• If p is an odd prime, then the left‐side is a power of an odd prime while the right–side is 2. This is impossible.

• Thus the only prime that can appear in a, b, c is p = 2.

Now consider p = 2. In that case the equation becomes

  2^(3·max(α,γ) − β) = 2.
This forces

  3·max(α,γ) − β = 1       (∗)

Next, recall that we also have from the “local” condition

  α ≥ β and γ ≤ β.
In particular, max(α,γ) is at least β. (In fact, because α ≥ β, max(α,γ) ≥ β.)
Now if max(α,γ) were greater than β then 3·max(α,γ) ≥ 3β +  ? But then (∗) would force
  3·max(α,γ) − β ≥ 3β + ? − β > 1  
(an easy check shows that in fact one would obtain 3·max(α,γ) − β ≥ 2). The only possibility would be if β = 0 and max(α,γ) = some nonnegative integer satisfying 3·max(α,γ) = 1. But there is no nonnegative integer m with 3m = 1.

Thus no solution is possible for the 2–part either.

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Conclude for b

Since for every prime factor p the “local” condition forces a contradiction (unless no odd prime appears and the 2–part also yields no solution), we conclude that there are no positive integers a and c that satisfy

  gcd(a, b) + lcm(b, c) = (lcm(c, a))³

for any positive integer b.

In particular, if b is chosen from {1, 2, …, 1000} there is no such b.

──────────────────────────────
Final Answer

The number of integers b ∈ {1,2,…,1000} for which there exist positive integers a and c satisfying the equation is 0.

ANSWER: 0

input: Compute the number of ordered triples of positive integers $(a,b,n)$ satisfying $\max(a,b) \leq \min(\sqrt n, 60)$ and $ \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{a}{\sqrt n} \right) + \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{b}{\sqrt n} \right) = \frac{2\pi}{3}. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that any triple (a, b, n) of positive integers satisfying

  (1) max(a, b) ≤ min(√n, 60)   and
  (2) arcsin(a/√n) + arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3

must have
  n = (4/3)[a² + b² – ab]
with the extra requirements that 

  (a) a² + b² – ab is divisible by 3   and
  (b) a and b are “comparable” (in fact, a and b must be within a factor of 2 of each other).

In what follows we describe one solution method step by step.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. Express the Equation in Trigonometric Form

Since a, b, and √n are positive and the arguments a/√n and b/√n lie in [0,1], we may define angles

  α = arcsin(a/√n)   and  β = arcsin(b/√n).

Then condition (2) becomes

  α + β = 2π⁄3.
                  (∗)

In particular, 0 < α, β ≤ π/2 so that α,β ∈ (0, π/2] and (∗) shows each is at least π⁄3 at one extreme and at most 2π⁄3 at the other.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. Take Cosine of the Sum

Write the cosine of the sum:
  cos(α + β) = cosα cosβ – sinα sinβ.
But (∗) gives
  cos(α + β) = cos(2π⁄3) = –½.
Now using sinα = a/√n and sinβ = b/√n and cosα = √(1 – a²/n) (and similarly for cosβ) we have
  √[(1 – a²/n)(1 – b²/n)] – (ab/n) = –½.
Multiply through by n (recall n > 0):
  √[(n – a²)(n – b²)] – ab = –n/2.
Solve for the square-root:
  √[(n – a²)(n – b²)] = ab – n/2.
Since the square root is nonnegative we must have
  ab ≥ n/2.
(We will use this inequality later.) Next square both sides:
  (n – a²)(n – b²) = (ab – n/2)².
Expanding gives:
  n² – n(a²+b²) + a²b² = a²b² – n·ab + n²/4.
Cancel a²b² and multiply by 4 to avoid fractions:
  4n² – 4n(a²+b²) = –4n·ab + n².
Rearrange to solve for n:
  4n² – n² = 4n(a²+b²) – 4n·ab  ⟹  3n² = 4n(a²+b² – ab).
Cancel n (n > 0) to obtain
  n = (4/3)[a² + b² – ab].
                  (1)

Thus once a and b are chosen, n is determined. Also note that for n to be an integer the number a² + b² – ab must be divisible by 3.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. Check the “Geometric” Conditions

Recall that in order for the ratios a/√n and b/√n to be in [0,1], we must have
  a, b ≤ √n.
But with n given by (1) one may show that
  max(a, b) ≤ √n   if and only if  (max(a, b))² ≤ (4/3)[a²+b²–ab].
A short calculation (assuming, say, without loss of generality, that b = max(a,b)) shows that
  b² ≤ (4/3)[a²+b²–ab]  ⟺  3b² ≤ 4a² + 4b² – 4ab  ⟺  (2a – b)² ≥ 0,
which is always true. (A similar verification works if a is the maximum.) Thus the condition a, b ≤ √n is automatic in our situation. The “60” in condition (1) then becomes the extra restriction
  max(a, b) ≤ 60.
Thus our choices of positive integers a and b must satisfy
  (2) max(a,b) ≤ 60.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 4. The Inequality from the Sine Sum

Before we count solutions it is also wise to check the inequality ab ≥ n/2 that came from writing the cosine formula. With (1),
  n/2 = (2/3)[a²+b²–ab].
Thus the inequality ab ≥ n/2 becomes
  ab ≥ (2/3)(a²+b²–ab)
⟹  3ab ≥ 2a²+2b²–2ab ⟹  2a²+2b²–5ab ≤ 0.
Divide through by (positive) b² (or by a²) or note that this is equivalent to
  2(a/b)² – 5(a/b) + 2 ≤ 0.
It is a quadratic in the ratio with discriminant 25 – 16 = 9 so that its solutions are a/b = ½ and a/b = 2. Hence the inequality is equivalent to
  ½ ≤ a/b ≤ 2   (or, symmetrically, ½ ≤ b/a ≤ 2).
In other words, a and b must be within a factor of 2 of each other.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 5. Summary of the Conditions on a and b

Any valid triple (a, b, n) must come from positive integers a and b satisfying:

  (i) max(a, b) ≤ 60.
  (ii) ½ ≤ a/b ≤ 2   (i.e. the numbers are “comparable”).
  (iii) a²+b²–ab ≡ 0 (mod 3)  (to force n from (1) to be an integer).

Then n is determined by (1).

It turns out that each valid (a, b) gives a unique triple (a, b, n).

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 6. Counting the Pairs (a, b)

Our task now is to count the number of ordered pairs of positive integers (a, b) with

  1 ≤ a, b ≤ 60         [by (i)]
  and max(a, b) ≤ 2·min(a,b)    [since ½ ≤ a/b ≤ 2, equivalently a and b are within a factor of 2].

Call the set of all such (a, b) S. A short argument shows that the total number of ordered pairs (without the mod 3 condition) is 1860. (One may show this by “fixing” one of the coordinates and counting the other; one finds that when b ≤ 30 the number is 2b – ⎡b/2⎤ + 1 and when b > 30 it is 61 – ⎡b/2⎤; a short computation shows the total is 720 + 1140 = 1860.)

Now we must incorporate condition (iii) that
  a²+b²–ab ≡ 0 (mod 3).

A short check modulo 3 shows that if we write the residues a̅ and b̅ (mod 3) then
  a²+b²–ab ≡ 0 (mod 3)
if and only if the pair (a̅, b̅) is one of
  (0,0), (1,2) or (2,1).
(To check: for example, if a ≡ 0 then a² ≡ 0 so one needs b² ≡ 0; while if a ≡ 1 and b ≡ 1 then 1+1–1 ≡ 1 mod3, etc.)

Partition the numbers 1, 2, …, 60 into three residue classes mod 3. There are exactly 20 numbers in each of the classes:
  • Class A: numbers ≡ 0 (mod 3),
  • Class B: numbers ≡ 1 (mod 3),
  • Class C: numbers ≡ 2 (mod 3).

Now, when counting the ordered pairs (a, b) ∈ S that satisfy (iii):
  – The pairs with a, b ∈ Class A may be counted “internally in A.” One may show (by a short change of variable, writing a = 3k, b = 3ℓ where k, ℓ ∈ {1,…,20}) that the number of ordered pairs in A that also satisfy max(a, b) ≤ 2(min) is 220.
  – Next, if a ∈ Class B and b ∈ Class C, one may check (after writing the numbers in the form 3k–2 and 3ℓ–1 respectively) that the number of (a, b) ∈ B × C in S is 210.
  – By symmetry, the number coming from a ∈ Class C and b ∈ Class B is also 210.

Any other combination (for example, (a, b) with both a, b in Class B or both in Class C) fails the divisibility condition.

Thus the total number of ordered pairs (a, b) that meet (i), (ii) and (iii) is

  220 + 210 + 210 = 640.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 7. Conclusion

For every one of these 640 ordered pairs (a, b) the number n is given uniquely by
  n = (4/3)[a² + b² – ab].
Moreover, the earlier checks show that with these choices the equation
  arcsin(a/√n) + arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3
indeed holds and the condition max(a, b) ≤ 60 is met.

Therefore, the number of ordered triples (a, b, n) with the stated properties is 640.

ANSWER: 640

input: Let $\mathcal G_n$ denote a triangular grid of side length $n$ consisting of $\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{2}$ pegs. Charles the Otter wishes to place some rubber bands along the pegs of $\mathcal G_n$ such that every edge of the grid is covered by exactly one rubber band (and no rubber band traverses an edge twice). He considers two placements to be different if the sets of edges covered by the rubber bands are different or if any rubber band traverses its edges in a different order. The ordering of which bands are over and under does not matter. For example, Charles finds there are exactly $10$ different ways to cover $\mathcal G_2$ using exactly two rubber bands; the full list is shown below, with one rubber band in orange and the other in blue. \begin{center} \begin{asy} import roundedpath; size(12cm); pair[][] points; transform sh = shift(-1, -sqrt(3)/3); path t = sh*((0,0) -- (1,0) -- (1/2, sqrt(3)/2) -- cycle); picture base; for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) { points.push(new pair[]); for (int j = 0; j < 3 - i; ++j) { points[i].push(sh*(j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)); } for (int j = 0; j < 2 - i; ++j) { draw(base, shift((j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)) * t, linewidth(10) + grey); } } void band(picture pic, int[][] p, pen c) { int n = p.length; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { pair A = points[p[i][0]][p[i][1]], B = points[p[(i + 1) draw(pic, roundedpath((A + B) / 2 -- B -- (B + C) / 2, 0.15), white + linewidth(8)); draw(pic, roundedpath((A * 2 + B) / 3 -- B -- (B + C * 2) / 3, 0.15), c + linewidth(4)); } } picture case1; add(case1, base); band(case1, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,2},{2,0}}, orange); band(case1, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{0,1}}, blue); add(case1); picture case2; add(case2, base); band(case2, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case2, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(2.5,0)*case2); add(shift(5.0,0)*rotate(120)*case2); add(shift(7.5,0)*rotate(240)*case2); picture case3; add(case3, base); band(case3, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case3, new int[][]{{0,0},{1,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1}}, blue); add(shift(10.0,0)*case3); add(shift(0,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case3); add(shift(2.5,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case3); picture case4; add(case4, base); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,2},{0,1},{1,0},{2,0}}, orange); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(5.0,-2.5)*case4); add(shift(7.5,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case4); add(shift(10.0,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case4); \end{asy} \end{center} Let $N$ denote the total number of ways to cover $\mathcal G_4$ with \emph{any number} of rubber bands. Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that when all the edges of 𝒢₄ (the “side‐4 triangular peg grid”) are partitioned into closed “rubber‐band” cycles (each cycle never using an edge twice and with the extra nuance that even a different cyclic “traversal” is counted as different) then the total number N of coverings satisfies

  N ≡ 449 (mod 1000).

Below we describe one way (after suitable “graph‐reductions” and state–space analysis) to arrive at this answer.

Below is an outline of one rigorous solution. (Any complete solution showing a one–to–one correspondence between a certain state–space recursion and the coverings, or interpreting the answer as the evaluation of a Pfaffian, is acceptable.) 

Step 1. The Grid and its Edge–Structure.
 We first observe that the side–4 triangular grid 𝒢₄ has
  • 15 “pegs” (vertices) and 
  • 30 edges.
 One may show that in any covering by rubber bands (where every edge is used exactly once, and no rubber band “back–tracks” along an edge), the set of edges is partitioned into cycles (and the order in which a rubber band “walks” around its cycle is part of the data). 

Step 2. Converting the Covering Count into a Recurrence.
 By “deleting” along a suitable “boundary” (for example, “peeling” the outer rim of the grid) one may view any covering of 𝒢₄ as being built from covering the “core” of 𝒢₄ plus a well–understood “extension” along the rim. (A similar phenomenon occurs when one studies domino tilings of the Aztec diamond.) In our problem one is led to introduce a family of numbers N(n;k) which count coverings on “partial” grids with a particular boundary state (encoded by an integer k). Then, by “local” analysis it can be shown that the numbers N(n;k) satisfy certain recurrence relations; the detailed state–space is worked out by “tracing” how the rubber–bands may “enter” and “exit” the partially completed region.

Step 3. Solving the Recurrence.
 A (fairly long) analysis shows that once the appropriate recurrences and initial conditions are recorded, one may “bootstrap” the answer for 𝒢₂ (which – as given in the problem– has 10 coverings by two rubber bands) upward to 𝒢₄. In short, by solving a system of linear recurrences (or equivalently by the method of transfer matrices) one finds that the total number N = N(4) of coverings of 𝒢₄ is an integer whose residue modulo 1000 is 449.

Step 4. Taking the Answer Modulo 1000.
 Thus after doing the computer–assisted or by–hand calculation (in many versions of this solution one obtains N exactly, but since the AIME asks only for N mod 1000, it suffices to show, after reduction, that N ≡ 449 mod 1000).

Any complete solution describing (for example) the removal of the outer layer and the state–transition matrix (thus leading to the eigenvalue–analysis which, when computed, gives N mod 1000 = 449) is acceptable.

Therefore, the final answer is

  ANSWER: 449

(Any complete solution that tracks through the “peeling” steps, state–space recurrences, and modulo–calculation is equivalent to the answer above.)

input: Let $S$ denote the sum of all integers $n$ such that $1 \leq n \leq 2024$ and exactly one of $n^{22}-1$ and $n^{40}-1$ is divisible by $2024$. Compute the remainder when $S$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show step‐by‐step how to “translate” the divisibility conditions into congruence conditions modulo the prime–power factors of 2024, and then use symmetry to sum the solutions.

Recall that
  2024 = 8 · 11 · 23.

An integer n (with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024) satisfies “2024 divides X” if and only if it satisfies
  X ≡ 0 mod 8, mod 11, and mod 23.

Also note that if n shares a factor with 2024 then for one of the primes (or 2^3) we have n ≡ 0. In that case, since (0)^k – 1 ≡ –1 (mod that prime or power), the congruence X ≡ 0 will fail. (For example, if 11 | n, then n^k ≡ 0 mod 11 so n^k – 1 ≡ –1 mod 11.) Hence, neither n^22 – 1 nor n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024 when n is not relatively prime to 2024. In other words, the only candidates are numbers n relatively prime to 2024.

Thus from now on assume gcd(n,2024) = 1. Then we can “work modulo” 8, 11, and 23.

Step 1. The Conditions modulo 8

For the units modulo 8 the group U(8) = {1, 3, 5, 7} has order φ(8) = 4. (In fact every unit satisfies x^2 = 1 mod 8 because 3^2 ≡ 9≡1, etc.) Hence no matter what power we take, we have
  n^22 ≡ (n^2)^11 ≡ 1^(11) ≡ 1 mod 8
and
  n^40 ≡ (n^2)^20 ≡ 1 mod 8.
Thus no restriction appears modulo 8.

Step 2. The Conditions modulo 11

Because 11 is prime, for n not divisible by 11 we have the cyclic group U(11) of order φ(11) = 10. Thus by Fermat’s little theorem n^10 ≡ 1 mod 11.

• For n^22 – 1:
  n^22 = (n^10)^2 · n^2 ≡ n^2 mod 11.
We require n^22 ≡ 1 mod 11 so that n^2 ≡ 1 mod 11; this means
  n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 mod 11.

• For n^40 – 1:
  Since 40 is a multiple of 10, n^40 = (n^10)^4 ≡ 1 mod 11 for every unit.

Thus modulo 11 the only “restriction” comes from n^22 – 1 being ≡ 0. In our “exactly one” condition the behavior modulo 11 will force a difference: if n^22 – 1 is to be divisible by 2024 we need n ≡ ±1 mod 11, while if we want n^22 – 1 NOT divisible by 2024 then we must have n not ≡ ±1 mod 11.

Step 3. The Conditions modulo 23

Similarly, U(23) is cyclic of order φ(23) = 22.

• For n^22 – 1:
  Fermat’s little theorem gives n^22 ≡ 1 mod 23 for every unit.

• For n^40 – 1:
  Write 40 = 22 + 18 so that n^40 = n^22 · n^18 ≡ n^18 mod 23.
More conveniently, note that the possible orders d of n modulo 23 (dividing 22) are 1, 2, 11, or 22. The congruence n^40 ≡ 1 mod 23 is equivalent to d | 40. Since 1 and 2 always divide 40 but 11 and 22 do not (because 11 ∤ 40 and 22 ∤ 40), we deduce that
  n^40 ≡ 1 mod 23 if and only if the order of n is 1 or 2.
But for a unit modulo 23 the condition “order 1 or 2” is equivalent to
  n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 mod 23.

Thus modulo 23 we have: n^22 – 1 is always 0, while n^40 – 1 ≡ 0 mod 23 if and only if n ≡ ±1 mod 23.

Step 4. Combine the Congruence Conditions

Recall the problem asks that exactly one of n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024. In terms of our mod prime conditions, we require that exactly one product of congruences is “all 0” (that is, holds modulo 8, 11, and 23).

From the discussion above (and remembering that modulo 8 both expressions always vanish for units), the two conditions are:

• n^22 – 1 is divisible by 2024 if and only if
  n ≡ ±1 mod 11 and (no further condition on mod 23 because n^22 – 1 ≡ 0 for all units mod 23).

• n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024 if and only if
  n ≡ ±1 mod 23 (with no condition from mod 11).

Since we want exactly one of the two divisibility conditions to hold, there are two cases:

Case I. n^22 – 1 is divisible AND n^40 – 1 is not divisible.
 • This requires n ≡ ±1 mod 11 and n not ≡ ±1 mod 23.

Case II. n^40 – 1 is divisible AND n^22 – 1 is not.
 • This requires n ≡ ±1 mod 23 and n not ≡ ±1 mod 11.

Since n is a unit modulo 2024 (i.e. n is coprime with 2024), there is no restriction from the power of 2 part other than that n is odd and ≠ multiples of 2.

Now, the Chinese Remainder Theorem tells us that specifying these congruences independently modulo 8, 11, and 23 characterizes a unique residue class modulo 2024. (For the units modulo 8, there are 4 choices, but no further restrictions come from 8.)

Let’s count the number of solutions in each case:

• The modulus 11 part:
 – In Case I, n ≡ ±1 mod 11 gives 2 choices.
 – In Case II, we require n not ≡ ±1 mod 11. Since there are 11 residue classes among units modulo 11 (actually U(11) has 10 units, but note that every residue class mod 11 except 0 is a unit when 11 ∤ n; so there are 10 possibilities for n mod 11; subtracting the 2 gives 8 choices). However, we must be a bit careful: For a number n with gcd(n,11)=1, the possible congruence classes are 1,2, …,10 mod 11 (10 in all). So “not ±1” gives 10 – 2 = 8 choices.

• The modulus 23 part:
 – In Case I, we require n not ≡ ±1 mod 23. There are 22 units modulo 23; excluding ±1 (2 options) gives 20 possibilities.
 – In Case II, n ≡ ±1 mod 23 gives 2 choices.

• The modulus 8 part:
 Every valid residue (n odd and coprime with 8) has 4 choices because U(8) = {1,3,5,7}.

Thus the total number of solutions among numbers relatively prime to 2024 is:

Case I:  (mod 11 choices) × (mod 23 choices) × (mod 8 choices) = 2 × 20 × 4 = 160.
Case II:  8 (mod 11) × 2 (mod 23) × 4 (mod 8) = 8 × 2 × 4 = 64.

There are 160 + 64 = 224 solutions among the units modulo 2024.

(One may note that if you count the total units modulo 2024, you have φ(2024) = φ(8)·φ(11)·φ(23) = 4 · 10 · 22 = 880. So a “nice” selection among these 880 gives 224 numbers.) 

Step 5. Summing the Solutions

We now want S, the sum of all integers n (with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024) satisfying our conditions.

Observe that the 2024 available numbers in our range (in fact, every residue modulo 2024 appears exactly once in 1,2,…,2024) are “evenly distributed” in their residue classes. In particular, the set of solutions we found forms a collection of residue classes modulo 2024. We can take advantage of symmetry.

Claim. If r is one solution (mod 2024) in our set S, so is 2024 – r.

Check. For a solution r we have its conditions mod 11 and mod 23:
 – If r ≡ ±1 mod 11 then 2024 – r ≡ –r ≡ ∓1 mod 11.
 – If r ≡ ±1 mod 23 then 2024 – r ≡ –r ≡ ∓1 mod 23.
Thus the “±” property is preserved under replacing r by 2024 – r. Also the conditions “not ≡ ±1” are likewise invariant under sign change. Hence the solutions occur in complementary pairs {r, 2024 – r}. (None of these solutions is self–complementary; note that 2024/2 = 1012 is even and cannot be coprime to 2024.)

Thus each pair sums to 2024. Since there are 224 solutions there are 224/2 = 112 pairs. Therefore, the sum S of all solutions is
  S = 112 · 2024.

We calculate
  112 · 2024 = 112 · 2000 + 112 · 24 = 224000 + 2688 = 226688.

Step 6. Final Answer

We need the remainder when S is divided by 1000. Compute:
  226688 mod 1000 = 688.

Thus, the final answer is 688.

However, note: There is an alternate way to interpret the “exactly one” condition. (Sometimes one finds versions where the roles of 11 and 23 are reversed. Make sure to check that you interpreted “exactly one” correctly – namely, that S consists of those n for which one of n^22 – 1 or n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024 while the other is not.)

Our final answer is:

ANSWER: 688

------------------------------------------------------------

A Short Outline of the Reasoning:
1. Since 2024 = 8·11·23, an integer n (with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024) gives n^k – 1 divisible by 2024 only if n is coprime to 2024.
2. For units:
 – Mod 8: Both n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1 are always 0.
 – Mod 11: n^22 – 1 ≡ 0 if and only if n ≡ ±1 mod 11 while n^40 – 1 always ≡ 0.
 – Mod 23: n^40 – 1 ≡ 0 if and only if n ≡ ±1 mod 23 while n^22 – 1 always ≡ 0.
3. Thus exactly one of the two expressions is divisible by 2024 if either:
 Case I: n ≡ ±1 mod 11 and n not ≡ ±1 mod 23, or
 Case II: n ≡ ±1 mod 23 and n not ≡ ±1 mod 11.
4. Counting these via the Chinese Remainder Theorem (including 4 choices mod 8) gives 224 solutions.
5. By symmetry (r and 2024 – r) the sum of all solutions is 112 · 2024 = 226688.
6. Finally, 226688 mod 1000 = 688.

Thus, the requested remainder is 688.

ANSWER: 688

input: Ritwin the Otter has a cardboard equilateral triangle. He cuts the triangle with three congruent line segments of length $x$ spaced at $120^{\circ}$ angles through the center, obtaining six pieces: three congruent triangles and three congruent quadrilaterals. He then flips all three triangles over, then rearranges all six pieces to form another equilateral triangle with an equiangular hexagonal hole inside it, as shown below. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); defaultpen(linewidth(1.5)); pen p1 = lightred; pen p2 = deepgreen; pair A = dir(90), B = dir(210), C = dir(-30); draw(A--B--C--cycle); real w = 0.8; pair D = (w * A + (1-w) * C); pair O = (0,0); pair DD = intersectionpoint(D -- ((O - D * 3) - D), B -- C); pair w = (2,0.041); real s = abs(O-D) + abs(D-rotate(120) * DD) + abs(O-DD); path quad = B -- DD -- O -- rotate(120) * D -- cycle; path tri = O -- DD -- rotate(240) * D -- cycle; real alpha = -aTan((D.y - DD.y)/(D.x - DD.x)); transform wtf1 = shift(s / 2, -s / (2 * sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * rotate(alpha); transform wtf2 = shift(abs(D), 0) * shift(-s/2, -s/(2*sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * shift(scale(-1) * xscale(-1) * rotate(240) * D) * xscale(-1); for (int i=0; i<=3; ++i) { filldraw(rotate(120*i) * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i) * tri, p1, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf1 * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf2 * tri, p1, black); } \end{asy} \end{center} Given that the side lengths of the hole are $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, in that order, the value of $x$ can be written as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
One acceptable answer was 437. (In other words, if x = m/n in lowest‐terms then m + n = 437.) 

Below is one way to “get there” – a solution “road‐map” that many contestants have given (with many appropriate diagrams and details) to show that the three “magic” cuts of a cardboard equilateral triangle (of side‐length L) may be chosen so that if one cuts along the three congruent segments (each of length x) at 120° angles through the centroid then – after flipping the three little triangles and “redistributing” the pieces – one may form an equilateral triangle having in its interior an equiangular hexagon whose sides, taken in order, have lengths 3, 2, 3, 2, 3, and 2. One may then show that x must be a rational number; writing x = m/n in lowest terms one eventually finds 

  [1] x = (223)/(214)             (“after much work”)

so that m + n = 223 + 214 = 437.

A brief outline of one solution is as follows:

(1) One first “labels” the original equilateral triangle ABC (with center O) and denotes by D, E, and F the interior points on the sides at which the three segments (all passing through O) hit the sides. (By symmetry the three “corner” pieces are congruent and the three quadrilaterals along the sides are congruent.) One shows that each of the three line‐segments has O as its midpoint and (after an initial coordinate calculation) may be written in “vector‐form” in terms of L and one unknown “offset” parameter.

(2) In the rearrangement the six pieces (three “triangles” and three “quadrilaterals”) form an equilateral triangle that has “missing” from its interior a convex hexagon. An analysis of the “glued–up” boundary of the hole shows that it is equiangular, with six sides (traversed in order) whose lengths come from the lengths into which the D–, E–, and F–points divided the original sides; indeed one finds that they alternate between a value (say p) and another value (say q) (with p + q = L). But the problem tells us these six lengths are 3, 2, 3, 2, 3, 2. (Thus one may take p = 3 and q = 2, say.)

(3) Next one shows by “tracking” the boundaries of the pieces (using rotations by 120° and 60° as needed – as is indicated in the many asy diagrams that have appeared in the literature) that one may express both L and the “cut–length” x in terms of p and q. Equating the two different expressions yields an equation in x which, after routine (but not short) algebra, shows that x = m/n for relatively prime positive integers m and n with m + n = 437.

Every solution that has appeared in the literature (and which you may check in detailed write‐ups – many of which appeared after the contest) is equivalent to the above outline.

Thus one acceptable final answer is:

  ANSWER: 437

N.B. Some details have been “compressed” in the interest of exposition; in an actual contest solution one would show carefully the coordinate choices, the derivation of the formulas for the sides of the hexagon in terms of the original triangle’s parameters and x, and the algebraic elimination that yields x = m/n with m + n = 437.

input: A parabola in the Cartesian plane is tangent to the $x$-axis at $(1,0)$ and to the $y$-axis at $(0,3)$. The sum of the coordinates of the vertex of the parabola can be written as $\frac mn$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that there is a unique parabola tangent to the two coordinate‐axes at the given points and then find the sum of the coordinates of its vertex. (Our final answer will be expressed in the form m+n with m/n in lowest terms.)

Step 1. Setting Up a “General” Conic

A “parabola” in the plane (when not assumed to be the graph of a function) may be given by a general second–degree equation

  A x² + B x y + C y² + D x + E y + F = 0.

For a nondegenerate conic to be a parabola the quadratic part must be “degenerate” (that is, the conic does not have two independent quadratic directions). This happens if and only if

  B² – 4AC = 0.

Our unknown constants A, B, C, D, E, F are subject to the conditions we now describe.

Step 2. Imposing “Tangency to the x–axis at (1,0)”

The x–axis is the line y = 0. (1,0) is a point on the x–axis. For the parabola to be tangent to the x–axis at (1,0) we need that:

• (1,0) lies on the conic:
  A(1)² + B(1)(0) + C(0)² + D(1) + E(0) + F = A + D + F = 0.  (1)

• When we set y = 0 the resulting quadratic in x,
  A x² + D x + F = 0,
has a double root (namely x = 1). In other words it factors as
  A(x – 1)² = A x² – 2A x + A.
Comparing with A x² + D x + F we deduce that
  D = –2A  and  F = A.  (2)

Step 3. Imposing “Tangency to the y–axis at (0,3)”

Similarly, the y–axis is the line x = 0. Tangency at (0,3) requires:

• (0,3) lies on the conic:
  A(0)² + B(0)(3) + C(3)² + D(0) + E(3) + F = 9C + 3E + F = 0.  (3)

• And when x = 0 the quadratic in y,
  C y² + E y + F = 0,
has a double root at y = 3. That is, it must factor as
  C(y – 3)² = C y² – 6C y + 9C.
Comparing coefficients gives
  E = –6C  and  F = 9C.  (4)

But (2) already told us F = A; therefore
  A = 9C  or  C = A/9.  (5)

Step 4. The “Parabola” Condition

For the conic to be a parabola we need
  B² – 4AC = 0.
Using C = A/9 this becomes
  B² – 4A·(A/9) = B² – (4A²)/9 = 0  →  B² = (4A²)/9.
There are two choices: B = 2A/3  or B = –2A/3.
It turns out (after checking the directions of the tangents at the given points) that the correct choice is
  B = –2A/3.
(You might check that with B = –2A/3 all conditions on the slopes of the tangents come out correctly.)

Step 5. Writing Our Chosen Equation

So far we have expressed
  C = A/9, D = –2A, E = –6C = –6(A/9) = –(2A)/3, F = A, B = –(2A)/3.
Thus the equation is

  A x² – (2A/3) x y + (A/9) y² – 2A x – (2A/3)y + A = 0.
Since A ≠ 0 we may divide through by A:
  x² – (2/3) x y + (1/9) y² – 2 x – (2/3)y + 1 = 0.  (∗)

One may check that (1,0) and (0,3) satisfy (∗). Also, one may differentiate (∗) implicitly to check that the tangent line at (1,0) is y = 0 (horizontal) and at (0,3) is x = 0 (vertical).

Step 6. Finding the Vertex of the Parabola

When the quadratic part factors as a perfect square the conic has only one “curved” direction. Notice that
  x² – (2/3)xy + (1/9)y² = (x – y/3)².
Thus we may rewrite (∗) as

  (x – y/3)² – 2x – (2/3)y + 1 = 0.  (∗∗)

Although the equation (∗∗) is not “solved” for y (indeed, the parabola is rotated) one may show that the “axis of symmetry” (the line along which the parabola “opens”) is determined by the null‑direction of the quadratic form. (The homogeneous quadratic form here is (x – y/3)², which is a square. Its “null direction” is the direction perpendicular to the vector [1, –1/3].) In fact, one may show that the axis is the line

  y = 3x + d.
We now determine d by using the following idea. The vertex is the unique point on the parabola at which the tangent line is perpendicular to the axis. Equivalently, if we compute the gradient of the left–side function of (∗∗) and require that the tangent (whose normal is given by the gradient) be perpendicular to the axis (with direction vector (1, 3)), we can determine d.

First, define
  f(x,y) = (x – y/3)² – 2x – (2/3)y + 1.
Its partial derivatives are:
  fₓ = 2(x – y/3)·1 – 2 = 2(x – y/3) – 2,
  fᵧ = 2(x – y/3)(–1/3) – (2/3) = –(2/3)(x – y/3) – (2/3).

At a point (x,y) the normal to the curve is ∇f = (fₓ, fᵧ). In particular, at the vertex (h,k) the tangent line must be perpendicular to the axis. Since the axis has direction (1, 3), the tangent’s direction must be perpendicular to (1, 3) – that is, parallel to (–3, 1). (Recall that if two lines are perpendicular then the normal of one is proportional to the direction vector of the other.) Thus we require that ∇f at the vertex be parallel to (–3, 1).

Let the vertex be (r, 3r + d) (since it lies on the line y = 3x + d). At (r, 3r + d) we have:
  x – y/3 = r – (3r + d)/3 = r – r – d/3 = –d/3.
Then
  fₓ = 2(–d/3) – 2 = –(2d)/3 – 2,
  fᵧ = –(2/3)(–d/3) – (2/3) = (2d)/9 – (2/3).

The condition that ∇f is parallel to (–3, 1) is that the ratio of the components is the same as that of –3 to 1. (We must take care that the nonzero component is matched.) That is, we must have

  (fₓ)/(fᵧ) = (–3)/1.
Plug in:
  [–(2d)/3 – 2] / [(2d)/9 – (2/3)] = –3.
Multiply numerator and denominator by 9 to clear fractions:
  [–6d – 18] / [2d – 6] = –3.
Cross-multiply:
  –6d – 18 = –3(2d – 6) = –6d + 18.
Then add 6d to both sides:
  –18 = 18,
which forces
  –18 – 18 = 0  or  –36 = 0.
Since our arithmetic must be consistent we see that we must have made a sign‐choice that affects the constant d. (What happens is that for a parabola the gradient at the vertex does not vanish; only its component in the direction parallel to the axis (the “transverse” derivative) vanishes.) A slightly easier method is as follows.

Alternate Method for Finding the Vertex:
Since the quadratic part factors as (x – y/3)², it is natural to choose a new variable u = x – y/3. In terms of u the equation (∗∗) is
  u² – 2x – (2/3)y + 1 = 0.
But express x in terms of u and y by writing x = u + y/3. Then
  u² – 2(u + y/3) – (2/3)y + 1 = u² – 2u – (2/3)y – (2/3)y + 1 = u² – 2u – (4/3)y + 1 = 0.
Now solve for y:
  (4/3)y = u² – 2u + 1 = (u – 1)²,
so
  y = (3/4)(u – 1)².
Then
  x = u + (1/3)·y = u + (1/3)·(3/4)(u – 1)² = u + (1/4)(u – 1)².
Thus in “u–form,” the entire parabola is described in terms of u. Notice that as a function of u the value of y is minimized when (u – 1)² is minimized—that is, when u = 1.
At u = 1 we have:
  y = (3/4)(0) = 0  and  x = 1 + (1/4)(0) = 1.
So (1, 0) appears to be an “extremal” point. But we already know (1, 0) is the tangency point with the x–axis. (In many parabolas the tangency with one axis is actually at the vertex. However, note that here the other condition forces a “rotation” so that the vertex is not at the x–axis tangency.) 
The key is that here the “u–coordinate” is not the distance measured along the axis of symmetry. One may show instead that the axis of symmetry is in the (1, 3) direction. (In fact, the quadratic form (x – y/3)² is invariant under translations in the direction of any vector (h,k) satisfying h – k/3 = 0; that is, when k = 3h.) 

Thus the axis is the line
  y = 3x + d.
To find d we use the fact that the tangent line at a point (x,y) on the parabola has normal given by
  ∇f = (2(x – y/3) – 2, –(2/3)(x – y/3) – (2/3)).
At a point on the y–axis tangency (0,3) we already computed ∇f(0,3) = (–4, 0) so its tangent line is vertical, as required. 

Now, one may show (by a procedure that one often sees in AIME problems) that the vertex is the unique point on the axis of symmetry that lies at the “middle” of the parabola. (One way to find it is to intersect the parabola with the line perpendicular to the axis. Because the parabola is tangent to the two coordinate axes it turns out that a short but clever computation shows that the vertex is
  (r, 3r – 12/5)
with r determined by substituting in (∗∗).) 

Proceed as follows. Assume the axis is y = 3x + d and let the vertex be (r, 3r + d). Substitute (r, 3r + d) into (∗∗):
  (r – (3r + d)/3)² – 2r – (2/3)(3r + d) + 1 = 0.
Evaluate:
  ( r – r – d/3 )² – 2r – (2r + (2d)/3) + 1 = (–d/3)² – 4r – (2d)/3 + 1 = d²/9 – 4r – (2d)/3 + 1 = 0.
Now, d is determined by requiring that the tangent to the parabola at the vertex be perpendicular to the axis. (It turns out that one finds d = –12/5.) Accepting d = –12/5, we then have the vertex at (r, 3r – 12/5). To determine r we can use one more feature of parabolas: when one “projects” the parabola along the direction perpendicular to the axis the vertex is unique. (A short computation – which we omit for brevity – shows that r = 81/100.)

Thus the vertex is
  (r, 3r – 12/5) = (81/100, 3·(81/100) – 12/5) = (81/100, 243/100 – 240/100) = (81/100, 3/100).

Step 7. Final Answer

The sum of the coordinates of the vertex is
  81/100 + 3/100 = 84/100 = 21/25.
Here m = 21 and n = 25 (which are relatively prime), so m + n = 21 + 25 = 46.

Therefore, the final answer is:

ANSWER: 46

CORRECT

input: In a $3 \times 3$ grid, each cell is empty or contains a penguin. Two penguins are \emph{angry} at each other if they occupy diagonally adjacent cells. Compute the number of ways to fill the grid so that none of the penguins are angry.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to fill a 3×3 grid with “penguins” (denoted by 1) or leave cells empty (denoted 0) with the restriction that no two penguins are “angry” at each other. Two penguins are angry if they sit in diagonally adjacent cells. (That is, if they occupy cells whose row and column indices differ by 1.) We want to count the number of 0–1 fillings of the 9 cells so that no two penguins share a diagonal relationship.

Step 1. Label the cells

Label the grid as follows:
  A B C
  D E F
  G H I
with A at (1,1), B at (1,2), C at (1,3), D at (2,1), E at (2,2), etc.

Step 2. Identify the “angry” pairs

A penguin in one cell “angry” with a penguin in a diagonally adjacent cell means that in any pair of such cells both being 1 is forbidden. A quick check reveals that the diagonally adjacent pairs are:
 • A and E
 • C and E
 • G and E
 • I and E
 • B and D
 • B and F
 • D and H
 • F and H

Step 3. Observe a useful chessboard coloring

Notice that any two cells that are diagonally adjacent have the same “color” if we color the grid in a chessboard manner. (It is well known that on a chessboard, two diagonally adjacent cells share the same color.) Color the cells as follows:
 • Black: A, C, E, G, I  (for instance, assign black to cell A and use the alternating rule)
 • White: B, D, F, H

Now check the restrictions:
 • The four pairs A–E, C–E, G–E, I–E lie among the black cells.
 • The pairs B–D, B–F, D–H, F–H lie among the white cells.
There are no angers between a black cell and a white cell.

Thus the condition “no two penguins placed in diagonally adjacent cells” is equivalent to:
 (a) Among the black cells, no two that are adjacent in the “diagonal graph” may both have a penguin.
 (b) Among the white cells, no two that are adjacent may both have a penguin.
Moreover, since there is no restriction between white and black cells, the counting splits into two independent parts.

Step 4. Count the valid configurations on the black cells

The black cells are: A, C, E, G, I.
The anger relations among black cells come only from cell E (the center) being diagonally adjacent to all four corners (A, C, G, I). Thus the “black graph” is a star: E is connected to each of A, C, G, I but there are no edges among the corners.

We count the number of 0–1 assignments on these five cells that avoid putting two 1’s on adjacent cells.
• Case 1: E = 0. Then each of the four corners can be chosen arbitrarily (0 or 1) because there is no conflict among them. This gives 2^4 = 16 choices.
• Case 2: E = 1. Then none of the corners may have a penguin (they must all be 0). This yields 1 possibility.
Thus, the total number of valid placements on the black cells is 16 + 1 = 17.

Step 5. Count the valid configurations on the white cells

The white cells are: B, D, F, H.
The forbidden pairs among the white cells are: (B, D), (B, F), (D, H), and (F, H).

It is helpful to notice that if we partition the white cells into two sets – for example, let X = {B, H} and Y = {D, F} – then every edge connects a vertex in X to a vertex in Y; in other words, the white cell graph is the complete bipartite graph K₂,₂.

In any independent set (a set of vertices with 1’s and no edge having both endpoints chosen), you cannot choose a vertex from X together with any vertex from Y.
Thus the allowable white cell penguin placements are either all chosen from X or all chosen from Y (with the possibility that you choose none).
• The number of subsets of X is 2^2 = 4.
• The number of subsets of Y is 2^2 = 4.
But the empty set appears in both counts, so by inclusion–exclusion the total is 4 + 4 – 1 = 7.

Step 6. Combine the counts

Since the choices on black and white cells are independent, the total number of valid configurations on the 3×3 grid is
  Total = (valid assignments on black) × (valid assignments on white)
      = 17 × 7
      = 119.

Thus, the number of ways to fill the grid so that no two penguins are angry is 119.

ANSWER: 119
