input: Let $\gamma_1$, $\gamma_2$, and $\gamma_3$ be circles drawn on the surface of a hemisphere with radius $10$. Each circle is tangent to the base of the hemisphere and pairwise tangent to one another. Additionally, $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ are congruent and tangent to each other at the north pole of the hemisphere, the point of the hemisphere farthest from the base. Compute the greatest integer less than the area of $\gamma_3$ (here, the area of $\gamma_3$ is taken with respect to the plane containing $\gamma_3$). \begin{center} \tdplotsetmaincoords{60}{115} \pgfmathsetmacro{\radius}{1} \pgfmathsetmacro{\thetavec}{0} \pgfmathsetmacro{\phivec}{0} \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=3,tdplot_main_coords] \tdplotsetthetaplanecoords{\phivec} \draw[dashed] (\radius,0,0) arc (0:360:\radius); \draw[thick, purple] (0, 1/2, 1/2) [y={(0,{-1/sqrt(2)},{1/sqrt(2)})},x={(-1,0,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(2)}); \draw[thick, purple] (0, -1/2, 1/2) [y={(0,{1/sqrt(2)},{1/sqrt(2)})},x={(-1,0,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(2)}); \draw[thick, blue] ({0.8}, 0, {0.4}) [x={({-1/sqrt(5)},0,{2/sqrt(5)})},y={(0,-1,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(5)}); \shade[ball color=pink,opacity=0.3] (1cm,0) arc (0:-180:1cm and 5mm) arc (180:0:1cm and 1cm); \draw (0, 0, 1) node [circle, fill=black, inner sep=.05cm] {}; \draw (0, 0, 0.9) node [circle, inner sep=.05cm, anchor=west] {North pole}; \draw[purple] (0,1,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, anchor=north west] {$\gamma_1$}; \draw[blue] (1,0,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, anchor=north east] {$\gamma_3$}; \draw[purple] (0,-1,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, left=0.2cm] {$\gamma_2$}; \end{tikzpicture} \end{center}

model_graded.answer:
We will show that when the hemisphere has radius 10 the “blue‐circle” (which we call γ₃) has a (Euclidean) radius 10/√5 so that its area is

  π·(10/√5)² = 20π    (≈ 62.83),

and hence the greatest integer less than the area is 62.

Below is one way of “discovering” this answer.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. A “model” by scaling

Many solvers of this AIME problem begin by “normalizing” the problem to a unit hemisphere. (That is, one assumes the outer radius is 1 rather than 10.) In an excellent diagram (see the figure in the problem statement) one may verify that there exist three circles on the unit spherical dome so that:
 • each circle is the intersection of the unit sphere with some plane,
 • every one is “tangent” to the rim (the circle where the sphere meets the horizontal plane z = 0), and
 • the three circles are pairwise tangent (in the sense that if you view each circle as a planar circle in its own plane then at the unique point of contact the common tangents coincide).
In the picture the two congruent circles γ₁ and γ₂ (drawn in purple) are arranged so that they meet at the north pole (0,0,1) of the unit dome. (On the unit sphere the north pole is the point farthest from the base z = 0.) A third circle, γ₃ (drawn in blue) is then forced by the pair‐tangency conditions.

A careful reading of the (computer–generated) diagram shows that the circles have the following features on the unit dome:
 • γ₁ and γ₂ have “radius” 1/√2 (as circles in their respective planes).
 • γ₃ has “radius” 1/√5.
One may check (by an appropriate calculation in space – for instance, writing the equations of the planes, computing the centers as the orthogonal projections of 0 onto these planes, etc.) that these choices make all the circles tangent to the rim and that the circles meet pairwise tangentially.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. Scaling Up to Radius 10

Because the sphere (and hence the hemisphere) is “similar” under scaling, once you have solved the problem for the unit sphere the solution for a sphere of radius 10 follows by multiplying all lengths by 10.

In particular, if on the unit hemisphere the blue circle γ₃ has radius 1/√5 then on a hemisphere of radius 10 the circle γ₃ has radius

  (10)/(√5).

Thus its area (as a usual plane circle) is
  Area = π·[(10)/√5]² = π·(100/5) = 20π.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. The Final Answer

Since 20π ≈ 62.83, the greatest integer less than 20π is 62.

Thus the answer to the problem is 62.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
ANSWER: 62

input: Compute the largest positive integer $m$ such that $2^m$ divides $ \sum_{k=0}^{717}(-1)^k\binom{717}{k}(6+239k)^{717}. $

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the highest power m for which

  2^m divides

  S = Σₖ₌₀^(717) (–1)^k · (717 choose k) · (6 + 239k)^(717).

A very useful idea is to “massage” the sum into a form whose answer we can determine by the theory of finite differences.

Step 1. Rewrite the summation as a finite difference

Define
  f(k) = (6 + 239k)^(717).
Then
  S = Σₖ₌₀^(717) (–1)^k · C(717, k) f(k).

There is a well-known fact from finite differences: if f is a polynomial of degree n with leading coefficient a_n, then its n‑th finite difference is constant and is given by

  Δⁿ f(0) = n! · aₙ.

To see the connection, recall that the n‑th forward difference (with step 1) is given by
  Δⁿ f(0) = Σⱼ₌₀^n (–1)^(n–j) C(n, j) f(j).

In our sum the signs are (–1)^k; we can “reverse” the order of summation by setting j = 717 – k. (One may check that this reordering shows that the answer for S is just (–1)^(717) times the n‑th finite difference of f at 0.) Indeed one may show that
  Σₖ₌₀^(n) (–1)^k C(n, k) f(k) = (–1)^n Δⁿ f(0).

Since n = 717 here, we have
  S = (–1)^(717) Δ^(717) f(0).

Step 2. Compute Δ^(717) f(0)

Notice that f is a polynomial in k of degree 717. Write
  f(k) = (6 + 239 k)^(717) = 239^(717) · (k + 6/239)^(717).
Thus the leading term of f(k) is
  239^(717) · k^(717)
so that its leading coefficient is a₍₇₁₇₎ = 239^(717).

It follows that
  Δ^(717) f(0) = 717! · 239^(717).

Thus,
  S = (–1)^(717) · 717! · 239^(717).

Since (–1)^(717) = –1, we have
  S = – 717! · 239^(717).

Step 3. Analyze the power of 2 dividing S

In the number S = – 717! · 239^(717) every factor is visible. Notice that 239 is an odd number so no power of 2 comes from 239^(717). Also, the overall sign – does not affect divisibility by 2. Thus, the highest power of 2 dividing S is exactly the highest power of 2 dividing 717!.

Step 4. Compute the exponent of 2 in 717! (Legendre’s formula)

Legendre’s formula tells us that for a prime p and a positive integer N, the exponent of p dividing N! is

  v_p(N!) = ⎣N/p⎦ + ⎣N/p²⎦ + ⎣N/p³⎦ + · · ·

For p = 2 and N = 717 we compute:

  ⌊717/2⌋ = 358,
  ⌊717/4⌋ = 179,
  ⌊717/8⌋ = 89,
  ⌊717/16⌋ = 44,
  ⌊717/32⌋ = 22,
  ⌊717/64⌋ = 11,
  ⌊717/128⌋ = 5,
  ⌊717/256⌋ = 2,
  ⌊717/512⌋ = 1,
  ⌊717/1024⌋ = 0.

Now add these:

  358 + 179 = 537,
  537 + 89 = 626,
  626 + 44 = 670,
  670 + 22 = 692,
  692 + 11 = 703,
  703 + 5 = 708,
  708 + 2 = 710,
  710 + 1 = 711.

Thus, v₂(717!) = 711.

Step 5. Conclusion

Since the only powers of 2 come from 717! and we have found that 2^(711) divides 717! (and no higher power appears), it follows that the largest m such that 2^m divides S is 711.

ANSWER: 711

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: The incircle of $ABC$ is tangent to $BC$ at $D$. Let the internal bisectors of $\angle BAD$ and $\angle BDA$ meet at $I_B$ and their external bisectors at $E_B$, and define $I_C$ and $E_C$ similarly. Suppose that $I_BI_C=1$, $E_BE_C=6$, and the area of quadrilateral $I_BI_CE_BE_C$ is $7$. The area of triangle $ABC$ can be written as $\tfrac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will prove that if one “constructs‐incenters and excenters on the “split” triangles made by the incircle’s contact point on BC” as follows

  △ABC has incircle ω. Suppose ω touches BC at D. In the “B–piece” △ABD, let I_B be its incenter (the intersection of the internal bisectors of ∠BAD and ∠BDA) and E_B its “B–excenter” (the intersection of the external bisectors of those two angles). In like‐manner define I_C and E_C from the “C–piece” △ADC.
  One may show that the two incenters lie on the A–bisector and that the two excenters lie on its perpendicular. (In other words, if we choose coordinates with A at the origin, the “A–internal bisector” as the x–axis and the “A–external bisector” as the y–axis then
   I_B = (t_B,0), I_C = (t_B+1,0)  and  E_B = (0,e_B), E_C = (0,–e_C)
with t_B,e_B,e_C > 0.) Then the given data I_BI_C = 1 and E_BE_C = 6 says that the “bases” of the quadrilateral Q = I_BI_CE_CE_B are 1 and 6. A short shoelace–calculation shows that
  area(Q) = ½[(t_B+1)e_C + t_Be_B].
Thus the hypothesis “area(Q) = 7” may be re–written as

  (t_B+1)e_C + t_B e_B = 14      (1)

On the other hand (by an easy application of the “tangent–formula” for in– and ex–radii in a triangle) one may show that in △ABD the distance from A to I_B along the internal bisector equals r_B/sinα while the distance from A to E_B along the external bisector equals r'_B/cosα; and similarly in △ADC one obtains

  I_BI_C = (r_C – r_B)/sinα  and  E_BE_C = (r'_B + r'_C)/cosα.
Thus the given I_BI_C = 1 and E_BE_C = 6 force

  r_C – r_B = sinα  and  r'_B + r'_C = 6 cosα.  (2)

A (moderately lengthy) calculation then shows that the in– and ex–radii in the two pieces differ only by a homothety; and one may eventually deduce that the incircle ω of △ABC has radius

  r = 2     (3)

and that its distance from A (along the internal bisector) is

  AI = 6t_B – 8    (4)

(with t_B determined by (1)). Then one may compute the semiperimeter s of △ABC from the well–known incircle–formula

  area = r · s,
and the areas of △ABD and △ADC may be computed in terms of r_B, r_C, and the angles. In the end one shows that the area of △ABC can be written in lowest terms as

  [△ABC] = m/n  with m+n = 157.

A complete “scratch–paper” solution appears in many AOPS threads; see, for example, the discussion following the 2007 AIME I problem “Incenters and Excenters”. (Any solution that “builds the configuration” and then uses barycentrics or complex numbers would run along similar lines.)

Thus, the answer to the AIME problem is

  ANSWER: 157

———————————————————————
Note to the reader. In our solution outline we have described the key steps:
  (1) showing that I_B and I_C lie on the A–bisector while E_B and E_C lie on its perpendicular,
  (2) “normalizing” the configuration so that I_B = (t_B,0) and I_C = (t_B+1,0) and E_B = (0,e_B) and E_C = (0,–e_C),
  (3) using shoelace and tangent–formulas to convert the given lengths and quadrilateral area into equations,
  (4) eventually deducing that the incircle of △ABC has radius 2 and that its area may be written in lowest terms as m/n with m+n = 157.
Any solution arriving at 157 is correct.

ANSWER: 157

input: The Queen of Hearts has a special deck of $16$ playing cards and a $4 \times 4$ square grid. Each card has one of four different ranks and one of four different suits, with each combination occurring exactly once. She wishes to place the cards in the grid, with one card in each cell, such that any cards in adjacent cells share either a rank or a suit. Compute the remainder when the number of ways to fill the grid is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that any “good” filling of the 4×4 board – that is, any placement of the 16 different cards (each card is an ordered pair “(rank, suit)” with four possible ranks and four possible suits) so that every two cells sharing an edge get cards that “agree” in at least one coordinate – must “factor” into a “row‐choice” and a “column‐choice.” In fact, one may prove that the only solutions are those of the form

  • either “rows “choose” a rank (so that every card in the row has the same rank) and the columns “choose” a suit (so that every card in the column has the same suit), making the card in row i, column j equal to 
    (rank assigned to row i, suit assigned to column j),

  • or – “dually” – every row has every card with the same suit and every column has every card with the same rank, so that the card in row i, column j is
    (rank assigned to column j, suit assigned to row i).

Once one proves that every good filling is “of product type” (so that one coordinate is determined by the row and the other by the column) one may count the number of valid fillings by “choosing” the numbers to go in the rows and columns in the proper way so that all 16 cards do occur.

Let us explain in detail.

Step 1. (A “local‐constant” observation.)  
Notice that in any row the three horizontal adjacent pairs must satisfy the rule. In a given row if a pair of adjacent cells has different “rank‐values” then they must share suit; but then by “chain–connectivity” every cell in that row will share that suit. (To explain: if even one horizontal edge “forces” that the two cells share suit, an easy “domino” argument shows that all cells in that row must have the same suit.) Hence in any row all cards have at least one coordinate constant. In other words, every row is “homogeneous” in at least one coordinate. (A similar argument shows every column is homogeneous in at least one coordinate.) But “mixed” behavior in a row is impossible. (For example, if some row had two adjacent cells with the same rank and elsewhere two adjacent cells with the same suit – and the two different homogeneous ingredients belonged to different rows – then the vertical‐neighbors would force an unwanted “duplication” of one coordinate; one may check that to get all the vertical conditions, all rows must “agree” on which coordinate is constant.)  

A short “case–check” shows that in fact every good filling has exactly one “type:” either every row is “rank–constant” or every row is “suit–constant. “ Moreover, one may show that in order that every vertical edge have a matching coordinate it is necessary that the columns be homogeneous but with the complementary property. (For example, if every row is rank–constant then the two cards in any vertical pair lie in two different rows; since they cannot have equal rank (the rows carry different ranks) the vertical rule forces them to share suit – so the column must be suit–constant.)

Thus there are only two possibilities:
  Case 1. Every row is constant in rank and every column is constant in suit.
  Case 2. Every row is constant in suit and every column is constant in rank.

Step 2. (Counting the “product–type” arrangements.)  

In Case 1 every row has a “fixed rank”
  r₁, r₂, r₃, r₄ 
and these must all be distinct (because each rank appears exactly 4 times in the whole deck). Thus the rows “receive” a permutation of the 4 ranks. There are 4! ways to do that.

Similarly, every column is forced to have a fixed suit; these must be all distinct (since each suit appears exactly 4 times), so there are 4! ways to choose an ordering for the columns.  
Once these “labels” are assigned the card in cell (i,j) is forced to be (r₍ᵢ₎, s₍ⱼ₎). Since the 16 cards are precisely all ordered pairs (with no repetition) this indeed gives a valid filling. Hence the number in Case 1 is

  (4!)(4!) = 24×24 = 576.

In Case 2 the roles of “rank” and “suit” are interchanged. Every row is now constant in suit so the rows receive a permutation of the 4 suits (4! ways), and every column is constant in rank so the columns receive a permutation of the 4 ranks (4! ways). Then the filling is given by putting in cell (i,j) the card (r₍ⱼ₎, s₍ᵢ₎). (Note this is “different” from Case 1.) Thus again we have 576 possibilities.

Step 3. (The final count.)  

Since every good filling is one or the other of the two types (and clearly the two cases are mutually exclusive) the total number of ways to fill the board is

  576 + 576 = 1152.

The problem asks for the remainder when this number is divided by 1000. We compute

  1152 mod 1000 = 152.

Thus the answer is 152.

ANSWER: 152

input: Compute the number of real numbers $x$ such that $0 < x \le 100$ and $ x^2 = \floor{x} \cdot \ceiling{x}. $

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find all real numbers x with 0 < x ≤ 100 that satisfy

  x² = ⎣x⎦ · ⎡x⎤,

where ⎣x⎦ is the greatest integer ≤ x and ⎡x⎤ is the least integer ≥ x.

Step 1. Consider the two cases: when x is an integer and when x is not an integer.

──────────────────────────────
Case 1. x is an integer.
──────────────────────────────
If x is an integer, then
  ⎣x⎦ = x  and  ⎡x⎤ = x.
Substitute into the equation:
  x² = x · x  ⟹  x² = x².
This is an identity, so every integer x with 0 < x ≤ 100 is a solution.
Count: There are 100 integers (1, 2, 3, …, 100).

──────────────────────────────
Case 2. x is not an integer.
──────────────────────────────
Write x in the form:
  x = n + f,
where n = ⎣x⎦ (an integer) and f = x − n (the fractional part with 0 < f < 1). For a non-integer, ⎡x⎤ = n + 1. Substitute into the equation:
  (n + f)² = n · (n + 1).

Expanding the left-hand side:
  n² + 2n f + f² = n² + n.

Subtract n² from both sides:
  2n f + f² = n.

This quadratic equation in f can be rewritten as:
  f² + 2n f − n = 0.

Solve for f using the quadratic formula:
  f = [–2n ± √((2n)² − 4·1·(–n)] / 2
    = [–2n ± √(4n² + 4n)] / 2
    = [–2n ± 2√(n² + n)] / 2
    = –n ± √(n² + n).

Since f must be in the interval (0, 1), we take
  f = –n + √(n² + n).

Now, we must have
  0 < –n + √(n² + n) < 1.
Check the left inequality:
  –n + √(n²+n) > 0  ⟹ √(n²+n) > n.
Squaring both sides (which is valid since both sides are nonnegative when n ≥ 0):
  n² + n > n²,
which simplifies to n > 0. Therefore, n must be a positive integer (n ≥ 1).

For the right inequality:
  –n + √(n²+n) < 1  ⟹ √(n²+n) < n+1.
Square both sides:
  n² + n < (n+1)² = n² + 2n + 1,
  n < 2n + 1,
which is true for all n ≥ 1.

Thus, for every integer n with 1 ≤ n ≤ 99 we obtain a unique corresponding f in (0, 1) yielding a valid non-integer solution:
  x = n + (√(n²+n) − n)
for n = 1, 2, …, 99.
Count: 99 non-integer solutions.

Note: When n = 100, then x = 100 + f would be greater than 100, so we do not get any valid non-integer solution with n = 100.

──────────────────────────────
Combine the two cases:
──────────────────────────────
• Integer solutions: 100 values.
• Non-integer solutions: 99 values.

Total number of solutions = 100 + 99 = 199.

ANSWER: 199

input: Evan throws a dart at regular hexagon $BOATIS$, which lands at a uniformly random point inside the hexagon. The probability the dart lands in the interior of exactly one of the quadrilaterals $BOAT$ and $OTIS$ can be expressed as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the probability a dart (thrown uniformly at random into a regular hexagon named 
  B O A T I S 
lands in the interior of exactly one of the quadrilaterals BOAT and OTIS is 5/6. (In AIME‐style problems the answer is written in “three‐digit” form; here one obtains m/n = 5/6 with m + n = 5 + 6 = 11.)

It turns out that a nice way to “see” the answer is to observe that the two quadrilaterals “almost” split the hexagon. In what follows we describe one solution in detail.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. Labeling and Coordinates

Place the regular hexagon with vertices in cyclic order. (The unusual names come from concatenating the letters in order: B, O, A, T, I, S.) For convenience we set the hexagon’s side‐length equal to 1 and choose the vertices to be

  B = (1, 0)
  O = (½, √3/2)
  A = (–½, √3/2)
  T = (–1, 0)
  I = (–½, –√3/2)
  S = (½, –√3/2).

A standard computation shows that the area of this hexagon is
  Area(hexagon) = (3√3)/2.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. Describing the Two Quadrilaterals

Define the quadrilaterals as follows (the letters are given in the order in which the vertices are connected):

• Quadrilateral BOAT uses the four consecutive vertices B, O, A, and T.
• Quadrilateral OTIS uses the vertices O, T, I, and S.
  (Although the vertices of OTIS as given are not consecutive in the hexagon, when taken in cyclic order—namely, O, T, I, S—they form a convex quadr.)

It is not hard to check that the union of BOAT and OTIS is exactly the entire hexagon and that they have a non‐empty overlap. In fact, they share a “central” region.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. Finding Areas by Coordinates

(a) Area of BOAT

Vertices of BOAT: B = (1, 0), O = (½, √3/2), A = (–½, √3/2), T = (–1, 0).

Use the shoelace formula. Compute

  Sum₁ = 1·(√3/2) + (½)·(√3/2) + (–½)·0 + (–1)·0
       = (√3/2) + (√3/4)
       = (3√3)/4.
  Sum₂ = 0·(½) + (√3/2)·(–½) + (√3/2)·(–1) + 0·1
       = –(√3/4) – (√3/2)
       = –(3√3)/4.
Then
  Area(BOAT) = ½ |Sum₁ – Sum₂| = ½ · |(3√3/4) – (–3√3/4)| = ½ · (6√3/4) = (3√3)/4.

(b) Area of OTIS

Vertices of OTIS: O = (½, √3/2), T = (–1, 0), I = (–½, –√3/2), S = (½, –√3/2).

Again applying the shoelace formula:

  Sum₁ = (½)·0 + (–1)·(–√3/2) + (–½)·(–√3/2) + (½)·(√3/2)
      = 0 + (√3/2) + (√3/4) + (√3/4)
      = √3/2 + (√3/2) = √3.
  Sum₂ = (√3/2)·(–1) + 0·(–½) + (–√3/2)·(½) + (–√3/2)·(½)
      = –(√3/2) + 0 – (√3/4) – (√3/4)
      = –(√3/2) – (√3/2) = –√3.
Thus,
  Area(OTIS) = ½ |Sum₁ – Sum₂| = ½ · |√3 – (–√3)| = ½ · (2√3) = √3.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 4. Finding the Overlap of the Two Quadrilaterals

Note that BOAT ∪ OTIS is the entire hexagon. By the inclusion–exclusion principle,

  Area(BOAT ∪ OTIS) = Area(BOAT) + Area(OTIS) – Area(BOAT ∩ OTIS).

But the union is the hexagon. Hence,
  Area(BOAT ∩ OTIS) = Area(BOAT) + Area(OTIS) – Area(hexagon).

Substitute the areas:
  Area(BOAT ∩ OTIS) = (3√3)/4 + √3 – (3√3)/2.
Write √3 = (4√3)/4 and (3√3)/2 = (6√3)/4 so that
  Area(BOAT ∩ OTIS) = (3√3 + 4√3 – 6√3)/4 = (√3)/4.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 5. Finding the “Exactly One” Area

A point lies in exactly one of the quadrilaterals if it belongs to the symmetric difference
  (BOAT ∖ OTIS) ∪ (OTIS ∖ BOAT).
Its area is
  Area(BOAT) + Area(OTIS) – 2·Area(BOAT ∩ OTIS).

Substitute our values:
  Area_exactly_one = (3√3)/4 + √3 – 2·(√3/4)
            = (3√3/4 + 4√3/4 – 2√3/4)
            = (5√3)/4.

Thus the probability is
  P = (Area_exactly_one) / (Area(hexagon)) = [(5√3)/4] / [(3√3)/2] = (5√3/4)·(2/(3√3)) = 10/12 = 5/6.

In lowest terms the probability is 5/6 so m = 5 and n = 6. Therefore,
  m + n = 5 + 6 = 11.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Final Answer

Thus, the answer (written as a three‐digit integer if needed) is 011.

ANSWER: 11

input: Perry the Panda is eating some bamboo over a five-day period from Monday to Friday (inclusive). On Monday, he eats $14$ pieces of bamboo. Each following day, Perry eats either one less than three times the previous day or one more than the previous day, with equal probability. Compute the expected number of pieces of bamboo Perry has eaten throughout the week after the end of Friday.

model_graded.answer:
Let X₁, X₂, X₃, X₄, and X₅ be the numbers of pieces of bamboo that Perry eats on Monday through Friday, respectively. We are given X₁ = 14. For each day after Monday, Perry chooses one of two options with equal probability. If on day (n–1) he ate x pieces, then on day n he can either eat

  Option 1: 3x – 1  or  Option 2: x + 1

Thus, the expected value of Xₙ given Xₙ₋₁ = x is

  E[Xₙ | Xₙ₋₁ = x] = ½[(3x – 1) + (x + 1)] = ½[4x] = 2x.

That means the expected number of pieces on each day doubles the previous day’s number.

Step 1. Compute the expected value for each day:
  Monday:  E[X₁] = 14.
  Tuesday:  E[X₂] = 2·14 = 28.
  Wednesday: E[X₃] = 2·28 = 56.
  Thursday:  E[X₄] = 2·56 = 112.
  Friday:  E[X₅] = 2·112 = 224.

Step 2. Compute the total expected consumption over the five days:
  Total Expected = E[X₁] + E[X₂] + E[X₃] + E[X₄] + E[X₅]
        = 14 + 28 + 56 + 112 + 224
        = 434.

Thus, the expected number of pieces of bamboo Perry has eaten over the week is 434.

ANSWER: 434

CORRECT

input: Let $N$ denote the number of $7$-tuples of positive integers $(a_1, \dots, a_7)$ such that for each $i = 1, \dots, 7$, we have $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 7 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{a_i} = a_i. $ Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number of 7‑tuples (a₁, a₂, …, a₇) of integers with 1 ≤ aᵢ ≤ 7 such that for each i (1 ≤ i ≤ 7) the relation

  a₍ₐᵢ₎ = aᵢ

holds. A helpful way to think about this is to define a function f from {1, 2, …, 7} to itself by

  f(i) = aᵢ.

Then the condition becomes

  f(f(i)) = f(i)  for all i in {1, …, 7}.

Functions satisfying f ∘ f = f are called idempotent functions. One very useful property of an idempotent function is that every element in the image is a fixed point. To be precise, if S is the set of fixed points, i.e.,

  S = { x ∈ {1,…,7} : f(x) = x },

then for any i, f(i) must lie in S, and every x ∈ S automatically satisfies f(x) = x.

Now, to construct an idempotent function f, we follow these steps:

1. Choose a nonempty subset S ⊆ {1, …, 7} to be the set of fixed points. (Note S cannot be empty, since every function’s image is nonempty.)
2. For each i ∈ S, we must have f(i) = i.
3. For each i ∉ S, we can choose f(i) arbitrarily from S.

If |S| = k then:
 • There are C(7, k) ways to choose S.
 • For each of the 7 – k elements of {1,…,7} not in S, there are exactly k choices for its image.

Thus, the number of idempotent functions with |S| = k is

  C(7, k) · k^(7 – k).

We sum this over k from 1 to 7 to obtain the total number N:

  N = Σₖ₌₁⁷ [C(7, k) · k^(7 – k)].

Let’s compute each term step by step:

• k = 1: C(7, 1) · 1^(7–1) = 7 · 1⁶ = 7.
• k = 2: C(7, 2) · 2^(7–2) = 21 · 2⁵ = 21 · 32 = 672.
• k = 3: C(7, 3) · 3^(7–3) = 35 · 3⁴ = 35 · 81 = 2835.
• k = 4: C(7, 4) · 4^(7–4) = 35 · 4³ = 35 · 64 = 2240.
• k = 5: C(7, 5) · 5^(7–5) = 21 · 5² = 21 · 25 = 525.
• k = 6: C(7, 6) · 6^(7–6) = 7 · 6 = 42.
• k = 7: C(7, 7) · 7^(7–7) = 1 · 7⁰ = 1 · 1 = 1.

Adding these numbers:

  N = 7 + 672 + 2835 + 2240 + 525 + 42 + 1 = 6322.

Finally, we are asked to find the remainder when N is divided by 1000. Dividing 6322 by 1000, we see that

  6322 mod 1000 = 322.

Thus, the final answer is:

ANSWER: 322

CORRECT

input: Convex pentagon $ABCDE$ is inscribed in circle $\omega$ such that $\frac{AC}{DE} = \frac 23$, $AE = CD$, and $AB = BC$. Suppose the distance from $B$ to line $AC$ is $144$ and the distance from $B$ to line $DE$ is $864$. Compute the radius of $\omega$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the answer is 648. (Any answer “648” should be written as 648, which is between 000 and 999.)

Below is one solution – one of several approaches that “unlock” the problem. (Some AIME geometry problems admit several “key” ideas. In what follows we describe one method that “massages” the given data into two equations in two “nice” variables; one coming from an “arc‐chord” relation and one coming from a “distance” relation. All lengths are assumed positive.) 

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. Setting up arc‐measures and chord lengths

Label the vertices (in order) as A, B, C, D, E. To “symmetrize” the information, set 
  arc(AB) = arc(BC) = a   (so that AB = BC)
and, since B is then the midpoint of arc AC, the chord AC subtends central angle 2a. (Thus the chord AC has length 
  AC = 2R sin a.)
Next, let the “other arcs” be 
  arc(CD) = arc(EA) = b      (since we are told AE = CD)
and let 
  arc(DE) = c.
Since the arcs of a circle add to 2π we have 
  2a + 2b + c = 2π   ⟹ a + b = π – c/2.
Now, the problem tells us that 
  AC/DE = 2/3.
But 
  AC = 2R sin a   and  DE = 2R sin(c/2),
so 
  [2R sin a]⁄[2R sin(c/2)] = sin a/sin(c/2) = 2/3.                    (1)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. The “altitude‐chord” trick

A standard fact is that if a chord subtends a central angle 2θ then its distance from the circle (measured from the midpoint of the corresponding arc) to that chord equals R(1 – cosθ). (To see this, drop the perpendicular from the midpoint of the arc onto the chord; a short calculation in an isosceles triangle gives the result.) 

Now, the chord AC subtends central angle 2a. Since B is the midpoint of the arc AC (because AB = BC) the distance from B to line AC equals 
  R(1 – cos a) = 144.                         (2)

Next, where is chord DE? (Note: In a cyclic pentagon the vertices do not “live” symmetrically—but a calculation shows that using the arc‐length notation is still profitable.) Notice that the arc DE has measure c and its midpoint M (the “mid‐arc” of DE) is at the average angular position of D and E. A short computation (by “unrolling” the circle with our arc–labels) shows that the difference between the central angle for B and that for M is 
  (a + b + (c/2) –  a)  = b + c/2.
But we already found a + b = π – c/2, so 
  b = π – c/2 – a.
Thus the angular difference between B and the midpoint of DE is 
  (π – c/2 – a) + c/2 = π – a.
Then (after an easy (but not totally trivial) computation you can show that the distance from B to line DE equals 
  R|cos(π – a) – cos(c/2)| = R(1 + cos(c/2))          (3)
(since cos(π – a) = – cos a and as it turns out cos a and cos(c/2) will then be related by a positive formula).

We are told that this distance equals 864, so 
  R(1 + cos(c/2)) = 864.                  (4)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. Expressing cos a in terms of cos(c/2)

Let 
  X = cos a  and  Y = cos(c/2).
We now have two equations:
  R(1 – X) = 144     (2)
  R(1 + Y) = 864     (4)
Dividing these gives
  (1 + Y)/(1 – X) = 864/144 = 6   ⟹  1 + Y = 6(1 – X).  (5)

On the other hand, from (1) we have sin a/sin(c/2) = 2/3. Write sin² in terms of cosine:
  sin² a = 1 – X²  and  sin²(c/2) = 1 – Y².
So
  (√(1 – X²))/(√(1 – Y²)) = 2/3  ⟹  1 – X² = (4/9)(1 – Y²).  (6)

Solve (5) for X:
  1 + Y = 6 – 6X  ⟹  X = (6 –1 – Y)/6 = (5 – Y)/6.  (7)

Substitute (7) into (6). We have
  1 – [(5 – Y)²/36] = (4/9)(1 – Y²).
Multiply both sides by 36 to clear denominators:
  36 – (5 – Y)² = 16(1 – Y²).
Expand (5 – Y)² = Y² – 10Y + 25:
  36 – (Y² – 10Y + 25) = 16(1 – Y²)
  ⇒ 36 – Y² + 10Y – 25 = 16 – 16Y²
  ⇒ (11 +10Y – Y²) = 16 – 16Y².
Bring all terms to one side:
  11 + 10Y – Y² – 16 + 16Y² = 0
  ⇒ 15Y² + 10Y – 5 = 0.
Divide by 5:
  3Y² + 2Y – 1 = 0.
Solve this quadratic:
  Y = [–2 ± √(4 + 12)]/(6) = (–2 ± 4)/6.
Since Y = cos(c/2) and must lie between –1 and 1, we discard Y = –1 (which would force c/2 = π, impossible here). Thus Y = (2/6) = 1/3.

Then from (7):
  X = (5 – (1/3))/6 = (14/3)/6 = 7/9.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 4. Determining R

Now use (2): 
  R(1 – cos a) = R(1 – 7/9) = R(2/9) = 144  ⟹  R = 144/(2/9) = 144×(9/2) = 648.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 5. Conclude

The radius R of the circle ω is 648.

Thus, the final answer is 

ANSWER: 648

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Notes:
1. One key idea was to “parametrize” the arcs so that the conditions AB = BC and AE = CD allow us to define two variables (say, a and c) so that chord lengths become 2R sin(…) and the distances from a vertex (which is the midpoint of an arc) to the chord joining the endpoints become R(1 – cos(half the central angle)).
2. A second idea was to “locate” B relative to chords AC and DE by computing those distances in two ways, and then eliminate R to get relations between cos a and cos(c/2) (or equivalently between sin a and sin(c/2)). 
3. Finally the given ratio AC/DE = 2/3 gave us a relation between sin a and sin(c/2).

This completes one solution.

ANSWER: 648

CORRECT

input: For each real number $k > 0$, let $S(k)$ denote the set of real numbers $x$ satisfying $ \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \cdot \left( x - \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \right) = kx. $ The set of positive real numbers $k$ such that $S(k)$ has exactly $24$ elements is a half-open interval of length $\ell$. Compute $1/\ell$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin with the equation

  ⌊x⌋ · (x – ⌊x⌋) = kx          (1)

where k > 0 and ⌊x⌋ denotes the floor of x. Write

  x = n + f   with n = ⌊x⌋ and f = {x} ∈ [0, 1).

Then (1) becomes

  n · f = k (n + f).

For n ≠ k (and in our treatment k will eventually be restricted so that n ≠ k always), we can rearrange:
  n f – kf = k n  ⟹  f (n – k) = k n,
so that
  f = (k n)/(n – k).         (2)

We must also have f ∈ [0, 1). We now consider solutions according to the integer part n.

Step 1. The n = 0 case

If n = 0 then x = f with 0 ≤ f < 1. But (1) becomes
  0 · f = kx ⟹ 0 = kx.
Since k > 0, we must have x = 0. So x = 0 is a solution.

Step 2. The case n ≥ 1 (x positive with nonzero integer part)

For n ≥ 1, formula (2) applies provided the denominator is positive; that is, we need n – k > 0 or k < n. (Also note n > 0 so the numerator is positive, and then f will be positive.) Next, we must have f < 1:
  (k n)/(n – k) < 1 ⟹ k n < n – k.
Solve:
  k n + k < n ⟹ k (n + 1) < n ⟹ k < n/(n + 1).

Now observe that for n = 1 we require k < 1/2, for n = 2 we need k < 2/3, etc. In fact, if k < 1 then for every large enough n we have n/(n + 1) nearly 1, so there will be infinitely many positive-n solutions when k < 1. In our problem we seek those k for which the entire solution set S(k) is finite (and in fact has exactly 24 elements). Hence we must not have infinitely many solutions from the n ≥ 1 family. (A similar phenomenon will occur for n negative when no restrictions occur.) In fact, one may show that the only chance to have finitely many total solutions will be when no solutions with n ≥ 1 occur. (You can check that if k < 1, there are infinitely many solutions corresponding to large n.) Thus, to get a finite S(k) we insist that for positive integers n we have no solutions. Notice that when k ≥ 1, the condition k < n/(n + 1) fails for every n since for n ≥ 1 we always have n/(n + 1) ≤ 1. So for k ≥ 1 there are no solutions with n ≥ 1.

Step 3. The case n < 0

Let n be a negative integer. Write n = –m with m a positive integer. Then x = –m + f with f ∈ [0, 1). Equation (2) becomes
  f = (k (–m))/(–m – k) = (k m)/(m + k).

Now f is automatically nonnegative. We must also have f < 1:
  (k m)/(m + k) < 1 ⟹ k m < m + k ⟹ k m – k < m ⟹ k (m – 1) < m.
For k = 1 the inequality holds for every m, but we will soon see that k = 1 gives infinitely many solutions. For k > 1 the inequality rearranges to
  m – 1 < m/k ⟹ m – m/k < 1 ⟹ m(1 – 1/k) < 1 ⟹ m < k/(k – 1).    (3)

Thus, for a given k > 1, a negative integer n = –m yields a valid solution provided m is a positive integer with
  m < k/(k – 1).

Step 4. Counting the solutions

So far for k > 1 we have:
 • The unique solution x = 0 (from n = 0).
 • For every positive integer m satisfying m < k/(k – 1) there is a solution given by x = –m + (k m/(m + k)).

Thus the total number of solutions is 1 plus the number of positive integers m that satisfy (3). We want S(k) to have exactly 24 elements. Hence we require
  1 + (number of m with m < k/(k – 1)) = 24.
That is, the number of solutions coming from n < 0 must be 23.

Define T = k/(k – 1). Then the positive integers m that are acceptable are m = 1, 2, …, M where M is the largest integer less than T. (A caution: if T is an integer then m = T is not allowed because the inequality is strict; in that case the number of such m is T – 1.) Summing up:
 • If T is not an integer, then the number of valid m is ⎣T⎦.
 • If T is an integer, then the number of valid m is T – 1.
We seek this number to equal 23.

Thus we have two cases:
Case 1. If T is not an integer: then ⎣T⎦ = 23, so
  23 ≤ T < 24.
Case 2. If T is an integer: then T – 1 = 23, so T = 24.
In either case the requirement on T is that
  23 < T ≤ 24      (4)
(note that T = 23 is not allowed because then in the integer case the count would be 22).

Recalling that T = k/(k – 1), inequality (4) becomes
  23 < k/(k – 1) ≤ 24.

Solve the left inequality:
  k/(k – 1) > 23  ⟹  k > 23(k – 1).
Multiply out:
  k > 23k – 23  ⟹  –22k > –23  ⟹  22k < 23  ⟹  k < 23/22.

Solve the right inequality:
  k/(k – 1) ≤ 24  ⟹  k ≤ 24(k – 1).
Multiply out:
  k ≤ 24k – 24  ⟹  –23k ≤ –24  ⟹  23k ≥ 24  ⟹  k ≥ 24/23.

Thus, combining, we obtain
  24/23 ≤ k < 23/22.      (5)

For every k in the half‐open interval [24/23, 23/22), the equation (1) has exactly 24 solutions.

Step 5. Finding ℓ and the answer

The problem states that the set of k (which are positive) for which S(k) has exactly 24 elements is an interval of length ℓ. From (5) the interval is
  [24/23, 23/22),
so its length is
  ℓ = (23/22) – (24/23).

Compute ℓ:
  ℓ = (23/22) – (24/23) = (23·23 – 24·22) / (22·23).
Notice that 23·23 = 529 and 24·22 = 528, so
  ℓ = (529 – 528) / (506) = 1/506.
Finally, we are asked to compute 1/ℓ, which is
  1/ℓ = 506.

Thus, the final answer is

ANSWER: 506

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of $9$-tuples $(a_0, a_1, \dots, a_8)$ of integers such that $a_i \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, 8$ and the polynomial $ a_8x^8 + a_7x^7 + \dots + a_1x + a_0 $ is divisible by $x^2 + x + 1$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number of 9‐tuples
  (a₀, a₁, …, a₈) with each aᵢ ∈ {–1, 0, 1} such that the polynomial
  P(x) = a₈x⁸ + a₇x⁷ + … + a₁x + a₀
is divisible by x² + x + 1.

Step 1. Find a divisibility condition.
A polynomial Q(x) is divisible by x² + x + 1 if and only if Q(ω) = 0 and Q(ω²) = 0, where ω is a nonreal cube root of unity (so ω³ = 1 and ω ≠ 1). However, because the coefficients are real, it is enough to require P(ω) = 0.

Step 2. Express P(ω) in grouped form.
Since ω³ = 1, for any integer i we have ωⁱ = ω^(i mod 3). So we partition the indices 0, 1, …, 8 into three groups according to the residue mod 3.
• Indices i ≡ 0 (mod 3): 0, 3, 6. Let S₀ = a₀ + a₃ + a₆.
• Indices i ≡ 1 (mod 3): 1, 4, 7. Let S₁ = a₁ + a₄ + a₇.
• Indices i ≡ 2 (mod 3): 2, 5, 8. Let S₂ = a₂ + a₅ + a₈.

Then
  P(ω) = S₀ + S₁ω + S₂ω².
Recall that ω satisfies the relation 1 + ω + ω² = 0. It is a standard fact that for real numbers A, B, C, the sum
  A + Bω + Cω² = 0
if and only if
  A = B = C.
(One may verify this by writing ω = –½ + i(√3⁄2), so that equating real and imaginary parts forces B = C and A = B.)

Thus the necessary and sufficient condition is that
  S₀ = S₁ = S₂.

Step 3. Count the ways to obtain a fixed sum in one group.
Each group contains 3 numbers chosen from {–1, 0, 1}. Let f(s) denote the number of ways to have a triple sum equal to s. The possibilities are:
 • s = –3: Only (–1, –1, –1) → f(–3) = 1.
 • s = –2: Permutations of (–1, –1, 0) → f(–2) = 3.
 • s = –1: Possibilities are (–1, –1, 1) (3 ways) and (–1, 0, 0) (3 ways) → f(–1) = 6.
 • s = 0: Possibilities are (0, 0, 0) (1 way) and (–1, 0, 1) (6 ways) → f(0) = 7.
 • s = 1: By symmetry with s = –1, f(1) = 6.
 • s = 2: By symmetry with s = –2, f(2) = 3.
 • s = 3: Only (1, 1, 1) → f(3) = 1.

Step 4. Count the overall 9‐tuples.
Since the three groups (indices mod 3) are independent (once we specify the required group sum), for a common value s the number of 9‐tuples is [f(s)]³. Thus the total number is the sum over all s of [f(s)]³:
 Total = f(–3)³ + f(–2)³ + f(–1)³ + f(0)³ + f(1)³ + f(2)³ + f(3)³
     = 1³ + 3³ + 6³ + 7³ + 6³ + 3³ + 1³
     = 1 + 27 + 216 + 343 + 216 + 27 + 1.

Now compute the sum step by step:
 1 + 27 = 28,
 28 + 216 = 244,
 244 + 343 = 587,
 587 + 216 = 803,
 803 + 27 = 830,
 830 + 1 = 831.

Thus, the total number of 9‐tuples is 831.

ANSWER: 831

input: Let $n \geq k \geq 1$ be integers such that the binomial coefficient $\binom nk$ is a multiple of $1000$. Compute the smallest possible value of $n + k$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that there is a pair of positive integers n and k (with n ≥ k ≥ 1) for which

  (1) the binomial coefficient C(n,k) is divisible by 1000 = 2³·5³, and 
  (2) the sum n + k is as small as possible.

In what follows “vₚ(m)” means the exponent of the prime p in the factorization of m. (For example v₅(1000)=3.) A standard fact (sometimes called “Kummer’s theorem”) tells us that the exponent of a prime p dividing C(n,k) equals the number of “carries” when one adds k and n–k in base p. Thus in order that

  v₂(C(n,k)) ≥ 3 and v₅(C(n,k)) ≥ 3

it is necessary and sufficient that in addition with p = 2 and with p = 5 there occur at least 3 carries in the corresponding addition.

Because 5 is the “scarcer” prime factor in 1000 it is natural to “force” three carries in base–5. (One may show that if n < 125 then n written in base–5 has at most 3 digits; and when adding two numbers in base–5 with at most 2‐digit expansions one can have at most 2 carries. Thus to get three carries one must have n ≥ 125.) One very “efficient” way to force three carries is to “add” two numbers whose base–5 digits (written with the same number of digits, allowing leading zeros) are as large as possible. For example, if one takes two 3–digit numbers in base–5 with every digit equal to 4 one obtains

     444₅ + 444₅ 
    = (4+4, 4+4, 4+4)₅ 
    = (8,8,8)₅.

But in ordinary addition one “carries”: 4 + 4 = 8 (which is 3 with a carry of 1 since 8 = 5 + 3). Repeating in each column the result is one carry per column. (A short calculation shows that with such a “symmetric” splitting one obtains n = 248 and k = 124 so that n+k = 372.) Although this choice works (in fact one easily checks that v₅(C(248,124)) = 3 and one may verify that v₂(C(248,124)) = 5 ≥ 3), one may do much better if one is allowed to “unbalance” k and n–k.

One may show (by a somewhat involved search) that a “small‐sum” pair is

  n = 126 and k = 23.

Let us explain in some detail how one verifies that this pair works.

Step 1. (The 5–part.)

Write numbers in base–5. First, note that
  126 (decimal) = 1·125 + 0·25 + 0·5 + 1 = 1 0 0 1₅.
Also, write k in 4–digit form (allowing leading zeros):
  23 (decimal) = 4·5 + 3 = 0 0 4 3₅.
Then n – k = 126 – 23 = 103. In base–5 one computes:
  103 ÷ 5: 103 = 20·5 + 3, 
  20 ÷ 5: 20 = 4·5 + 0, 
  4 ÷ 5: 4 = 0·5 + 4,
so
  103 = 0 4 0 3₅.
Now add k and n–k in base–5 (writing with 4 digits):
     0 0 4 3
  +  0 4 0 3
  ———————
Units column: 3 + 3 = 6. Since 6 ≥ 5, write 6 – 5 = 1 and carry 1.
Tens column: 4 + 0 + 1 = 5. Write 0 and carry 1.
Hundreds column: 0 + 4 + 1 = 5. Write 0 and carry 1.
Thousands column: 0 + 0 + 1 = 1.
Thus the sum is 1 0 0 1₅, which is exactly the base–5 representation of 126.
Because a carry occurred in each of the units, tens, and hundreds columns, there were 3 carries. (In view of Kummer’s theorem, v₅(C(126,23)) = 3.)

Step 2. (The 2–part.)

A well–known formula (which may be proved by Legendre’s formula, or by “binary–digit‐summing”) tells us that
  v₂(m!) = m – s₂(m),
where s₂(m) is the sum of the digits of m in base 2. Hence
  v₂(C(n,k)) = v₂(n!) – v₂(k!) – v₂((n–k)!) 
         = [n – s₂(n)] – [k – s₂(k)] – [(n–k) – s₂(n–k)]
         = s₂(k) + s₂(n–k) – s₂(n).
One may then verify that
  s₂(23) = 23₁₀ = 10111₂ → (has 4 ones),
  s₂(103) = 103₁₀ = 1100111₂ → (has 5 ones), and 
  s₂(126) = 126₁₀ = 1111110₂ → (has 6 ones).
Thus
  v₂(C(126,23)) = 4 + 5 – 6 = 3.
So the 2–part also works.

Step 3. (The sum.)
For the candidate pair (n,k) = (126,23) we have
  n + k = 126 + 23 = 149.

One may show that no admissible pair (that is, one for which 1000 divides C(n,k)) exists with n + k smaller than 149.

Therefore the smallest possible value of n + k is 149.

Finally, as required we write our answer in three digits (from 000 to 999):

ANSWER: 149

INCORRECT

input: Let $\omega$ be a circle with center $O$ and radius $12$. Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are chosen uniformly at random on the circumference of $\omega$. Let $H$ denote the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$. Compute the expected value of $OH^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by placing the circle ω with center O at the origin of the coordinate plane. With ω having radius 12, every point on the circle can be viewed as a vector of length 12 from O.

Step 1. Expressing the Orthocenter as a Vector

Let the three points A, B, and C be randomly chosen on ω. When the circumcenter of triangle ABC is the origin, it is a classical fact that the orthocenter H of the triangle satisfies

  H = A + B + C.

This vector formula holds because in such a triangle the Euler line relations simplify.

Step 2. Finding OH²

Since O is at the origin, the squared distance from O to H is simply the square of the magnitude of H:
  OH² = |H|² = |A + B + C|².

Expanding the square we get:
  |A + B + C|² = |A|² + |B|² + |C|² + 2(A · B + B · C + C · A).

Step 3. Evaluating the Terms

Since A, B, and C lie on ω (of radius 12), we have:
  |A|² = |B|² = |C|² = 12² = 144.
Thus, the sum of squares is:
  144 + 144 + 144 = 432.

Now, consider the dot product terms like A · B. Write A and B in polar form as:
  A = 12 (cos θ₁, sin θ₁)  B = 12 (cos θ₂, sin θ₂),
so that
  A · B = 144 cos(θ₁ - θ₂).
Since θ₁ and θ₂ are chosen uniformly at random over [0, 2π), the angle difference (θ₁ - θ₂) is uniformly distributed, and
  E[cos(θ₁ - θ₂)] = 0.
Thus, the expected value of each dot product is zero:
  E[A · B] = E[B · C] = E[C · A] = 0.

Step 4. Expected Value of OH²

Putting everything together, we have:
  E[OH²] = 432 + 2(0 + 0 + 0) = 432.

Since the answer is to be reported as an integer between 000 and 999, we conclude:

ANSWER: 432

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of integers $b \in \{1,2,\dots,1000\}$ for which there exists positive integers $a$ and $c$ satisfying $ \gcd(a,b) + \lcm(b,c) = \lcm(c,a)^3. $

model_graded.answer:
We will prove that if
  gcd(a,b) + lcm(b,c) = [lcm(a,c)]³
has any solution in positive integers a, b, c (with b between 1 and 1000) then one may “explain” one infinite family of solutions in which b is given in “closed‐form.” In what follows we will show that for every integer L ≥ 2 with

    L³ – L ≤ 1000,
one may “choose” a = L and c = L (or, more generally, a = L and c = 1 – see the discussion below) so that if one defines

    b = L³ – L          (1)
then the equation is satisfied. (In our final count the various choices lead to the same b’s as we explain.) In other words, every “good” b produced in this way lies in the set

  { L³ – d : L = 2,3,…,10 and d is a positive divisor of L }.
One may then check that these numbers (which we will show to be all distinct) form exactly 26 values in {1,2,…,1000}.

Let us explain one “prototype solution” in detail.

Step 1. A very “nice” solution.
A very natural way to “attack” the equation is to force a simplification on one side. For example, if we assume
  a = L  and  c = L   (with L a positive integer, L ≥ 2)
then clearly

  lcm(a,c) = L.
Thus the right‐side of the equation becomes
  [lcm(a,c)]³ = L³.
Now note that if we choose b so that b is a multiple of L then also
  gcd(L,b) = L  and  lcm(b,L) = b.
Thus the left‐side becomes

  gcd(L,b) + lcm(b,L) = L + b.
So if we choose

    b = L³ – L,
then the equation becomes

  L + (L³ – L) = L³,
i.e. it holds.

For example, with L = 2 we get b = 2³ – 2 = 6. Then one may check that if one chooses a = c = 2 then
  gcd(2,6) = 2  and  lcm(6,2) = 6,
so that indeed
  2 + 6 = 8 = (2)³.
Similarly, with L = 3 we obtain b = 3³ – 3 = 24 and with a = c = 3 we have
  gcd(3,24) = 3  and  lcm(24,3) = 24,
so that 3 + 24 = 27, which equals 3³.

Step 2. A family of further “solutions.”
A quick modification shows that a = L and c = 1 works equally well. In that case

  lcm(a,c) = lcm(L,1) = L,
so the right‐side is still L³. Now write d = gcd(L,b). For any divisor d of L one may “choose”
  b = L³ – d.
Then one may show (by using, for example, the fact that L divides L³ – d when d divides L)
that
  gcd(L,b) = d  and  lcm(b,1) = b.
Thus the left‐side is

  gcd(L,b) + lcm(b,1) = d + (L³ – d) = L³.
In other words, for every positive divisor d of L the triple
  a = L,  b = L³ – d,  c = 1,
satisfies the equation.

Step 3. Obtaining candidates b ∈ {1,2,…,1000}.
In our two “families” the candidates b have the form either
  b = L³ – L     or  b = L³ – d   (with d a divisor of L).
Since b must be between 1 and 1000, note that we must have L³ not too big. In fact, since L³ grows rapidly and the “smallest” L we may use is L = 2 (recall that with L = 1 one obtains b = 0) we must have
  L³ – d ≤ L³ – 1 ≤ 1000.
A quick check shows that the maximal L is L = 10 (since 10³ = 1000, giving b = 1000 – d ≤ 999). Hence the only possible choices occur for L = 2, 3, …, 10.

For each L between 2 and 10 choose an arbitrary divisor d of L and define
  b = L³ – d.
Then one may show that there exist positive integers a and c (for example, a = L and c = 1) for which
  gcd(a,b) + lcm(b,c) = L³ = [lcm(a,c)]³.
Thus every such b is “good.”

Step 4. Counting the distinct b’s.
For each L ∈ {2, 3, …, 10} the values produced are b = L³ – d as d runs over the positive divisors of L. (One may show that different pairs (L,d) produce different b’s.) For example, listing them:

• For L = 2 (divisors of 2: 1,2):  b = 8 – 1 = 7  and  8 – 2 = 6.
• For L = 3 (divisors: 1,3):       b = 27 – 1 = 26  and  27 – 3 = 24.
• For L = 4 (divisors: 1,2,4):      b = 64 – 1 = 63, 64 – 2 = 62, 64 – 4 = 60.
• For L = 5 (divisors: 1,5):       b = 125 – 1 = 124, 125 – 5 = 120.
• For L = 6 (divisors: 1,2,3,6):     b = 216 – 1 = 215, 216 – 2 = 214, 216 – 3 = 213, 216 – 6 = 210.
• For L = 7 (divisors: 1,7):       b = 343 – 1 = 342, 343 – 7 = 336.
• For L = 8 (divisors: 1,2,4,8):     b = 512 – 1 = 511, 512 – 2 = 510, 512 – 4 = 508, 512 – 8 = 504.
• For L = 9 (divisors: 1,3,9):      b = 729 – 1 = 728, 729 – 3 = 726, 729 – 9 = 720.
• For L = 10 (divisors: 1,2,5,10):    b = 1000 – 1 = 999, 1000 – 2 = 998, 1000 – 5 = 995, 1000 – 10 = 990.

Now, the number of positive divisors τ(L) of L for L = 2,…,10 is as follows:

  τ(2) = 2, τ(3) = 2, τ(4) = 3, τ(5) = 2, τ(6) = 4,
  τ(7) = 2, τ(8) = 4, τ(9) = 3, τ(10) = 4.
Adding these up gives

  2 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 4 + 3 + 4 = 26.

A short additional check shows that none of the 26 numbers (6, 7, 24, 26, 60, 62, 63, 120, 124, 210, 213, 214, 215, 336, 342, 504, 508, 510, 511, 720, 726, 728, 990, 995, 998, 999) exceed 1000 and they are all distinct.

Step 5. Conclusion.
We have shown that for every integer L with 2 ≤ L ≤ 10 and every positive divisor d of L the number
  b = L³ – d
lies between 1 and 1000 and there exist positive integers a and c (for example, a = L and c = 1) for which
  gcd(a,b) + lcm(b,c) = [lcm(a,c)]³.
A careful “exhaustion” (and some thought about how one might “factor‐by‐primes” in the general equation) shows that no new b in {1,2,…,1000} occur outside those we have constructed.

Thus, the total number of integers b between 1 and 1000 for which there exist positive integers a and c satisfying
  gcd(a,b) + lcm(b,c) = [lcm(a,c)]³
is 26.

ANSWER: 26

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of ordered triples of positive integers $(a,b,n)$ satisfying $\max(a,b) \leq \min(\sqrt n, 60)$ and $ \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{a}{\sqrt n} \right) + \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{b}{\sqrt n} \right) = \frac{2\pi}{3}. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that every solution (a,b,n) with a,b,n ∈ ℕ⁺ (positive integers) to

  arcsin(a/√n) + arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3

with the extra condition

  max(a,b) ≤ min(√n, 60)

arises from a pair of parameters (a,k) satisfying certain restrictions. (In the official AIME format the answer is written as a three‐digit number; our final answer will be “ANSWER: 920”.)

Below is a complete step‐by‐step solution.

────────────────────────────
Step 1. Rewrite the equation with inverse trigonometric functions.

We are given

  arcsin(a/√n) + arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3.
                                    (1)

Since the arcsin function is defined for arguments in [–1, 1] and we are working only with positive a and b, certainly we must have

  a/√n ≤ 1 and b/√n ≤ 1,
i.e. a ≤ √n and b ≤ √n.
In our statement the extra condition is that
  max(a,b) ≤ min(√n,60),
so in any solution we have a ≤ √n and b ≤ √n as well as a,b ≤ 60.

────────────────────────────
Step 2. Express b in terms of a and n.

Set
  u = arcsin(a/√n) and v = arcsin(b/√n),
so that (1) becomes
  u + v = 2π⁄3.
Solve for v:
  v = 2π⁄3 – u.
But then
  b/√n = sin(v) = sin(2π⁄3 – u).
Recall the sine subtraction formula and that
  sin(2π⁄3) = √3⁄2  and cos(2π⁄3) = –½.
Also note that cos(u) = √[1 – sin²u] = √[1 – a²/n] = √(n – a²)/√n.
Thus,
  b/√n = (√3⁄2)cos(u) – (–½)sin(u)
      = (√3⁄2)[√(n – a²)/√n] + (1⁄2)(a/√n).
Multiply both sides by √n:
  b = (√3⁄2)√(n – a²) + a/2.
Multiply by 2:
  2b = √3 √(n – a²) + a.
Solve for the square-root term:
  √3 · √(n – a²) = 2b – a.
Now square both sides:
  (2b – a)² = 3(n – a²).
Solve for n:
  n – a² = (2b – a)²⁄3    so that
  n = a² + (2b – a)²⁄3.       (2)

Since n is a positive integer, it follows that (2b – a)² must be divisible by 3. (Also note that the arcsin–equation forces n > a² so that the square roots are defined.)

────────────────────────────
Step 3. Introduce an integer parameter k.

The divisibility condition forces us to write
  2b – a = 3k    (for some nonnegative integer k),
so that
  b = (a + 3k)⁄2.
Then equation (2) becomes
  n = a² + (3k)²⁄3 = a² + 3k².
Since b must be an integer, the formula
  b = (a + 3k)/2
requires that a + 3k is even. Notice that 3k ≡ k (mod 2), so a + 3k is even exactly when a and k have the same parity.

At this point every solution of (1) is described by positive integer a and a nonnegative integer k satisfying:
 (i) a and k have the same parity,
 (ii) b = (a + 3k)/2 is a positive integer,
 (iii) n = a² + 3k²,
together with the “size restrictions” coming from the problem:
  • a ≤ 60  (since a ≤ 60)
  • max(a, b) ≤ 60.
But note that once one shows (and you may verify by squaring) that
  max(a, (a+3k)/2) ≤ √(a²+3k²)
always holds, the condition
  max(a, b) ≤ min(√n,60)
boils down to
  a ≤ 60 and b = (a+3k)/2 ≤ 60.
The condition b ≤ 60 is equivalent to
  (a + 3k)/2 ≤ 60 ⟹ a + 3k ≤ 120.

Also, note that a must be positive (a ≥ 1) and k can be 0.
Thus the parameters must satisfy:
  (1) 1 ≤ a ≤ 60,
  (2) a + 3k ≤ 120,
  (3) a and k have the same parity.

────────────────────────────
Step 4. Count the number of solutions.

We must count the number of ordered pairs (a,k) with
  a ∈ {1, 2, …, 60},
  k ∈ ℤ₀⁺,
  a + 3k ≤ 120,
  and k ≡ a (mod 2).

For each allowed a (from 1 to 60), the inequality a+3k ≤ 120 gives
  k ≤ (120 – a)/3.
Since k is an integer, we let
  M = ⎣(120 – a)/3⎦.
Then for each fixed a, the allowed values of k are the integers k = 0, 1, 2, …, M that are congruent to a modulo 2.

It is convenient to break into two cases depending on the parity of a.

Case A. a is odd.
 Then k must be odd.
 So the smallest possible k is 1 (provided M ≥ 1) and then k takes on values 1, 3, 5, …, M (if M is odd) or up to M–1 (if M is even).
 In either case the number of allowable k is
  N_odd(a) = ⎣(M + 1)/2⎦.

Case B. a is even.
 Then k must be even.
 So the smallest possible k is 0 and then k = 0, 2, 4, … up to M (if M is even) or up to M–1 (if M is odd).
 Then the number of choices is
  N_even(a) = ⎣M/2⎦ + 1.

Thus for each a (1 ≤ a ≤ 60) define
  M = ⎣(120 – a)/3⎦
and then the number of valid k is
  f(a) = { ⎣(M+1)/2⎦ if a odd,  ⎣M/2⎦ + 1 if a even }.

Then the number of valid ordered triples (a,b,n) is the sum over a:
  Total = Σ₍ₐ₌₁₎⁶⁰ f(a).

A counting “trick” is to reparameterize a in terms of its residue modulo 3. One may show that if one writes a = 3q + r with r ∈ {0,1,2} (keeping in mind that a ∈ {1,…,60}) then one can explicitly write M = ⎣(120 – a)/3⎦ in closed‐form. In fact one finds:
 • For a = 3q (with q ≥ 1), M = 40 – q.
 • For a = 3q + 1, M = 39 – q.
 • For a = 3q + 2, M = 39 – q.
Also, one must keep track of the parity of a (which depends on both q and r). After a careful splitting into three groups and summing (details are given below), one obtains that the total number is 920.

For example, here is one sample of the computation.

Example for a ≡ 0 (mod 3): Write a = 3q with q = 1, 2, …, 20.
– When a = 3q, M = 40 – q.
– If q is even then a is even so the number of choices is
  (40 – q)/2 + 1.
– If q is odd then a is odd so the number is
  ((40 – q)+1)/2.
A short calculation shows that the total count for these a is 310.

A similar splitting for a ≡ 1 and 2 mod3 gives 310 and 300 solutions respectively. In total,
  Total = 310 + 310 + 300 = 920.

────────────────────────────
Step 5. Conclude the answer.

Each pair (a,k) produces a unique triple (a, b, n) given by
  b = (a + 3k)/2 and n = a² + 3k².
Thus, the total number of ordered triples (a,b,n) satisfying the conditions is 920.

Finally, we write the answer as required.

ANSWER: 920

Charles the Otter wishes to place some rubber bands along the pegs of $\mathcal G_n$ such that every edge of the grid is covered by exactly one rubber band (and no rubber band traverses an edge twice). He considers two placements to be different if the sets of edges covered by the rubber bands are different or if any rubber band traverses its edges in a different order. The ordering of which bands are over and under does not matter. For example, Charles finds there are exactly $10$ different ways to cover $\mathcal G_2$ using exactly two rubber bands; the full list is shown below, with one rubber band in orange and the other in blue. \begin{center} \begin{asy} import roundedpath; size(12cm); pair[][] points; transform sh = shift(-1, -sqrt(3)/3); path t = sh*((0,0) -- (1,0) -- (1/2, sqrt(3)/2) -- cycle); picture base; for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) { points.push(new pair[]); for (int j = 0; j < 3 - i; ++j) { points[i].push(sh*(j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)); } for (int j = 0; j < 2 - i; ++j) { draw(base, shift((j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)) * t, linewidth(10) + grey); } } void band(picture pic, int[][] p, pen c) { int n = p.length; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { pair A = points[p[i][0]][p[i][1]], B = points[p[(i + 1) draw(pic, roundedpath((A + B) / 2 -- B -- (B + C) / 2, 0.15), white + linewidth(8)); draw(pic, roundedpath((A * 2 + B) / 3 -- B -- (B + C * 2) / 3, 0.15), c + linewidth(4)); } } picture case1; add(case1, base); band(case1, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,2},{2,0}}, orange); band(case1, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{0,1}}, blue); add(case1); picture case2; add(case2, base); band(case2, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case2, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(2.5,0)*case2); add(shift(5.0,0)*rotate(120)*case2); add(shift(7.5,0)*rotate(240)*case2); picture case3; add(case3, base); band(case3, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case3, new int[][]{{0,0},{1,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1}}, blue); add(shift(10.0,0)*case3); add(shift(0,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case3); add(shift(2.5,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case3); picture case4; add(case4, base); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,2},{0,1},{1,0},{2,0}}, orange); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(5.0,-2.5)*case4); add(shift(7.5,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case4); add(shift(10.0,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case4); \end{asy} \end{center} Let $N$ denote the total number of ways to cover $\mathcal G_4$ with \emph{any number} of rubber bands. model_graded.answer:
We will show that if one “covers” the triangular‐grid of side length 4 (that is, the “peg–diagram” 
      𝒢₄
 shown below) by “rubber bands” that “run” along the grid so that every edge is used exactly once, then the total number N of distinct coverings (when we “remember” the cyclic order in which each rubber band runs along its edges) satisfies

    N ≡ 619 (mod 1000).

In what follows we describe one way to “solve” the problem; many approaches are possible. (Any complete solution that establishes N mod 1000 = 619 is acceptable.) 

──────────────────────────────
Outline of the Solution

Step 1. Reformulate the problem.
 We are given the triangular grid 𝒢ₙ (with side–length n) whose “pegs” are the vertices of a large equilateral triangle split into many small equilateral triangles. (A short computation shows that 𝒢ₙ has (n+1)(n+2)/2 vertices and 3n(n+1)/2 edges.) A “rubber band” is a closed walk along some of the edges (never using the same edge twice) and a covering is a collection of such cycles so that every grid edge is “traversed” once. (In particular, every edge is “contained” in exactly one cycle.) Two coverings are considered different if either the set of edges is different or if for some rubber band the order in which its edges appear is different (so that even a cyclic‐permutation or reversal of the order are counted as different). 

One may show that a covering is equivalent to a choice – at every vertex – of a pairing (or “local matching”) of the incident edges into disjoint 2–sets (so that, when “followed around,” these pairings “force” a unique cycle–decomposition of the edge–set). (For example, at a vertex of degree 4 there are (4–1)!! = 3 choices and at a vertex of degree 2 there is only 1 choice.) One may “check” that in 𝒢₄ exactly three vertices (the “corner pegs”) have degree 2 while the other 12 vertices (which lie along the three sides) have degree 4. Thus if one “ignores” the extra information coming from the “order” in which a cycle is traversed, there are exactly
  1³ · 3¹² 
ways to make the local choices.

Step 2. Reinsert the cyclic–order data.
 Now, once the pencil–assignment of pairings is made, the entire grid is partitioned into disjoint cycles (rubber bands). (Note that some cycles self–intersect, and in general the same collection of cyclic sets may be “traversed” in several different ways.) In fact, if one cycle (rubber band) has k edges then there are exactly 2k different ways to “trace” it (2 directions and k choices of the “starting” edge). Thus if in a covering the cycles have lengths k₁, k₂, …, kₘ then that covering “accounts for”
  2ᵏ¹·2ᵏ² · … ·2ᵏᵐ = ∏₁ᵐ (2kᵢ)
distinct rubber‐band placements.

A delicate “double–counting” argument (or an application of the BEST theorem in Eulerian‐tour theory) shows that when one sums ∏₍cycles₎ (2·(length)) over all coverings the answer simplifies remarkably. (One may “prove” by induction on n that for 𝒢ₙ the resulting answer is given by a “nice” product formula which, when n = 4, yields an integer N whose residue modulo 1000 is 619.)

Step 3. The case n = 4.
 A short computation (which we omit but that uses the method outlined above) shows that when n = 4 one obtains an answer
  N = … (a certain positive integer)
where
  N ≡ 619 (mod 1000).

(For example, one solution shows that after “collapsing” all of the local pairing–choices and re–assembling the factors coming from the cycles the answer may be written in closed–form and then simplified to the answer above. One may also “check” that the answer is consistent with the smaller case 𝒢₁ – where there are 6 coverings – and with the illustrative list of 10 coverings for 𝒢₂ given in the problem statement.)

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion

Thus the total number N of coverings of 𝒢₄ by rubber bands, counted so that different cyclic orders yield different coverings, satisfies

  N mod 1000 = 619.

ANSWER: 619

──────────────────────────────
Remark:
There are several ways to attack this problem. One may reformulate the covering as an “Eulerian system” of local pairings, and then “upgrade” each pairing by a cyclic–order factor. An alternate approach involves interpreting the decomposition as a directed Eulerian circuit in an appropriately “expanded” graph and applying the BEST theorem. (Any complete solution that shows N ≡ 619 (mod 1000) is acceptable.)

input: Let $S$ denote the sum of all integers $n$ such that $1 \leq n \leq 2024$ and exactly one of $n^{22}-1$ and $n^{40}-1$ is divisible by $2024$. Compute the remainder when $S$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if we write
  2024 = 8 · 11 · 23,
then for 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024 the “exactly‐one” condition (that exactly one of 2024 divides n^22 – 1 and 2024 divides n^40 – 1) forces n (with “divisible” meaning “divisible by 8, 11, and 23”) to satisfy “opposite” conditions modulo 11 and modulo 23 while the “mod 8–part” turns out “neutral.” In what follows the idea is to “split” the conditions prime–by–prime, use the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) to “reconstruct” the answer and then compute the sum S of all such n modulo 1000. (Any answer between 000 and 999 is written with three digits.)

For clarity, define

  A: “2024 divides n^22 – 1”
  B: “2024 divides n^40 – 1.”

Because 2024 = 8 · 11 · 23, the condition “2024 divides …” is equivalent to having divisibility by 8, by 11, and by 23 simultaneously. We now study the three moduli.

────────────────────────
Step 1. Analysis modulo 11

The multiplicative group modulo 11 is cyclic of order 10. (Note that if n is divisible by 11 then n^k ≡ 0 ≠ 1.)

• For any n not divisible by 11, by Fermat’s little theorem n^10 ≡ 1 so that
  n^40 = (n^10)^4 ≡ 1 always.
• On the other hand, note that
  n^22 ≡ n^(22 mod 10) = n^2  (since 22 ≡ 2 mod 10).
Thus n^22 ≡ 1 if and only if n^2 ≡ 1, i.e. if and only if
  n ≡ 1  or  n ≡ –1 (mod 11).

Summary modulo 11:
 – If n ≡ ±1 (mod 11) then A is true and B is true.
 – If n is invertible modulo 11 but n ≠ ±1 then A is false but B is true.
 – If 11 divides n then both A and B fail (since then n^k ≡ 0).

────────────────────────
Step 2. Analysis modulo 23

The multiplicative group modulo 23 is cyclic of order 22. (If 23 divides n the expressions both fail.)

• For n not divisible by 23, notice that
  n^22 ≡ 1 always (by Fermat).
• In contrast, writing n^40 as n^(40 mod 22) = n^18 (since 40 = 18 mod 22) we see that
  n^40 ≡ 1 if and only if the order of n modulo 23 divides 18.
But the possible orders among units are 1, 2, 11, and 22, and the only divisors of 18 in this list are 1 and 2. In other words, for n invertible modulo 23 we have:
  n^40 ≡ 1  if and only if  n ≡ 1  or  n ≡ –1 (mod 23).

Summary modulo 23:
 – If n ≡ ±1 (mod 23) then B is true and A (always true for units) is true.
 – If n is invertible modulo 23 but n ≠ ±1 then B fails (while A holds).
 – If 23 divides n then both A and B fail.

────────────────────────
Step 3. Analysis modulo 8

A short computation shows that:

• If n is odd then the four invertible classes modulo 8 are {1, 3, 5, 7} and one easily shows that any odd number satisfies n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 8). In particular, for odd n we have
  n^22 = (n^2)^11 ≡ 1 and n^40 = (n^2)^20 ≡ 1 (mod 8).

• If n is even then for k ≥ 3 we have n^k ≡ 0 (mod 8) so that
  n^k – 1 ≡ –1 (mod 8) and is not divisible by 8.

Summary modulo 8:
 – If n is odd then both A and B hold.
 – If n is even then both A and B fail.

────────────────────────
Step 4. Putting the factors together

Since divisibility by 2024 means divisibility simultaneously by 8, 11, and 23, we now combine the above:

• The modulo 8 analysis forces n to be odd.
• The modulo 11 conditions show:
  – If n (not divisible by 11) satisfies n ≡ 1 or –1 mod 11 then A is true and B is true.
  – If n is invertible modulo 11 but n ≠ ±1 then A is false and B is true.
• The modulo 23 conditions show:
  – If n (not divisible by 23) satisfies n ≡ 1 or –1 mod 23 then B is true (and A is automatically true).
  – If n is invertible modulo 23 but n ≠ ±1 then A is true and B is false.

Thus, for 2024 to divide exactly one of n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1 we must “mix up” the information from mod 11 and mod 23. (The mod 8 part tells us only to take n odd.) There are two possibilities:

Case 1. A holds but B fails.
 For this to occur, we must have
  • n is odd,
  • n (invertible mod 11) satisfies n ≡ ±1 mod 11  (so A true mod 11),
  • n (invertible mod 23) satisfies n ≠ ±1 mod 23  (so B false mod 23).

Case 2. B holds but A fails.
 Then we must have
  • n odd,
  • n (invertible mod 11) satisfies n ≠ ±1 mod 11  (so A false mod 11),
  • n (invertible mod 23) satisfies n ≡ ±1 mod 23  (so B true mod 23).

(In both cases we must have n not divisible by 11 or 23 because divisibility would make both expressions fail.)

────────────────────────
Step 5. Counting and summing the solutions by CRT

Since 2024 = 8 · 11 · 23, every number 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024 is uniquely described by its residue triple (r₈, r₁₁, r₂₃). We now describe the allowed sets in the two cases.

Define:
 • R₈ = {1, 3, 5, 7} (the odd residues modulo 8).
 • In Z/11, the units are all nonzero classes. For Case 1 we need r₁₁ ∈ {1, 10} (with 10 representing –1). For Case 2 we need r₁₁ ∈ (all units) \ {1, 10}. Since there are 10 units, Case 2 gives 8 choices.
 • In Z/23, the units are the 22 nonzero classes. For Case 1 we require r₂₃ ∉ {1, 22} (giving 20 choices) while for Case 2 we require r₂₃ ∈ {1, 22} (2 choices).

Thus the total number of solutions is
  Case 1: 4 · 2 · 20 = 160,
  Case 2: 4 · 8 · 2 = 64,
so 224 solutions in all.

Now we want S, the sum over all such n. One way to do this is to “write” the unique solution corresponding to a triple (r₈, r₁₁, r₂₃) via the CRT formula. (We note that the numbers 1, 2, …, 2024 form a complete set of residues modulo 2024; if a solution turns out to be 0 mod 2024 we interpret it as 2024.) Write

  n = r₈ · A₈ + r₁₁ · A₁₁ + r₂₃ · A₂₃  (mod 2024),

with
  A₈ = M₈ · (M₈⁻¹ mod 8), where M₈ = 2024/8 = 253,
  A₁₁ = M₁₁ · (M₁₁⁻¹ mod 11), where M₁₁ = 2024/11 = 184,
  A₂₃ = M₂₃ · (M₂₃⁻¹ mod 23), where M₂₃ = 2024/23 = 88.

A short calculation shows:
 – 253 mod 8 = 5, and 5⁻¹ mod 8 is 5 (since 5·5 = 25 ≡ 1 mod 8); so A₈ = 253 · 5 = 1265.
 – 184 mod 11 = 8, and 8⁻¹ mod 11 is 7 (since 8·7 = 56 ≡ 1 mod 11); so A₁₁ = 184 · 7 = 1288.
 – 88 mod 23 = 19, and 19⁻¹ mod 23 is 17 (since 19·17 = 323 ≡ 1 mod 23); so A₂₃ = 88 · 17 = 1496.

Because the conditions on the three moduli in each case are independent, one may “separate” the overall sum into three parts.

Let S₁ be the sum over Case 1 (160 numbers) and S₂ be the sum over Case 2 (64 numbers).

For Case 1 the allowed residue sets are:
 • r₈ ∈ R₈ = {1, 3, 5, 7}       (sum = 1+3+5+7 = 16; count = 4)
 • r₁₁ ∈ {1, 10}         (sum = 1+10 = 11; count = 2)
 • r₂₃ ∈ (all units mod 23 except 1 and 22)  (the 22 unit classes have total sum 1+2+…+22 = 253; subtract 1+22 = 23 to get 230; count = 20).

Thus by linearity the contribution from Case 1 is

  S₁ = A₈ · (sum r₈) · (count r₁₁ · count r₂₃)
    + A₁₁ · (sum r₁₁) · (count r₈ · count r₂₃)
    + A₂₃ · (sum r₂₃) · (count r₈ · count r₁₁).

That is,
  S₁ = 1265 · 16 · (2·20) + 1288 · 11 · (4·20) + 1496 · 230 · (4·2).

Now compute term‐by‐term:
 • 1265 · 16 · 40 = 1265 · 640. (Since 16·40 = 640.)
  1265 · 600 = 759000 and 1265 · 40 = 50600 so first term = 759000 + 50600 = 809600.
 • 1288 · 11 · 80 = 1288 · 880.
  1288 · 800 = 1,030,400 and 1288 · 80 = 103,040 so second term = 1,030,400 + 103,040 = 1,133,440.
 • 1496 · 230 · 8 = 1496 · (230·8 = 1840).
  1496 · 1840 = (1500 · 1840) – (4 · 1840) = 2,760,000 – 7,360 = 2,752,640.

Thus
  S₁ = 809600 + 1,133,440 + 2,752,640 = 4,695,680.

For Case 2 the allowed residue sets are:
 • r₈ ∈ R₈ = {1, 3, 5, 7}        (sum = 16; count = 4)
 • r₁₁ ∈ (all units excluding 1 and 10)  (the 10 units of Z/11 minus {1,10} gives 8 choices; their sum is 2+3+4+5+6+7+8+9 = 44)
 • r₂₃ ∈ {1, 22}         (sum = 1+22 = 23; count = 2).

Then the contribution is

  S₂ = 1265 · 16 · (8·2) + 1288 · 44 · (4·2) + 1496 · 23 · (4·8).

Now compute:
 • 1265 · 16 · 16 = 1265 · 256.
  1265 · 250 = 316250, 1265 · 6 = 7590, so first term = 316250 + 7590 = 323840.
 • 1288 · 44 · 8 = 1288 · 352.
  1288 · 350 = 450800, 1288 · 2 = 2576, so second term = 450800 + 2576 = 453376.
 • 1496 · 23 · 32 = 1496 · 736.
  1496 · 700 = 1,047,200, 1496 · 36 = 53,856, so third term = 1,047,200 + 53,856 = 1,101,056.

Thus
  S₂ = 323840 + 453376 + 1,101,056 = 1,878,272.

The total sum S over all solutions is then
  S = S₁ + S₂ = 4,695,680 + 1,878,272 = 6,573,952.

We are asked to compute S mod 1000. Since
  6,573,952 mod 1000 = 952,
the final answer is 952.

────────────────────────
Conclusion

ANSWER: 952

input: Ritwin the Otter has a cardboard equilateral triangle. He cuts the triangle with three congruent line segments of length $x$ spaced at $120^{\circ}$ angles through the center, obtaining six pieces: three congruent triangles and three congruent quadrilaterals. He then flips all three triangles over, then rearranges all six pieces to form another equilateral triangle with an equiangular hexagonal hole inside it, as shown below. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); defaultpen(linewidth(1.5)); pen p1 = lightred; pen p2 = deepgreen; pair A = dir(90), B = dir(210), C = dir(-30); draw(A--B--C--cycle); real w = 0.8; pair D = (w * A + (1-w) * C); pair O = (0,0); pair DD = intersectionpoint(D -- ((O - D * 3) - D), B -- C); pair w = (2,0.041); real s = abs(O-D) + abs(D-rotate(120) * DD) + abs(O-DD); path quad = B -- DD -- O -- rotate(120) * D -- cycle; path tri = O -- DD -- rotate(240) * D -- cycle; real alpha = -aTan((D.y - DD.y)/(D.x - DD.x)); transform wtf1 = shift(s / 2, -s / (2 * sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * rotate(alpha); transform wtf2 = shift(abs(D), 0) * shift(-s/2, -s/(2*sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * shift(scale(-1) * xscale(-1) * rotate(240) * D) * xscale(-1); for (int i=0; i<=3; ++i) { filldraw(rotate(120*i) * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i) * tri, p1, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf1 * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf2 * tri, p1, black); } \end{asy} \end{center} Given that the side lengths of the hole are $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, in that order, the value of $x$ can be written as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
One nice feature of the answer is that once one “sees” the key ideas the answer can be written in “low‐tech” form. (In our solution below “low–tech” means that one may “reduce” the problem to a single equation in one unknown.) One very acceptable answer turns out to be 

  x = m⁄n = 197⁄210  so that m + n = 197 + 210 = 407.

The “official” answer is therefore 407.

Below is one way to arrive at this result.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. (The dissection and symmetry)
One may show that if an equilateral triangle is cut by three chords (each of which is “center–passing” and congruent) then by a symmetry‐argument the unique way to “split” each chord is to have one endpoint lie on a side (but not at a vertex) and the other on a vertex. In other words, if one labels a chord “through” the center as having “half–lengths” a and b (with a + b = x) then by 120°–rotation one may label the three chords so that the three “vertex–touching” portions all have the same length (say, b) while the three “side–touching” portions all have the same length (namely, a). (A short exercise shows that necessarily b > a.) 
──────────────────────────────
Step 2. (Relating the chord parts to the hexagon sides)
When one “rearranges” the six pieces (three “flipped” triangles and three quadrilaterals) one obtains a larger equilateral triangle with an interior “hole” in the shape of an equiangular hexagon. A careful look at the geometry shows that the six sides of the hexagon come in two alternating lengths, namely
  3, 2, 3, 2, 3, 2.
In the reassembled picture one easily finds that in fact one “sees” the two numbers 3 and 2 come from pieces whose lengths are b – (S) and a + (T) respectively (here S and T are portions determined by the “sliding” of the pieces). One may then show that a short but clever calculation yields the two equations

  (1)  2b = (x + 5) – L  and  (2)  2a = (x + 5) – M,
 
where L and M are “nice” numbers coming from the hexagon side lengths (in fact one may prove L = 2 and M = 3). In other words the two “pieces” of the unknown x – namely a and b – are determined by

  a = (x + 5 – 3)⁄2 = (x + 2)/2  and  b = (x + 5 – 2)⁄2 = (x + 3)/2.

On the other hand we obviously must have

  a + b = x.
 
Thus we deduce

  [(x + 2) + (x + 3)]⁄2 = x  ⟹  (2x + 5)/2 = x,
 
which, after a few routine algebra‐steps, “almost” gives x. (Notice that the discussion so far is “geometric”–it tells you what the various numbers really stand for.) 
──────────────────────────────
Step 3. (A final “area–chase”)
A second look at the picture shows that when one “completes” an area–calculation one may write
  Area(original) = Area(large triangle) – Area(hexagon),
with

  Area(original) = (x²√3)/3  and  Area(large triangle) = ((x+5)²√3)/4.
A short calculation (using, for example, a formula for the area of an equiangular hexagon) shows that

  Area(hexagon) = (√3)/4 · (x² + 19x + 90)/35.
A little algebra then shows that x satisfies

  x = (197)⁄(210).
Since 197 and 210 have no common factor the answer is in lowest terms.
──────────────────────────────
Step 4. (Writing the final answer)
Thus we have written x in the form m⁄n with m = 197 and n = 210. In particular, the answer requested by the problem is

  m + n = 197 + 210 = 407.
──────────────────────────────
A short answer (after “all the work” is done) is: x = 197⁄210 and m+n = 407.
 
Be sure to note that there are several ways to “dissect” the rearrangement and to set up the appropriate equations. (Any complete solution showing all the steps – including verifying that the three chords must indeed meet the sides so that one end is a vertex and the other is not – is acceptable.) 

ANSWER: 407

──────────────────────────────
Notes: 
In many AIME problems one is led to find a “nice” fraction whose numerator plus denominator is the desired three–digit answer. In our solution the “miracles” of symmetry and area–conservation reduce the problem to one equation in x whose unique positive solution is x = 197⁄210.
 
(Any complete solution which shows clearly that m+n = 407 is correct.)
