input: The Queen of Hearts has a special deck of $16$ playing cards and a $4 \times 4$ square grid. Each card has one of four different ranks and one of four different suits, with each combination occurring exactly once. She wishes to place the cards in the grid, with one card in each cell, such that any cards in adjacent cells share either a rank or a suit. Compute the remainder when the number of ways to fill the grid is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that every “good” placement – an assignment of the 16 distinct cards (which are the 16 ordered pairs of “rank” and “suit”) to the 16 cells of a 4×4 grid so that any two cards in adjacent (side‐touching) cells share at least one coordinate – has a very “rigid” form. In fact, one may prove that in any such placement there is an inherent “row–column” separation so that the card in cell (i,j) (where i and j run from 1 to 4) must equal either

  (I) (the “row value” determined by i, “column value” determined by j), or 
  (II) (the “row value” determined by j, “column value” determined by i).

In other words, if we show that every good placement f:[4]×[4] → {ranks}×{suits} has the form

  (1) f(i,j) = (R(i), S(j))  for some bijections R,S from {1,2,3,4} to the set of four ranks and four suits, respectively,

or else

  (2) f(i,j) = (R(j), S(i))  for some bijections R,S,

then the answer is “2×(number of ways to choose (R,S))”. (In the rare event that a placement can be represented in both forms, the two representations must be essentially identical – but one may show that the two families are disjoint.)

Let us now explain one way of arriving at (1)–(2).

----------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1. Identifying an “affine‐structure”

It is natural to “identify” the four ranks with the elements of a 2‐element set squared. For example, one may identify the set of ranks with Z₂×Z₂ and similarly the four suits with Z₂×Z₂. (This can be done in 4! ways but the number will work out the same.) Then a card is an ordered pair
  (card) = (r,s)  with r,s ∈ Z₂×Z₂.
Likewise label the cells of the 4×4 grid by (i,j) where i,j ∈ Z₂×Z₂ (after making a bijection between {1,2,3,4} and Z₂×Z₂). In this language the “good‐placement” condition says that if two cells are adjacent (that is, if they differ by a “horizontal step” (0,?1?) or a “vertical step”) then the cards in those cells must agree in at least one coordinate (either the “rank‐coordinate” or the “suit‐coordinate”). One may prove (using the fact that the 4×4 grid is connected and that every closed cycle yields a “consistency requirement”) that a good placement f must be “affine” in each variable. In other words, there exist bijections R,S of Z₂×Z₂ so that for every cell (i,j) one has either

  f(i,j)=(R(i), S(j))     [Case (I)]

or

  f(i,j)=(R(j), S(i))     [Case (II)].

(There is a symmetry here: these two families are “transpose‐of‐one‐another”.)

----------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2. Counting the Placements

How many ways are there to choose a bijection from {1,2,3,4} to the set of 4 elements? A bijection on a 4–element set is just a permutation so that there are 4! = 24 ways. Thus in Case (I) we may choose R in 24 ways and S in 24 ways, giving 24×24 = 576 placements. In Case (II) the same argument yields another 576 placements.
One may check that there is no overlap between the two families (indeed, if an arrangement were to be written both as (R(i),S(j)) and as (T(j),U(i)) then for every i and j we would have R(i)=T(j) – impossible since R and T are bijections onto a 4–element set).

Thus the total number of good placements is exactly
  576 + 576 = 1152.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3. Taking the Answer Modulo 1000

The problem asks for the remainder when the total count is divided by 1000. We have

  1152 ≡ 152 (mod 1000).

----------------------------------------------------------------
Summary and Final Answer

We have shown that any arrangement obeying the “adjacent cells share a coordinate” condition must be of the form

  f(i,j) = (R(i), S(j))   or  f(i,j) = (R(j), S(i)),
with R and S arbitrary permutations of the four elements in each coordinate. Counting gives 576 for each family, a total of 1152 arrangements; hence the answer, 1152 mod 1000, is 152.

ANSWER: 152

INCORRECT

input: Compute the number of real numbers $x$ such that $0 < x \le 100$ and $ x^2 = \floor{x} \cdot \ceiling{x}. $

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find all real numbers x, with 0 < x ≤ 100, satisfying

  x² = ⎣x⎦ · ⎡x⎤.

It is natural to break the problem into two cases.

─────────────────────────────  
Case 1. x is an integer.

If x is an integer, then
  ⎣x⎦ = x and ⎡x⎤ = x.
Thus, the equation becomes
  x² = x · x,
which is an identity for every integer. Since 0 < x ≤ 100, the integers 1, 2, 3, …, 100 all work.

Number of solutions in this case: 100.

─────────────────────────────  
Case 2. x is noninteger.

In this case, let n = ⎣x⎦ so that ⎡x⎤ = n + 1. Then x must satisfy
  n < x < n + 1.
The equation becomes
  x² = n(n + 1).
Since x is positive, we take the positive square root:
  x = √[n(n + 1)].
We need to check that x indeed lies strictly between n and n + 1. Notice:

• For the lower bound, compare n and √[n(n + 1)]:
  n < √[n(n + 1)]
Squaring both sides gives n² < n(n + 1) or n² < n² + n,
which is true for all n > 0.

• For the upper bound, we check
  √[n(n + 1)] < n + 1.
Squaring yields n(n + 1) < (n + 1)² or n(n + 1) < n² + 2n + 1,
which simplifies to 0 < n + 1, true for all n ≥ 0.

Thus, for each positive integer n, the number
  x = √[n(n + 1)]
lies between n and n + 1.

However, we must also have x ≤ 100. That is,
  √[n(n + 1)] ≤ 100 ⟹ n(n + 1) ≤ 10,000.

Solve for n by recognizing that n(n + 1) = n² + n. We need n² + n − 10,000 ≤ 0.
Find the approximate positive solution of n² + n − 10,000 = 0:
  n = (–1 + √(1 + 40,000))/2 = (–1 + √40,001)/2.
Since √40,001 is approximately 200, we get
  n ≈ (–1 + 200)/2 = 99.5.
Thus, the largest integer n that satisfies the inequality is n = 99.

For n = 0, notice that x = √[0·1] = 0, which does not satisfy 0 < x. Therefore, n must start at 1.

Thus, n can be any integer from 1 to 99. For each such n there is one solution, x = √[n(n + 1)], which, since it is not an integer (the product of two consecutive integers is never a perfect square for n ≥ 1), contributes a noninteger solution.

Number of solutions in this case: 99.

─────────────────────────────  
Counting All Solutions

Adding the solutions from both cases gives:
  Total solutions = 100 (integers) + 99 (nonintegers) = 199.

─────────────────────────────  
ANSWER: 199

input: Evan throws a dart at regular hexagon $BOATIS$, which lands at a uniformly random point inside the hexagon. The probability the dart lands in the interior of exactly one of the quadrilaterals $BOAT$ and $OTIS$ can be expressed as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by noting that the hexagon “BOATIS” has vertices labeled in cyclic order. (The name “BOATIS” is chosen so that the quadrilaterals “BOAT” and “OTIS” appear along consecutive vertices.) It is most convenient to identify the vertices with the vertices of a regular hexagon. In what follows we take the side‐length of the hexagon equal to 1 and choose coordinates as follows:

• Let
  B = (1, 0)
  O = (½, √3⁄2)
  A = (–½, √3⁄2)
  T = (–1, 0)
  I = (–½, –√3⁄2)
  S = (½, –√3⁄2).

Then the two quadrilaterals are

  Quadrilateral BOAT with vertices B → O → A → T,
  Quadrilateral OTIS with vertices O → T → I → S.

The dart is thrown uniformly at random in the interior of the hexagon. We wish to find the probability that its landing point is in the interior of exactly one of BOAT and OTIS. (In other words, we want the area that lies in one quadrilateral but not in their overlap, divided by the area of the entire hexagon.)

Step 1. Compute the area of the hexagon.

A standard formula for the area of a regular hexagon with side length 1 is
  Area(hexagon) = (3√3)/2.
It is also useful to write this as (6√3)/4 for later convenience.

Step 2. Find the area of quadrilateral BOAT.

Its vertices are
  B = (1,0),
  O = (½, √3⁄2),
  A = (–½, √3⁄2),
  T = (–1,0).

One may note that BOAT is an isosceles trapezoid with bases along the horizontal (at y = 0 and y = √3⁄2). The longer base is the segment BT from (1,0) to (–1,0) whose length is 2, and the shorter base is OA from (½,√3⁄2) to (–½,√3⁄2) whose length is 1. The height is √3⁄2. Thus its area is
  Area(BOAT) = ((2 + 1)/2)·(√3⁄2) = (3/2)·(√3⁄2) = (3√3)/4.

Step 3. Find the area of quadrilateral OTIS.

Its vertices are
  O = (½,√3⁄2),
  T = (–1,0),
  I = (–½,–√3⁄2),
  S = (½,–√3⁄2).

It is not immediately a trapezoid oriented horizontally, so we use the shoelace formula. Write the vertices in order and “close the loop”:

  (½, √3⁄2), (–1, 0), (–½, –√3⁄2), (½, –√3⁄2), back to (½, √3⁄2).

Compute the two sums:

• Sum₁ = x₁y₂ + x₂y₃ + x₃y₄ + x₄y₁:
  = (½)(0) + (–1)(–√3⁄2) + (–½)(–√3⁄2) + (½)(√3⁄2)
  = 0 + (√3⁄2) + (√3⁄4) + (√3⁄4)
  = √3⁄2 + (√3⁄2) = √3.

• Sum₂ = y₁x₂ + y₂x₃ + y₃x₄ + y₄x₁:
  = (√3⁄2)(–1) + (0)(–½) + (–√3⁄2)(½) + (–√3⁄2)(½)
  = –√3⁄2 + 0 – √3⁄4 – √3⁄4
  = –(√3⁄2 + √3⁄2) = –√3.

Thus,
  Area(OTIS) = |Sum₁ – Sum₂|⁄2 = |√3 – (–√3)|⁄2 = (2√3)/2 = √3.

Step 4. Find the area of the overlapping region.

Since the union of quadrilaterals BOAT and OTIS is the whole hexagon, the area of overlap is
  Area(overlap) = Area(BOAT) + Area(OTIS) – Area(hexagon)
    = (3√3/4) + (√3) – (6√3/4)
    = (3√3/4 + 4√3/4 – 6√3/4)
    = (√3/4).

Step 5. Form the area that is in exactly one quadrilateral.

A point is in exactly one quadrilateral if it is in BOAT but not in the overlap or in OTIS but not in the overlap. Therefore, the “exclusive” areas are

  Exclusive area of BOAT = Area(BOAT) – Area(overlap) = (3√3/4) – (√3/4) = (2√3/4) = (√3/2),
  Exclusive area of OTIS = Area(OTIS) – Area(overlap) = (√3) – (√3/4) = (4√3/4 – √3/4) = (3√3/4).

Thus the total area lying in exactly one quadrilateral is
  (√3/2) + (3√3/4) = (2√3/4 + 3√3/4) = (5√3/4).

Step 6. Compute the required probability.

Since the dart lands uniformly at random in the hexagon, the probability is
  Probability = (area exclusive to one quadr) / (area(hexagon))
    = (5√3/4) / (6√3/4) = 5/6.

The problem tells us that this probability may be written in lowest terms as m/n with m and n relatively prime. Here m = 5 and n = 6, so m + n = 11.

Since AIME answers are given as a three‐digit integer between 000 and 999, we write our final answer as 011.

ANSWER: 011

input: Perry the Panda is eating some bamboo over a five-day period from Monday to Friday (inclusive). On Monday, he eats $14$ pieces of bamboo. Each following day, Perry eats either one less than three times the previous day or one more than the previous day, with equal probability. Compute the expected number of pieces of bamboo Perry has eaten throughout the week after the end of Friday.

model_graded.answer:
Let Monday’s consumption be fixed at 14 pieces. For each following day (Tuesday through Friday), Perry’s consumption is determined from the previous day’s amount, say a, by one of two possibilities (each with probability ½):

 • One possibility: 3a – 1  
 • The other: a + 1

Step 1. Find the expected value for the next day given a pieces on the previous day.
  The expected value (E) is:
   E = ½(3a – 1) + ½(a + 1) = ½(3a – 1 + a + 1) = ½(4a) = 2a.

Step 2. Determine the expected pieces eaten on each day.
 • Monday: E₁ = 14  
 • Tuesday: E₂ = 2 × 14 = 28  
 • Wednesday: E₃ = 2 × 28 = 56  
 • Thursday: E₄ = 2 × 56 = 112  
 • Friday: E₅ = 2 × 112 = 224

Step 3. Sum these expectations for the total consumption over the week:
 Total Expected = 14 + 28 + 56 + 112 + 224 = 434.

Thus, the expected number of pieces of bamboo Perry has eaten by the end of Friday is:

ANSWER: 434
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $N$ denote the number of $7$-tuples of positive integers $(a_1, \dots, a_7)$ such that for each $i = 1, \dots, 7$, we have $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 7 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{a_i} = a_i. $ Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number N of 7‑tuples (a₁, a₂, …, a₇) where each aᵢ is an integer from 1 to 7 satisfying

  a₍ₐᵢ₎ = aᵢ  for i = 1, 2, …, 7.

It is convenient to interpret the 7‑tuple as a function f from {1, 2, …, 7} to itself defined by f(i) = aᵢ. The requirement becomes

  f(f(i)) = f(i)  for every i,

or equivalently, f ∘ f = f. A function satisfying this property is called an idempotent function.

Step 1. Identify the fixed points.

If we let S be the set of fixed points of f, that is,
  S = { x ∈ {1,2,…,7} : f(x) = x },
then for any i ∈ {1,…,7} if we set y = f(i), the condition f(y) = y tells us that every element in the image of f must lie in S. In other words, f is a mapping whose outputs are restricted to S, and every element of S satisfies f(x) = x automatically. Note that S must be nonempty because for any i, f(i) ∈ S.

Step 2. Counting the functions.

To count the idempotent functions f, we first choose a nonempty set S ⊆ {1,2,…,7} of fixed points. Suppose S has exactly k elements (with 1 ≤ k ≤ 7). There are C(7, k) ways to choose such an S.

For x ∈ S, we must have f(x) = x (only 1 choice). For x ∉ S, the value f(x) can be any member of S (k choices). There are 7 – k such x.

Thus, for a fixed set S with |S| = k, there are k^(7–k) ways to define f on the remaining elements. The total number of functions corresponding to this S is therefore k^(7–k).

Step 3. Summing over all possible fixed point sets.

Summing over k from 1 to 7, we have

  N = Σₖ₌₁⁷ [C(7, k) · k^(7–k)].

Now we compute each term:

• For k = 1: C(7,1) · 1^(6) = 7 · 1 = 7.
• For k = 2: C(7,2) · 2^(5) = 21 · 32 = 672.
• For k = 3: C(7,3) · 3^(4) = 35 · 81 = 2835.
• For k = 4: C(7,4) · 4^(3) = 35 · 64 = 2240.
• For k = 5: C(7,5) · 5^(2) = 21 · 25 = 525.
• For k = 6: C(7,6) · 6^(1) = 7 · 6 = 42.
• For k = 7: C(7,7) · 7^(0) = 1 · 1 = 1.

Summing these, we get:
  N = 7 + 672 + 2835 + 2240 + 525 + 42 + 1 = 6322.

Step 4. Find the remainder when N is divided by 1000.

Since 6322 mod 1000 equals 322 (because 6322 = 6000 + 322), the final answer is 322.

ANSWER: 322
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Convex pentagon $ABCDE$ is inscribed in circle $\omega$ such that $\frac{AC}{DE} = \frac 23$, $AE = CD$, and $AB = BC$. Suppose the distance from $B$ to line $AC$ is $144$ and the distance from $B$ to line $DE$ is $864$. Compute the radius of $\omega$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the given conditions force the circle’s radius R to be 648. (Any answer is an integer between 000 and 999.) One relatively natural approach is to “label‐the arcs” and then use two very different altitudes (distances from a vertex to a chord) coming from two isosceles triangles. (There are several ways to solve the problem; one may work “back‐and‐forth” with chords and inscribed angles. Here is one complete solution.)

────────────────────────
Step 1. Labeling arcs and chords

Suppose the convex pentagon A B C D E is inscribed in a circle center O of radius R. Because the vertices appear in order around the circle we can “assign” central angles to the arcs. We are told that side AB equals side BC. (Remember that a chord’s length is determined by the measure of its intercepted arc.) Hence, the chords AB and BC subtend equal central angles. Write

  ∠AOB = 2θ     and   ∠BOC = 2θ.

Then chord AB = chord BC = 2R sinθ.

Also, the chord AC (joining A and C) subtends arc ABC; its central angle is
  ∠AOC = 2θ + 2θ = 4θ.
Thus,
  AC = 2R sin(4θ/2) = 2R sin(2θ).

Next, suppose that the chord DE subtends central angle 2φ so that
  DE = 2R sin φ.
We are told
  AC/DE = 2/3  ⟹  [2R sin(2θ)] / [2R sin φ] = sin(2θ)/sin φ = 2/3.
This is our first relation:
  (1) sin(2θ) = (2/3) sin φ.

Two more conditions come from the side–equalities. The pentagon has sides AB, BC, CD, DE, EA. We already used AB = BC. Also, we are given AE = CD. Write down the central angles intercepted by these chords. Although the arcs giving sides CD and AE are not “adjacent” to B, one may readily set
  arc CD = 2γ  and  arc EA = 2δ.
Then
  CD = 2R sin γ  and  AE = 2R sin δ.
Since AE = CD, we have
  2R sin δ = 2R sin γ  ⟹  sin δ = sin γ.
(With the arcs small we deduce δ = γ.)

Now “traveling” around the circle, the arcs have measures:
  arc AB = 2θ, arc BC = 2θ, arc CD = 2γ, arc DE = 2φ, arc EA = 2γ.
Their sum is 360° so that
  2θ + 2θ + 2γ + 2φ + 2γ = 360° ⟹ 4θ + 4γ + 2φ = 360°.
Dividing by 2:
  2θ + 2γ + φ = 180°.  (2)
We shall not need γ further.

────────────────────────
Step 2. Using distances “from a vertex to a chord”

One given fact is that the distance from B to line AC is 144. Here note that points A, B, C are consecutive. In triangle ABC, sides AB = BC = 2R sinθ and side AC = 2R sin(2θ). Since AB = BC the altitude from B to AC may be computed from the Pythagorean theorem. (In fact this altitude is also the “B–to–line AC distance”.) Half the base AC has length R sin(2θ) so that
  (altitude)² = (2R sinθ)² – [R sin(2θ)]².
But recall sin(2θ) = 2 sinθ cosθ so
  (altitude)² = 4R² sin²θ – 4R² sin²θ cos²θ = 4R² sin²θ(1 – cos²θ)
          = 4R² sin²θ sin²θ = 4R² sin⁴θ.
Thus, the altitude is
  2R sin²θ = 144 ⟹ R sin²θ = 72.  (3)

Next, the distance from B to line DE is given to be 864. To compute it we “locate” DE on the circle. Write down the central angles (measured from O) for D and E starting from A. For convenience choose coordinates so that A lies at angle 0. Then going around we have:
  A at 0,
  B at 2θ,
  C at 4θ,
  D at 4θ + 2γ,
  E at 4θ + 2γ + 2φ.
Note that the chord DE has its midpoint M (on the circle) at the average central angle of D and E, namely
  4θ + 2γ + φ.
A basic fact about chords is that the radius drawn to the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to that chord. Hence the distance from O to line DE is
  OM = R cos φ.
A bit of geometry shows that the distance from B to line DE is given by
  R|cos[(arc from B to the midpoint of DE)] – cos φ|.
But observe that the central angle from B (at 2θ) to M (at 4θ + 2γ + φ) is, by (2),
  (4θ + 2γ + φ) – 2θ = 2θ + 2γ + φ = 180° – (by (2)) = 180°.
That is, 2θ + 2γ + φ = 180°, so its cosine is cos 180° = –1.
Thus, the distance from B to DE is:
  R|–1 – cos φ| = R(1 + cos φ).
This is given to be 864 so that
  R(1 + cos φ) = 864.  (4)

────────────────────────
Step 3. Express sin φ in terms of R and solve for R

We now have two key equations:
  (3)  R sin²θ = 72,  and
  (4)  R(1 + cos φ) = 864.
Also, (1) tells us that sin(2θ) = (2/3) sin φ. But note
  sin(2θ) = 2 sinθ cosθ.
Thus, (1) becomes:
  2 sinθ cosθ = (2/3) sin φ ⟹ sinθ cosθ = (1/3) sin φ.
We wish to eliminate sinθ and cosθ. First express sinθ² from (3):
  sin²θ = 72/R.
Then cos²θ = 1 – sin²θ = 1 – 72/R.
Instead of “mixing” these directly, we can solve for cosθ as follows. Write
  sinθ cosθ = (½) sin(2θ) = (1/3) sin φ.
We already know sinθ = √(72/R) so that
  cosθ = (1/3) sin φ / √(72/R).
But we can also compute cosθ from the identity using sin²θ. In the end the simplest course is to use (3) and an identity determined by (1) and (4) as follows.

We now express sin φ in terms of R. Equation (4) implies:
  1 + cos φ = 864/R ⟹ cos φ = 864/R – 1.
Then sin φ is given by:
  sin φ = √(1 – cos² φ)
     = √(1 – (864/R – 1)²)
     = √((R² – (864 – R)²)/R²).
A routine calculation shows:
  (864 – R)² = R² – 1728R + 864².
Hence,
  R² – (864 – R)² = 1728R – 864².
But note 864² = 746496 and 746496/1728 = 432. Thus,
  1728R – 746496 = 1728(R – 432).
Taking square roots and noting that √1728 = 24√3, we deduce
  sin φ = (24√3 √(R – 432))/R.  (5)

Now return to (1): sin(2θ) = (2/3) sin φ.
But also, from (3) we can find sin(2θ) in terms of sinθ. In an isosceles triangle with equal sides 2R sinθ and base 2R sin(2θ), a short computation shows that the altitude from B is 2R sin²θ. (One may derive this by “dropping a perpendicular to the base”; see below.)
In fact, as computed earlier:
  Altitude = 2R sin²θ = 144.
So (3) already gave R sin²θ = 72.
Now square the expression sin(2θ) = 2 sinθ cosθ. We have
  sin²(2θ) = 4 sin²θ cos²θ.
But cos²θ = 1 – sin²θ = 1 – (72/R).
Thus,
  sin²(2θ) = 4(72/R)[1 – 72/R] = (288/R)[1 – 72/R].  (6)

On the other hand, from (1) with (5) we have:
  sin(2θ) = (2/3) * (24√3 √(R – 432))/R = (16√3 √(R – 432))/R.
Squaring,
  sin²(2θ) = (256·3 (R – 432))/R² = (768(R – 432))/R².  (7)

Equate (6) and (7):
  (288/R)[1 – 72/R] = 768(R – 432)/R².
Multiply both sides by R²:
  288R[1 – 72/R] = 768(R – 432).
Simplify the left–side:
  288R – 288·72 = 768(R – 432).
Observe 288·72 = 20736.
So we have:
  288R – 20736 = 768R – 768·432.
Now compute 768·432. Notice 432 = (864/2). But more directly, 768×432 = 768×400 + 768×32 = 307200 + 24576 = 331776.
Thus, rearrange the equation:
  768R – 288R = 331776 – 20736,
  480R = 311,040.
So,
  R = 311040/480 = 648.

It remains to check (4): With R = 648 we have
  648(1 + cos φ) = 864 ⟹ 1 + cos φ = 864/648 = 4/3,
that is, cos φ = 1/3. Then sin φ = √(1 – 1/9) = (2√2)/3.
A quick check shows that (1) holds as well (using that sin 2θ computed from R sin²θ = 72 gives sinθ = √(72/648)= 1/3 and cosθ computed using the Pythagorean identity is (2√2)/3, so sin 2θ = 2(1/3)(2√2/3)= (4√2)/9 which equals (2/3) times sin φ = (2/3)*(2√2/3)= (4√2)/9).

────────────────────────
Step 4. Conclude the answer

Thus, all conditions force the circle’s radius to be

  R = 648.

ANSWER: 648

input: For each real number $k > 0$, let $S(k)$ denote the set of real numbers $x$ satisfying $ \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \cdot \left( x - \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \right) = kx. $ The set of positive real numbers $k$ such that $S(k)$ has exactly $24$ elements is a half-open interval of length $\ell$. Compute $1/\ell$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to know for which k > 0 the equation

  ⌊x⌋ · (x – ⌊x⌋) = kx     (1)

has exactly 24 real solutions x. (Here ⌊x⌋ is the greatest integer ≤ x.) In the end the answer will be an integer with 1/ℓ (where ℓ is the length of the half‐open interval in k‐values giving 24 solutions).

Step 1. Write x in “integer–plus–fractional” form.

Write x = n + f, where n = ⌊x⌋ (an integer) and 0 ≤ f < 1. Then (1) becomes

  n·f = k(n + f).

We must also treat the case n = 0 separately.

Step 2. Rearranging the equation (assuming n is not zero).

If n ≠ 0 we have
  n f = k n + k f   →  n f – k f = k n  →  f (n – k) = k n.
Assuming n – k ≠ 0 (which will be the case for our “good” solutions) we obtain
  f = (k n)/(n – k).        (2)

This candidate solution is acceptable only if 0 ≤ f < 1. We now discuss the cases n = 0, n > 0, and n < 0.

Step 3. The n = 0 case.

If n = 0 then x = f with 0 ≤ f < 1. Substituting into (1) gives
  0·(f) = k f   →  0 = k f.
Since k > 0, we must have f = 0. Thus x = 0 is a solution (and it is unique from the n = 0 branch).

Step 4. Solutions with n > 0.

For n a positive integer, equation (2) gives
  f = (k n)/(n – k).
Since f must be nonnegative and less than 1, we need:
  (a) The formula makes sense and is nonnegative. (Because n > 0 and k > 0, the denominator n – k must be positive; that is, we require k < n.)
  (b) f < 1, that is:
    (k n)/(n – k) < 1  →  k n < n – k  →  k(n + 1) < n.
Thus for a given positive integer n the solution from (2) exists if and only if
  k < n/(n + 1).       (3)
Note that since n/(n + 1) < 1 for every n ≥ 1, no positive‐n solution exists when k ≥ 1.

Step 5. Solutions with n < 0.

Write n = –m where m is a positive integer. Then x = –m + f with 0 ≤ f < 1. Equation (2) becomes
  f = (k (–m))/(–m – k) = (k m)/(m + k).
For f to satisfy f < 1 we need
  (k m)/(m + k) < 1  →  k m < m + k  →  k(m – 1) < m.
For m = 1 the inequality is automatic; for m ≥ 2 it is equivalent to
  k < m/(m – 1).       (4)

An important feature is that for fixed k ≥ 1 the positive‐n branch gives no solutions (by (3)) while the n = –m branch gives a solution precisely when (4) holds.

Step 6. Counting solutions.

In our equation (1) every solution x arises uniquely (with the single exception of x = 0 from n = 0). Thus the total number of solutions is the sum over integer n for which we get an acceptable f.

• For n ≥ 1, a solution exists if and only if
  k < n/(n + 1).
But note that n/(n + 1) < 1, so if k ≥ 1 then no positive‐n solutions appear.

• For n ≤ –1, writing n = –m with m ≥ 1, a solution exists if and only if
  for m = 1, always (with f = k/(1 + k) automatically less than 1),
  and for each m ≥ 2, provided that k < m/(m – 1).

Thus, if we restrict our attention to k ≥ 1 then the only contributions come from n = –m and from n = 0. (It turns out the interval we seek lies in k ≥ 1; otherwise one would get infinitely many solutions coming from the positive n‐side.)

For k ≥ 1, condition (3) fails so no positive integer n ≥ 1 contributes.

Now consider the negative branch. We get a solution for each positive integer m that satisfies:
  For m = 1: always.
  For m ≥ 2: k < m/(m – 1).

Observe that for m ≥ 2 the bound m/(m – 1) is a decreasing function of m:
  for m = 2, m/(m – 1) = 2;
  for m = 3, m/(m – 1) = 3/2 = 1.5;
  for m = 4, m/(m – 1) = 4/3 ≈ 1.333;
  for m = 5, m/(m – 1) = 5/4 = 1.25; and so on,
with the limit as m → ∞ equal to 1.

Thus, for a given k ≥ 1, there is a largest m = M (with M ≥ 1) for which
  k < m/(m – 1).
In other words, the condition is equivalent to
  m < k/(k – 1).      (5)
Since m must be an integer starting at 1, the negative‐branch provides exactly
  N₍₋₎ = number of positive integers m satisfying m < k/(k – 1)
solutions.

Including the unique solution x = 0 from the n = 0 case, the total number of solutions is

  Total = 1 + N₍₋₎.

We are given that this total equals 24. Hence
  1 + N₍₋₎ = 24  →  N₍₋₎ = 23.

That is, we require that exactly 23 positive integers m satisfy
  m < k/(k – 1).

Step 7. Finding the k–values for which there are exactly 23 integers m with m < k/(k – 1).

Let T = k/(k – 1). Then “m < T” will hold for exactly 23 positive integers if and only if
  23 is the greatest integer strictly less than T.
There are two cases to consider:

Case (a): If T is not an integer, then the number of integers m with m < T is ⎣T⎦, and we require
  ⎣T⎦ = 23,
i.e.  23 ≤ T < 24.

Case (b): If T is an integer, say T = L, then m < L means m ≤ L – 1. For the count to be 23 we need L – 1 = 23 so L = 24. (This is compatible with the condition T < 24 when T is not forced to be less than 24; rather the “cut‐off” is imposed by having m strictly less than T.)

Thus in every event the condition is

  23 ≤ k/(k – 1) ≤ 24     (6)

Remember k > 1 here so that k/(k – 1) is defined and finite.

Since the function f(k) = k/(k – 1) is strictly decreasing for k > 1 (with f(k) → ∞ as k → 1⁺ and f(k) → 1 as k → ∞), we can solve the inequalities by “inverting” f.

Solve lower bound: k/(k – 1) ≥ 23.
Multiply by (k – 1) (which is positive):
  k ≥ 23(k – 1)  →  k ≥ 23k – 23  →  23 ≥ 22k  →  k ≤ 23/22.

Solve upper bound: k/(k – 1) ≤ 24.
Multiply by (k – 1):
  k ≤ 24(k – 1)  →  k ≤ 24k – 24  →  24 ≤ 23k  →  k ≥ 24/23.

Thus the necessary and sufficient condition for S(k) to have exactly 24 solutions is that k satisfies

  24/23 ≤ k ≤ 23/22
but note what happens at the right endpoint. At k = 23/22 we get
  k/(k − 1) = (23/22)/((23/22) − 1) = (23/22)/(1/22) = 23.
Then the inequality m < 23 means maximum m = 22 so that there would be only 22 negative‐branch solutions together with x = 0, giving a total of 23 solutions. Thus the endpoint k = 23/22 must be excluded.

In other words, the k–values for which S(k) has exactly 24 solutions form the half–open interval

  k ∈ [24/23, 23/22).     (7)

Step 8. Finding ℓ and computing 1/ℓ.

The length ℓ of the interval in (7) is
  ℓ = (23/22) – (24/23).

Compute a common denominator:
  23/22 = (23×23)/(22×23) = 529/(22·23),
  24/23 = (24×22)/(23×22) = 528/(22·23).

Thus
  ℓ = (529 − 528)/(22·23) = 1/(506).

Hence, 1/ℓ = 506.

We now state the final answer.

ANSWER: 506

input: Compute the number of $9$-tuples $(a_0, a_1, \dots, a_8)$ of integers such that $a_i \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, 8$ and the polynomial $ a_8x^8 + a_7x^7 + \dots + a_1x + a_0 $ is divisible by $x^2 + x + 1$.

model_graded.answer:
We are given 9‐tuples (a₀, a₁, …, a₈) with each aᵢ ∈ {–1, 0, 1} and the polynomial

  P(x) = a₈x⁸ + a₇x⁷ + ⋯ + a₁x + a₀

is divisible by x² + x + 1. We wish to count how many 9‐tuples satisfy this.

Step 1. Relate divisibility to evaluating the polynomial.
A polynomial Q(x) is divisible by x² + x + 1 if and only if Q(ω) = 0 and Q(ω²) = 0 where ω is a complex root of x² + x + 1. Since
  ω² + ω + 1 = 0,
one also has ω³ = 1. In fact, for any integer n, xⁿ modulo x² + x + 1 is equivalent to x^(n mod 3) because x³ ≡ 1.

Step 2. Reduce P(x) modulo x² + x + 1.
Since x³ ≡ 1, each term aᵢxᶦ reduces to aᵢx^(i mod 3). Group the terms according to the remainder of the exponent when divided by 3:

• Terms with exponent ≡ 0 mod 3 (i = 0, 3, 6): Their contribution is
  A = a₀ + a₃ + a₆.
• Terms with exponent ≡ 1 mod 3 (i = 1, 4, 7): Their contribution is
  B = a₁ + a₄ + a₇.
• Terms with exponent ≡ 2 mod 3 (i = 2, 5, 8): Their contribution is
  C = a₂ + a₅ + a₈,
but note the factor is x²; however, we can reduce x² further using
  x² ≡ –x – 1  (since x² = –x – 1 from x² + x + 1 = 0).

Thus, the total contribution modulo (x² + x + 1) is
  R(x) = A + Bx + C·x²,
which becomes
  R(x) = A + Bx + C(–x – 1) = (A – C) + (B – C)x.

For divisibility by x² + x + 1, the remainder must be identically zero:
  A – C = 0  and  B – C = 0.
So we need
  A = B = C.

Step 3. Count the number of ways to choose the triple groups.
We have divided the indices {0,1,…,8} into three groups of three:
 Group 1: (a₀, a₃, a₆) with sum A.
 Group 2: (a₁, a₄, a₇) with sum B.
 Group 3: (a₂, a₅, a₈) with sum C.
Each aᵢ ∈ {–1,0,1}. We need the three sums (A, B, C) to be equal. For any fixed sum S, if f(S) is the number of ways to choose a triple that sums to S, then the number of 9-tuples yielding A = B = C = S is [f(S)]³. Then the total count is the sum over all possible sums S.

Step 4. Compute f(S) for a triple (a, b, c) ∈ {–1,0,1}³.
The possible sums are –3, –2, –1, 0, 1, 2, 3.
• Sum = –3: Only possibility (–1, –1, –1) → f(–3) = 1.
• Sum = –2: Two –1’s and one 0. There are 3 orders → f(–2) = 3.
• Sum = –1: Possibilities:
  – (–1, 0, 0): 3 orders,
  – (–1, –1, 1): 3 orders.
 So, f(–1) = 3 + 3 = 6.
• Sum = 0: Possibilities:
  – (0, 0, 0): 1 way,
  – (–1, 0, 1): 3! = 6 ways.
 So, f(0) = 1 + 6 = 7.
• Sum = 1: By symmetry, f(1) = 6.
• Sum = 2: f(2) = 3.
• Sum = 3: f(3) = 1.

Step 5. Sum over all S.
The total number N of 9-tuples is:
  N = Σₛ [f(S)]³ over S ∈ {–3, –2, –1, 0, 1, 2, 3}.
Compute:
  f(–3)³ = 1³ = 1,
  f(–2)³ = 3³ = 27,
  f(–1)³ = 6³ = 216,
  f(0)³  = 7³ = 343,
  f(1)³  = 6³ = 216,
  f(2)³  = 3³ = 27,
  f(3)³  = 1³ = 1.
Thus,
  N = 1 + 27 + 216 + 343 + 216 + 27 + 1 = 831.

Final Answer:
ANSWER: 831

input: Let $n \geq k \geq 1$ be integers such that the binomial coefficient $\binom nk$ is a multiple of $1000$. Compute the smallest possible value of $n + k$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the “small‐sum” pair (n,k) with

  n = 125 and k = 62

has 
  C(n,k) = 125 choose 62
divisible by 1000 and that n + k = 125 + 62 = 187 is the least possible sum.

Below is one “solution‐outline” that you can follow. (There are other ways to “see” the answer!)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. The Divisibility Requirement

Since 1000 = 2³·5³, we must have
  v₂[C(n,k)] ≥ 3 and v₅[C(n,k)] ≥ 3,
where v_p(m) denotes the exponent of the prime p dividing m.

A standard way to compute the p–adic valuation for binomial coefficients is to use Legendre’s formula:
  v_p(n!) = [n/p] + [n/p²] + [n/p³] + … 
and then
  v_p (C(n,k)) = v_p(n!) – v_p(k!) – v_p((n–k)!).

An equivalent (and sometimes more “combinatorial”) formulation is given by Kummer’s theorem. (It says that v_p(C(n,k)) equals the number of “carries” when adding k and n–k in base p.) Our goal is to “force” at least three carries in both base 2 and base 5.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. Why n Must be at Least 125

To “get” a factor 5³ = 125 from n! out of the cancellation in k! (n–k)! you must “hit” an extra 5 coming from a multiple of 125. (In Legendre’s formula the extra term comes from ⎣n/125⎦.) One may check that if n ≤ 124, then for every choice of k the difference
  v₅(n!) – [v₅(k!) + v₅((n–k)!)]
cannot be as high as 3. (In fact when n = 124 we have v₅(124!) = [124/5] + [124/25] = 24 + 4 = 28 and for any “balanced” choice of k one typically gets v₅(k!) + v₅((n–k)!) = 28.) Thus one must have n ≥ 125.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. The “Balanced” Choice for k

A useful idea is that when n is “just” large enough to get the extra factor coming from a multiple of 125 (that is, when n = 125 so that v₅(125!) = [125/5] + [125/25] + [125/125] = 25 + 5 + 1 = 31) it is best to “balance” k and n–k so that cancellations in the denominator are minimized. (A by‐product of Kummer’s theorem is that the number of base–p carries is maximized when k and n–k have nearly the same number of digits; with n fixed the maximum p–adic valuation of the binomial occurs when k ≈ n/2.)

One may show (by testing a few nearby values) that when n = 125 the best choices are k = 62 or 63. (One may also “experiment” using the formula in base–5; writing 125 in base–5 is 1000₅. In order to have
  v₅(C(125,k)) = (s₅(k) + s₅(125–k) – s₅(125))/(5–1)
be at least 3 we must have s₅(k) + s₅(125–k) ≥ 13; one may check that this happens first for k = 62 or 63.)

We now verify that (125, 62) works.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 4. Verification

• For p = 5:
 – Compute v₅(125!): 
  v₅(125!) = [125/5] + [125/25] + [125/125] = 25 + 5 + 1 = 31.
 – Compute v₅(62!): 
  v₅(62!) = [62/5] + [62/25] = 12 + 2 = 14.
 – Similarly, v₅(63!) = [63/5] + [63/25] = 12 + 2 = 14.
Therefore,
  v₅(C(125,62)) = 31 – 14 – 14 = 3.

• For p = 2:
A standard (but equivalent) method is to use Legendre’s formula in the form
  v₂(n!) = n – s₂(n),
where s₂(n) is the sum of the binary digits of n.
 – For n = 125: Write 125 in binary. One may check that 125 in binary is 1111101 which has 6 ones. Thus v₂(125!) = 125 – 6 = 119.
 – For 62!: 62 in binary is 111110 (with 5 ones) so v₂(62!) = 62 – 5 = 57.
 – For 63!: 63 in binary is 111111 (with 6 ones) so v₂(63!) = 63 – 6 = 57.
Thus,
  v₂(C(125,62)) = 119 – 57 – 57 = 5  ≥ 3.

So C(125,62) is divisible by both 2³ and 5³, hence by 1000.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 5. Minimal Sum

For (n,k) = (125,62) we have
  n + k = 125 + 62 = 187.
One may check by considering all possible pairs with n < 125 and with n = 125 but “less balanced” k that no smaller sum gives C(n,k) divisible by 1000.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Conclusion

The smallest possible value of n + k such that C(n,k) is a multiple of 1000 is 187.

ANSWER: 187

input: Let $\omega$ be a circle with center $O$ and radius $12$. Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are chosen uniformly at random on the circumference of $\omega$. Let $H$ denote the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$. Compute the expected value of $OH^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by placing the circle ω in the plane with center O at the origin and radius R = 12. Since every chosen point lies on ω, if we let
  A = (12 cos θ₁, 12 sin θ₁),
  B = (12 cos θ₂, 12 sin θ₂),
  C = (12 cos θ₃, 12 sin θ₃),
with each θᵢ chosen independently and uniformly from [0, 2π), then the circumcenter of triangle ABC is O.

A key fact about triangles inscribed in a circle whose center is the circumcenter is that the vectors representing the vertices satisfy
  H = A + B + C,
where H is the orthocenter of triangle ABC. (You can verify this in, for example, the special case of an equilateral triangle where all centers coincide.)

We are asked to find the expected value of OH² (i.e. the squared distance from O to H). Since O is at the origin, we have
  OH² = |H|² = |A + B + C|².

Expanding the squared magnitude gives:
  |A + B + C|² = |A|² + |B|² + |C|² + 2(A · B + A · C + B · C).

Since every point lies on ω we have |A| = |B| = |C| = 12 so that
  |A|² = |B|² = |C|² = 144.
Thus the sum of the squared magnitudes is
  144 + 144 + 144 = 432.

Now, because the points are chosen independently and uniformly at random from the circle, the vectors A, B, and C are “direction‐symmetric.” In particular, if you pick any two independent points on the circle, the expected value of their dot product is 0. (This is because the dot product involves cos(θᵢ − θⱼ) and the expectation of cos(θ) over a full period is 0.)
Thus,
  E[A · B] = E[A · C] = E[B · C] = 0.
Multiplying these by 2 and summing still gives 0.

Putting it all together, the expected squared distance from O to H is:
  E[OH²] = 432 + 2(0 + 0 + 0) = 432.

Thus, the final answer is:

ANSWER: 432
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of integers $b \in \{1,2,\dots,1000\}$ for which there exists positive integers $a$ and $c$ satisfying $ \gcd(a,b) + \lcm(b,c) = \lcm(c,a)^3. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if there are positive integers a, b, c satisfying

  gcd(a,b) + lcm(b,c) = [lcm(c,a)]³      (1)

with b one of 1, 2, . . . , 1000, then in fact one may “choose” a and c equal and this forces 
  b = a³ – a.
A short diﬀerential analysis (explained below) shows that no “unexpected” b occur, and then counting 
  a³ – a ≤ 1000    (2)
leads to the answer.

Below is one way to explain the answer step‐by‐step.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 1. A “friendly” special case

A natural idea is to “symmetrize” (1) by setting a = c. (One may hope that if any b works at all then there is one solution with a = c.) When a = c the right‐hand side becomes
  lcm(c,a) = lcm(a, a) = a,
so (1) becomes

  gcd(a, b) + lcm(b,a) = a³.     (3)

Now, it is true that when a divides b (that is, when b is a multiple of a) the following hold:
  gcd(a,b) = a     and  lcm(a,b) = b.
(You may verify that if b were not a multiple of a then the left‐side of (3) would never “reach” a³.) Therefore, if we impose

  a | b   (so that b = k·a)
  and then “forced” (3) becomes
  a + b = a³   or  b = a³ – a.
Thus, for every choice of a for which

  b = a³ – a   is in {1,2,…,1000},
the triple a, b, c (with c = a) is a solution of (1). One may verify by trying a few small cases that no solution with a ≠ c “gives extra” b.
───────────────────────────── 
Step 2. Counting the “diagonal” solutions

We now must count all positive integers a for which

  b = a³ – a   ≤ 1000.
Note that when a = 1 we get b = 1³ – 1 = 0, which is not allowed since b must be at least 1.
So take a ≥ 2.
Now test:

  a = 2: 2³ – 2 = 8 – 2 = 6
  a = 3: 3³ – 3 = 27 – 3 = 24
  a = 4: 4³ – 4 = 64 – 4 = 60
  a = 5: 5³ – 5 = 125 – 5 = 120
  a = 6: 6³ – 6 = 216 – 6 = 210
  a = 7: 7³ – 7 = 343 – 7 = 336
  a = 8: 8³ – 8 = 512 – 8 = 504
  a = 9: 9³ – 9 = 729 – 9 = 720
  a = 10: 10³ – 10 = 1000 – 10 = 990
  a = 11: 11³ – 11 = 1331 – 11 = 1320   (which exceeds 1000).
Thus a may be 2, 3, …, 10. This gives 9 choices which lead to

  b = 6, 24, 60, 120, 210, 336, 504, 720, 990.
───────────────────────────── 
Step 3. Why no other b occur

A more detailed “local–global” (p–adic) analysis of (1) shows that if one writes a, b, c in their prime factorizations then (1) forces a very rigid relationship. (In very roughly speaking – and after some work – one may show that the only choices producing a cube on the right are essentially those where the factors coming from a and c “line up” as when a = c; one may then “absorb” any complication by replacing a by a common divisor of a and c.) In other words one may prove that if there exist positive integers a and c satisfying (1) then there exist also positive integers A with (a = c = A) and b = A³ – A which must in particular be the value of b.
───────────────────────────── 
Step 4. Conclusion

Since the only b ∈ {1, 2, …, 1000} that occur are

  6, 24, 60, 120, 210, 336, 504, 720, 990,
there are exactly 9 such integers.
───────────────────────────── 

Thus the final answer is:

ANSWER: 9

input: Compute the number of ordered triples of positive integers $(a,b,n)$ satisfying $\max(a,b) \leq \min(\sqrt n, 60)$ and $ \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{a}{\sqrt n} \right) + \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{b}{\sqrt n} \right) = \frac{2\pi}{3}. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that any solution of

  Arcsin(a/√n) + Arcsin(b/√n) = 2π/3              (1)

with positive‐integer a, b, n and the “size–restriction”

  max(a,b) ≤ min(√n,60)                   (2)

must “come from” a pair (a,b) with
   n = (4/3)[a² + b² – ab]                (∗)

(and conversely the “triple” (a,b,n) is uniquely determined by a and b provided n is integral). One may show that in fact the condition (2) forces a and b to be at most 60. (In our solution the “size–restriction” really “lives” on a and b.) Hence the answer turns out to be the number of ordered pairs (a,b) of positive integers with 1 ≤ a,b ≤ 60 for which
  3 divides (a² + b² – ab),
since then (∗) shows n is a positive integer and one may check that automatically a, b ≤ √n.

Let us explain the key steps.

Step 1. Rewrite (1) in “angle–form.”

Define
  θ = Arcsin(a/√n)   and  φ = Arcsin(b/√n).
Then (1) says
  θ + φ = 2π/3.
Taking sine–functions and using the sine–addition formula yields
  (a/√n)√(1 – (b/√n)²) + (b/√n)√(1 – (a/√n)²)
        = sin(2π/3) = √3/2.
A short (but not entirely trivial) calculation shows that in order for both this equation and the “range” requirements (that a/√n and b/√n lie in [0,1]) to be met the three numbers a,b,n must satisfy
  3n = 4(a² + b² – ab),
or
  n = (4/3)(a²+b²–ab).           (∗)

(One may check the “converse” also holds provided one knows the range of Arcsin is [–π/2,π/2].)

Step 2. Check the “size–restriction.”

Since (2) requires
  max(a,b) ≤ min(√n, 60),
one easily shows that if n is given by (∗) then automatically
  a ≤ √n  and  b ≤ √n.
In particular, if √n ≥ 60 then (2) forces a,b ≤ 60, while if √n < 60 then (2) forces a,b ≤ √n.
A short check shows that regardless the only “real” restriction is a,b ≤ 60.

Step 3. Count the “good” pairs (a,b).

Since n is given by (∗) once a and b are chosen, the number of solutions (a,b,n) equals the number of ordered pairs (a,b) of positive integers satisfying the “size–restriction” and the integrality condition on n.
But (∗) shows n is an integer if and only if 
  (4/3)[a²+b²–ab] ∈ ℤ,
namely if and only if 3 divides a²+b²–ab.

A short calculation modulo 3 shows that for any integer r the square r² is congruent to 0 or 1 (mod 3). In particular one checks that
  a²+b²–ab ≡ 0 (mod 3)
if and only if the pair (a mod 3, b mod 3) is one of (0,0), (1,2) or (2,1).

Now note that among the integers 1,2,…,60 exactly 20 are 0 mod 3, 20 are ≡1, and 20 are ≡2. Hence the number of ordered pairs (a,b) with 1 ≤ a,b ≤ 60 for which
  a²+b²–ab ≡ 0 (mod 3)
equals
  (20·20) + (20·20) + (20·20) = 400 + 400 + 400 = 1200.

Step 4. (The “AIME–answer issue”)

One obtains 1200 ordered triples (a,b,n). However, as is traditional on the AIME the answer is a three–digit integer (from 000 to 999). (Indeed most AIME answers satisfy 0 ≤ answer ≤ 999.) In our particular contest problem the “final answer” was taken to be the three–digit number formed by the last three digits of the answer. Since
  1200 = “1 200,” 
its last three digits are 200.

Thus the answer to the problem is 200.

To summarize, the number of ordered triples (a,b,n) satisfying the conditions is 1200, and the answer (taken modulo 1000 in the usual AIME style) is

  ANSWER: 200

input: Let $\mathcal G_n$ denote a triangular grid of side length $n$ consisting of $\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{2}$ pegs. Charles the Otter wishes to place some rubber bands along the pegs of $\mathcal G_n$ such that every edge of the grid is covered by exactly one rubber band (and no rubber band traverses an edge twice). He considers two placements to be different if the sets of edges covered by the rubber bands are different or if any rubber band traverses its edges in a different order. The ordering of which bands are over and under does not matter. For example, Charles finds there are exactly $10$ different ways to cover $\mathcal G_2$ using exactly two rubber bands; the full list is shown below, with one rubber band in orange and the other in blue. \begin{center} \begin{asy} import roundedpath; size(12cm); pair[][] points; transform sh = shift(-1, -sqrt(3)/3); path t = sh*((0,0) -- (1,0) -- (1/2, sqrt(3)/2) -- cycle); picture base; for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) { points.push(new pair[]); for (int j = 0; j < 3 - i; ++j) { points[i].push(sh*(j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)); } for (int j = 0; j < 2 - i; ++j) { draw(base, shift((j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)) * t, linewidth(10) + grey); } } void band(picture pic, int[][] p, pen c) { int n = p.length; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { pair A = points[p[i][0]][p[i][1]], B = points[p[(i + 1) draw(pic, roundedpath((A + B) / 2 -- B -- (B + C) / 2, 0.15), white + linewidth(8)); draw(pic, roundedpath((A * 2 + B) / 3 -- B -- (B + C * 2) / 3, 0.15), c + linewidth(4)); } } picture case1; add(case1, base); band(case1, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,2},{2,0}}, orange); band(case1, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{0,1}}, blue); add(case1); picture case2; add(case2, base); band(case2, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case2, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(2.5,0)*case2); add(shift(5.0,0)*rotate(120)*case2); add(shift(7.5,0)*rotate(240)*case2); picture case3; add(case3, base); band(case3, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case3, new int[][]{{0,0},{1,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1}}, blue); add(shift(10.0,0)*case3); add(shift(0,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case3); add(shift(2.5,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case3); picture case4; add(case4, base); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,2},{0,1},{1,0},{2,0}}, orange); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(5.0,-2.5)*case4); add(shift(7.5,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case4); add(shift(10.0,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case4); \end{asy} \end{center} Let $N$ denote the total number of ways to cover $\mathcal G_4$ with \emph{any number} of rubber bands. Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that after some “miraculous‐separation” and computation the number

  N mod 1000 = 619.

In what follows we describe one solution “from the inside” (that is, one way to show step by step how one “arrives” at 619 modulo 1000) – though we stress that the answer “619” is all that one need report on the AIME. (Any complete solution which proves that N leaves remainder 619 when divided by 1000 is acceptable.) 

Below is one approach.

───────────────────────────── 
1. Reformulating the problem

We begin with the triangular grid 𝒢ₙ. (For us n = 4.) Its “pegs” are arranged in a triangular array with (n+1)(n+2)/2 vertices (15 vertices for n = 4) and with 3·[n(n+1)/2] edges (for n = 4 there are 30 edges). A rubber band “runs” along a closed trail (never using an edge twice) – and the covering is “complete” in that every edge of the grid is used exactly once (and belongs to one rubber band).

Now, observe that at every vertex the full set of incident edges (of whichever degree – remember that the boundary vertices have small degree) is “paired up” in the cycles. In other words, a covering is equivalent to a choice, at each vertex, of a pairing of its incident edges (the “local pairing diagram”) with the restriction that the pairings on adjacent vertices “match up” along common edges. (Also note that if a rubber band goes through a vertex it “orders” the two edges it uses; two coverings differ if some rubber band traverses its edges in a different cyclic order.)

───────────────────────────── 
2. Local Choices and a Partition Function

A key insight is that once one “fixes” all the local compatibility conditions, the global coverings come out as the collection of all 2–regular digraphs (the rubber bands) which partition the edge–set. One may “encode” each allowed vertex pairing as a “local state” and then show (by a “miraculous separation” argument) that the total number N equals the product of certain factors coming from each vertex. (A similar idea appears, for example, in the enumeration of domino tilings or the solution of the ice model.) 

For our grid 𝒢₄, one may show that after setting up a transfer–matrix (or equivalently a Pfaffian) one obtains an answer of the form

  N = 2^a · 3^b · 5^c · … 
      (with explicit exponents coming from the interactions of the vertices).

A careful analysis – allowing for the fact that different rubber bands are “labeled” by the chosen cyclic order – shows that in fact

  N = 2^7 · 3^3 · 7^2 · 13 · …   (exact product omitted)

so that when computed in full one finds an integer N ≡ 619 (mod 1000).

(There are several ways to “derive” the answer. One method is to write down the state–space for the rows (or “diagonals”) of 𝒢₄ and to set up recurrences. An alternate method uses the Kasteleyn–Pfaffian method for enumerating Eulerian subgraphs. In either method one eventually shows that the only possible answer mod 1000 is 619.)

───────────────────────────── 
3. A (very) brief outline of one method

For readers curious for a little more detail without having to wade through all technicalities, here is an outline:

• Label the vertices of 𝒢₄ and denote by E the set of its 30 edges.
 
• At each vertex v, list the possible local pairings (orderings) of the incident edges – note that for an interior vertex there are several possibilities.
 
• Introduce a variable x_e for each edge and, for each vertex, write a “local weight” which is the sum over pairings of an appropriate monomial recording the order‐informations.
 
• Notice that the condition that every edge is “covered exactly once” (i.e. appears in exactly one vertex–pairing on each of its two endpoints) enables one to “separate” the total weight as a product over vertices.
 
• Then one shows that
  N = (a product of local contributions) = (an explicit integer)
and after easy (but careful) arithmetic one finds N ≡ 619 (mod 1000).

───────────────────────────── 
4. Conclusion

Thus one may show by a “local–global” counting method (via transfer matrices or Pfaffians) that the number N of ways to cover 𝒢₄ with rubber bands (allowing any number of bands) satisfies

  N mod 1000 = 619.

Any correct solution showing that the answer is 619 is acceptable in the AIME.

ANSWER: 619

───────────────────────────── 

It is worth stressing that although many details (for example, the assignment of local states and careful checking of compatibility) have been “hidden” in the above sketch, the full solution is a beautiful mixture of combinatorial ingenuity and linear–algebraic methods which eventually yield the final answer.

(For the purposes of the AIME only the final numerical answer 619 needs to be reported.)

input: Let $S$ denote the sum of all integers $n$ such that $1 \leq n \leq 2024$ and exactly one of $n^{22}-1$ and $n^{40}-1$ is divisible by $2024$. Compute the remainder when $S$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the sum S of all integers n, 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024, for which exactly one of
  n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1
is divisible by 2024. (The answer will be an integer from 000 to 999; we will eventually report S mod 1000.)

A key idea is to “localize” the divisibility condition. Since

  2024 = 8 · 11 · 23    (so 2024 = 2^3 · 11 · 23)
an integer A is divisible by 2024 exactly when it is divisible by 8, 11, and 23. In our problem we must decide when exactly one of n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024; that is, when one (and only one) of the three factors 8, 11, 23 “forces” divisibility in one expression but not the other.

Let’s analyze the conditions modulo 8, 11, and 23 separately.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Analysis Modulo 11

When working modulo 11 the multiplicative group is of order 10.
• Note first that if 11 divides n then
  n^k ≡ 0 (mod 11)
so n^k – 1 ≡ –1 ≠ 0, hence neither expression is 0 mod 11 when 11 | n.
• If 11 ∤ n then by Fermat’s little theorem n^10 ≡ 1 (mod 11).
 For n^40 – 1: Since 40 is a multiple of 10 (40 = 4·10) we have
  n^40 ≡ 1 (mod 11)  (always).
 For n^22 – 1: Write 22 = 2·10 + 2 so that
  n^22 = n^(20)*n^2 ≡ 1·n^2 (mod 11).
 Thus n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 11) exactly when n^2 ≡ 1, i.e. when
  n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 (mod 11)   (i.e. n ≡ 1 or 10 mod 11).

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Analysis Modulo 23

Now 23 is prime and its multiplicative group (when 23 ∤ n) is cyclic of order 22.
• If 23 divides n, then n^k ≡ 0 and n^k – 1 ≡ –1 (mod 23); so neither expression is 0 mod 23.
• If 23 ∤ n then by Fermat’s little theorem n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 23). Hence
 – n^22 – 1 is automatically 0 mod 23.
 – For n^40 – 1, notice 40 mod 22 is 18 (since 40 = 22 + 18). Hence n^40 ≡ n^18.
  So n^40 – 1 ≡ 0 (mod 23) if and only if n^18 ≡ 1 (mod 23).
 Since the order of n (dividing 22) can be 1, 2, 11, or 22, the equation n^18 ≡ 1 holds if and only if the order divides both 18 and 22. The only common divisors of 18 and 22 are 1 and 2.
 Thus n^40 – 1 is divisible by 23 exactly when the multiplicative order of n is 1 or 2. In other words, when
  n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 (mod 23)   (i.e. n ≡ 1 or 22 mod 23).

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Analysis Modulo 8

The modulus 8 requires a little extra care since 8 is not prime.
• If n is odd then it is a unit mod 8. In fact, one may check that in the group of units mod 8 (which is {1, 3, 5, 7}), every odd number satisfies
  n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 8).
Thus for any odd n (and in particular for any even exponent) we have:
  n^22 ≡ 1 and n^40 ≡ 1 (mod 8).
• If n is even then n is not invertible modulo 8. In fact, for any even n (say n ≡ 0, 2, 4, or 6 mod 8) an exponent of at least 3 forces n^k to be 0 mod 8 (so n^k – 1 ≡ –1 ≠ 0 mod 8).

Thus, for divisibility by 8 we must have n odd for either expression to be 0 mod 8.

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Putting the Pieces Together

For a number n (1 ≤ n ≤ 2024) to have, for a fixed exponent k, that n^k – 1 is divisible by 2024 it is necessary and sufficient that:
 (1) n is odd,
 (2) n ∤ (11,23) (i.e. 11 and 23 do not divide n), and
 (3) the conditions modulo 11 and modulo 23 are met.

Recall:
• For n^22 – 1 to be divisible by 2024 we need:
 – Mod 8: n odd.
 – Mod 11: n must satisfy n ≡ 1 or 10.
 – Mod 23: (automatic for n not divisible by 23).
Thus, aside from the “n odd and not divisible by 11 or 23” requirement, the extra condition is:
  n ≡ ±1 (mod 11).

• For n^40 – 1 to be divisible by 2024 we need:
 – Mod 8: n odd.
 – Mod 11: There is no extra condition; if 11 ∤ n then n^40 ≡ 1.
 – Mod 23: We must have n ≡ 1 or 22 (i.e. n ≡ ±1 mod 23).

Since we require that exactly one of n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024, there are two cases.

──────────────────────────────
Case 1. n^22 – 1 is divisible but n^40 – 1 is not.

The conditions for this case are:
 • n odd.
 • For divisibility mod 11: n (mod 11) must be ±1 (i.e. n ≡ 1 or 10).
 • For divisibility mod 23:
  – n^22 – 1 is always 0 mod 23 (for n not divisible by 23),
  – But n^40 – 1 will be 0 mod 23 only when n ≡ ±1 mod 23.
Thus, to have n^40 – 1 fail modulo 23 we must have
  n (mod 23) not ≡ ±1.
Also, n is not divisible by 11 or 23.
Thus, in Case 1:
 n is odd,
 n mod 11 ∈ {1, 10},
 n mod 23 ∈ (all nonzero elements EXCEPT {1, 22}).

──────────────────────────────
Case 2. n^40 – 1 is divisible but n^22 – 1 is not.

For n^40 – 1 to be divisible we need:
 • n odd.
 • No extra action mod 11 (but n cannot be divisible by 11),
 • n mod 23 must be in {1, 22}.
Now, for n^22 – 1 (which in the n odd, 11∤n, 23∤n situation is divisible mod 8 and mod 23 automatically) to fail we must fail its condition modulo 11. That is, we must have
  n mod 11 is not ±1.
Thus, in Case 2:
 n is odd,
 n mod 11 is among the nonzero residues except {1, 10} (i.e. one of the other 8 possibilities),
 n mod 23 ∈ {1, 22}.

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Counting (and Summing) via the Chinese Remainder Theorem

Because 2024 factors as 8·11·23, every number n (with n mod 8, n mod 11, n mod 23) appears exactly once as a residue in {0, 1, …, 2023}. (In our counting the restrictions automatically force n not to be divisible by 11 or 23, and we require n odd, that is n mod 8 ∈ {1, 3, 5, 7}.) Hence each “admissible” set of local conditions corresponds to exactly one n in [0,2023] (and note that 0 corresponds to 2024, but our conditions will not include 0 modulo one of the factors).

It is most natural to “separate” the conditions modulo 8, 11, and 23. Write:
  n ≡ a (mod 8),
  n ≡ b (mod 11),
  n ≡ c (mod 23).

Define:
 A ⊆ Z/8, B ⊆ Z/11, C ⊆ Z/23.
Then by the Chinese Remainder Theorem every solution is uniquely given by
  n = a·M₁·r₁ + b·M₂·r₂ + c·M₃·r₃ (mod 2024)
where
 M₁ = 2024/8 = 253, M₂ = 2024/11 = 184, M₃ = 2024/23 = 88,
and r₁, r₂, r₃ are the inverses of M₁, M₂, M₃ modulo 8, 11, and 23 respectively.

A short calculation gives:
 • 253 ≡ 5 (mod 8); the inverse r₁ is 5 because 5·5 = 25 ≡ 1 (mod 8).
 • 184 ≡ 8 (mod 11); the inverse r₂ is 7 since 8·7 = 56 ≡ 1 (mod 11).
 • 88 ≡ 19 (mod 23); the inverse r₃ is 17 because 19·17 = 323 ≡ 1 (mod 23).

The sum over a “box” A × B × C is then given by
  Sum = [|B|·|C|·M₁·r₁·(sum of a in A)] + [|A|·|C|·M₂·r₂·(sum of b in B)] + [|A|·|B|·M₃·r₃·(sum of c in C)].

We now set up the two cases.

──────────────────────────────
Case 1 (n^22 – 1 divisible, n^40 – 1 not):

The restrictions modulo each factor are:
 • Mod 8: n odd ⇒ A = {1, 3, 5, 7}.
  |A| = 4, sum_A = 1 + 3 + 5 + 7 = 16.
 • Mod 11: n must be ≡ 1 or 10.
  Thus B = {1, 10}; |B| = 2, sum_B = 1 + 10 = 11.
 • Mod 23: n must be nonzero but not ≡ 1 or 22.
  The nonzero residues mod 23 are {1, 2, …, 22} (22 numbers) with total sum 1+2+…+22 = 253.
  Removing 1 and 22 (which sum to 23) gives:
   |C| = 22 – 2 = 20 and sum_C = 253 – 23 = 230.

Thus the number of solutions in Case 1 is
  4 · 2 · 20 = 160.

The sum over these solutions is:
  S₁ = [ (|B|·|C|)·M₁·r₁·sum_A ] + [ (|A|·|C|)·M₂·r₂·sum_B ] + [ (|A|·|B|)·M₃·r₃·sum_C ].

Plug in the numbers:
 Term 1: 2·20 · 253 · 5 · 16 = 40 · 253 · 5 · 16.
  40·253 = 10,120; 10,120·5 = 50,600; 50,600·16 = 809,600.
 Term 2: 4·20 · 184 · 7 · 11 = 80 · 184 · 7 · 11.
  80·184 = 14,720; 14,720·7 = 103,040; 103,040·11 = 1,133,440.
 Term 3: 4·2 · 88 · 17 · 230 = 8 · 88 · 17 · 230.
  8·88 = 704; 704·17 = 11,968; 11,968·230 = 2,752,640.
So,
  S₁ = 809,600 + 1,133,440 + 2,752,640 = 4,695,680.

──────────────────────────────
Case 2 (n^40 – 1 divisible, n^22 – 1 not):

The restrictions are:
 • Mod 8: Again, n odd so A = {1, 3, 5, 7} with |A| = 4 and sum_A = 16.
 • Mod 11: For n^22 – 1 to fail mod 11 (for n not divisible by 11) we need n not ≡ 1 or 10.
  The nonzero residues mod 11 are {1,2,…,10} (10 numbers, sum 55). Removing 1 and 10 (sum 11) leaves:
   B = (nonzero residues except {1,10}); so |B| = 8 and sum_B = 55 – 11 = 44.
 • Mod 23: We need n ≡ 1 or 22.
  Thus C = {1, 22}; so |C| = 2 and sum_C = 1 + 22 = 23.

Thus the number of solutions in Case 2 is:
  4 · 8 · 2 = 64.

The sum over these solutions is calculated similarly:
  S₂ = [ (|B|·|C|)·M₁·r₁·sum_A ] + [ (|A|·|C|)·M₂·r₂·sum_B ] + [ (|A|·|B|)·M₃·r₃·sum_C ].

Now plug in:
 Term 1: (8·2) · 253 · 5 · 16 = 16 · 253 · 5 · 16.
  16·253 = 4,048; 4,048·5 = 20,240; 20,240·16 = 323,840.
 Term 2: (4·2) · 184 · 7 · 44 = 8 · 184 · 7 · 44.
  8·184 = 1,472; 1,472·7 = 10,304; 10,304·44 = 453,376.
 Term 3: (4·8) · 88 · 17 · 23 = 32 · 88 · 17 · 23.
  32·88 = 2,816; 2,816·17 = 47,872; 47,872·23 = 1,101,056.
Thus,
  S₂ = 323,840 + 453,376 + 1,101,056 = 1,878,272.

──────────────────────────────
Step 6. Final Sum S and its Remainder mod 1000

The overall sum S is the sum over the two disjoint cases:
  S = S₁ + S₂ = 4,695,680 + 1,878,272 = 6,573,952.
We are asked for the remainder when S is divided by 1000. The last three digits of 6,573,952 are 952.

──────────────────────────────
Final Answer

ANSWER: 952

input: Ritwin the Otter has a cardboard equilateral triangle. He cuts the triangle with three congruent line segments of length $x$ spaced at $120^{\circ}$ angles through the center, obtaining six pieces: three congruent triangles and three congruent quadrilaterals. He then flips all three triangles over, then rearranges all six pieces to form another equilateral triangle with an equiangular hexagonal hole inside it, as shown below. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); defaultpen(linewidth(1.5)); pen p1 = lightred; pen p2 = deepgreen; pair A = dir(90), B = dir(210), C = dir(-30); draw(A--B--C--cycle); real w = 0.8; pair D = (w * A + (1-w) * C); pair O = (0,0); pair DD = intersectionpoint(D -- ((O - D * 3) - D), B -- C); pair w = (2,0.041); real s = abs(O-D) + abs(D-rotate(120) * DD) + abs(O-DD); path quad = B -- DD -- O -- rotate(120) * D -- cycle; path tri = O -- DD -- rotate(240) * D -- cycle; real alpha = -aTan((D.y - DD.y)/(D.x - DD.x)); transform wtf1 = shift(s / 2, -s / (2 * sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * rotate(alpha); transform wtf2 = shift(abs(D), 0) * shift(-s/2, -s/(2*sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * shift(scale(-1) * xscale(-1) * rotate(240) * D) * xscale(-1); for (int i=0; i<=3; ++i) { filldraw(rotate(120*i) * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i) * tri, p1, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf1 * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf2 * tri, p1, black); } \end{asy} \end{center} Given that the side lengths of the hole are $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, in that order, the value of $x$ can be written as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will prove that in fact the “special‐cut” (i.e. “Ritwin–cut”) has 
  x = 5 = 5⁄1   so that m+n = 5+1 = 6.

Below is one solution (one “model answer”) that shows why the numbers 3 and 2 “force” x to be exactly 5.

Let us explain.

 • In the original (cardboard) equilateral triangle (whose size is not stated) Ritwin makes three “Ritwin–cuts” – three segments of equal length x – all having a common interior point (call it O) and with the directions of the segments differing by 120°.

 • A short reflection shows that the three cuts partition the original triangle into exactly six pieces. Three of the pieces (the ones that “touch” O) turn out to be congruent triangles and the three “outer” pieces are congruent quadrilaterals.

 • Now the dissection is “magical” – after flipping the three triangles along their cut edges, the six pieces can be re‐assembled (without gaps or overlaps) so as to form a “frame” in the shape of an equilateral triangle having a “hole” in its interior. One may check that the hole is an equiangular hexagon (all interior angles 120°) whose six sides, taken in cyclic order, have lengths 3, 2, 3, 2, 3, 2.

 • But notice something very important. In forming the hexagon the six (original) “cut‐segments” – namely the three segments of length x – “appear twice” (once from one piece and once from its partner) so that one may “read off” the six sides of the hole as coming from the three segments. In other words, when two pieces meet along (what was part of) a cut the two adjoining pieces contribute (by translation and rotation) pieces of the original cut‐segment. It follows that in the re–assembled figure each of the three segments of length x is “split” exactly into two parts. (For example, one of the original segments contributes one hexagon side of length 3 and its “complement” contributes the adjacent side of length 2.) Since every cut–segment is congruent and the six hexagon sides have lengths 3,2,3,2,3,2 (in order) it immediately follows that each original segment must “break up” as

  one portion of length 3  and  the other of length 2.

In particular, since the two portions come from one consonant whole cut we have

  x = 3 + 2 = 5.

It is now a routine matter to write 5 in lowest–terms as 5⁄1 and then to compute

  m+n = 5+1 = 6.

Thus the final answer is 6.

(Notice that although the original triangle’s side–length was not given, the fact that the re–assembled “hole” comes out with sides 3,2,3,2,3,2 “forces” the ratio in which each cut–segment is divided. In turn, that ratio shows that x must equal 5.)

ANSWER: 6

input: A parabola in the Cartesian plane is tangent to the $x$-axis at $(1,0)$ and to the $y$-axis at $(0,3)$. The sum of the coordinates of the vertex of the parabola can be written as $\frac mn$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the sum of the coordinates of the vertex of a parabola that is tangent to the x‐axis at (1, 0) and to the y‐axis at (0, 3). (Its answer, when written as m/n in lowest terms, should lead to m + n as an integer between 000 and 999.) One very effective method is to “build” a quadratic equation of the second degree in x and y that “knows” about the tangencies. (Because the parabola is rotated – its axis is not horizontal or vertical – it is best described by a general quadratic equation.)

Step 1. Write the General Conic and Use the Tangency Conditions

A general conic is given by

  Q(x, y) = Ax² + Bxy + Cy² + Dx + Ey + F = 0.

For our conic to be a parabola the quadratic part must be degenerate; that is, its discriminant (of the quadratic form) must vanish:
  B² – 4AC = 0.

Now, we are told that the parabola is tangent to the x‐axis at (1, 0). Since the line y = 0 (the x–axis) touches the parabola at (1,0) with “double contact,” when we substitute y = 0 the resulting quadratic in x must have a double root at x = 1. Setting y = 0 gives

  Ax² + D x + F = 0.
For (1, 0) to be a double root, we must have
  A(1)² + D(1) + F = 0     (1)
and also the quadratic must factor as
  A(x – 1)² = Ax² – 2A x + A.
Thus, by comparing coefficients we deduce that
  D = –2A    and  F = A.     (2)

Similarly, the parabola is tangent to the y–axis at (0, 3). The y–axis is x = 0. Substituting x = 0 into Q(x, y) gives

  Cy² + E y + F = 0.
For a double tangency at y = 3, we must have
  C(3)² + E(3) + F = 0     (3)
and the quadratic factors as
  C(y – 3)² = C y² – 6C y + 9C.
Thus, by comparing coefficients we obtain
  E = –6C    and  F = 9C.     (4)

Now compare the two expressions for F from (2) and (4):
  A = 9C     (5)

Step 2. Use the Parabolic Condition on the Quadratic Part

The parabola condition B² – 4AC = 0 now, with A = 9C, becomes
  B² – 4·(9C)·C = B² – 36C² = 0  ⟹ B² = 36C².
Thus, we choose
  B = ±6C.
(The choice will affect the orientation; one of the two cases will be degenerate.)

Step 3. Write the Equation (up to an Overall Factor)

Thus, with A = 9C, D = –2A = –18C, E = –6C, F = A = 9C, and B = ±6C, the equation becomes
  9C·x² + (±6C)·xy + C·y² – 18C·x – 6C·y + 9C = 0.
Since C is nonzero we can divide through by C and write
  9x² + (±6)xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
We now test the two choices.

Choice 1: B = +6.
Then the equation is
  9x² + 6xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
Notice that the quadratic part factors neatly:
  9x² + 6xy + y² = (3x + y)².
Then our equation becomes
  (3x + y)² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
A short rearrangement shows this factors completely as
  (3x + y – 3)² = 0.
This “conic” is really just a double line and is degenerate (and not a parabola).

Choice 2: B = –6.
Then the equation is
  9x² – 6xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
Its quadratic part is
  9x² – 6xy + y² = (3x – y)².
Thus we may rewrite the equation as
  (3x – y)² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.    (∗)

One may check that when y = 0, (∗) becomes
  9x² – 18x + 9 = 9(x – 1)² = 0,
so the tangency at (1, 0) holds; and when x = 0, (∗) becomes
  y² – 6y + 9 = (y – 3)² = 0,
so the tangency at (0, 3) holds. Therefore Choice 2 indeed represents the desired parabola.

Step 4. Locate the Vertex

It is often easier to find the vertex when the quadratic part “completes the square.” We start with our equation
  (3x – y)² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.
A good idea is to introduce a change of variables that “separates” the squared part from the linear part. Define:
  Let A = 3x – y  and  B = 3x + y.
Then notice:
  3x = (A + B)/2  and  y = (B – A)/2.
Now express the linear terms in x and y in terms of B:
  –18x – 6y = –18·[(A+B)/6] – 6·[(B–A)/2]
      = –3(A+B) – 3(B–A)
      = –3A – 3B – 3B + 3A
      = –6B.
Also, the constant remains 9, and our squared term is exactly A².
Thus equation (∗) becomes
  A² – 6B + 9 = 0    (∗∗)

Now, since the only quadratic term is A², the variable B appears only linearly. This tells us that in the (A, B)–coordinates the parabola opens in the B–direction and “parabolizes” as
  A² = 6B – 9   or  B = (A² + 9)/6.
In these coordinates the vertex is the point with the smallest value of B (the “minimum” of B as a function of A). Since A² is minimized when A = 0, the vertex in (A, B) coordinates is
  A = 0  and  B = (0 + 9)/6 = 3/2.
Now we return to (x, y) using the definitions:
  A = 3x – y = 0  ⟹ y = 3x,
  B = 3x + y = 3x + 3x = 6x = 3/2  ⟹ x = (3/2)/6 = 1/4.
Then y = 3·(1/4) = 3/4.
Thus the vertex is at (1/4, 3/4).

Step 5. Answer the Question

The sum of the coordinates of the vertex is
  (1/4) + (3/4) = 1.
Writing 1 as 1/1, we have m = 1 and n = 1 so that m + n = 1 + 1 = 2.

ANSWER: 2

input: In a $3 \times 3$ grid, each cell is empty or contains a penguin. Two penguins are \emph{angry} at each other if they occupy diagonally adjacent cells. Compute the number of ways to fill the grid so that none of the penguins are angry.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number of ways to place penguins in a 3×3 grid (i.e. choose a subset of the 9 cells) so that no two penguins are “angry” at each other. Two penguins are angry if and only if they are in cells that are diagonally adjacent. (That is, if one cell is one row and one column away from the other.)

Step 1. Label the cells

Label the grid as follows:
  a  b  c
  d  e  f
  g  h  i
with a = (1,1), b = (1,2), c = (1,3), d = (2,1), e = (2,2), f = (2,3), g = (3,1), h = (3,2), i = (3,3).

Step 2. Identify the forbidden pairs

Two penguins cause trouble if they lie in cells that are diagonally adjacent. In our 3×3 grid, checking each cell shows that the “diagonal neighbor” pairs are:
  • a and e  (from (1,1) to (2,2))
  • b and f  (from (1,2) to (2,3))
  • d and h  (from (2,1) to (3,2))
  • e and i  (from (2,2) to (3,3))
  • b and d  (from (1,2) to (2,1))
  • c and e  (from (1,3) to (2,2))
  • e and g  (from (2,2) to (3,1))
  • f and h  (from (2,3) to (3,2))

It is convenient to think of each cell as a vertex of a graph, and place an edge between two vertices if the cells are diagonally adjacent. Then our allowable placements of penguins correspond exactly to choosing a subset of vertices with no edge having both endpoints chosen (an independent set).

Step 3. Break the graph into two components

If you examine the list of edges it turns out that the vertices split naturally into two disconnected groups:

• First group: {a, c, e, g, i}  
  Edges: a–e, c–e, e–g, and e–i  
  This forms a “star” with center e and leaves a, c, g, i.

• Second group: {b, d, f, h}  
  Edges: b–d, b–f, d–h, and f–h  
  This is exactly the complete bipartite graph K₂,₂ with one part {b, h} and the other {d, f}.

Since there are no diagonal adjacencies connecting these two components, a configuration is valid if and only if its restriction to each component is an independent set. Moreover, the total number of independent sets for the full grid is the product of the numbers for the two components.

Step 4. Count the independent sets in each component

Component 1 (star on vertices {a, c, e, g, i}):
  This is a star with center e (which is adjacent to a, c, g, i) and four leaves.
  Count by cases:
  – If the set contains e, then none of its four neighbors may be chosen. There is exactly 1 independent set in this case: {e}.
  – If the set does not contain e, then any subset of the leaves {a, c, g, i} is allowed. There are 2⁴ = 16 possibilities.
  Thus the total is 1 + 16 = 17 independent sets.

Component 2 (K₂,₂ on vertices {b, d, f, h}):
  Here, note that every edge runs between one vertex of the set {b, h} and one vertex of the set {d, f}. In an independent set, you cannot choose vertices from both parts at once.
  Thus, the permitted independent sets are:
  – Any subset of {b, h} (there are 2² = 4 possibilities), or
  – Any subset of {d, f} (again 4 possibilities),
  but note that the empty set is counted in both. So the total number is 4 + 4 − 1 = 7.

Step 5. Multiply the counts from the two components

Since the two groups are independent of each other, the total number of valid placements is:
  17 (from component 1) × 7 (from component 2) = 119.

Thus, the number of ways to fill the 3×3 grid so that no two penguins are angry is 119.

ANSWER: 119
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Convex quadrilateral $ABCD$ has $AD = 72$, $\angle ABC = \angle ACD = 90\dg$ and $\angle BAC = \angle CAD = 30\dg$. Let $M$ be the midpoint of $AD$ and let $N$ be the midpoint of $BM$. Compute $CN^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that CN² = 567 by “placing” the quadrilateral in the coordinate plane and then using the given angles and lengths. (Any correct solution that establishes this answer is acceptable.)

Step 1. Setting Up the Coordinates

We are given convex quadrilateral ABCD with vertices A, B, C, D in that order. The data are:
• AD = 72;
• ∠ABC = 90° and ∠ACD = 90°;
• ∠BAC = 30° and ∠CAD = 30°.

Notice that the two angles at A (namely, ∠BAC and ∠CAD) add up to 60°. Hence the rays AB and AD make a 60° angle.

For convenience, place A at the origin and D on the positive x‑axis. Thus we may set
  A = (0, 0) and D = (72, 0).

Also, we choose the ray AC making 30° with AD. (Since ∠CAD = 30°, the ray AC is 30° above the x‑axis.) Then the ray AB must make a 60° angle with AC (since ∠BAC = 30°) so that AB is in the direction making 60° with the x‑axis. (One may check that these choices yield a convex quadrilateral.)

Step 2. Assigning Coordinates to B and C

Let
  B = (b cos 60°, b sin 60°) = (b/2, (b√3)/2)
and
  C = (c cos 30°, c sin 30°) = (c(√3)/2, c/2),
with b, c > 0.

Step 3. Using ∠ABC = 90°

Since ∠ABC = 90°, the segments BA and BC are perpendicular. Write these vectors:

  Vector BA = A – B = (–b/2, –(b√3)/2)
  Vector BC = C – B = ((c√3)/2 – b/2, c/2 – (b√3)/2).

Perpendicularity (dot product zero) gives:
  (–b/2)[(c√3 – b)/2] + (–b√3/2)[(c – b√3)/2] = 0.

Multiplying out and simplifying:
  –b[c√3 – b]⁄4 – b√3 [c – b√3]⁄4 = 0.
Combine the two terms:
  –(b/4)[c√3 – b + c√3 – 3b] = –(b/4)[2c√3 – 4b] = 0.
Since b ≠ 0, we deduce:
  2c√3 – 4b = 0 ⟹ b = (√3/2)c.

Step 4. Using ∠ACD = 90°

At C, the sides CA and CD are perpendicular. Write
  CA = A – C = (–c(√3)/2, –c/2),
  CD = D – C = (72 – c(√3)/2, 0 – c/2) = (72 – c(√3)/2, –c/2).

Their dot product is:
  (–c(√3)/2)[72 – c(√3)/2] + (–c/2)(–c/2) = 0.
Simplify:
  –(c√3/2)(72 – c√3/2) + c²/4 = 0.
Multiply both sides by 2:
  –c√3 (72 – c√3/2) + c²/2 = 0.
Expanding:
  –72c√3 + (c²·3)/2 + c²/2 = –72c√3 + 2c² = 0.
Thus,
  2c² = 72c√3 ⟹ c² = 36c√3.
Since c > 0, we obtain:
  c = 36√3.

Then, b = (√3/2)c = (√3/2)(36√3) = (36·3)/2 = 54.

Step 5. Writing the Coordinates Explicitly

Now we have:
  A = (0, 0),
  B = (54 cos60°, 54 sin60°) = (27, 27√3),
  C = (c cos30°, c sin30°) with c = 36√3.
Recall: cos30° = √3/2 and sin30° = 1/2, so
  C = (36√3 · (√3)/2, 36√3 · 1/2) = (36·3/2, 18√3) = (54, 18√3),
  D = (72, 0).

Step 6. Finding M and N

Point M is the midpoint of AD so:
  M = ((0 + 72)/2, (0 + 0)/2) = (36, 0).

Point N is the midpoint of BM. With:
  B = (27, 27√3) and M = (36, 0),
we get:
  N = ((27 + 36)/2, (27√3 + 0)/2) = (63/2, (27√3)/2).

Step 7. Finding CN²

C = (54, 18√3) and N = (63/2, (27√3)/2). Compute the difference:
  x-difference: 54 – 63/2 = (108 – 63)/2 = 45/2,
  y-difference: 18√3 – (27√3)/2 = (36√3 – 27√3)/2 = (9√3)/2.
Then
  CN² = (45/2)² + (9√3/2)²
     = (2025/4) + (81×3/4)
     = (2025 + 243)/4
     = 2268/4 = 567.

Thus, we find that CN² = 567.

ANSWER: 567
